KTU expects students to draw this loop and explain each node. $$HCD_{cycle} = \{Empathy \rightarrow Define \rightarrow Ideate \rightarrow Prototype \rightarrow Test\}^{\infty}$$ The infinity superscript denotes that the process **never truly ends**. Each loop refines understanding, and insights from later stages often loop back to reshape earlier empathy findings. ### 2.4 How HCD Bridges the Three Divides — Mechanism Analysis **Bridging Mechanism 1 — Empathic Research Closes the Empathy Divide:** Empathic tools (interviews, shadowing) replace assumption with **evidence**. The output is a *validated* user need rather than a *projected* one. **Bridging Mechanism 2 — Visual Artifacts Close the Communication Divide:** HCD replaces jargon with **shared artifacts** — journey maps, personas, storyboards. A picture of a struggling grandmother is understood instantly; the phrase "decreased usability for geriatric cohorts" is not. **Bridging Mechanism 3 — Iterative Prototyping Closes the Execution Divide:** By building cheap, throwaway prototypes early, HCD surfaces real-world failures **before** capital is committed. Failure cost is reduced by orders of magnitude. ### 2.5 Real-World Utility of HCD in Engineering Fields | Engineering Domain | HCD Application Example | |--------------------|-------------------------| | **Biomedical** | Designing prosthetics by amputee-led co-design sessions | | **Civil** | Slum-upgradation projects where residents map their own water access routes | | **Mechanical** | Reframing a tractor's seat design around Indian farmer body-anthropometry rather than European averages | | **Computer Science / AI** | UX flows for differently-abled users in e-Governance apps | | **Electrical** | Solar lantern designs co-created with rural users for nighttime study | ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — The HCD Process in Detail Since this is a conceptual design topic rather than a mathematical derivation, this section performs an **exhaustive, step-by-step deconstruction** of each HCD stage, mirroring the rigor expected in KTU Part B answers. ### 3.1 STAGE 1: EMPATHIZE (The "Hear" Phase) **Purpose:** Move from assumption to understanding. **Step 1.1 — Set the Research Frame** Define the *boundary* of the design problem. Without a frame, research becomes directionless wandering. $$\text{Research Frame} = \{User_{segment}, Context_{environment}, Activity_{in\ scope}\}$$ **Step 1.2 — Conduct Ethnographic Fieldwork** Engage with users in their *natural environment*. Never interview users in a sterile conference room about a problem that occurs in a chaotic street market. **Step 1.3 — Apply the "5 Whys" Technique** Repeatedly ask *"Why?"* to surface root causes beneath surface symptoms. ``` User: "I hate this app." Why? → "It's slow." Why? → "It loads images every time." Why? → "Images aren't cached." Why? → "Backend doesn't send cache headers." Why? → "Original dev prioritized features over performance." ROOT CAUSE = Engineering culture, not technical debt alone. ``` **Step 1.4 — Synthesize into an Empathy Map** Capture observations across four quadrants: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Empathy Map} = \{&Says, Thinks, Does, Feels\} \\ &+ \text{Pains (fears, frustrations)} \\ &+ \text{Gains (wishes, needs, hopes)} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *what the user says* with *what the user actually does*. HCD stresses the **gap between Say-Do**. Always trust observed behavior over stated preference. ### 3.2 STAGE 2: DEFINE (The "Need" Phase) **Purpose:** Convert messy empathy data into a sharp, actionable problem statement. **Step 2.1 — Affinity Mapping** Cluster individual observations into themes. Each cluster becomes a thematic insight. **Step 2.2 — Construct the Point-of-View (POV) Statement** A canonical HCD sentence structure: $$POV = [\text{User}] + [\text{Needs}] + [\text{Because}] + [\text{Surprise Insight}]$$ **Worked Example:** > *"A **rural school teacher** needs **access to curriculum-aligned digital content offline** because **electricity and 4G connectivity are unreliable during school hours**, which she currently compensates for by carrying printed notes — a method that fails when content updates annually."* **Step 2.3 — Formulate "How-Might-We" Questions** Reframe the POV into a generative challenge: $$HMW = \text{How Might We} + [\text{Verb in open form}] + [\text{Context from POV}]$$ Example: *"How might we make offline digital content **auto-update** for teachers the moment they enter a connectivity zone?"* ### 3.3 STAGE 3: IDEATE (The "Create" Phase) **Purpose:** Generate a *volume* of diverse concepts before converging. **Step 3.1 — Defer Judgment** The first rule of ideation: **no criticism**. Criticism during ideation shrinks the solution space prematurely. **Step 3.2 — Apply Divergent Techniques** | Technique | Mechanism | |-----------|-----------| | **Brainstorming** | Free-flowing, time-boxed idea generation | | **SCAMPER** | Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put-to-other-use, Eliminate, Reverse | | **Bodystorming** | Physically act out the scenario to surface embodied constraints | | **Worst Possible Idea** | Inverting the problem to bypass mental blocks | **Step 3.3 — Cluster and Vote** Use dot-voting or impact-effort matrices to democratically narrow the concept pool. ### 3.4 STAGE 4: PROTOTYPE (The "Make" Phase) **Purpose:** Make ideas *tangible* at the **lowest possible cost and fidelity**. **Step 4.1 — Select Prototype Fidelity** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Fidelity Level} = \begin{cases} \text{Low (Paper, Sketch)} & \text{for early concept validation} \\ \text{Medium (Wireframe, 3D print)} & \text{for interaction validation} \\ \text{High (Functional MVP)} & \text{for final user testing} \end{cases} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4.2 — Build Multiple Prototypes** The KTU gold rule: **"Prototype in parallel, not in series."** Multiple rough prototypes prevent premature commitment. ### 3.5 STAGE 5: TEST (The "Learn" Phase) **Purpose:** Validate prototypes with real users and feed insights *back* into earlier stages. **Step 5.1 — Observe, Don’t Explain** Place the prototype in front of the user and watch what they do *without* coaching them. **Step 5.2 — Iterate** Testing almost always reveals flaws that re-open the Define or Ideate phase. This is **not failure — it is the entire point of HCD**. $$HCD_{iteration} = \text{Test results} \rightarrow \text{Refined Empathy} \rightarrow \text{Sharper POV}$$ ### 3.6 Bridging Summary — Why HCD Works The cumulative effect of the 5 stages can be expressed as: $$\begin{aligned} HCD_{value} &= \Delta_{\text{Empathy}} + \Delta_{\text{Clarity}} + \Delta_{\text{Validation}} \\ &= (\text{Know user}) + (\text{Frame problem}) + (\text{Prove solution}) \end{aligned}$$ Where each delta represents the **incremental reduction of risk** achieved at each HCD stage. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Complete HCD Cycle (Iterative Loop) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Empathize
Observe and Listen] --> B[Define
Frame the Problem] B --> C[Ideate
Generate Ideas] C --> D[Prototype
Make it Tangible] D --> E[Test
Validate with Users] E --> F{Insights Gained} F -- Major Reframe Needed --> A F -- Refinement Needed --> B F -- Concept Weak --> C F -- Build Flaw --> D F -- Solution Validated --> G[Implementation] G --> A ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** Notice how the arrows do **not** form a clean linear pipeline. HCD explicitly mandates that the **test stage feeds back** into any earlier stage. This non-linearity is the most commonly missed concept in KTU answers. ### 4.2 The Three Pillars of HCD (Sweet Spot Diagram) ```mermaid graph LR D[Desirability
User Wants It] --> SS[Innovation Sweet Spot] F[Feasibility
Engineers Can Build It] --> SS V[Viability
Business Can Sustain It] --> SS SS --> OUT[Successful HCD Solution] ``` ### 4.3 The Bridging Mechanism — How HCD Closes Divides ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BEFORE[Before HCD] A1[Designer Assumption] -->|Gap| A2[Engineer Solution] A2 -->|Mismatch| A3[Frustrated User] end subgraph HCD[HCD Process] B1[Empathy] --> B2[Define POV] B2 --> B3[Prototype] B3 --> B4[Test] end subgraph AFTER[After HCD] C1[Validated User Need] --> C2[Co-Created Solution] C2 --> C3[Satisfied User] end A3 -. Pain Drives .-> B1 B4 -. Learning Loops Back .-> C1 ``` ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an HCD Project Team ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Project Lead] --> R[Researcher
Ethnography and Interviews] P --> D[Designer
Personas and Journey Maps] P --> I[Ideator
Brainstorming Lead] P --> PR[Prototyper
Maker and Builder] P --> T[Tester
User Validation] R --> SYN[Synthesis Workshop] D --> SYN I --> SYN PR --> SYN T --> SYN SYN --> P ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Interpretation:** HCD is a **team sport**. No single engineer can perform all 5 stages. Cross-functional teams are mandatory for genuine HCD practice — a critical KTU board-exam emphasis. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Define Human-Centered Design. List any two of its core principles. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Human-Centered Design (HCD), as defined by ISO 9241-210, is an approach to interactive systems development that focuses on making systems usable by prioritizing user needs, human factors, and usability throughout the design lifecycle. **Two core principles:** 1. **Empathy-first approach** — Designers must deeply understand users' needs, contexts, and constraints before proposing solutions. 2. **Iterative refinement** — Solutions are continuously tested and improved through cycles of prototyping and feedback rather than delivered as a single finished artifact. *(Marks distribution: 1 for definition, 1+1 for two principles.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Distinguish between **desirability, feasibility, and viability** in the context of HCD. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Dimension | Question Answered | Owner in HCD Team | |-----------|-------------------|-------------------| | **Desirability** | Does the user *want* this solution? | UX Designer, Ethnographer | | **Feasibility** | Can it be *built* with current technology? | Engineer, Architect | | **Viability** | Can the solution be *sustained* as a business? | Product Manager, Business Strategist | A successful HCD solution must satisfy all three dimensions simultaneously — the so-called *Innovation Sweet Spot*. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **five stages** of the Human-Centered Design process with suitable examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss how HCD helps in **bridging the divide** between engineers and end-users in a real-world product development scenario. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Five Stages of HCD (7 marks):** 1. **Empathize (1.4 marks):** Conducting ethnographic research, contextual interviews, and observation to understand the user's world. *Example: IDEO's redesign of the shopping cart began by shadowing supermarket shoppers and cashiers for days.* 2. **Define (1.4 marks):** Synthesizing empathy data into a clear **Point-of-View (POV)** statement and **How-Might-We** questions to frame the right problem. 3. **Ideate (1.4 marks):** Generating a broad set of ideas through brainstorming, SCAMPER, and bodystorming without judgment. *Example: Generating 100+ concepts in 90 minutes during a Google Design Sprint.* 4. **Prototype (1.4 marks):** Building cheap, low-fidelity artifacts (paper sketches, wireframes, 3D-printed mockups) to make ideas tangible. 5. **Test (1.4 marks):** Validating prototypes with real users; observing what users do rather than what they say. Insights loop back to refine earlier stages. **(b) Bridging the Divide (7 marks):** The traditional product development model assumes the engineer *knows* what the user wants, leading to a failure rate of over 70% for new products (Forrester Research). HCD bridges this divide through three mechanisms: - **Empathic Research (2.5 marks):** Replaces assumption with field evidence. Engineers visit user environments, observe real workflows, and co-discover pain points. This eliminates the *Empathy Divide*. - **Shared Visual Artifacts (2 marks):** Personas, journey maps, and storyboards replace engineering jargon. A non-technical stakeholder instantly understands "Mrs. Lakshmi, 62, cannot read SMS in English" — far more impactful than "low digital literacy in geriatric demographics." - **Iterative Prototyping (2.5 marks):** Build-test-learn cycles surface real-world failures at minimal cost. A ₹5,000 paper prototype can reveal the same flaw that a ₹50 lakh launch would expose after six months — closing the *Execution Divide*. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently write HCD as a *linear* pipeline. **It is not.** Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if you draw HCD as 5 sequential boxes without showing the iterative feedback loops from Test → Empathize/Define. Always include an arrow curving back from Test to Empathize. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** What are the **three mindsets** of Human-Centered Design? Explain each with a suitable engineering example. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A team of mechanical engineering students wants to redesign the **public bus seating in Kerala** for elderly commuters. Apply the **first three stages** of HCD (Empathize, Define, Ideate) to this problem. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Three Mindsets of HCD (7 marks):** 1. **The Beginner’s Mindset (2.3 marks):** Approaching problems without expert assumptions. *Engineering Example:* A senior embedded engineer designing a smart irrigation controller must suppress the urge to immediately propose IoT solutions and instead first observe a farmer's actual daily irrigation routine — what time they wake up, how they currently decide watering duration, whether they can read digital displays at all.* 2. **Radical Empathy (2.4 marks):** Feeling what the user feels. *Engineering Example:* A biomedical engineer developing a low-cost prosthetic leg must spend a day walking on rough rural terrain with a makeshift leg to genuinely understand the user's pain, balance issues, and social stigma.* 3. **Bias Toward Action (2.3 marks):** Building to think rather than thinking to build. *Engineering Example:* Instead of debating for weeks whether a new turbine blade shape will be more efficient, a mechanical team 3D-prints three rough prototypes and wind-tunnel-tests all of them in two days.* **(b) HCD Application — Kerala Bus Seating (7 marks):** **Stage 1 — Empathize (2.5 marks):** - Conduct **contextual interviews** with 20 elderly commuters at KSRTC bus stands in Thiruvananthapuram and Thrissur. - **Shadow** them through ticket purchase, boarding, seating, and exit. - Build an **empathy map**: Says (expresses discomfort), Thinks (worried about falling), Does (holds seat handles tightly), Feels (dizzy during sharp turns), Pains (hard to rise from low seats), Gains (wants dignity and safety). **Stage 2 — Define (2 marks):** - **POV Statement:** *"An elderly KSRTC commuter **needs a seat that supports slow, safe boarding and rising** because **low, hard, edge-shaped seats cause knee strain and fall risk**, especially during peak-hour crowded boarding."* - **How-Might-We Question:** *"How might we design bus seats that **actively assist elderly users in sitting and standing** without slowing down passenger turnover?"* **Stage 3 — Ideate (2.5 marks):** - Generate 30+ concepts across **SCAMPER axes**: - *Substitute:* Spring-assisted tilting seats. - *Combine:* Hand-rails integrated with seat backrest. - *Adapt:* Hospital-bed-style rising levers. - *Modify:* Slightly elevated seat height (45 cm) for reduced knee flexion. - *Eliminate:* Sharp edges in favor of rounded cushion geometry. - Converge on the top 3 concepts using dot-voting and impact-effort matrix. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Block — Read Before Every KTU Exam** - ☐ **HCD Definition** = ISO 9241-210 approach prioritizing users, human factors, and usability in system design. - ☐ HCD is **iterative**, not linear. Always draw feedback loops from Test → Empathize/Define. - ☐ **Three Mindsets:** Beginner’s Mindset + Radical Empathy + Bias Toward Action. - ☐ **Three Pillars:** Desirability + Feasibility + Viability = Innovation Sweet Spot. - ☐ **Five Stages:** Empathize → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test. - ☐ **POV Formula:** `[User] + [Need] + [Because] + [Insight]`. - ☐ **HMW Formula:** `How Might We + [Open Verb] + [Context]`. - ☐ **Bridging Mechanisms:** Empathic research closes *Empathy Divide*; Visual artifacts close *Communication Divide*; Iterative prototyping closes *Execution Divide*. - ☐ **Key Distinction:** HCD ≠ UX. HCD is a macro-philosophy; UX is a sub-discipline. - ☐ **KTU Favourite Phrases** to memorize: *"Bias toward action," "Prototype in parallel," "Trust observed behavior over stated preference," "Cheap prototypes beat expensive arguments."* - ☐ **Common Pitfall to Avoid:** Do not write HCD as 5 linear boxes; do not confuse *desirability* with *feasibility*; do not omit the feedback loop arrow. - ☐ **Top Real-World Examples to Quote:** IDEO shopping cart, Google Design Sprint, d.school bootcamp bootleg, Apple iPhone iterative prototyping, Aravind Eye Care's empathic redesign of cataract surgery workflow.

topics.title: Designing for inclusivity in product development - Embracing user diversity - Long term impact - Sustainability encompassing environmental, economic and social dimensions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GZEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Designing for inclusivity in product development - Embracing user diversity - Long term impact - Sustainability encompassing environmental, economic and social dimensions # SECTION 1 — Core Definitions & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is "Designing for Inclusivity"? **Inclusive Design** is a methodology that enables and draws on the full range of human diversity. It is a process, not an outcome. The most common formal definition (adopted by the British Standards Institute **BS 7000-6:2005** and Microsoft's Inclusive Design Toolkit) is: > *"Inclusive design is a general approach to design in which a product, service, or environment is designed to be usable by as many people as possible, without the need for special adaptation or specialized design."* This is distinct from **Accessibility** (designing for people with disabilities) and **Universal Design** (a seven-principle North-American framework, originally from architecture). Inclusive design is the broader, human-centered lens that *intentionally* uses diversity as a source of innovation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU distinction** — KTU examiners often test the difference between these three terms. Memorize: *Accessibility = removing barriers for people with disabilities; Universal Design = prescriptive 7 principles; Inclusive Design = a design process that embraces diversity as a strength.* ## 1.2 Embracing User Diversity User diversity refers to the **complete range of human variation** that a product must serve. Engineers classify this diversity into three dimensions (per Microsoft's Persona Spectrum): | Dimension | Examples | |---|---| | **Permanent** | Blindness, colour-blindness, amputee, chronic fatigue | | **Temporary** | Broken arm, pregnancy, eye-infection, temporary injury | | **Situational** | Carrying a child, bright sunlight, noise in a cafe, gloves on | > [!NOTE] > **The Curb-Cut Effect (the foundational analogy)**: Sidewalk curb-cuts were originally mandated only for wheelchair users. But the same dip in the pavement turned out to be invaluable for parents with prams, delivery workers with trolleys, cyclists, and people pulling suitcases. **Designing for the "edge case" of a disabled user ends up benefiting every user.** This is the philosophical heart of inclusive design — what was once a niche requirement becomes a mainstream advantage. ## 1.3 Long-Term Impact of a Product Long-term impact is the **cumulative consequence** of a product on people, planet, and profit across its **entire life cycle** — from raw-material extraction, through manufacture, distribution, use, and end-of-life. KTU examiners frame this as the difference between **First-Cost Thinking** (what the buyer pays at the counter) and **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** thinking (the full economic, environmental, and social cost across decades). ## 1.4 Sustainability — The Triple Bottom Line **Sustainability** in the KTU 2024 Scheme is defined exactly as **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** sustainability, a term coined by **John Elkington in 1994**. The three dimensions are non-negotiable and inter-dependent: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Triple Bottom Line (TBL): A development is sustainable only when it is simultaneously —** > 1. **Environmentally sound** (planet) > 2. **Economically viable** (profit) > 3. **Socially equitable** (people) > > Removing *any one* leg collapses the "stool" of sustainability. This is the single most-tested definition in the KTU ESE for this module. ### The Intuitive Analogy — The Three-Legged Stool Imagine sustainability as a **three-legged wooden stool**. The seat is the future you want for humanity. If one leg (environmental) is shorter than the others, the stool tips and the future falls. Inclusive design is the *carpenter* who ensures each leg is shaped to support the *widest possible range of bodies* — which is why diversity and sustainability are taught together: a sustainable product that only serves wealthy, able-bodied users in rich countries is not, in fact, sustainable. --- ## 1.5 Quick-Reference Terminology | Term | One-line meaning | |---|---| | **Persona Spectrum** | Tool to map a disability across permanent, temporary, situational states | | **Assistive Technology** | A device used *by* a person with disability (e.g., screen reader) | | **Accessibility** | Removing barriers for people with disabilities | | **Universal Design** | Prescriptive 7-principle North-American framework (Ron Mace, 1997) | | **Inclusive Design** | A design *process* that uses human diversity as a creative resource | | **TBL** | Triple Bottom Line — Planet, Profit, People | | **Cradle-to-Cradle** | Closed-loop design where waste = food for another system | | **LCA** | Life Cycle Assessment — quantifies environmental impact across stages | | **6R Hierarchy** | Rethink → Refuse → Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover | | **TCO** | Total Cost of Ownership over the product's whole life | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Seven Principles of Universal Design (Ron Mace, 1997) These are examinable as **part (a) of a 7-mark question**. Memorize them verbatim: 1. **Equitable Use** — Useful and marketable to people with diverse abilities. 2. **Flexibility in Use** — Accommodates a wide range of individual preferences and abilities. 3. **Simple and Intuitive Use** — Easy to understand, regardless of user's experience or literacy. 4. **Perceptible Information** — Communicates necessary information effectively to the user. 5. **Tolerance for Error** — Minimizes hazards and adverse consequences of accidental actions. 6. **Low Physical Effort** — Can be used efficiently and comfortably with minimum fatigue. 7. **Size and Space for Approach and Use** — Appropriate size and space for approach, reach, manipulation, and use. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in KTU**: Question patterns frequently give you a product (e.g., a smartphone, an ATM, a website) and ask you to *evaluate* it against these principles. Practice applying each principle to a real artefact. ## 2.2 The Inclusive Design Process (BS 7000-6) A canonical four-stage process that maps directly to the **Double Diamond** design model taught in Module 1: 1. **Recognize diversity & inequality** — Audit the full range of users, including those currently excluded. 2. **Use inclusive methods & tools** — Persona Spectrum, exclusion calculator, co-design workshops. 3. **Reflect on what is being created** — Identify biases, blind-spots, and unintended exclusions. 4. **Solve with everyone in mind** — Design solutions that work for the widest range of users. ## 2.3 Sustainability Frameworks — Cheat Sheet ### 2.3.1 The Three Pillars (TBL) $$\text{Sustainability Score} = f(E_{env}, E_{econ}, E_{soc})$$ where each $E$ is a normalized index in $[0, 1]$. The product/system is sustainable only if all three $E \geq 0.6$ (a common KTU threshold). ### 2.3.2 The 6R Waste Hierarchy (most-tested in KTU) $$\text{6R Priority} = \text{Rethink} > \text{Refuse} > \text{Reduce} > \text{Reuse} > \text{Recycle} > \text{Recover}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Common student mistake**: Students list "Reduce, Reuse, Recycle" and stop. KTU expects all **6 R's** in the correct descending order. **Rethink** and **Refuse** are the most-impactful and the most-missed. ### 2.3.3 Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) $$\boxed{TCO = C_{acq} + C_{op} + C_{maint} + C_{disposal} - C_{residual}}$$ Where: - $C_{acq}$ = Acquisition cost (purchase price) - $C_{op}$ = Operating cost (energy, consumables) over life - $C_{maint}$ = Maintenance & repair cost - $C_{disposal}$ = End-of-life disposal cost - $C_{residual}$ = Resale or scrap value **Unit of TCO**: INR / USD / EUR over the product's service life (typically 5–10 years for consumer electronics). ### 2.3.4 Carbon Footprint (Basic) $$\boxed{CF = \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \times EF_i}$$ Where $A_i$ = activity data (e.g., kWh of electricity, litres of fuel) and $EF_i$ = emission factor (e.g., $\mathbf{0.82 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e/kWh}}$ for the Indian grid average, **CEA 2023 baseline**). ### 2.3.5 Circular Economy Material Loop $$\text{Linear Model} \rightarrow \text{Reuse} \rightarrow \text{Refurbish} \rightarrow \text{Recycle} \rightarrow \text{Regenerate (Cradle-to-Cradle)}$$ The Circular Economy aims for **zero waste** by treating the output of one process as the input of another (biological or technical nutrient cycles). ### 2.3.6 UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) The 17 SDGs adopted in 2015 provide the **global vocabulary** for sustainability. KTU often asks you to map a product to 3–4 relevant SDGs. Most-common mappings: - **SDG 3** — Good Health & Well-being - **SDG 7** — Affordable & Clean Energy - **SDG 9** — Industry, Innovation & Infrastructure - **SDG 10** — Reduced Inequalities (links directly to inclusive design) - **SDG 12** — Responsible Consumption & Production - **SDG 13** — Climate Action --- ## 2.4 Summary Cheat Sheet Table | Framework | Core Question It Answers | Best For KTU Part | |---|---|---| | **7 Principles of UD** | Is this artefact usable by all? | 3-mark definitions, 7-mark evaluation | | **Persona Spectrum** | Who are we excluding? | 7-mark case study | | **Triple Bottom Line** | Is this development truly sustainable? | 7-mark essay | | **6R Hierarchy** | How do we prioritize waste strategies? | 3-mark short answer | | **TCO** | What is the real cost over time? | 7-mark calculation | | **LCA** | What is the cumulative environmental impact? | 14-mark full question | | **Circular Economy** | How do we eliminate waste? | 7-mark essay | | **SDGs** | How does this align with global goals? | 3-mark or 7-mark mapping | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Inclusive design** reduces litigation risk (e.g., ADA lawsuits), expands addressable market (the "disability market" is over **1 billion people globally**, WHO 2023), and improves brand reputation. - **Sustainability engineering** is now mandated by **EU's Ecodesign for Sustainable Products Regulation (ESPR, 2024)** and India's **EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) rules** for e-waste, plastics, and tyres. Engineers designing for the Indian/global market in 2024+ *cannot* ignore sustainability. - The two converge: a sustainable product that no one can afford or use is a failed product — and an inclusive product that pollutes is equally failed. The job of the KTU-trained engineer is to design **at the intersection**. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > While this module is conceptual, KTU examiners still award marks for **quantitative reasoning**. Below are the most-likely worked calculations. Each step is fully written out — no shortcuts. ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — TCO Calculation for a Smartphone (7-mark style) **Problem Statement (typical KTU Part B):** A premium smartphone has a purchase price of ₹80,000. Its expected service life is 5 years. The user spends ₹3,000/year on accessories and ₹1,500/year on screen-protector replacements. Battery replacement (after 3 years) costs ₹6,000. Average annual electricity for charging is 12 kWh at ₹8/unit. The phone has a residual value of ₹12,000 at year 5. Calculate the TCO and comment on its sustainability implications. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify all cost components and time horizons.** $$\begin{aligned} C_{acq} &= \text{Rs. } 80{,}000 \\ C_{accessories} &= 5 \times 3{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 15{,}000 \\ C_{screenprotector} &= 5 \times 1{,}500 = \text{Rs. } 7{,}500 \\ C_{battery} &= \text{Rs. } 6{,}000 \text{ (one-time, year 3)} \\ C_{electricity} &= 5 \times 12 \times 8 = \text{Rs. } 480 \\ C_{residual} &= \text{Rs. } 12{,}000 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the TCO formula.** $$\begin{aligned} TCO &= C_{acq} + C_{op} + C_{maint} + C_{disposal} - C_{residual} \\ TCO &= 80{,}000 + (15{,}000 + 7{,}500 + 480) + 6{,}000 + 0 - 12{,}000 \\ TCO &= 80{,}000 + 22{,}980 + 6{,}000 - 12{,}000 \\ TCO &= \text{Rs. } 96{,}980 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Sustainability comment (this is where KTU marks are won or lost).** > *The TCO is ~21% higher than the sticker price of ₹80,000. More importantly, the device generates approximately $60$ kg of embodied $\text{CO}_2$ during manufacture. Designing for **modular repair** (replaceable battery, swappable screen) and **longer software-support windows** would reduce the social and environmental cost, demonstrating the intersection of inclusive + sustainable design.* --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Carbon Footprint of a Product (7-mark style) **Problem Statement:** A factory produces 10,000 units of a product per year. Each unit consumes 4 kWh of grid electricity and uses 2 kg of a raw material whose emission factor is 1.5 kg $\text{CO}_2$e/kg. Calculate the annual carbon footprint of the production line and suggest two engineering interventions aligned with SDG 13. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify activity data and emission factors.** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Electricity per unit} &= 4 \text{ kWh} \\ EF_{electricity} &= 0.82 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e/kWh (India CEA 2023)} \\ \text{Material per unit} &= 2 \text{ kg} \\ EF_{material} &= 1.5 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e/kg} \\ \text{Annual production} &= 10{,}000 \text{ units} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Compute carbon footprint per unit.** $$\begin{aligned} CF_{electricity,per\,unit} &= 4 \times 0.82 = 3.28 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e} \\ CF_{material,per\,unit} &= 2 \times 1.5 = 3.00 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e} \\ CF_{per\,unit} &= 3.28 + 3.00 = 6.28 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e/unit} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Scale to annual factory output.** $$CF_{annual} = 6.28 \times 10{,}000 = 62{,}800 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e} = 62.8 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e/year}$$ **Step 4 — Engineering interventions aligned with SDG 13 (Climate Action):** 1. **Material substitution** (Rethink): Replace 1 kg of the high-emission material with a low-carbon alternative ($EF \leq 0.5$ kg $\text{CO}_2$e/kg), saving $\sim 1{,}000$ kg $\text{CO}_2$e/year. 2. **Renewable energy integration** (Refuse/Reuse): Install a 50 kWp rooftop solar PV system, displacing ~50% of grid electricity and saving $\sim 31{,}400$ kg $\text{CO}_2$e/year. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Inclusive Design Evaluation Matrix (7-mark style) **Problem Statement:** Evaluate the standard Indian Railways ticket-vending machine (TVM) against the **Seven Principles of Universal Design**. Identify the principle it *best satisfies* and the principle it *most violates*, with a redesign suggestion for the latter. ### Analytical Evaluation Matrix | Principle | Current TVM Compliance | Score (1–5) | Evidence | |---|---|---|---| | Equitable Use | Low | 2 | No tactile buttons for blind users | | Flexibility in Use | Medium | 3 | Language toggle exists but limited | | Simple & Intuitive | High | 4 | Pictograms used alongside text | | Perceptible Information | **Lowest** | 1 | Visual-only interface | | Tolerance for Error | Medium | 3 | Refund option exists but slow | | Low Physical Effort | High | 4 | Standing-height reachable | | Size & Space | Medium | 3 | Crowded at peak hours | ### Redesign Recommendation To satisfy **Perceptible Information**, integrate (i) a 3.5 mm audio-jack with multi-language voice output, (ii) a Braille overlay on keypad, and (iii) haptic feedback on confirmation. This single redesign pushes Principle 4 from **1 → 4** and lifts the entire product toward SDG 10 (Reduced Inequalities). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Linking Inclusive Design ↔ Sustainability ↔ SDGs | Inclusive Design Action | Sustainability Pillar Strengthened | SDG Mapping | Engineering Lever | |---|---|---|---| | Modular / repairable product | Environmental + Economic | 12, 9 | Design-for-Disassembly | | Multi-language, multi-modal UI | Social | 10, 4 | Accessibility engineering | | Affordable product variant | Economic + Social | 1, 10 | Frugal engineering, JUGAAD | | Long software support | Environmental | 12, 13 | Right-to-repair compliance | | Local materials & labour | Environmental + Economic | 8, 12 | Localised supply chain | | Energy-efficient operation | Environmental + Economic | 7, 13 | Low-power electronics design | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Triple Bottom Line as an Inter-locking Venn System ```mermaid flowchart TB env["ENVIRONMENTAL
Planet
Carbon, Water, Biodiversity"] econ["ECONOMIC
Profit
Viability, Jobs, Value"] soc["SOCIAL
People
Equity, Health, Inclusion"] center["SUSTAINABILITY
Triple Bottom Line
Elkington 1994"] env --- center econ --- center soc --- center env --- econ econ --- soc soc --- env ``` **Reading the diagram**: A sustainable product must sit in the central intersection of all three circles. A "green" product (only environmental) is **not** sustainable. A "profitable but polluting" product is **not** sustainable. This is the most-asked conceptual diagram in KTU ESE for this module. --- ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Microsoft Persona Spectrum (Permanent → Temporary → Situational) ```mermaid flowchart LR p1["PERMANENT
Blindness
Deafness
Amputation
Chronic pain"] t1["TEMPORARY
Broken arm
Eye infection
Pregnancy
Ear infection"] s1["SITUATIONAL
Holding baby
Bright sunlight
Loud cafe
Wearing gloves"] design["INCLUSIVE DESIGN
Touchscreen
Voice UI
High contrast
Large targets"] p1 --> design t1 --> design s1 --> design out1["Mainstream users
benefit (Curb-cut effect)"] design --> out1 ``` --- ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — The 6R Waste Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD R1["R1 RETHINK
Question the need"] R2["R2 REFUSE
Say no to waste"] R3["R3 REDUCE
Use less material"] R4["R4 REUSE
Use again as-is"] R5["R5 RECYCLE
Process into raw"] R6["R6 RECOVER
Energy from waste"] R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 --> R5 --> R6 note1["Most impactful end
of the hierarchy"] R6 -.- note1 ``` > [!NOTE] > KTU examiners expect you to draw this hierarchy **top-down**, with arrows pointing from most-preferred (Rethink) to least-preferred (Recover). Energy recovery (incineration) sits at the *bottom* — a fact students frequently get wrong. --- ## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Product Life Cycle Stages (Cradle-to-Grave) ```mermaid flowchart LR s1["S1 RAW MATERIAL
Extraction"] s2["S2 MANUFACTURE
Fabrication"] s3["S3 DISTRIBUTION
Logistics"] s4["S4 USE PHASE
Operation"] s5["S5 END OF LIFE
Disposal / Recycle"] s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 impact["Cumulative environmental impact
accumulates across all stages"] s1 -.- impact s2 -.- impact s3 -.- impact s4 -.- impact s5 -.- impact c2c["Circular Economy target:
S5 becomes S1 again"] s5 -.- c2c ``` --- ## 4.5 Diagram 5 — The Inclusive Design Process (BS 7000-6, mapped to Double Diamond) ```mermaid flowchart TB p1["P1 RECOGNIZE
Map diversity
Identify exclusion"] p2["P2 USE INCLUSIVE TOOLS
Persona Spectrum
Co-design workshops"] p3["P3 REFLECT
Audit bias
Surface assumptions"] p4["P4 SOLVE FOR EVERYONE
Universal solutions
Modular options"] dd1["DOUBLE DIAMOND STAGE 1
DISCOVER"] dd2["DOUBLE DIAMOND STAGE 2
DEFINE"] dd3["DOUBLE DIAMOND STAGE 3
DEVELOP"] dd4["DOUBLE DIAMOND STAGE 4
DELIVER"] p1 --> dd1 dd1 --> p2 p2 --> dd2 dd2 --> p3 p3 --> dd3 dd3 --> p4 p4 --> dd4 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]` **Differentiate between Inclusive Design, Accessible Design, and Universal Design. Give one real-world example for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Inclusive Design**: A *design process* that uses the full range of human diversity as a source of innovation. *Example*: Microsoft's Adaptive Controller — designed *with* gamers with disabilities, benefits all gamers. - **Accessible Design**: The practice of making products *usable by people with disabilities* by removing barriers. *Example*: Wheelchair ramps added to a building after construction. - **Universal Design**: A set of *seven prescriptive principles* (Ron Mace, 1997) for designing artefacts usable by all, without adaptation. *Example*: OXO Good Grips kitchen tools — large soft handles usable by people with arthritis, children, and everyone else. *(1 mark per correct differentiation + example. Total: 3 marks.)* --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]` **List the six R's of the waste hierarchy in their correct order of priority. Why is "Rethink" placed at the top?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The 6R's in descending order of priority are: **Rethink → Refuse → Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover**. "Rethink" is placed at the top because the *most sustainable* product is the one that was never made wastefully in the first place. By questioning the fundamental need for a product, redesigning its function, or choosing a service-based alternative (e.g., sharing economy), we avoid waste generation at the design stage itself — which is more effective than any downstream recycling. *(2 marks for the list, 1 mark for the justification.)* --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Rule**: Each Part-B question has **internal choice** — answer either Question A *or* Question B. Each question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts. --- ### **Question B1A** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model]` — **14 Marks** **(a)** Explain the **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** framework in detail. Using any one engineering product of your choice, evaluate how it satisfies (or fails) the three pillars of TBL. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **Seven Principles of Universal Design**. Apply any three of these principles to evaluate the design of a **smartphone-based UPI payment app** used in India, and suggest one improvement for each. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for B1A(a) — 7 marks **Definition (2 marks):** The Triple Bottom Line, coined by **John Elkington in 1994**, expands the traditional accounting framework (which only tracks financial profit) to include **environmental** and **social** performance alongside **economic** performance. A development is *sustainable* only when it advances all three pillars simultaneously. **Three Pillars (3 marks):** 1. **Environmental (Planet)**: Resource efficiency, pollution prevention, climate action, biodiversity protection. Measured by carbon footprint, water use, LCA scores. 2. **Economic (Profit)**: Long-term financial viability, decent jobs, fair wages, market competitiveness. Measured by ROI, TCO, life-cycle cost. 3. **Social (People)**: Equity, inclusion, health, safety, labour rights, community benefit. Measured by Gini coefficient, accessibility index, social return on investment (SROI). **Applied Evaluation: Electric Two-Wheeler in India (2 marks):** - *Environmental*: ✓ Reduces tailpipe emissions; ✓ uses electricity (cleaner than petrol). ✗ Manufacturing emissions from lithium-ion battery; ✗ grid electricity partly coal-based. - *Economic*: ✓ Low running cost (~₹0.5/km vs ₹2.5/km for petrol); ✗ High upfront cost (₹1.2 lakh vs ₹80k). - *Social*: ✓ Reduces urban air pollution (health benefit); ✗ Currently unaffordable for low-income groups — equity gap. **Verdict**: The electric two-wheeler is *partially* sustainable — strong on environmental pillar, mixed on economic, weak on social equity. To be truly TBL-compliant, it needs lower upfront cost (subsidies, local manufacturing) and a robust battery-recycling ecosystem. #### Model Solution for B1A(b) — 7 marks | Principle (of the 7) | Evaluation of UPI App | Improvement | |---|---|---| | **Perceptible Information** (2 marks) | The app uses visual QR codes & numeric PINs — fails for blind users. | Add voice-over screen-reader support (TalkBack/VoiceOver), audio confirmation of transaction success. | | **Simple & Intuitive Use** (2 marks) | UPI PIN entry is simple but error-prone; users forget PIN, leading to failed transactions. | Add biometric confirmation (fingerprint / face) as optional fallback. | | **Tolerance for Error** (2 marks) | Sending money to wrong VPA (Virtual Payment Address) is irreversible — no undo. | Introduce a 5-second "confirm or cancel" window before final settlement. | | **Conclusion** (1 mark) | UPI is a global leader in digital payments but still has inclusion gaps for the elderly, visually impaired, and first-time smartphone users. Applying UD principles makes it more equitable (aligned with SDG 10). | --- ### **Question B1B** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model]` — **14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** **(a)** Define **Sustainability** and explain the **6R waste hierarchy**. How does the **Circular Economy** differ from the traditional **Linear Economy**? Illustrate with the example of a plastic water bottle. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is the **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)**? An LED bulb (₹500) lasts 5 years and uses 8 W; an incandescent bulb (₹30) lasts 1 year and uses 60 W. Electricity costs ₹8/unit. Calculate the 5-year TCO for each and comment on which is more economically sustainable. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for B1B(a) — 7 marks **Sustainability Definition (1 mark):** Sustainability is the ability to meet the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs (Brundtland Report, 1987). In product design, it is operationalized through the **Triple Bottom Line**. **6R Waste Hierarchy (2 marks):** The 6R's in descending priority are **Rethink → Refuse → Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover**. The hierarchy guides designers to first prevent waste at the design stage (most impactful) and only resort to energy recovery as a last resort. **Linear vs Circular Economy — Plastic Water Bottle (4 marks):** | Aspect | Linear Economy ("Take-Make-Dispose") | Circular Economy ("Make-Use-Return-Regenerate") | |---|---|---| | Resource flow | One-way | Closed-loop | | Plastic bottle example | Crude oil → PET bottle → use → landfill | PET bottle → use →回收 → re-granulate → new bottle | | Waste | High — bottle persists 450 years | Minimal — bottle becomes raw material again | | Design intent | Designed for single use | Designed for 100+ cycles (Cradle-to-Cradle) | | Environmental outcome | Ocean plastic, microplastics | Zero waste to landfill | The Circular Economy treats **waste = food** for another industrial or biological cycle, requiring **Design-for-Disassembly** and **Design-for-Recycling** from the start. #### Model Solution for B1B(b) — 7 marks **TCO Definition (1 mark):** $$TCO = C_{acq} + C_{op} + C_{maint} - C_{residual}$$ **LED Bulb Calculation (2.5 marks):** - Acquisition: ₹500 - Electricity over 5 years: $8 \text{ W} \times 5 \times 365 \times 6 \text{ h/day} \times ₹8/\text{kWh} = ₹8 \times 5 \times 365 \times 6 / 1000 = ₹87.6$ - (Note: $8 \text{ W} \times 6 \text{ h} \times 365 \times 5 = 87.6$ kWh; $87.6 \times 8 = ₹700.8$) $$\begin{aligned} TCO_{LED} &= 500 + 700.8 = \text{Rs. } 1{,}200.8 \end{aligned}$$ **Incandescent Bulb Calculation (2.5 marks):** - Acquisition: 5 bulbs × ₹30 = ₹150 (one replaced every year) - Electricity over 5 years: $60 \text{ W} \times 6 \text{ h} \times 365 \times 5 = 657$ kWh → $657 \times 8 = ₹5{,}256$ $$\begin{aligned} TCO_{Inc} &= 150 + 5{,}256 = \text{Rs. } 5{,}406 \end{aligned}$$ **Comment (1 mark):** The LED bulb is **~4.5× cheaper** over 5 years, even though its sticker price is **~17× higher**. This is the canonical KTU example to demonstrate why **TCO thinking beats first-cost thinking** — and why the more sustainable product is also the more economically rational one in the long run. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic** > > 1. **Confusing "Accessibility" and "Inclusive Design"** — They are *not* synonyms. Accessibility = barrier removal for disabled users; Inclusive Design = a process embracing *all* diversity. Examiners deduct 1 mark if you use them interchangeably. > 2. **Forgetting the third pillar** — Students often list only environment + economy and forget **social**. KTU explicitly tests whether you remember all three. > 3. **Missing the 6th R** — Always list **all six** R's: Rethink, Refuse, Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover. Stopping at three = partial credit. > 4. **Sticker-price thinking in TCO questions** — A common mistake is to compare just the purchase price. Always compute electricity, maintenance, and disposal costs over the *full life*. > 5. **Forgetting units and currency** — Always write ₹, kWh, kg $\text{CO}_2$e. Numerical answer without unit = 0.5 mark deduction. > 6. **Linear vs Circular confusion** — "Recycling" alone is *not* a Circular Economy. True Circular Economy = closed-loop, zero-waste, Cradle-to-Cradle. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > Use this as the last 60-second scan before the exam. - **Inclusive Design** is a *process*, not an outcome. It uses the **Persona Spectrum** (permanent / temporary / situational diversity) as its core tool. - The **Curb-Cut Effect** explains why designing for the edge-case benefits the mainstream. - **Universal Design** has **7 prescriptive principles** (Mace, 1997) — Equitable, Flexible, Simple, Perceptible, Tolerant, Low-Effort, Sized-for-All. - **Sustainability = Triple Bottom Line**: **Planet + Profit + People**. All three are non-negotiable. - **6R hierarchy** (top-down): Rethink → Refuse → Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover. - **TCO formula**: $TCO = C_{acq} + C_{op} + C_{maint} + C_{disposal} - C_{residual}$. - **Carbon Footprint**: $CF = \sum A_i \times EF_i$, with Indian grid average $\mathbf{0.82 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e/kWh}}$ (CEA 2023). - **Circular Economy** is closed-loop, Cradle-to-Cradle; **Linear Economy** is Take-Make-Dispose. - **SDGs** (UN 2015) provide the global vocabulary; map products to SDG 3, 7, 9, 10, 12, 13. - **Engineering levers** at the intersection of inclusivity + sustainability: modular repair, frugal design, local materials, multi-modal UI, long software support, low-power electronics. - **Key quantitative takeaways**: LED beats incandescent 4.5× over 5 years; TCO usually exceeds sticker price by 15–25%; carbon footprint scales linearly with activity data.

topics.title: Technology Readiness Level in the innovation life-cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GZEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Technology Readiness Level in the innovation life-cycle # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Technology Readiness Level (TRL)** is a standardized, globally adopted measurement framework that systematically assesses the **maturity of a technology** along a nine-stage continuum — from the earliest observation of basic scientific principles (TRL 1) to the fully proven, commercially deployed system operating in its real-world environment (TRL 9). The framework was originally conceptualized by **NASA** in the 1970s to provide a uniform vocabulary for evaluating the risk and progression of aerospace technologies, and was later standardized by the **European Union (Horizon 2020/Europe framework)**, the **U.S. Department of Defense (DoD)**, **ISRO**, and adopted as a benchmark by the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for innovation and product development governance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Definition for KTU Board Exam:** > *Technology Readiness Level (TRL) is a 9-point graduated scale (TRL 1 to TRL 9) used to measure the technological maturity of a concept, component, or system as it progresses from fundamental research to full commercial deployment within the innovation life-cycle.* ## 1.2 The Nine-Tier Quick Reference (Formal Terminology) | TRL Index | Formal Designation (KTU 2024 terminology) | |:---------:|:------------------------------------------| | **TRL 1** | Basic Principles Observed | | **TRL 2** | Technology Concept Formulated | | **TRL 3** | Experimental Proof of Concept | | **TRL 4** | Technology Validated in Laboratory | | **TRL 5** | Technology Validated in Relevant Environment | | **TRL 6** | Technology Demonstrated in Relevant Environment | | **TRL 7** | System Prototype Demonstrated in Operational Environment | | **TRL 8** | System Complete and Qualified | | **TRL 9** | Actual System Proven in Successful Operational Environment | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sapling to Skyline" Metaphor To a first-year KTU student, the TRL scale can be visualized as the **growth journey of a building project**: - **TRL 1–2 (The Idea)**: An architect sketches a unique self-cooling skyscraper on paper. We have *observed* a basic physical principle (heat reflection) and *formulated* a concept. The drawing exists, but no brick has been laid. - **TRL 3 (The Cardboard Model)**: A 1:50 scale foam model is built on a desk to *prove* that the curved geometry can deflect sunlight. It is not yet a building, but the *concept works in principle*. - **TRL 4 (The Lab Test)**: A single reflective glass panel is tested in a climate-controlled chamber to confirm the thermal prediction. - **TRL 5–6 (The Pilot Wing)**: An actual 3-storey wing of the building is built in a desert to *demonstrate* the technology in a *real (relevant) environment*. - **TRL 7 (The Mini-Skyline)**: The complete building structure is constructed and tested with real occupants walking through it — *operational environment*. - **TRL 8 (The Certified Building)**: The skyscraper is built to final specifications and *qualified* by structural and safety auditors. - **TRL 9 (The Landmark in Use)**: The building is fully operational, hosting thousands daily, and *proven* over years of use. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in Design Thinking:** TRL provides the *objective evidence trail* that allows designers, investors, and regulators to confidently decide "Is this innovation ready to graduate from the lab to the market?" ## 1.4 The Innovation Life-Cycle Context The *innovation life-cycle* (also called the *technology life-cycle*) consists of four macro-phases. TRL is the **diagnostic instrument** that pinpoints exactly where a technology sits inside this cycle: | Innovation Phase | Corresponding TRL Range | Activity Focus | |:-----------------|:------------------------|:---------------| | **Research** | TRL 1 – TRL 3 | Discovery and proof of concept | | **Development** | TRL 4 – TRL 6 | Validation and prototyping | | **Demonstration** | TRL 7 – TRL 8 | Operational qualification | | **Deployment** | TRL 9 | Commercial rollout and continuous improvement | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Logic of TRL — A Step-by-Step Deconstruction The TRL framework is *not* a simple linear checklist; it is a **risk-decay instrument**. As a technology climbs the TRL ladder, three things systematically happen: 1. **Uncertainty shrinks** — technical, market, and integration risks are progressively resolved. 2. **Capital intensity rises** — investment jumps from small research grants (TRL 1–3) to large-scale industrial capital (TRL 7–9). 3. **Decision gates tighten** — go/no-go reviews become stricter at each level transition. ### Step 1 — Scientific Origin (TRL 1) At TRL 1, the innovation is in the *realm of natural science*. Researchers observe a phenomenon (e.g., graphene's electron mobility) and write papers. **No applied engineering work has begun.** ### Step 2 — Concept Articulation (TRL 2) Researchers *translate* the science into a potential application. Hypotheses are formed: *"Can graphene replace silicon in transistors?"* — but no experimental proof yet exists. ### Step 3 — Proof of Concept (TRL 3) Active R&D begins. **Analytical studies** (simulations, mathematical models) and **laboratory experiments** are conducted on individual components to *validate* the hypothesis. This is the "kitchen-table prototype" stage. ### Step 4 — Laboratory Validation (TRL 4) The individual components are now **integrated** into a basic breadboard. The system works in a *controlled lab* — a "best case" environment. Failures here are expected and cheap to fix. ### Step 5 — Relevant Environment Validation (TRL 5) A **high-fidelity prototype** is built and tested in a *simulated real-world environment*. For a drone, this might be a wind tunnel. For a drug, this is the preclinical animal-trial phase. ### Step 6 — Relevant Environment Demonstration (TRL 6) A **representative system** is demonstrated. Stakeholders (industry, investors) can now see the technology working in conditions that closely mimic the intended use. ### Step 7 — Operational Prototype (TRL 7) The full system is **deployed in the actual operational environment** — a pilot project. The technology is now being used by intended users, but is not yet a commercial product. ### Step 8 — System Qualification (TRL 8) The technology is **manufactured at commercial scale**, certified, and tested against regulatory standards. All design, integration, and quality issues are resolved. ### Step 9 — Operational Provenance (TRL 9) The technology is **fully commercialized** and has a track record of successful operation. Continuous improvement loops now dominate over breakthrough risk. > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked to "explain the innovation life-cycle," students must explicitly *anchor each TRL stage to a real-world activity*. Generic definitions alone score poorly; *contextual anchoring* scores full marks. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield TRL Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **single most important table for KTU exams**. Memorize the **evidence required** at each TRL to score full marks on Part B questions. | TRL | Designation | Required Evidence (Exit Criterion) | Innovation Phase | Risk Profile | |:---:|:-----------|:------------------------------------|:-----------------|:-------------| | **1** | Basic Principles Observed | Peer-reviewed publication of underlying science | Research | Speculative | | **2** | Concept Formulated | Documented application hypothesis | Research | Speculative | | **3** | Proof of Concept | Lab results confirming key assumptions | Research | High | | **4** | Lab Validation | Working breadboard; specifications defined | Development | High | | **5** | Relevant Environment Validation | High-fidelity prototype tested in simulated environment | Development | Moderate | | **6** | Demonstrated in Relevant Environment | Representative system demonstrated to stakeholders | Development | Moderate | | **7** | Operational Prototype | Pilot deployment in real environment | Demonstration | Lower | | **8** | System Qualified | Commercial-grade product, certifications obtained | Demonstration | Low | | **9** | Proven in Operations | Sustained commercial use; lessons documented | Deployment | Residual only | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **For Investors (VCs, Angel Funds):** TRL acts as a *due-diligence filter*. Most VCs refuse to fund below TRL 6 because commercial validation is missing. - **For Government Grant Agencies (e.g., DST, SERB, Horizon Europe):** TRL brackets determine *eligible funding windows*. Horizon Europe, for instance, uses TRL 3–6 as the sweet spot for innovation grants. - **For R&D Managers:** TRL provides a *common dialect* across multi-disciplinary teams (mechanical, software, regulatory) to align milestones. - **For Regulators (ISO, FDA, BIS):** TRL gates decide *when* a product is ready for certification testing. > [!NOTE] > **Design Thinking Connection:** TRL complements the *empathy-prototype-test* loop of Design Thinking. TRL measures the *technological depth* of the prototype, while Design Thinking ensures the prototype is *human-centered*. ## 2.4 Companion Frameworks (High-Yield for KTU) KTU examiners frequently ask *comparative* questions. The three readiness frameworks most often paired with TRL are: | Framework | Full Form | What It Measures | Typical Range | |:----------|:----------|:-----------------|:--------------| | **MRL** | Manufacturing Readiness Level | Production process maturity | MRL 1 – MRL 10 | | **IRL** | Integration Readiness Level | Interface maturity between components | IRL 1 – IRL 9 | | **CRL** | Commercial Readiness Level | Market and business-model maturity | CRL 1 – CRL 9 | # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough ## 3.1 The TRL Assessment Methodology — Exhaustive Derivation The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to articulate the *decision process* that classifies a technology into a specific TRL. Below is the **complete analytical derivation** of the TRL assessment procedure, written in a way that mirrors KTU board valuation key expectations. ### Step A — Identify the Subject of Assessment A TRL assessment can be applied to one of the following: - A **basic scientific phenomenon** (lowest granularity) - A **component** (e.g., a sensor chip) - A **subsystem** (e.g., a camera module) - A **full system** (e.g., an autonomous vehicle) - A **system-of-systems** (e.g., a smart city infrastructure) *Decision rule:* The TRL is assigned to *each* element separately, and the overall innovation is reported as a TRL *profile*, not a single number. ### Step B — Match to Functional Objectives The evaluator asks: *"What is the technology supposed to do, and in what environment?"* This is the same *empathy-definition* step of Design Thinking applied at a technical depth. ### Step C — Evaluate Evidence Against Exit Criteria For each candidate TRL, the **exit criterion** (see Section 2.2) must be demonstrably met. The evaluator looks for: - **TRL 1–2:** Papers, white papers, hypothesis documents - **TRL 3:** Lab notebooks, simulation logs, prototype schematics - **TRL 4–5:** Test reports, validation data, fidelity-rating documents - **TRL 6–7:** Demonstration videos, pilot project reports, user feedback - **TRL 8–9:** Certification documents, sales records, field performance logs ### Step D — Conduct a Gate Review A TRL gate review is a formal meeting where cross-functional stakeholders (engineering, finance, regulatory, marketing) vote on the *upward transition* of a technology. The decision tree is: $$ \text{Gate Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Advance} & \text{if all exit criteria are evidenced AND no critical risk is unresolved} \\ \text{Hold} & \text{if evidence is partial but mitigable} \\ \text{Stop / Pivot} & \text{if a foundational assumption is invalidated} \end{cases} $$ ### Step E — Assign and Publish the TRL The assigned TRL is documented in the *Technology Management Plan (TMP)* and serves as the *baseline* for the next development cycle. ## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — A Smart Water Purifier (IoT Device) Consider a KTU student project: a *gravity-fed IoT water purifier* with a mobile app that tracks filter life and water purity. | Analytical Step | Reasoning Applied | Resulting TRL | |:----------------|:------------------|:--------------| | Observe basic principles (adsorption of contaminants on activated carbon) | Underlying science is published and taught | TRL 1 satisfied | | Formulate concept (gravity + IoT tracking) | Application hypothesis documented in design report | TRL 2 satisfied | | Proof of concept (mini filter tested for 1 contaminant) | Lab test confirms adsorption works on tap water | TRL 3 satisfied | | Lab validation (full filter tested on 5 contaminants in lab) | Breadboard tested in controlled lab | TRL 4 satisfied | | Relevant environment validation (filter tested in student hostel tank) | High-fidelity prototype in real but constrained environment | TRL 5 satisfied | | Demonstrated in relevant environment (pilot in 3 Kerala households) | Stakeholders (users) saw the device work in real homes | TRL 6 satisfied | | Operational prototype (100 units deployed across a panchayat) | Real users, real water, real environment | TRL 7 satisfied | | System qualified (BIS certification, app store publication) | Commercial-grade product, regulatory clearance | TRL 8 satisfied | | Proven in operations (10,000 units sold, 1-year performance data) | Sustained commercial use | TRL 9 satisfied | ## 3.3 Analytical TRL × Innovation-Stage Matrix The following matrix maps *each* TRL against the five canonical stages of the *innovation life-cycle* (Invention → Incubation → Acceleration → Maturity → Decline). Use this for any 7-mark "discuss" or "illustrate" question in KTU ESE. | TRL | Invention | Incubation | Acceleration | Maturity | Decline | |:---:|:---------:|:----------:|:------------:|:--------:|:-------:| | 1–2 | Active | — | — | — | — | | 3–4 | — | Active | — | — | — | | 5–6 | — | — | Active | — | — | | 7–8 | — | — | — | Active | — | | 9 | — | — | — | — | Active | ## 3.4 Critical Pitfalls in TRL Assessment (Examiner-Tracked) 1. **Self-bias inflation:** Innovators over-rate their TRL to attract funding. KTU expects students to recognize this *subjectivity risk*. 2. **Environment mismatch:** A lab demo is *not* TRL 6 — TRL 6 strictly requires a *relevant* (i.e., quasi-operational) environment. 3. **Granularity confusion:** A sensor at TRL 4 does *not* make the parent system at TRL 4; the system integrates multiple sub-TRLs and its TRL is the *minimum* of the integrated parts (or weighted average in advanced models). 4. **Skipping gates:** KTU explicitly penalizes answers that "jump" from TRL 3 to TRL 6 in a single step without intermediate evidence. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 TRL Progression Flow (Mermaid Linear Diagram) The diagram below is the **canonical KTU board drawing** for the TRL innovation ladder. Memorize the node labels exactly as shown. ```mermaid graph LR node1["TRL 1: BASIC PRINCIPLES OBSERVED"] node2["TRL 2: CONCEPT FORMULATED"] node3["TRL 3: PROOF OF CONCEPT"] node4["TRL 4: LAB VALIDATION"] node5["TRL 5: RELEVANT ENV VALIDATION"] node6["TRL 6: DEMONSTRATED IN ENV"] node7["TRL 7: OPERATIONAL PROTOTYPE"] node8["TRL 8: SYSTEM QUALIFIED"] node9["TRL 9: PROVEN IN OPERATIONS"] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node5 --> node6 node6 --> node7 node7 --> node8 node8 --> node9 ``` ## 4.2 TRL-to-Innovation-Phase Block Architecture The block diagram below maps the TRL scale to the four innovation phases. Use it as the answer scaffolding for any 7-mark "discuss" question in Part B. ```mermaid graph TB researchBlock["RESEARCH PHASE - TRL 1 to TRL 3
Basic science to concept validation
Funded by grants and seed money"] devBlock["DEVELOPMENT PHASE - TRL 4 to TRL 6
Lab to relevant environment
Funded by Series A and corporate R and D"] demoBlock["DEMONSTRATION PHASE - TRL 7 to TRL 8
Pilot to qualified commercial system
Funded by venture capital and corporate scale-up"] deployBlock["DEPLOYMENT PHASE - TRL 9
Sustained commercial operation
Funded by market revenue"] researchBlock --> devBlock devBlock --> demoBlock demoBlock --> deployBlock ``` ## 4.3 TRL Gate-Review Decision Process The following block diagram captures the *gate-review* mechanism that governs TRL transitions — a high-yield visual for KTU 14-mark answers. ```mermaid graph TD gateInput["CURRENT TRL STATE"] evidenceCheck["EVIDENCE GATHERED"] exitCriteria["EXIT CRITERIA SATISFIED?"] riskCheck["CRITICAL RISK RESOLVED?"] advance["ADVANCE TO NEXT TRL"] hold["HOLD - PARTIAL EVIDENCE"] stop["STOP OR PIVOT"] gateInput --> evidenceCheck evidenceCheck --> exitCriteria exitCriteria --> riskCheck riskCheck --> advance riskCheck --> hold exitCriteria --> stop ``` ## 4.4 TRL × Risk × Capital Decision Matrix (Schematic) ```mermaid graph LR lowRisk["LOW RISK HIGH CAPITAL"] highRisk["HIGH RISK LOW CAPITAL"] axisX["TRL INCREASING"] axisY["INVESTMENT INCREASING"] lowRisk --- highRisk axisX --- axisY ``` **Reading the matrix:** As TRL increases along the horizontal axis, technological risk *decreases* (uncertainty is resolved) and capital investment *increases* (more money is required for prototyping, certification, and scale-up). This is the core trade-off that drives innovation funding decisions. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **KTU Past Year Pattern:** Two short-answer questions of 3 marks each, typically expecting a *definition + 3–4 supporting points*. ### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper] **Define Technology Readiness Level (TRL). List the 9 levels of TRL in sequence.** **Model Answer (Valuation Key Pattern):** Technology Readiness Level (TRL) is a standardized 9-point measurement scale used to assess the maturity of a technology as it progresses from basic research to full commercial deployment within the innovation life-cycle. The 9 levels in sequence are: 1. **TRL 1** — Basic Principles Observed 2. **TRL 2** — Technology Concept Formulated 3. **TRL 3** — Experimental Proof of Concept 4. **TRL 4** — Technology Validated in Laboratory 5. **TRL 5** — Technology Validated in Relevant Environment 6. **TRL 6** — Technology Demonstrated in Relevant Environment 7. **TRL 7** — System Prototype Demonstrated in Operational Environment 8. **TRL 8** — System Complete and Qualified 9. **TRL 9** — Actual System Proven in Successful Operational Environment *(Examiner note: 1 mark for clean definition, 2 marks for correctly listing the 9 levels in order. Any 2 levels swapped = −0.5 mark.)* ### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Explain the significance of TRL 4 to TRL 6 in the innovation life-cycle.** **Model Answer:** The TRL 4 to TRL 6 band is the **"Valley of Death"** zone of the innovation life-cycle — the most critical transition where basic research graduates into applied development. - **TRL 4** establishes that individual components work together in a controlled laboratory, providing the *first evidence of integration*. - **TRL 5** extends validation to a *high-fidelity prototype* in a simulated real-world environment, reducing *technical risk* to a moderate level. - **TRL 6** demands a *representative system demonstration* to external stakeholders, making the technology *fundable* by venture capital and corporate R&D budgets. This band is the **decision pivot** where most innovations fail due to inadequate evidence, environmental mismatches, or premature scale-up. ## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Pattern (Module Internal Choice):** A single 14-mark question with sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. Students must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B. ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper] **Question A:** **(a)** Explain in detail the 9 levels of TRL with an appropriate example for each. (7 marks) **(b)** Discuss how TRL integrates with the **Design Thinking process** to enable successful product development. (7 marks) #### Model Solution for Question A(a) The 9 levels of TRL can be explained with the example of developing an **electric two-wheeler** as follows: - **TRL 1 — Basic Principles Observed:** Research papers on lithium-ion battery electrochemistry and Brushless DC motor physics are studied. Example: Faraday's law observations. - **TRL 2 — Concept Formulated:** A concept document proposes replacing the IC engine with a hub-mounted BLDC motor powered by a swappable Li-ion battery pack. - **TRL 3 — Proof of Concept:** A single battery cell and a small motor are tested on a lab bench to confirm voltage, RPM, and torque outputs match the hypothesis. - **TRL 4 — Lab Validation:** A complete powertrain (battery pack + motor + controller) is assembled on a test rig and validated in a temperature-controlled lab for 100 hours. - **TRL 5 — Relevant Environment Validation:** A prototype vehicle is tested on a treadmill dynamometer that simulates Indian road gradients and ambient heat. - **TRL 6 — Demonstrated in Relevant Environment:** A working prototype is ridden on closed campus roads of an engineering college, with telemetry data recorded. - **TRL 7 — Operational Prototype:** A small fleet of 10 vehicles is deployed for last-mile delivery trials in a Kochi locality. - **TRL 8 — System Qualified:** The vehicle is certified by ARAI under FAME-II norms; production tooling is finalized. - **TRL 9 — Proven in Operations:** Over 5,000 units sold and serviced nationwide with field data feeding continuous improvement. #### Model Solution for Question A(b) The Design Thinking process consists of **Empathize → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test**. TRL integrates with each stage as follows: - **Empathize & Define:** Customer pain points (range anxiety, charging time) are translated into *technology requirements* (battery capacity, motor power). TRL 1–2 evidence is gathered to scope feasibility. - **Ideate:** Multiple concept options are evaluated against TRL readiness — teams choose concepts with *complementary maturity* to reduce integration risk. - **Prototype:** Each prototype represents a TRL *checkpoint* (TRL 4 = lab prototype, TRL 6 = field-demonstrated prototype). Design Thinking ensures prototypes are *human-centered*; TRL ensures they are *technically credible*. - **Test:** User feedback (Design Thinking) is *merged* with TRL exit-criteria evidence to decide on upward transition to TRL 7 and beyond. - **Iteration:** The TRL gate review is the *formal moment* of Design Thinking iteration — go back to Ideate if evidence is missing, advance if validated. Thus, **TRL is the technical backbone** of Design Thinking, ensuring that the empathy-driven insights are translated into *engineered reality* with auditable evidence. ### ⭐ Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper] **Question B:** **(a)** Discuss the role of TRL in **technology risk management and investment decisions**. (7 marks) **(b)** Compare and contrast TRL with **Manufacturing Readiness Level (MRL)** and **Integration Readiness Level (IRL)**. (7 marks) #### Model Solution for Question B(a) TRL plays a pivotal role in managing innovation risk and informing investment decisions: - **Risk Quantification:** TRL provides a *common metric* to express technical risk. Lower TRLs imply higher failure probability; higher TRLs imply residual operational risk. - **Capital Allocation:** Government grants (DST, SERB, Horizon Europe) typically fund TRL 1–3; corporate R&D funds TRL 4–6; venture capital and private equity target TRL 7–9. - **Portfolio Management:** Organizations maintain a *TRL portfolio* — a mix of low-TRL moonshots and high-TRL near-commercial bets — to balance risk and return. - **Gate Decisions:** TRL gate reviews act as *go/no-go checkpoints* preventing premature commercialization and sunk-cost escalation. - **Insurance and Liability:** Underwriters and regulatory bodies use TRL to price risk premiums and determine certification timing. In summary, TRL is the **risk-decoding instrument** that aligns *technology evidence* with *capital deployment*. #### Model Solution for Question B(b) | Dimension | TRL | MRL | IRL | |:----------|:----|:----|:----| | Full Form | Technology Readiness Level | Manufacturing Readiness Level | Integration Readiness Level | | Measures | Maturity of the *technology itself* | Maturity of the *production process* | Maturity of *interfaces* between components | | Range | 1 to 9 | 1 to 10 | 1 to 9 | | Key Question | *"Does it work?"* | *"Can we make it at scale?"* | *"Do the parts fit together?"* | | Owner | R&D / Engineering | Manufacturing / Operations | Systems Engineering | | KTU Phase | Concept to Deployment | Late Development to Deployment | Development to Demonstration | | Origin | NASA (1970s) | U.S. DoD (2000s) | U.S. DoD (2000s) | | Synergy | Provides technology evidence | Provides production evidence | Provides system-integration evidence | **Contrast:** TRL focuses on the *what* (the innovation's capability), MRL on the *how* (production-readiness), and IRL on the *between* (interface-readiness). **Complementarity:** A product at TRL 9 with MRL 4 is a *failed commercialization*; a product at MRL 9 with TRL 4 is a *manufactured failure*. The three frameworks must be assessed jointly for holistic readiness. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Listing TRLs without context:** Writing "TRL 1, TRL 2, ..." without describing the *evidence required* at each level loses 2–3 marks. > 2. **Confusing TRL with MRL:** Many students interchange the two. TRL is *technology*; MRL is *manufacturing*. Examiners actively test this distinction. > 3. **Skipping the Design Thinking link:** Module 1 of GZEST605 is *Design Thinking and Product Development*. A pure technical TRL answer without connecting to Design Thinking stages loses *integration marks*. > 4. **Forgetting the 9-point scale:** Some answers mention 6 or 7 levels. NASA and KTU use exactly **9 levels**. This is an automatic deduction trigger. > 5. **Treating TRL as linear:** Skipping intermediate levels (e.g., jumping from TRL 3 to TRL 6) is technically invalid. Mention *gate reviews* to demonstrate depth. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Save this section for last-minute KTU exam revision.** - **TRL** stands for *Technology Readiness Level*; it is a 9-point scale (**TRL 1 to TRL 9**). - **Originated at NASA** in the 1970s for aerospace risk assessment; now global standard. - **TRL 1** = *Basic Principles Observed*; **TRL 9** = *Proven in Operations*. - The **Valley of Death** in innovation sits at **TRL 4 to TRL 6**. - **TRL exit criteria** are the *evidence-based checkpoints* that gate every upward transition. - **Companion frameworks:** **MRL** (manufacturing), **IRL** (integration), **CRL** (commercial). - **TRL × Innovation Phases:** Research = TRL 1–3; Development = TRL 4–6; Demonstration = TRL 7–8; Deployment = TRL 9. - **Risk declines and capital rises** as TRL increases — a key trade-off for funding decisions. - **Design Thinking link:** TRL provides *technical credibility* to the empathy-driven prototype-test loop. - **Gate Reviews** are the formal *go/no-go* mechanism between consecutive TRL levels. - **Subjectivity risk:** Innovators may *over-state* TRL; KTU expects students to recognize this bias. - **Granularity rule:** A system TRL is bounded by the *lowest* of its sub-component TRLs. - **Examiner mantra:** Always pair *TRL number* with *evidence type* and *innovation phase*.

topics.title: Performing a self-check on innovative ideas - Originality of idea - Understanding innovation landscape - Patentability - Understanding the economic landscape - Unique Selling Proposition (USP) - Repeatability and Manufacturability - Sustainability - Leveraging business models for comprehensive analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Performing a self-check on innovative ideas - Originality of idea - Understanding innovation landscape - Patentability - Understanding the economic landscape - Unique Selling Proposition (USP) - Repeatability and Manufacturability - Sustainability - Leveraging business models for comprehensive analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Performing a Self-Check on Innovative Ideas

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A *self-check on innovative ideas* is a structured, multi-dimensional evaluation framework that an innovator applies *independently and critically* to a proposed concept **before** committing significant time, capital, or resources toward development. It systematically probes the idea across **eight pillars**: Originality, Innovation Landscape, Patentability, Economic Landscape, USP, Repeatability, Manufacturability, and Sustainability — all framed within a viable Business Model.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of the self-check as the **"pre-flight checklist"** a pilot runs before takeoff. A pilot does not "test the airplane" by flying it blindly — they verify fuel, instruments, weight balance, weather, and route *first*. Similarly, an innovator does not "test the idea" by building it — they verify originality, protection, economics, uniqueness, feasibility, and sustainability *first*. Skipping this checklist is the #1 reason startups and product innovations crash before reaching market.

---

## 1.2 The Eight Pillars of the Self-Check Framework

### Pillar 1 — Originality of the Idea

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU):** Originality is the degree to which a proposed idea is **novel, unprecedented, and non-derivative** when compared against the existing body of solutions in the problem domain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine walking into a library with **10 million books** and asking — "Has my book already been written?" Originality is the answer to that question for your idea. If the answer is "yes, exactly," your idea has *zero originality*. If the answer is "no, nothing quite like it exists," your idea has *high originality*.

**Three Levels of Originality (KTU Framework):**
- **Level 1 — Derivative:** A small variation of an existing solution (low originality).
- **Level 2 — Combinative:** A novel combination of two or more existing ideas (medium originality).
- **Level 3 — Pioneering:** A fundamentally new principle or approach (high originality).

---

### Pillar 2 — Understanding the Innovation Landscape

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The *innovation landscape* is the **ecosystem map** of all existing solutions, competing technologies, research papers, startups, patents, and emerging trends that surround the problem space the innovator is operating in.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine you want to open a restaurant in Kozhikode. Before you do, you walk around the city and observe — how many restaurants exist? What cuisines do they serve? What are their prices? Who are their customers? What is *missing*? That mental map of "what's out there" is your **innovation landscape**.

---

### Pillar 3 — Patentability

> [!IMPORTANT]
