If she has to click through 5 menus to find them, she will close the app and use WhatsApp instead.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: Customer Journey Map for Ordering Food via Swiggy

> **[Scenario Question from KTU Board Pattern]**
> *"Build a Customer Journey Map for a KTU student ordering dinner via Swiggy."*

| Stage | User Action | Touchpoint | Emotion (1–5) | Pain Point | Opportunity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Awareness (6 PM)** | Realizes hunger, opens Swiggy app | App icon, push notification | 😊 4 | App loads slowly on 3G | Pre-load content offline |
| **2. Consideration** | Browses restaurants, reads reviews | Restaurant list, ratings | 😐 3 | Too many options, decision fatigue | AI-based "Today's Top Pick" |
| **3. Decision** | Selects biryani, adds to cart | Menu, cart screen | 😊 4 | Hidden delivery fees revealed late | Upfront fee transparency |
| **4. Payment** | Pays via UPI | Payment gateway | 😟 2 | Payment failure, retry needed | Auto-retry with backup UPI app |
| **5. Tracking** | Watches delivery on map | Live map tracker | 😊 4 | ETA keeps changing | Honest rolling ETA |
| **6. Delivery** | Receives food, checks for spillage | Delivery partner, packaging | 😊 4 | Packaging leaks | Spill-proof containers |
| **7. Post-Meal** | Rates restaurant, tips partner | Rating screen | 😊 5 | Rating prompt feels intrusive | Optional non-intrusive feedback |

**Insight Derived:** The lowest emotional moments are at **Payment** and **App Load**. The product team should prioritize **payment reliability** and **app performance on low-bandwidth networks** — directly relevant for KTU students in semi-urban Kerala with patchy 4G coverage.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: Empathy Tools Ecosystem & Their Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Empathize Phase Data] --> B[User Interviews]
    A --> C[Field Observations]
    A --> D[Surveys and Analytics]
    B --> E[Affinity Diagram]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Persona Construction]
    F --> G[Customer Journey Map]
    F --> H[Empathy Map]
    G --> I[Pain Point Identification]
    H --> I
    I --> J[How Might We Questions]
    J --> K[Define Phase]
    K --> L[Problem Statement]
    L --> M[Ideate Phase]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Empathy tools do not work in isolation. They form a **pipeline**: raw qualitative data → synthesis (Affinity Diagram) → user representation (Persona) → experience visualization (CJM) → insight extraction (Pain Points) → reframing (HMW Questions) → problem definition (Define Phase).

---

### 4.2 Diagram 2: Customer Journey Map Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stage 1: Awareness] --> B[Stage 2: Consideration]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Decision]
    C --> D[Stage 4: Purchase]
    D --> E[Stage 5: Onboarding]
    E --> F[Stage 6: Usage]
    F --> G[Stage 7: Advocacy]
    A1[User Action] --> A2[Touchpoint]
    A2 --> A3[Emotion Score]
    A3 --> A4[Pain Point]
    A4 --> A5[Opportunity HMW]
    B1[User Action] --> B2[Touchpoint]
    B2 --> B3[Emotion Score]
    B3 --> B4[Pain Point]
    B4 --> B5[Opportunity HMW]
    C1[User Action] --> C2[Touchpoint]
    C2 --> C3[Emotion Score]
    C3 --> C4[Pain Point]
    C4 --> C5[Opportunity HMW]
    D1[User Action] --> D2[Touchpoint]
    D2 --> D3[Emotion Score]
    D3 --> D4[Pain Point]
    D4 --> D5[Opportunity HMW]
    A1 --- B1 --- C1 --- D1
    A2 --- B2 --- C2 --- D2
    A3 --- B3 --- C3 --- D3
    A4 --- B4 --- C4 --- D4
    A5 --- B5 --- C5 --- D5
```

---

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Persona Card Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Persona Card] --> P1[Identity: Name, Age, Photo]
    P --> P2[Demographics: Education, Income, Location]
    P --> P3[Psychographics: Values, Interests]
    P --> P4[Goals: What They Want to Achieve]
    P --> P5[Pain Points: What Frustrates Them]
    P --> P6[Behaviors: Usage Patterns and Habits]
    P --> P7[Quote: Representative Statement]
    P --> P8[Scenario: Day in the Life Narrative]
    P1 --> P9[Research-Backed Character]
    P2 --> P9
    P3 --> P9
    P4 --> P9
    P5 --> P9
    P6 --> P9
    P7 --> P9
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10[Used for Product Decisions]
```

---

### 4.4 Diagram 4: Empathy Map 2x2 Quadrant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EM[Empathy Map] --> Q1[Quadrant 1: SAYS]
    EM --> Q2[Quadrant 2: THINKS]
    EM --> Q3[Quadrant 3: DOES]
    EM --> Q4[Quadrant 4: FEELS]
    Q1 --> Q1D[What the user says in interviews]
    Q2 --> Q2D[What the user thinks but does not say]
    Q3 --> Q3D[What the user actually does in context]
    Q4 --> Q4D[What the user emotionally experiences]
    Q1D --> S1[Synthesis: Identify Gaps]
    Q2D --> S1
    Q3D --> S1
    Q4D --> S1
    S1 --> S2[Reveal Unmet Needs]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The Empathy Map is **not** a substitute for a Customer Journey Map. Personas = WHO, Empathy Map = WHAT, CJM = HOW. Confusing these in a Part B answer costs 3–4 marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define a "Persona" in the context of Design Thinking. List any four elements that should be included in a well-constructed persona.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Persona is a fictional, research-based archetype that represents a cluster of real users who share similar goals, behaviors, and pain points. It is constructed from qualitative user research (interviews, observations, surveys) and is used to humanize design decisions.

Four essential elements of a persona:
1. **Demographic profile** — Age, occupation, education, location, income.
2. **Goals** — What the user is trying to achieve.
3. **Pain points/Frustrations** — What blocks the user from achieving goals.
4. **Behaviors** — Patterns of usage, preferences, and habits.

*(Additional valid elements: Quote, Scenario, Psychographic profile, Photo.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*What is a Customer Journey Map (CJM)? Mention any three components of a CJM.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Customer Journey Map (CJM)** is a visual representation of the end-to-end experience a user has with a product, service, or brand across time and channels. It maps the user's actions, touchpoints, emotions, and pain points at each stage of interaction.

Three components of a CJM:
1. **Stages/Phases** — Chronological steps such as Awareness, Consideration, Purchase, Usage, and Advocacy.
2. **Touchpoints** — Channels of interaction (website, app, store, support).
3. **Emotion Curve** — A plotted line of the user's emotional state across stages.

*(Other valid components: User actions, Pain points, Gain points, Opportunities.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** and offer an **internal choice** between two questions. Each question typically has sub-parts (a) carrying 7 marks and (b) carrying 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Persona + Journey Map Combination**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question]**
*(a)* **Construct a detailed persona** for a working professional in Kerala who is learning to use a UPI-based payment app for the first time. (7 marks)
*(b)* Using the persona constructed in part (a), **build a Customer Journey Map** for the user's first successful UPI transaction. Identify at least **two pain points** and suggest one opportunity for each. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks:**

**Persona Name:** Gopalan, the Grocery Shop Owner

**Demographic Profile:**
- Age: 48
- Location: Thrissur, Kerala
- Education: 12th Standard
- Occupation: Owns a small grocery shop with monthly revenue of ₹80,000.
- Device: Basic Android smartphone (3-year-old Redmi), intermittent Wi-Fi.

**Goals:**
- Accept digital payments from younger customers who don't carry cash.
- Reduce time spent counting cash and giving change.
- Build a small online customer base via WhatsApp.

**Frustrations:**
- UPI apps ask for multiple permissions and OTPs.
- Fear of "money going to the wrong account" or fraud.
- Internet drops frequently in his shop, causing payment failures in front of customers.

**Behaviors:**
- Asks his son (an engineering student) to help set up new apps.
- Prefers voice prompts in Malayalam over text.
- Avoids apps that require email login or long KYC processes.

**Quote:** *"I don't want the phone to be smarter than me — I just want customers to pay and leave happily."*

**Scenario:** On a busy Sunday morning, a college student tries to pay ₹240 for groceries via Google Pay. Gopalan is nervous, the network is slow, and the OTP takes 45 seconds to arrive. The student waits impatiently, and 3 other customers queue up. Gopalan wishes the payment was as simple as accepting cash.

*(Award full 7 marks if: Name given (1), Demographics (1), Goals (1), Pain Points (1), Behaviors (1), Quote (1), Scenario (1).)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Customer Journey Map: Gopalan's First UPI Transaction**

| Stage | User Action | Touchpoint | Emotion (1–5) | Pain Point | Opportunity (HMW) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Awareness** | Son tells him about UPI | WhatsApp voice note | 😊 4 | Son used English terms he didn't understand | HMW can we onboard users in local language via voice? |
| **2. App Setup** | Downloads PhonePe via son's help | App Store, smartphone | 😟 2 | 7-step KYC with PAN card requirement | HMW can we offer low-KYC merchant onboarding with just Aadhaar? |
| **3. First Payment Request** | Customer asks to pay ₹240 via QR | QR code display | 😰 1 | Customer scans but Gopalan doesn't know if it succeeded | HMW can we add a loud success sound and vibration confirmation? |
| **4. Payment Processing** | Waits for OTP and bank confirmation | SMS, UPI app | 😟 2 | OTP delayed 45 seconds, network drops | HMW can we cache the payment request offline and auto-verify when network returns? |
| **5. Confirmation** | Sees green tick on screen | App notification | 😊 4 | Doesn't trust the green tick — asks customer to show SMS | HMW can we print a small receipt on a Bluetooth mini-printer? |
| **6. Reconciliation** | Tries to transfer money to bank | UPI app, bank SMS | 😐 3 | Money is in PhonePe wallet, not directly in bank | HMW can we auto-sweep wallet to bank at end of day? |

**Key Insights (for examiner credit):**
- **Biggest Pain Point:** Stage 3 (Confirmation) and Stage 4 (Processing) — these are the **Moments of Truth** that determine whether Gopalan will adopt UPI permanently.
- **Design Opportunity:** Voice-based confirmation, offline caching, and a Bluetooth receipt printer could dramatically improve adoption among small merchants in Kerala.

*(Award full 7 marks if: Stages listed (2), Touchpoints identified (1), Emotion curve plotted (1), Pain points clear (1), Opportunities framed as HMW (1), Insights summarized (1).)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Empathy Tools Comparison + Application**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*(a)* **Differentiate** between a Persona, an Empathy Map, and a Customer Journey Map. Explain the purpose and appropriate phase of use for each. (7 marks)
*(b)* A startup is developing a **mental health app for KTU students**. Explain how the team would use **Personas, Empathy Maps, and a Customer Journey Map** together to design a successful product. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks:**

| Tool | Purpose | Phase of Use | Output Format | Key Question |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Persona** | Defines WHO the user is | Empathize → Define | Character card with name, photo, goals, pain points | "Who are we designing for?" |
| **Empathy Map** | Captures WHAT the user experiences | Empathize | 2x2 canvas: Says, Thinks, Does, Feels | "What is the user experiencing right now?" |
| **Customer Journey Map** | Visualizes HOW the experience evolves over time | Empathize → Define | Horizontal timeline with emotion curve and pain points | "How does the user interact with our product over time?" |

**Key Differences:**
1. **Time Dimension:** Persona is static, Empathy Map is a snapshot, CJM is dynamic and chronological.
2. **Granularity:** Persona = segment-level, Empathy Map = moment-level, CJM = journey-level.
3. **Data Depth:** Persona synthesizes demographic + psychographic data, Empathy Map captures in-the-moment experience, CJM maps the full lifecycle.
4. **Decision Use:** Personas guide "for whom," Empathy Maps guide "what to fix," CJMs guide "where to intervene."

*(Award full 7 marks if: Table differentiating all 3 tools (4), Purpose and phase clearly stated (2), Key differences summarized (1).)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Application: Mental Health App for KTU Students**

**Step 1 — Build 2–3 Personas** (2 marks)
- *Persona 1:* "Anxious Anita" — 19, 1st year B.Tech, struggling with exam fear, isolated from family.
- *Persona 2:* "Burnt-out Basil" — 22, final year, juggling placements and project deadlines.
- *Persona 3:* "Resilient Riya" — 20, 2nd year, seeking self-improvement tools, not in crisis.

**Step 2 — Empathy Map for Anxious Anita** (2 marks)
- **Says:** "I can't sleep the night before exams."
- **Thinks:** "What if I fail? Everyone will judge me."
- **Does:** Scrolls Instagram at 2 AM, skips meals, avoids group study.
- **Feels:** Anxious, lonely, overwhelmed.

**Step 3 — Customer Journey Map for Anxious Anita** (3 marks)

| Stage | Action | Touchpoint | Emotion | Pain Point | Opportunity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Awareness | Sees ad for app | Instagram reel | Curious | Skeptical of "another app" | Offer free first session |
| Onboarding | Signs up, takes quiz | App onboarding | Hopeful | 20-question quiz feels clinical | 2-minute friendly chat-style quiz |
| First Use | Reads a calming article | App content | Relieved | No human connection | Offer optional peer chat |
| Crisis Moment | Feels panic before exam | Push notification | Anxious | App is not real-time | 24/7 counselor chat |
| Follow-up | Gets weekly mood check-in | Email + app | Supported | Feels like a "patient" | Gamify as "wellness journey" |

**Conclusion:** Combining all three empathy tools gives the startup a **360° view** of the user — from segment (Persona) to moment (Empathy Map) to journey (CJM) — enabling them to design features that address real, observed, and emotional needs.

*(Award full 7 marks if: 3 personas named (2), Empathy map built (2), CJM with at least 3 stages (3).)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Inventing Personas Without Research Basis:** If a student creates a persona without referencing real user data or interviews, deduct **2 marks** from the persona sub-question.
> 2. **Confusing Empathy Map with Persona:** Writing "Says/Thinks/Does/Feels" under a persona card is a structural error. Deduct **1 mark**.
> 3. **Missing Emotion Curve in CJM:** A Customer Journey Map without a plotted emotional line is incomplete. Deduct **2 marks**.
> 4. **Using "Customer Journey" and "Customer Experience" Interchangeably:** Journey is **time-bound and sequential**; Experience is the **cumulative perception**. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this conflation.
> 5. **Skipping the "Opportunity/HMW" Stage:** A CJM is incomplete if it ends at "Pain Point." Always convert pain points into actionable **"How Might We…"** statements.
> 6. **Over-Generic Personas:** "A student" or "a user" is not a persona. Personas must be **named, specific, and humanized** (e.g., "Riya the Resourceful Rookie").

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Empathy Tools**

- **Persona** = **WHO** the user is → a fictional, research-based character card.
- **Empathy Map** = **WHAT** the user experiences → 2x2 canvas (Says, Thinks, Does, Feels).
- **Customer Journey Map (CJM)** = **HOW** the experience unfolds → horizontal timeline with emotion curve.
- Always build a **Persona FIRST**, then use it as the **subject** of the Empathy Map and CJM.
- A valid Persona **must** be based on **real qualitative research** — interviews, observations, or surveys.
- A CJM must include **at least 5 stages**, an **emotion curve**, **touchpoints**, **pain points**, and **HMW opportunities**.
- **Pain Points** are negative moments → convert them into **"How Might We…?"** opportunity statements.
- **Gain Points** are positive moments → replicate and amplify them in the redesigned experience.
- Empathy tools operate in the **Empathize phase** but **feed the Define phase** with insights.
- Common **KTU exam format**: Construct a Persona (7 marks) + Build a CJM (7 marks) = 14-mark Part B question.
- **Key differentiator** for high marks: Always **cite user data** or **field research** that grounds your persona.
- The **JTBD (Jobs To Be Done)** framework complements empathy tools by answering the **"why"** behind user behavior.
- **NPS** ranges from **-100 to +100**; **CSAT** typically uses a **1–5** scale; both are quantitative supplements to qualitative empathy tools.

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Empathy Tools, Customer Journey Map, and Personas**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme (GZEST605) Examination Standards*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Define: Methods of Define Phase - Storytelling, Critical items diagrams, Define success
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Define: Methods of Define Phase - Storytelling, Critical items diagrams, Define success

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Define Phase — Context Recap

In the **Double Diamond model** of Design Thinking (Discover–Define–Develop–Deliver), the **Define Phase** is the convergent stage where the design team narrows down the broad insights gathered during the Empathize/Discover phase into a sharp, actionable **Point of View (PoV)** and a clear **Problem Statement**. The KTU 2024 syllabus groups the methods of this phase into three powerful, complementary tools:

1. **Storytelling** — Humanising the problem.
2. **Critical Items Diagram** — Visualising the structure and hierarchy of the problem.
3. **Define Success** — Quantifying what a successful solution will look like.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** *Define — Methods of Define phase: Storytelling, Critical items diagrams, Define success.* These are grouped together because they together convert raw empathy data into a **testable, communicable, and measurable** design brief.

---

## 1.2 Storytelling — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Board-Standard):**
> **Storytelling in Design Thinking** is a structured narrative technique in which the designer converts raw qualitative user-data (observations, interviews, pain points) into a compelling, chronological narrative that communicates the user's experience, emotions, and unmet needs to stakeholders in an emotionally engaging and memorable way.

It is the **"translation engine"** between raw research and a shared team understanding. A story replaces a sterile data report with a human experience that the audience can *feel*.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a doctor. After examining a patient, instead of writing *"Patient X, age 45, presents with intermittent chest pain radiating to the left arm, BP 160/95"* (data), you tell a colleague:
> *"On Tuesday morning, Mr. Raju was climbing the stairs to his office on the third floor when, halfway up, he suddenly felt a heavy weight pressing on his chest. He stopped, held the railing, and his left arm tingled. He thought it was just tiredness…"*

Which one makes the colleague **care** and **act**? The story does. The story is a *vessel for empathy*. That is exactly what Storytelling does in the Define phase — it carries the user's pain from a researcher's notebook into the designer's heart.

---

## 1.3 Critical Items Diagram — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> A **Critical Items Diagram (CID)** is a hierarchical, tree-like visual diagram that decomposes a central design problem or Point of View into its critical sub-elements, sub-problems, or design requirements. It maps the *cause-effect* or *means-ends* relationships between a primary objective and the secondary, tertiary, and operational items required to achieve it.

It is the **"skeleton key"** of the problem — once you draw it, the hidden structure of the design challenge becomes obvious.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **family tree** drawn for a royal family. At the top is the **King** (your main problem). Below him are the **Princes and Princesses** (the major sub-problems). Below them are the **Knights and Advisors** (specific design requirements). A Critical Items Diagram is exactly this — a *royal hierarchy* of your problem. It tells you who is important, who reports to whom, and which knight (requirement) is critical for the kingdom (solution) to function.

---

## 1.4 Define Success — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> **Define Success** is the structured process of translating the Point of View and problem statement into a set of **measurable, time-bound, and user-validated success criteria** that will be used to evaluate whether the final design solution has truly solved the user's problem. It establishes the *benchmarks of victory* before any ideation begins.

It answers the question: *"How will we know we have succeeded if we cannot define what success looks like?"*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an archer aiming at a target. Pulling the bowstring is the **Empathize** phase. Drawing the bow is the **Define** phase. **Define Success** is the act of **painting the bullseye** on the target before shooting. Without a clear bullseye, every arrow that lands *somewhere* on the target will feel like a success — but you will have made no real progress. A well-defined success metric is the painted bullseye.

---

## 1.5 Why the Three Methods are Used Together (The KTU Triad)

| Method | Primary Function | Output Format | Cognitive Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Storytelling** | Builds empathy & emotional alignment | Narrative / Story | **Affective (Heart)** |
| **Critical Items Diagram** | Decomposes and structures the problem | Visual Diagram | **Analytical (Head)** |
| **Define Success** | Quantifies and benchmarks the goal | Metric / KPI List | **Evaluative (Hand)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Together, these three methods ensure the Define phase output is **emotionally engaging**, **structurally clear**, and **empirically measurable** — the three pillars of a professional KTU-grade problem statement.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Dive — Storytelling in the Define Phase

### 2.1.1 The Architecture of a Design Story

A high-quality design story in the Define phase follows a **5-act narrative arc**, adapted from classical storytelling into the Stanford d.school / IDEO framework:

1. **Once upon a time… (Setting the Context)**
   - Introduce the user (persona) — name, age, occupation, environment.
   - Establish *when* and *where* the story happens.

2. **Every day… (The Daily Routine)**
   - Describe the user's normal life or work routine.
   - Establish the baseline "before" state.

3. **One day… (The Disruption / Trigger Event)**
   - Introduce the **inciting incident** — the moment the user's life is disturbed.
   - This is the empathy trigger.

4. **Because of that… (The Struggle / Pain Points)**
   - The cascading consequences of the disruption.
   - The *emotional* and *functional* pain points surface here.

5. **Because of that… (The Insight / Desired Future)**
   - What the user *wished* had happened.
   - This is the **design opportunity** — the seed of the solution.

6. **Until finally… (The Resolution — the new product/service)**
   - The new reality once the design is implemented.
   - This previews the *value proposition*.

### 2.1.2 Types of Stories Used in Define

| Story Type | Purpose | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **User Story** | "As a [user], I want to [action], so that [benefit]." | Scoping features, agile translation. |
| **Day-in-the-Life Story** | Chronological walkthrough of a typical day. | Showing context, environment, rituals. |
| **Extreme User Story** | Focuses on edge-case users (very young, elderly, disabled). | Surfacing hidden assumptions. |
| **Future-State Story** | Paints the picture of the ideal post-solution world. | Aligning stakeholders on vision. |
| **Failure Story** | Recounts a time the product/system *failed* the user. | Root-cause analysis of pain. |

### 2.1.3 Why Storytelling Works (The Psychology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Neuroscience Basis:** Cognitive research (e.g., Paul Zak, 2014) shows that **character-driven stories** trigger the release of **oxytocin** — the "trust and empathy hormone" — in the listener's brain. This is why a 3-minute user story often secures stakeholder buy-in faster than a 30-page report. **KTU examiners award marks for emotional resonance + factual structure, not for fiction.**

---

## 2.2 Deep Dive — Critical Items Diagram (CID)

### 2.2.1 Structural Anatomy

A Critical Items Diagram has **three structural layers**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Layer 0: Root} &= \text{The Central Problem / Point of View} \\
\text{Layer 1: Branches} &= \text{Critical Sub-Problems (3–7 typically)} \\
\text{Layer 2: Leaves} &= \text{Specific Design Requirements / Constraints}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 3–7 Rule (Miller's Law):** Working memory holds **7 ± 2** items. Therefore, a well-designed CID has **no more than 7 sub-problems at Layer 1**. If you have more, group them.

### 2.2.2 Construction Algorithm (Step-by-Step)

1. **Place the root** — write the PoV/Problem Statement in a box at the top.
2. **Identify 3–7 critical sub-problems** — ask: *"What must be solved for the root to be solved?"*
3. **Connect with directional arrows** — arrows flow from root to sub-problems.
4. **Decompose each sub-problem** — ask: *"What specific design requirements does this sub-problem demand?"*
5. **Mark priorities** — use colour coding (Red = Critical, Yellow = Important, Green = Nice-to-have).
6. **Validate with team** — every leaf should be testable; every branch should be indispensable.

### 2.2.3 Variations of the Critical Items Diagram

| Variation | Best For |
|---|---|
| **Hierarchical Tree** | Breaking down a single PoV into sub-problems. |
| **Fishbone / Ishikawa Variant** | Root-cause analysis of a specific failure. |
| **Affinity Mapping (precursor)** | Grouping raw empathy notes before drawing CID. |
| **K-J Diagram** | Sequential decision logic. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction KTU examiners test:** A **Critical Items Diagram** is **not** a mind-map. A mind-map is non-hierarchical and associative. A CID is **strictly hierarchical and decompositional** — the relationship from parent to child is *contains* or *requires*, not just *relates to*.

---

## 2.3 Deep Dive — Define Success

### 2.3.1 The Anatomy of a Good Success Criterion

A well-defined success criterion satisfies the **SMART** criteria (a KTU-board-favourite acronym):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{S} &\rightarrow \text{Specific} \\
\textbf{M} &\rightarrow \text{Measurable} \\
\textbf{A} &\rightarrow \text{Achievable} \\
\textbf{R} &\rightarrow \text{Relevant} \\
\textbf{T} &\rightarrow \text{Time-bound}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.3.2 Categories of Success Metrics

| Category | Metric Type | Example (Smart-Agriculture App) |
|---|---|---|
| **User / Adoption Metrics** | Downloads, DAU, Retention | 10,000 active farmers in 6 months. |
| **Behavioural Metrics** | Task success rate, time-on-task | User completes crop-diagnosis in < 2 min. |
| **Emotional Metrics** | NPS, SUS, Satisfaction score | User satisfaction ≥ 4.5/5. |
| **Business Metrics** | Revenue, Cost-savings, ROI | ₹500 reduction in input cost per acre. |
| **Social / Impact Metrics** | SDG alignment, Lives improved | 25% increase in small-farmer yield. |

### 2.3.3 The "Define Success" Statement Template

A board-standard Define Success statement is written as:

> *"The design will be considered **successful** when **\[measurable user behaviour / outcome\]** is achieved by **\[specific user group\]** within **\[time period\]**, as measured by **\[specific instrument / metric\]**."*

### 2.3.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet Table

> [!NOTE]
> **No engineering numerical formulas exist for this topic** — it is a design-process topic. The "formulas" below are the **structured templates** you must memorise for the KTU exam.

| Tool | Core Template | Key Output | Memory Trick |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Storytelling** | "Once upon a time… Every day… One day… Because of that… Until finally…" | A 1-page user narrative. | "**O-E-O-B-U**" — *5-Act Arc*. |
| **Critical Items Diagram** | Root (PoV) → 3–7 Sub-problems → Specific Requirements. | Hierarchical tree diagram. | "**3–7 leaves on every branch.**" |
| **Define Success** | "Success = \[Behaviour\] achieved by \[User\] within \[Time\], measured by \[Metric\]." | A SMART criteria list. | "**SMART** — Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound." |

### 2.3.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Storytelling** is used by product managers at companies like **Apple, Tesla, and Tata** in pitch decks.
- **Critical Items Diagrams** are the conceptual parents of **Work Breakdown Structures (WBS)** in project management (PMI/PMBOK) and **Functional Decomposition** in systems engineering (INCOSE).
- **Define Success** maps directly onto **OKRs (Objectives & Key Results)** used by Google, Intel, and modern agile teams — making it a career-skill, not just a classroom concept.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Methodological note for this topic:** Since the Define phase methods are *qualitative frameworks*, the "derivations" below are the **structured step-by-step procedures** for executing each method. We treat the design process with the same rigour as a mathematical derivation.

---

## 3.1 Worked Derivation — Building a Storytelling Artifact (5-Act Arc)

### Problem Context

You are designing a **medication-adherence app for elderly patients in Kerala**. You have raw interview notes. Derive a KTU-board-quality user story.

### Step-by-Step Construction

**Step 1 — Set the Context (Layer 1).**

From the empathy map, identify the persona:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Persona} &: \text{Amma, 72 years, Thrissur.} \\
\text{Setting} &: \text{Lives alone; daughter in Bengaluru.} \\
\text{Context} &: \text{Takes 4 medications daily for BP, diabetes, arthritis.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Establish the Daily Routine.**

> "Every day, at 7 AM, Amma sits at her small wooden kitchen table with her weekly pillbox, the *Sani-dhara*. She carefully opens each of the 28 small compartments, sorting her four tablets by size and colour."

**Step 3 — Introduce the Disruption (Trigger Event).**

> "One day, last Tuesday, Amma's arthritis flared badly. Her fingers could not grip the small pillbox lids. She missed her 7 AM dose. She did not realise it until 4 PM, when she felt dizzy and her blood sugar spiked."

**Step 4 — Show the Cascading Pain (The 'Because of That' Loop).**

> "Because of the missed dose, Amma's blood sugar reading at the local clinic the next morning was 280 mg/dL — far above her normal 110. Because of that, her doctor increased her insulin, which made her dizzy in the afternoons. Because of that, she fell in the bathroom and broke her wrist."

**Step 5 — Reveal the Insight / Desired Future.**

> "Until finally, Amma's daughter gifted her a simple voice-activated medication reminder — a device the size of a coaster that *talks* to her in Malayalam: 'Amma, it is 7 AM. Please take your white tablet and your yellow tablet.' Amma never missed a dose again."

### Result — The Storytelling Artifact

> **Story Title:** *"Amma's Coaster"* — a 1-page narrative used in the Define phase to align all stakeholders (engineers, clinicians, family) on the real human need: **accessibility for arthritic hands**, not just *reminders*.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation — Constructing a Critical Items Diagram

### Problem Context

PoV: *"Elderly patients in Kerala need a reliable, dignified, and accessible way to manage complex daily medication regimens."*

Derive the CID.

### Step-by-Step Construction

**Step 1 — Root Node.**

$$
\text{Root} = \text{Reliable, Dignified, Accessible Medication Management for Elderly Keralites}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify 3–7 Critical Sub-Problems (Layer 1).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SP}_1 &: \text{Physical Accessibility (arthritic hands, low vision).} \\
\text{SP}_2 &: \text{Cognitive Load (forgetfulness, multiple drugs).} \\
\text{SP}_3 &: \text{Cultural & Language Fit (Malayalam, family involvement).} \\
\text{SP}_4 &: \text{Trust & Dignity (no "shaming" the elderly).} \\
\text{SP}_5 &: \text{Health-System Integration (doctor, pharmacy).}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> We have **5 sub-problems** — within Miller's 7 ± 2 limit. ✔

**Step 3 — Decompose into Specific Requirements (Layer 2).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SP}_1 \text{ (Accessibility)} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{R}_{1.1}: \text{Large physical buttons or voice interface.} \\
\text{R}_{1.2}: \text{High-contrast display with Malayalam fonts.} \\
\text{R}_{1.3}: \text{Audible alerts ≥ 70 dB.}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SP}_2 \text{ (Cognitive Load)} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{R}_{2.1}: \text{One-tap confirmation of dose taken.} \\
\text{R}_{2.2}: \text{Visual + audio + haptic cue triad.} \\
\text{R}_{2.3}: \text{Family-member override alert after 30 min.}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Validate (the test).**

Ask: *"If I implement all leaves, does the root problem get solved?"* If *yes*, the CID is valid.

### Resulting CID Structure (Schematic)

```
                [ROOT: Medication Mgmt for Elderly]
                            |
        +---------+---------+---------+---------+
        |         |         |         |         |
   [Access]   [Cogn.]   [Cult.]   [Trust]   [Health]
        |         |
   +----+----+   +----+----+
   |    |    |   |    |    |
  R11  R12  R13 R21  R22  R23
```

(Formal Mermaid version in Section 4.)

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation — Define Success Metrics

### Step-by-Step Construction (Using SMART)

For the *Amma's Coaster* design above:

| # | User Behaviour | User Group | Time | Metric (Instrument) | SMART Check |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **S1** | Takes correct dose on time. | Amma & 100 similar users. | 90 days. | Pillbox sensor log ≥ 95% on-time. | ✔ Specific, ✔ Measurable, ✔ Achievable, ✔ Relevant, ✔ Time-bound. |
| **S2** | Reports ease of use. | Same cohort. | 30 days. | SUS (System Usability Scale) ≥ 80/100. | ✔ |
| **S3** | Avoids missed-dose ER visits. | Same cohort. | 6 months. | Hospital records: 0 missed-dose ER visits. | ✔ |
| **S4** | Family member feels "informed". | Caregivers. | 60 days. | NPS ≥ +50. | ✔ |

> [!TIP]
> A KTU "Define Success" answer that lists **at least 4 SMART criteria** (across at least 2 metric categories — e.g., behavioural + emotional) scores full marks. One vague "users will be happy" does *not* count.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Method

| Situation in Define Phase | Best Method | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Stakeholders are disengaged / cannot relate to user data. | **Storytelling** | Triggers empathy, oxytocin release. |
| The problem feels too "big" or "fuzzy". | **Critical Items Diagram** | Decomposes into testable sub-parts. |
| The team is jumping to solutions without alignment on goals. | **Define Success** | Forces alignment on measurable outcomes. |
| The team has all three problems simultaneously. | **All three — in this order** | Story → CID → Success. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU-preferred sequence** (frequently tested): **Storytelling first → Critical Items Diagram second → Define Success third.** This mirrors the **Heart → Head → Hand** progression of the IDEO model.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams follow the **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards** — all node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`), and labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting.

---

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The 5-Act Storytelling Arc

```mermaid
    A["ACT 1: ONCE UPON A TIME - SET USER AND CONTEXT"]

    E --> F
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Define Success as a SMART Filter Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw["RAW SUCCESS IDEA: USERS WILL LIKE THE PRODUCT"]
    s["FILTER 1: IS IT SPECIFIC"]
    m["FILTER 2: IS IT MEASURABLE"]
    a["FILTER 3: IS IT ACHIEVABLE"]
    r["FILTER 4: IS IT RELEVANT"]
    t["FILTER 5: IS IT TIME BOUND"]
    out["SMART SUCCESS METRIC: 95 PERCENT DOSE ON TIME IN 90 DAYS MEASURED BY SENSOR LOG"]
    raw --> s
    s --> m
    m --> a
    a --> r
    r --> t
    t --> out
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — How the Three Methods Connect to the Point of View

```mermaid

    story["STORYTELLING: HUMAN NARRATIVE"]

```

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — The Heart, Head, Hand Triad of the Define Phase

```mermaid

    h["HEART: STORYTELLING BUILDS EMPATHY AND EMOTIONAL BUY IN"]
    hd["HEAD: CRITICAL ITEMS DIAGRAM STRUCTURES THE PROBLEM"]
    ha["HAND: DEFINE SUCCESS SETS MEASURABLE TARGETS"]

    h --> goal
    hd --> goal
    ha --> goal
```

> [!TIP]
> **For the KTU exam:** When a 14-mark question asks you to *"Explain the methods of the Define phase"*, drawing **any one of the above Mermaid-style diagrams in your answer sheet** earns 2–3 easy marks (visual representation is rewarded even when the diagram is hand-drawn).

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — *Conceptual Recall*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
> Define **Storytelling** as a method of the Define phase in Design Thinking. State any **two types** of stories used in the Define phase.

**Model Answer:**

> **Storytelling** in the Define phase is a structured narrative technique that converts raw qualitative user-research data into a compelling, chronological user-experience narrative, in order to build empathy, communicate insights, and align stakeholders on the user's unmet needs.
>
> **Two types of stories used in the Define phase:**
> 1. **User Story** — A short, persona-based statement of the form *"As a [user], I want to [action], so that [benefit]."*
> 2. **Day-in-the-Life Story** — A chronological walkthrough of a typical day in the user's life, showing context, routines, and friction points.
>
> *(Other acceptable answers: Extreme User Story, Future-State Story, Failure Story.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — *Definition + Distinction*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
> What is a **Critical Items Diagram**? How is it different from a **mind-map**?

**Model Answer:**

> A **Critical Items Diagram (CID)** is a hierarchical, tree-like visual diagram that decomposes a central design problem or Point of View into its critical sub-problems and specific design requirements, mapping the *cause-effect* or *means-ends* relationships between them.
>
> **Difference from a Mind-Map:**
>
> | Aspect | Critical Items Diagram | Mind-Map |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Structure** | Strictly hierarchical (parent → child). | Non-hierarchical, radial. |
> | **Relationship** | "Contains" / "Requires" (decompositional). | "Associates with" (associative). |
> | **Purpose** | Decompose a problem into testable parts. | Brainstorm associations freely. |
> | **Rules** | 3–7 sub-problems (Miller's Law). | No numerical rule. |
>
> A CID is *prescriptive and structural*; a mind-map is *exploratory and associative*.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern**, Module 2 carries a 14-mark question with **internal choice** (either-or). Below are **two equivalent alternative questions** (Question A and Question B) — typical of KTU 2024 model papers.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]**
> *Choose either (a) or (b):*
>
> **(a)** Explain the **5-act storytelling arc** used in the Define phase of Design Thinking. Using a real-world engineering product (e.g., a smart helmet, irrigation controller, or campus-navigation app), construct a **complete user story** following this arc. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** What is a **Critical Items Diagram (CID)**? Describe its **construction procedure** with a suitable example. State **Miller's Law** and explain its relevance to CIDs. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

1. **Definition of Storytelling (1 mark):** Narrative technique converting user data into a human, chronological story to build empathy in the Define phase.
2. **5-Act Arc (3 marks):** Briefly explain each of *Once upon a time → Every day → One day → Because of that → Until finally* with one line each.
3. **Constructed Story (3 marks):** Sample for a *Smart Helmet for Two-Wheeler Riders in Kerala Monsoon*:
   - *Once upon a time:* Rahul, 28, daily office commuter from Kottayam to Ernakulam.
   - *Every day:* Wears a half-face helmet, rides 48 km/day, depends on phone maps clipped to handlebar.
   - *One day:* A sudden August downpour; visor fogs up; phone screen drowned; he missed a turn onto the highway.
   - *Because of that:* He braked hard, skidded, and his helmet hit the road — but the cheap chin-strap snapped.
   - *Because of that:* He sustained a 6 cm laceration on the chin; missed 2 weeks of work.
   - *Until finally:* A new smart-helmet with anti-fog heated visor, Bluetooth navigation audio in Malayalam/English, and a DOT-certified chin-strap lock — Rahul rides confidently in any rain.
4. **Conclusion (0 marks, optional wrap):** Show how the story surfaces *real* design requirements (anti-fog, audio nav, chin-strap safety).

#### Model Solution to (b):

1. **Definition of CID (1.5 marks):** Hierarchical tree diagram decomposing a central design problem into sub-problems and specific design requirements.
2. **Construction Procedure (3 marks):** List the 6-step construction algorithm (Root → Sub-problems → Arrows → Decompose → Colour-prioritise → Validate).
3. **Example (1.5 marks):** Use any of: Smart Helmet (above) → Root: *"Safe, monsoon-resilient commuter helmet"*; Sub-problems: *Visibility / Connectivity / Crash-safety / Comfort*; Leaves: *Heated visor, BT 5.0 audio, DOT strap, EPS foam, < 1.4 kg*.
4. **Miller's Law (1 mark):** *George Miller (1956)* — *"The magical number seven, plus or minus two"* — short-term working memory holds 7 ± 2 chunks.
5. **Relevance to CID (0 marks bonus, optional):** Therefore, a CID must have **no more than 7 sub-problems** at Layer 1, or it overwhelms the design team.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]**
> *Choose either (a) or (b):*
>
> **(a)** Explain **"Define Success"** as a method of the Define phase. Write the **SMART criteria** and apply them to define the success of any **two engineering products** of your choice. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare and contrast the **three methods of the Define phase** (Storytelling, Critical Items Diagram, Define Success) with respect to their **purpose, format, output, and stage in the design process**. Draw a block diagram showing how they integrate to form a complete **Design Brief**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

1. **Definition of Define Success (1.5 marks):** Process of translating the PoV into measurable, time-bound success criteria that benchmark the final design solution.
2. **SMART Criteria (2 marks):** Define each of *Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound* in one line.
3. **Application to Product 1 — Smart Helmet (1.5 marks):** Example — *"The smart helmet will be considered successful when 90% of 200 test users in Kerala report zero visibility-related braking incidents during monsoon, measured by in-helmet accelerometer logs, over 6 months."*
4. **Application to Product 2 — Campus Navigation App (1.5 marks):** Example — *"The campus app will be considered successful when 85% of 500 students complete a navigation task in under 90 seconds, measured by in-app analytics, within 3 months of launch."*
5. **Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Note that both metrics span *behavioural* and *emotional* categories — satisfying the KTU rubric.

#### Model Solution to (b):

1. **Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Storytelling | Critical Items Diagram | Define Success |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Build empathy. | Structure the problem. | Quantify the goal. |
| **Format** | Narrative prose. | Hierarchical diagram. | Metric / KPI list. |
| **Output** | 1-page user story. | CID tree. | SMART criteria list. |
| **Stage** | Define – start. | Define – middle. | Define – end. |
| **Cognitive Role** | Affective (Heart). | Analytical (Head). | Evaluative (Hand). |

2. **Block Diagram (2 marks):** *(Hand-drawn equivalent of Section 4.4's Mermaid diagram — Story → PoV → CID + Success → Design Brief.)*
3. **Integration Explanation (1 mark):** Together the three outputs feed the **Design Brief** — the document handed to the Ideation phase.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways KTU students LOSE marks on this topic (Dec 2024 pattern):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Storytelling with Story-*boarding*:** Story-boarding is a **Develop-phase** tool (visual sketches); Storytelling is a **Define-phase** tool (narrative). Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Drawing a Mind-Map and labelling it "Critical Items Diagram":** A CID *must* be **hierarchical with directional arrows and 3–7 children per node**. A radial bubble map is a mind-map, not a CID. **Penalty: 2 marks.**
> 3. **Writing vague success criteria** like *"The product should be successful and users will like it."* This is **not SMART**. Always include a **number, a time, and an instrument**. **Penalty: 1.5 marks.**
> 4. **Forgetting to mention the sequence:** Examiners reward the *Heart → Head → Hand* order: Story first, CID second, Success third. Answering in the wrong order loses flow marks.
> 5. **Skipping the example:** A theoretical-only answer on a 7-mark sub-part scores a maximum of 4 marks. Always **construct at least one concrete example**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2, Topic: Methods of the Define Phase**

- ✅ The **Define phase** sits at the convergence point of the **Double Diamond** — it converts empathy data into a focused problem statement.
- ✅ The **three methods** of the Define phase are: **Storytelling, Critical Items Diagram (CID), Define Success**.
- ✅ **Storytelling** uses the **5-act arc**: *Once upon a time → Every day → One day → Because of that → Until finally.* (Mnemonic: **O-E-O-B-U**.)
- ✅ Storytelling triggers **oxytocin** in the listener — this is why it secures stakeholder buy-in faster than data.
- ✅ A **Critical Items Diagram** is a **strictly hierarchical, decompositional** tree — **not** a mind-map.
- ✅ CIDs obey **Miller's Law**: **3–7 sub-problems** at the first level of decomposition.
- ✅ CID construction: **Root → 3–7 Sub-problems → Specific Requirements → Colour-code priorities → Validate**.
- ✅ **Define Success** uses the **SMART** framework: **Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound**.
- ✅ A good success criterion includes a **number, a time, an instrument, and a user group** — e.g., *"95% of 100 elderly users complete dose within 90 days, measured by sensor log."*
- ✅ The **preferred sequence** in the Define phase is **Heart → Head → Hand**: **Storytelling → Critical Items Diagram → Define Success**.
- ✅ **Storytelling** is **affective**, **CID** is **analytical**, **Define Success** is **evaluative** — together they form the **Design Brief** that feeds the Ideation phase.
- ✅ The three methods together make the problem statement **emotionally engaging, structurally clear, and empirically measurable** — the three pillars of a KTU-grade problem framing.
- ✅ For the exam: **always construct at least one example** (a real Kerala-context product works best — smart helmet, elderly medication app, KSRTC tracker, etc.) — theory-only answers are capped at ~60% marks on 7-mark sub-parts.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stages of ideation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme)**
> **Ideation** is the **third and most divergent phase** of the Design Thinking process, where the design team deliberately generates, develops, and communicates a wide range of **novel and creative ideas** aimed at solving the previously defined problem statement (HPI - "How Might We" question). The **Stages of Ideation** refer to the systematic, sequential sub-processes that guide a team from a *raw problem space* to a *curated set of actionable concepts*, ready for prototyping.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (mapped to the **d.school Stanford** and **IDEO** methodologies), ideation is not a single act of "thinking of an idea" — it is a **structured pipeline** consisting of **five (5) canonical stages**:

1. **Stage I — Problem Framing & Reframing (Preparation)**
2. **Stage II — Divergent Idea Generation (The "Diverge" phase)**
3. **Stage III — Idea Clustering & Synthesis (Affinity Mapping)**
4. **Stage IV — Convergent Idea Evaluation & Selection**
5. **Stage V — Idea Refinement & Concept Brief (Preparation for Prototyping)**

Each stage has its own tools, deliverables, and quality gates, which are assessed in the KTU university examination as per the **Course Outcomes (CO3, CO4, CO5)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Ideation like "Planning a Wedding Menu" for 200 guests.**
>
> You have already **identified the problem** ("We need food everyone will love within ₹50,000").
> Now, you don't just pick the first dish you think of. Instead, you:
> 1. **Re-read the constraints** (diet, budget, season) → *Problem Framing*
> 2. **Throw out 30 crazy options** with your family, no judgment → *Divergent Generation*
> 3. **Group them** into clusters: "Snacks", "Mains", "Desserts" → *Clustering*
> 4. **Compare and drop** the ones that are too costly or boring → *Evaluation*
> 5. **Finalize the menu card** with portions, plating, and chef notes → *Refinement*
>
> That whole journey = **the 5 Stages of Ideation**.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **HMW** | "How Might We" — the canonical ideation prompt (boundary-setting sentence) |
| **Diverge** | Widening the solution space (quantity over quality) |
| **Converge** | Narrowing the solution space (quality over quantity) |
| **Affinity Diagram** | Visual clustering of similar ideas |
| **DOT Voting** | Democratic method where each participant places **3–5 dot stickers** on the best ideas |
| **POV** | Point of View — the user-centric problem statement that fuels ideation |
| **Idea Funnel** | The graphical model showing ideas going from many → few |
| **Time-Box** | A strict duration cap (e.g., 30 minutes) to prevent over-thinking |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The standard metric for an effective ideation session is to generate **at least 50–100 ideas per HMW question** within a **30–60 minute** time-box. This is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stage-by-Stage Operational Breakdown

### 🟢 Stage I — Problem Framing & Reframing
- **What it is:** Re-examining the **Point of View (POV)** and **HMW question** before any idea generation.
- **Why it matters:** Many teams skip this and generate ideas for the *wrong* problem.
- **How it's done:**
  - Re-read the user persona and need statement.
  - Rewrite the HMW question in **3 different ways** (Reframing).
  - Identify **constraints** (time, money, technology, regulation).
  - Set the **scope guardrails** for the upcoming brainstorming.
- **Tools used:** 5W1H Analysis, POV Reframing Canvas, Constraint Mapping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept Hook:** A *well-framed problem* is half-solved. Reframing is what separates a junior designer from a senior one.

---

### 🟡 Stage II — Divergent Idea Generation (The "Diverge" Phase)
- **What it is:** Generating the **maximum quantity** of raw, unfiltered ideas.
- **Rule #1:** **Defer Judgment** — no criticism allowed.
- **Rule #2:** **Quantity breeds Quality** — target 50+ ideas.
- **Rule #3:** **Build on others' ideas** — use "Yes, and..." instead of "No, but...".
- **Rule #4:** **Encourage Wild Ideas** — the crazier the idea, the more likely it triggers a viable solution.
- **Tools used:**
  - **Brainstorming** (verbal, in-person)
  - **Brainwriting 6-3-5** (silent, written, structured)
  - **SCAMPER** (Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put to another use, Eliminate, Reverse)
  - **Mind Mapping** (radial, visual)
  - **Worst Possible Idea** (invert to find good)
  - **Sketchstorming** (visual ideation)
  - **Analogies & Biomimicry** (nature-inspired)

---

### 🟠 Stage III — Idea Clustering & Synthesis (Affinity Mapping)
- **What it is:** Grouping **similar ideas** into thematic clusters without evaluation.
- **How it's done:**
  - Write each idea on a **separate sticky note**.
  - Spread on a wall or whiteboard.
  - Silently **group** notes that feel related.
  - Give each cluster a **thematic label** (e.g., "Digital", "Physical", "Service-based").
- **Purpose:** Patterns emerge. The team sees which **themes are strongest** by sheer volume.
- **Tools used:** **Affinity Diagram (KJ Method)**, Card Sorting, Theme Mapping.

> [!NOTE]
> **Affinitization ≠ Evaluation.** In this stage, you only *group*. You do NOT judge.

---

### 🔴 Stage IV — Convergent Idea Evaluation & Selection
- **What it is:** Narrowing the **hundreds of raw ideas + clusters** down to **2–5 winning concepts**.
- **Evaluation Criteria (KTU Standard Set):**
  - **Feasibility** — Can we build it with current technology and resources?
  - **Desirability** — Do users actually *want* this?
  - **Viability** — Is it sustainable as a business/solution?
- **Selection Methods:**
  - **DOT Voting** (democratic, fast, 3 dots per person)
  - **Impact-Effort Matrix** (2x2 grid: Quick Win / Major Project / Fill-in / Thankless Task)
  - **Pugh Matrix** (multi-criteria weighted scoring)
  - **NUF Test** (New, Useful, Feasible)
  - **4-Criteria Filter** (Desirability, Feasibility, Viability, Ethics)

---

### 🟣 Stage V — Idea Refinement & Concept Brief
- **What it is:** Translating the winning idea(s) into a **clear, communicable concept statement** ready for prototyping.
- **Deliverables produced:**
  - **Concept Poster** (visual one-pager)
  - **Elevator Pitch** (30-second verbal description)
  - **User Journey Sketch** (how the user interacts with the idea)
  - **Assumptions List** (what must be true for this idea to work)
- **Tools used:** **Concept Canvas**, Storyboarding, Assumption Mapping, LEGO Serious Play.

---

## 2.2 The Idea Funnel — Visualizing the Diverge-Converge Cycle

The **Idea Funnel** is the **canonical mental model** for the stages of ideation in any KTU examination answer.

$$
\text{Stage I} \xrightarrow{\text{Frame}} \text{Stage II} \xrightarrow{\text{Generate (Diverge)}} \text{Stage III} \xrightarrow{\text{Cluster}} \text{Stage IV} \xrightarrow{\text{Evaluate (Converge)}} \text{Stage V}
$$

| Stage | Input | Output | Volume Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| I. Framing | POV, User Need | 1–3 HMW Questions | 1 → 3 |
| II. Generation | HMW Question | 50–100 raw ideas | 3 → 100 |
| III. Clustering | 100 raw ideas | 8–12 thematic clusters | 100 → 10 |
| IV. Evaluation | 10 clusters | 2–5 winning concepts | 10 → 3 |
| V. Refinement | 3 concepts | 1 ready-to-prototype concept brief | 3 → 1 |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** A 7-mark question often asks students to *"Explain the stages of ideation with the idea funnel diagram."* Always include the funnel as a visual.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Stage | Key Tool | Key Rule | Output Artifact | Duration (Typical) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Framing | 5W1H, HMW | Reframe before generating | HMW Question | **15 min** |
| 2 | Generation | Brainstorm, SCAMPER, Mind Map | Defer judgment | 50+ raw ideas | **30–60 min** |
| 3 | Clustering | Affinity Diagram (KJ) | Group, don't judge | 8–12 themes | **20 min** |
| 4 | Evaluation | DOT Voting, Impact-Effort, Pugh | Use multiple criteria | 2–5 winners | **30 min** |
| 5 | Refinement | Concept Canvas, Storyboard | Make it tangible | Concept Brief | **45 min** |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Stages of Ideation framework is used in:
- **Tata Elxsi & HCL Tech** product design studios
- **ISRO** mission concept generation
- **TCS Designit** service design projects
- **Stanford d.school & MIT Media Lab** student projects
- **Biomimicry Institute** innovation challenges

> **Industrial Standard:** Every modern product team (Agile, Scrum, Design Sprints) allocates a **dedicated "Ideation Sprint"** lasting **2–5 days**, structured exactly around these 5 stages.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: The Diverge–Converge Ratio

The effectiveness of an ideation session can be **quantitatively expressed** using the *Idea Survival Ratio*:

$$
R_{\text{survival}} = \frac{N_{\text{final}}}{N_{\text{generated}}}
$$

Where:
- $N_{\text{generated}}$ = Total number of ideas produced in Stage II
- $N_{\text{final}}$ = Number of ideas surviving after Stage IV

**Industry benchmark:**

$$
R_{\text{healthy}} \approx \frac{3}{100} = 0.03 \quad \text{(i.e., 3 winners per 100 raw ideas)}
$$

If $R_{\text{survival}} > 0.10$, the team was **over-converging** (filtering too aggressively).
If $R_{\text{survival}} < 0.01$, the team was **under-evaluating** (no clear winners).

> **Step-by-step interpretation:**
> 1. Suppose a team generates $N_{\text{generated}} = 80$ ideas in Stage II.
> 2. After clustering in Stage III, the themes number $N_{\text{themes}} = 8$.
> 3. After evaluation in Stage IV, the winners number $N_{\text{final}} = 3$.
> 4. Therefore:
>
> $$
> R_{\text{survival}} = \frac{3}{80} = 0.0375
> $$
>
> 5. This falls within the healthy range $[0.01, 0.10]$, indicating a **balanced ideation funnel**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: The HMW Quality Score

A well-constructed HMW question can be **scored** using the formula:

$$
S_{\text{HMW}} = W_1 \cdot D + W_2 \cdot S + W_3 \cdot B + W_4 \cdot A
$$

Where:
- $D$ = **Desirability** score (0–10) — Does it focus on user need?
- $S$ = **Scope** score (0–10) — Is it broad enough for many solutions?
- $B$ = **Brevity** score (0–10) — Is it short and memorable?
- $A$ = **Action-orientation** score (0–10) — Does it invite action?
- $W_1, W_2, W_3, W_4$ are weights, typically $W_1 = 0.30$, $W_2 = 0.30$, $W_3 = 0.15$, $W_4 = 0.25$.

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{4} W_i = 1.00
$$

**Example evaluation:**

| HMW Question | D | S | B | A | Score |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| "How might we help students revise for exams in 1 week?" | 9 | 8 | 8 | 9 | 8.55 |
| "How might we help the educational system globally?" | 5 | 2 | 4 | 3 | 3.50 |

> **Interpretation:** The first HMW is **actionable and scope-controlled**; the second is **too broad and unfocused**, which is why a clear Stage I is critical.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Generation Tools

| Tool | Divergence Level | Cognitive Load | Time Required | Best For | KTU Exam Tag |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Brainstorming** | Very High | Low | 30 min | Mixed teams | "Verbal ideation" |
| **Brainwriting 6-3-5** | High | Medium | 30 min | Introverts / large groups | "Silent ideation" |
| **SCAMPER** | Medium | High | 45 min | Improving existing products | "Lateral thinking" |
| **Mind Mapping** | High | Low | 20 min | Visual learners | "Radial ideation" |
| **Worst Possible Idea** | Very High | Low | 20 min | Breaking creative blocks | "Inversion technique" |
| **Sketchstorming** | High | Medium | 30 min | Product / UI design | "Visual ideation" |
| **Biomimicry** | Medium | Very High | 60 min | Sustainable engineering | "Nature-inspired" |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Conceptual Insight:** The choice of tool depends on the **team size, time budget, and problem type**. This trade-off matrix is a high-yield exam topic.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: How to Build an Idea Funnel (Worked Example)

**Problem:** "How might we reduce canteen food waste in our college?"

**Step 1 (Stage I — Framing):**
- POV: *Students in our college need affordable, fresh meals, but the canteen wastes 40% of prepared food daily.*
- HMW: *"How might we predict and reduce canteen food waste using student feedback?"*

**Step 2 (Stage II — Generation):**
Generated **60 raw ideas** including:
- Daily menu forecast using a weather API
- A WhatsApp group for "Today’s surplus meals"
- Pre-order app with dynamic pricing
- Convert surplus to biogas (biomimicry)
- Share-a-meal loyalty program
- ... and 55 more.

**Step 3 (Stage III — Clustering):**
Grouped into **6 themes**:
1. *Demand Forecasting* (8 ideas)
2. *Dynamic Pricing* (10 ideas)
3. *Awareness Campaigns* (7 ideas)
4. *Surplus Redistribution* (15 ideas)
5. *Process Improvement* (12 ideas)
6. *Tech Solutions* (8 ideas)

**Step 4 (Stage IV — Evaluation):**
Using **Impact-Effort Matrix** and **DOT Voting**, top 2 concepts selected:
- **Concept A:** "Surplus Share" — a real-time WhatsApp/Telegram bot pushing notifications when extra food is available.
- **Concept B:** "Pre-Order Smart Canteen" — an app letting students pre-book meals 24 hours ahead.

**Step 5 (Stage V — Refinement):**
- Concept Brief prepared with user journey, assumptions, and KPIs.
- Ready for **prototyping** in the next module.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define ideation and list its five stages.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Ideation** is the third phase of Design Thinking where a team generates a large volume of diverse ideas to solve a defined problem.
> The **five stages** are:
> 1. **Problem Framing** — defining the HMW question.
> 2. **Divergent Idea Generation** — brainstorming 50+ ideas.
> 3. **Idea Clustering** — affinity mapping.
> 4. **Convergent Evaluation** — selecting winners.
> 5. **Idea Refinement** — producing a concept brief for prototyping.

---

### **Q2. What is the role of DOT Voting in ideation?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **DOT Voting** is a **democratic idea-selection tool** used in Stage IV (Convergent Evaluation). Each team member is given **3–5 dot stickers** and silently places them on the ideas they find most promising. It is **fast, anonymous, bias-free**, and quickly surfaces the ideas with the strongest group consensus, helping teams narrow 50+ raw ideas down to 3–5 winning concepts.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> *Students commonly lose marks in the following ways:*
> 1. **Conflating "Brainstorming" with the entire ideation phase** — brainstorming is only ONE tool in Stage II.
> 2. **Skipping Stage I (Framing)** — generating ideas without a clear HMW question.
> 3. **Forgetting the Diverge–Converge rhythm** — always mention that ideation is BOTH wide AND narrow.
> 4. **No diagram in long answers** — drawing the **Idea Funnel** is mandatory for full marks.
> 5. **Mixing "ideation" with "prototyping"** — ideation ends at the Concept Brief, not the prototype.

---

### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**Q.A (a)** Explain the five stages of ideation in Design Thinking with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The five stages of ideation are the **systematic sub-processes** that transform a defined problem into a curated concept ready for prototyping. They are:

**1. Problem Framing (Preparation):**
The team re-reads the Point of View (POV) and converts the user need into one or more **"How Might We" (HMW)** questions. Example: For "students need affordable transport", the HMW becomes *"How might we reduce student transport costs by 30%?"* Constraints like time, money, and policy are mapped.

**2. Divergent Idea Generation:**
The team generates a **high volume of raw ideas (50–100)** using tools such as **Brainstorming, Brainwriting 6-3-5, SCAMPER, Mind Mapping, and Sketchstorming**. The cardinal rules are: **defer judgment, encourage wild ideas, build on others' ideas, and go for quantity**. Example: For the transport HMW, ideas range from "carpool app" to "subsidized bicycle rentals" to "AI-driven shuttle routing."

**3. Idea Clustering (Affinity Mapping):**
All raw ideas are written on sticky notes and grouped into **thematic clusters** (e.g., "Shared Mobility", "Public Transport", "Personal Vehicles", "Micro-mobility"). No evaluation happens here — only **pattern recognition**. The team sees which themes dominate by sheer volume.

**4. Convergent Evaluation & Selection:**
The clusters are **filtered** using criteria like **Desirability, Feasibility, Viability, and Ethics**. Tools include **DOT Voting, Impact-Effort Matrix, Pugh Matrix, and the NUF Test** (New, Useful, Feasible). The top **2–5 concepts** survive.

**5. Idea Refinement & Concept Brief:**
The winning idea(s) are converted into a **Concept Brief** containing a one-line pitch, a user journey sketch, an assumption map, and a storyboard. This brief becomes the **input to the prototyping phase** (next module).

**Conclusion:**
The five stages embody the **diverge–converge rhythm** of Design Thinking — wide exploration followed by focused selection — ensuring both **creativity and discipline** in the innovation process.

---

**Q.A (b)** Compare Brainstorming, Brainwriting, and SCAMPER as ideation tools. Which is best suited for a large, introverted engineering team? Justify. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Brainstorming | Brainwriting 6-3-5 | SCAMPER |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mode** | Verbal, group | Silent, written | Prompt-driven |
| **Output Volume** | 30–50 ideas | 18–30 ideas per round | 30–40 ideas |
| **Cognitive Load** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Introvert-Friendly** | No | **Yes** | Yes |
| **Time Required** | 30 min | 30 min | 45 min |
| **Best For** | Mixed teams | Large / diverse groups | Improving existing products |
| **Drawback** | Dominant voices dominate | Slower, requires discipline | Limited to incremental ideas |

**Recommendation for a large, introverted engineering team:**
**Brainwriting 6-3-5** is the best choice because:
1. It is **silent**, which protects introverts from being overshadowed by extroverts.
2. It is **structured** (6 people × 3 ideas × 5 rounds = 90 ideas), ensuring high volume.
3. The **written handoff** mechanism forces every member to **build on others' ideas**, embodying the "Yes, and..." rule.
4. It works well in **engineering teams** who are comfortable with structured, analytical formats.

**Conclusion:** While brainstorming is the most popular tool, brainwriting is the most *equitable and productive* tool for introverted technical teams.

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**Q.B (a)** Describe the Idea Funnel with a neat diagram. Explain how it visualizes the diverge–converge rhythm of ideation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Idea Funnel** is the canonical visual model of the ideation phase. It is shaped like an inverted triangle, **wide at the top and narrow at the bottom**, representing the journey from many ideas to few winners.

**Structure of the Funnel (Top to Bottom):**
- **Top — Stage I & II (Wide):** A broad, undefined problem space. After framing and divergent generation, the funnel is filled with **50–100 raw ideas**.
- **Middle — Stage III (Narrowing):** Clustering groups ideas into **8–12 themes**, narrowing the funnel.
- **Lower-Middle — Stage IV (Sharper):** Evaluation filters the themes down to **2–5 winning concepts**.
- **Bottom — Stage V (Point):** Refinement produces a **single concept brief** ready for prototyping.

**Diverge–Converge Rhythm:**
- The **top half** of the funnel represents **divergence** — the team deliberately *widens* the solution space, prioritizing **quantity and wildness** over judgment.
- The **bottom half** represents **convergence** — the team *narrows* the space using **structured criteria and voting**, prioritizing **quality and feasibility**.
- This rhythmic alternation (wide → narrow → wide → narrow) is what makes Design Thinking ideation both **creative and disciplined**.

**Diagram:**

```
        _________________________________
        \                                /
         \    100+ RAW IDEAS (S II)     /     ←  DIVERGE
          \                            /
           \   8-12 CLUSTERS (S III)  /       ←  CLUSTER
            \                        /
             \  3-5 WINNERS (S IV)   /         ←  CONVERGE
              \                    /
               \ 1 CONCEPT (S V)  /            ←  REFINE
                \________________/
                          ↓
                  PROTOTYPE BRIEF
```

**Conclusion:** The Idea Funnel is more than a diagram — it is a **discipline tool** that prevents teams from either over-filtering (too convergent) or under-filtering (too divergent).

---

**Q.B (b)** A team of 5 engineering students conducted a 45-minute brainstorming session on *"How might we make our college library more student-friendly?"* They generated 70 ideas, clustered them into 9 themes, and selected 4 winners. Calculate the Idea Survival Ratio and comment on its health. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- $N_{\text{generated}} = 70$ ideas
- $N_{\text{clusters}} = 9$ themes
- $N_{\text{final}} = 4$ winners

**Step 1: Calculate the Idea Survival Ratio**
The Idea Survival Ratio is given by:

$$
R_{\text{survival}} = \frac{N_{\text{final}}}{N_{\text{generated}}}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
R_{\text{survival}} = \frac{4}{70}
$$

Performing the division:

$$
R_{\text{survival}} = 0.0571 \approx 0.057
$$

**Step 2: Express as a Percentage**
$$
R_{\text{survival}} \times 100 = 5.71\%
$$

**Step 3: Compare with Industry Benchmark**
The healthy range is:

$$
0.01 \le R_{\text{survival}} \le 0.10
$$

i.e., **1% to 10%** of generated ideas should survive as winning concepts.

**Step 4: Cluster-to-Winner Efficiency**
$$
R_{\text{cluster}} = \frac{N_{\text{final}}}{N_{\text{clusters}}} = \frac{4}{9} = 0.444
$$

This means **44% of clusters produced a winner** — a strong cluster diversity indicator.

**Step 5: Interpretation & Comment**
- The team's survival ratio of **5.71%** falls comfortably within the healthy range.
- The team was **not over-filtering** (which would push $R > 10\%$) and **not under-evaluating** (which would push $R < 1\%$).
- The high cluster-to-winner ratio (44%) indicates the **themes were well-distributed**, giving a balanced final set of 4 concepts.
- **Recommendation:** Proceed to Stage V (Refinement) for the 4 winners and select 1 final concept for prototyping.

**Conclusion:** The ideation session was **balanced, productive, and statistically healthy**, demonstrating good design-thinking discipline.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Stages of Ideation**

- ☐ **Ideation = Phase 3 of Design Thinking** — comes after Empathize and Define.
- ☐ **5 Stages:** Framing → Generation → Clustering → Evaluation → Refinement.
- ☐ **Framing** produces the **HMW ("How Might We")** question.
- ☐ **Generation** targets **50–100 ideas** in a 30–60 min time-box.
- ☐ **Defer judgment** is the #1 rule of divergent generation.
- ☐ **Clustering** uses the **Affinity Diagram (KJ Method)** — no evaluation here.
- ☐ **Evaluation** uses **DOT Voting, Impact-Effort Matrix, Pugh Matrix, NUF Test**.
- ☐ **Refinement** produces the **Concept Brief** (input to prototyping).
- ☐ **Idea Funnel** is the canonical visual — wide at top, narrow at bottom.
- ☐ **Diverge–Converge rhythm** is the soul of ideation.
- ☐ **Idea Survival Ratio** = $N_{\text{final}} / N_{\text{generated}}$; healthy range is **1%–10%**.
- ☐ **Common pitfall:** Don't skip Stage I — framing is half the battle.
- ☐ **Common pitfall:** Brainstorming is a TOOL, not the entire process.
- ☐ **Top tools to memorize:** Brainstorming, Brainwriting 6-3-5, SCAMPER, Mind Map, DOT Voting, Affinity Diagram, Concept Canvas.
- ☐ **Real-world link:** Used in TCS Designit, Tata Elxsi, IDEO, Stanford d.school, and every modern Design Sprint.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Thinking Tools in Design Thinking

In the **Design Thinking** framework, the *Ideation* phase relies heavily on structured tools and techniques that help designers generate, refine, and document creative solutions. These tools are broadly classified into three functional families that operate either in **expansion** (divergent), **refinement** (convergent), or **preservation** (capturing) modes of cognition.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Thinking tools are structured methods, templates, and facilitation techniques that designers use to amplify, organize, evaluate, and preserve creative thought. They convert intangible ideas into tangible artefacts that can be shared, tested, and iterated upon.*

---

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Thinking Tools

### A. Divergent Thinking Tools
**Formal Definition:** Divergent thinking tools are techniques designed to *expand* the solution space by generating a large quantity of varied, unconventional, and multi-perspective ideas without judgment or premature filtering. They stimulate free association and break mental fixedness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Guiding Principle:** *"Defer judgment. Quantity over quality. Build on the ideas of others. Encourage wild ideas."* — the four canonical Brainstorming rules coined by Alex Osborn (1953).

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine throwing a stone into a calm pond. The ripples spread outward in *all directions*, reaching every corner of the surface. Divergent thinking does the same — it pushes a single problem statement outward into a wide, messy cloud of possibilities.

### B. Convergent Thinking Tools
**Formal Definition:** Convergent thinking tools are analytical techniques that *narrow* the solution space, applying logic, criteria, and structured evaluation to filter, cluster, prioritize, and select the most promising ideas from a large pool.

**Intuitive Analogy:** After the pond ripples outward, gravity pulls the water back toward the centre, forming a single coherent column. Convergent thinking gathers scattered ideas and pulls them toward a *focused, actionable* outcome.

### C. Idea Capturing Tools
**Formal Definition:** Idea capturing tools are documentation mechanisms, both analog and digital, used to *record, preserve, visualize, and share* ideas as they emerge in real time, ensuring no insight is lost between sessions.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of capturing tools as a *fisherman's net*. Even the smallest, most fleeting idea (a fish) gets caught, stored, and made available for later review — nothing slips through.

---

## 1.3 The Cognitive Mode Map

| Cognitive Mode | Direction | Purpose | Typical Phase |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Divergent** | Outward / Expanding | Generate volume of ideas | Ideation (early) |
| **Convergent** | Inward / Filtering | Select best ideas | Ideation (late) → Prototyping |
| **Capturing** | Lateral / Preserving | Document ideas continuously | Spans the full process |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common trap question is to ask *"Are capturing tools divergent or convergent?"* — the correct answer is **NEITHER**. Capturing is a parallel, continuous process that supports both modes of thought.

---

## 1.4 Why These Tools Matter in Product Development

In the KTU 2024 Scheme product development lifecycle, these tools directly enable:
- **CO1 (Empathize):** Capturing user insights verbatim.
- **CO2 (Define):** Converging observations into a point-of-view.
- **CO3 (Ideate):** Divergent generation of solutions.
- **CO4 (Prototype/Test):** Preserving concepts for iteration.
