# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 Divergent Thinking Tools — Detailed Framework

Divergent tools operate on the principle of **cognitive deconstruction** — temporarily disabling the brain's critical filter (the *prefrontal cortex*) so that associative, lateral pathways dominate.

### 2.1.1 Brainstorming
- **Mechanism:** A facilitated group session (typically **6–10 participants**) generating ideas verbally.
- **Rules:** No criticism, wild ideas welcome, build on others, go for quantity (target: **50–100 ideas / 30 mins**).
- **Output:** A long, unfiltered list on a whiteboard or digital board.

### 2.1.2 Mind Mapping
- **Mechanism:** A radial diagram with a central concept at the core and branches radiating outward.
- **Structure:** Central node → main branches (categories) → sub-branches (details).
- **Strength:** Reveals non-linear relationships between ideas.

### 2.1.3 SCAMPER
- **Origin:** Bob Eberle (1971), derived from Alex Osborn's checklist.
- **Seven Lenses:**
  - **S** — Substitute
  - **C** — Combine
  - **A** — Adapt
  - **M** — Modify / Magnify / Minify
  - **P** — Put to another use
  - **E** — Eliminate
  - **R** — Reverse / Rearrange

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Acronym:** SCAMPER is a *favourite* 2-mark or 3-mark question. Memorize all **seven** triggers and provide a one-line example for each.

### 2.1.4 Worst Possible Idea
- **Mechanism:** Participants deliberately brainstorm the *worst* solutions.
- **Psychological Basis:** Removes performance anxiety, which paradoxically frees creative flow. "Reverse engineering" the bad ideas often surfaces hidden good ones.

### 2.1.5 Brainwriting 6-3-5
- **Mechanism:** **6 participants** each write **3 ideas** on a sheet, then pass it **5 times** so each idea is iteratively built upon.
- **Output:** Up to **6 × 3 × 5 = 90 ideas** in 30 minutes — higher output than verbal brainstorming and reduces the *production blocking* problem.

### 2.1.6 Analogies & Metaphors
- **Mechanism:** Map a known solution from a distant domain onto the problem (e.g., "What if our hospital were a *hotel*?").
- **Theoretical Basis:** *Structure-Mapping Theory* (Gentner, 1983).

### 2.1.7 Storyboarding
- A sequential visual narrative (typically **6–8 frames**) showing how a user interacts with a proposed solution.

### 2.1.8 Sketching
- Low-fidelity visual ideation, often called *"thinking on paper."* Faster than verbal articulation for spatial ideas.

---

## 2.2 Convergent Thinking Tools — Detailed Framework

Convergent tools apply **analytical rigour** to a large idea pool. They follow a three-stage micro-process: **Cluster → Filter → Prioritize**.

### 2.2.1 Affinity Diagram
- **Mechanism:** Group similar sticky notes into thematic clusters (typically **5–10 clusters**).
- **Origin:** Jiro Kawakita (KJ Method), 1960s.
- **Use:** Synthesizes qualitative data (interview notes, observations).

### 2.2.2 Dot Voting (Multi-Voting)
- Each participant receives **3–5 dots** (physical stickers or digital tokens) to place on their favourite ideas.
- Tallying dots produces a ranked shortlist.

### 2.2.3 Prioritization Matrix (Impact/Effort)
- A 2×2 grid plotting ideas on **Impact** (y-axis) vs. **Effort** (x-axis).
- **Quadrants:**
  - High Impact, Low Effort → **Quick Wins** (do first)
  - High Impact, High Effort → **Major Projects** (plan carefully)
  - Low Impact, Low Effort → **Fill-ins** (do later)
  - Low Impact, High Effort → **Money Pit** (avoid)

### 2.2.4 SWOT Analysis
- **S**trengths, **W**eaknesses, **O**pportunities, **T**hreats.
- Often applied to a single shortlisted idea to assess its strategic fit.

### 2.2.5 Decision Matrix (Weighted Scoring)
- A multi-criteria scoring sheet.
- **Formula (KTU Exam Favourite):**

$$
\text{Score}_i \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} \big( w_j \times r_{ij} \big)
$$

where $w_j$ is the weight of criterion $j$, $r_{ij}$ is the rating of idea $i$ on criterion $j$, and $n$ is the number of criteria.

### 2.2.6 Kano Model
- Classifies features into:
  - **Must-be (Basic)** — expected, dissatisfaction if absent.
  - **One-dimensional (Performance)** — more is better.
  - **Attractive (Excitement)** — delight users, but absence is not punished.
  - **Indifferent** — users don't care.
  - **Reverse** — some users actively dislike.

---

## 2.3 Idea Capturing Tools — Detailed Framework

Capturing tools ensure **no insight is lost** across the iterative design process.

| Tool | Format | Best Used For | Storage Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sticky Notes (Post-its)** | 3×3 inch paper | Brainstorming clusters | Physical / Digital (Miro, FigJam) |
| **Empathy Map** | 2×2 grid (Says / Thinks / Does / Feels) | User interview capture | Visual artefact |
| **Customer Journey Map** | Timeline + touchpoints | End-to-end experience | Visual artefact |
| **Audio / Video Recording** | Digital media | Ethnographic research | Cloud repository |
| **Research Repository** | Shared drive / Notion | Long-term archival | Digital database |
| **Photo Documentation** | JPEG / PNG | Field observations | Cloud repository |
| **Field Notes Journal** | Notebook | Solo fieldwork | Physical / Evernote |
| **User Quotes Log** | Spreadsheet | Verbatim user voice | Text file |

---

| Tool | Type | Stage | Group / Solo | Key Metric |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Brainstorming | Divergent | Ideation | Group | Idea count ≥ 50 |

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Worked Examples

Because this topic is **qualitative-framework-based** rather than numerical, the "derivations" below are **procedural workflows**. Each step is exhaustive and matches the rubric KTU examiners use to award marks.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: SCAMPER Application (Module 3, 3 marks)

**Problem:** *"Apply the SCAMPER technique to redesign a conventional school backpack."*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Substitute**
Replace a core component.
- *Solution:* Substitute the single-zipper opening with a **roll-top magnetic closure** for weatherproofing.

**Step 2 — Combine**
Merge two functions into one.
- *Solution:* Combine the backpack with a **foldable seat cushion** at the base, so students can sit anywhere.

**Step 3 — Adapt**
Borrow a feature from another product.
- *Solution:* Adapt the **hydration-pack tube routing** used in cycling bags into a side pocket for a water bottle.

**Step 4 — Modify**
Amplify or reduce a property.
- *Solution:* Modify the strap width — **magnify** the shoulder padding by 40% and add cooling gel inserts.

**Step 5 — Put to another use**
Identify a new context for the product.
- *Solution:* Repurpose the bag's **interior divider** as a removable laptop sleeve usable independently in a meeting.

**Step 6 — Eliminate**
Remove a non-essential element.
- *Solution:* Eliminate the **top carry handle** since side straps serve the same function, reducing material use.

**Step 7 — Reverse / Rearrange**
Invert the order or orientation.
- *Solution:* Reverse the bag's opening — **bottom-loading** with a hinged flap allows easier access to items at the bottom without unpacking the entire bag.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Score Pattern Wins Marks:** KTU examiners allocate **0.5 mark per SCAMPER prompt** (7 × 0.5 = 3.5) but round to 3. Writing a single-line example for *each* of the seven letters is the highest-yield strategy.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Weighted Decision Matrix (Module 3, 7 marks)

**Problem:** A team has shortlisted **3 ideas** for a campus food-delivery app. They must select the best one using a Decision Matrix with **3 criteria**.

**Shortlist:**
- Idea **A**: AI-based dietary recommendation engine.
- Idea **B**: Group-ordering and split-payment feature.
- Idea **C**: Real-time kitchen-cam transparency.

**Criteria & Weights (out of 10):**
- User Value (w₁ = **0.4**)
- Technical Feasibility (w₂ = **0.35**)
- Development Cost (w₃ = **0.25**)

**Ratings (1–10 scale, higher = better for value & feasibility, lower cost = higher rating):**

| Criterion | Weight (w) | Idea A | Idea B | Idea C |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| User Value | 0.40 | 9 | 7 | 6 |
| Tech Feasibility | 0.35 | 5 | 9 | 8 |
| Dev Cost (inverse) | 0.25 | 4 | 8 | 7 |

### Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1 — State the formula.**

$$
\text{Score}_i \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Score for Idea A.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_A &= (0.40 \times 9) + (0.35 \times 5) + (0.25 \times 4) \\
&= 3.60 + 1.75 + 1.00 \\
&= 6.35
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Score for Idea B.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_B &= (0.40 \times 7) + (0.35 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 8) \\
&= 2.80 + 3.15 + 2.00 \\
&= 7.95
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Score for Idea C.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_C &= (0.40 \times 6) + (0.35 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 7) \\
&= 2.40 + 2.80 + 1.75 \\
&= 6.95
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Rank the ideas.**

$$
\text{Score}_B \; (7.95) \;>\; \text{Score}_C \; (6.95) \;>\; \text{Score}_A \; (6.35)
$$

**Step 6 — Decision.**
Idea **B** (Group-ordering & split-payment) is the highest-scoring concept and should be taken forward to prototyping.

> [!TIP]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting to normalize the weights so that $\sum w_j = 1$. KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** for this oversight.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Dot-Voting Procedure (Module 3, 4 marks)

**Problem:** A team of 8 designers has generated 24 ideas on sticky notes. Conduct a dot-voting session to shortlist the top **3 ideas**.

### Step-by-Step Procedure

**Step 1 — Distribute votes.**
Each of the **8 designers** receives **3 dots** (24 dots in total). A common variant: each gets **5 dots** to be distributed freely.

**Step 2 — Display all ideas.**
The 24 sticky notes are arranged on a wall in a **visible grid** (4 rows × 6 columns), each note numbered for tally reference.

**Step 3 — Silent voting rule.**
No discussion is allowed during voting to prevent *groupthink* and *social conformity bias*.

**Step 4 — Place dots.**
Each designer places dots on their preferred ideas. A designer can place **multiple dots on one idea** (signalling strong conviction) or spread them across different ideas.

**Step 5 — Tally the votes.**
Hypothetical tally:
- Idea #7 — **7 dots** (top vote)
- Idea #12 — **6 dots**
- Idea #3 — **5 dots**
- Idea #19 — **3 dots**
- (rest ≤ 2 dots)

**Step 6 — Shortlist.**
The top **3 ideas** (#7, #12, #3) advance to the next stage (e.g., Decision Matrix or prototyping).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Use Dot Voting Over Open Discussion?** Dot voting gives *equal voice* to introverts and junior members, mitigates *authority bias* (where senior members dominate), and produces a *quantitative* basis for selection.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Impact-Effort Quadrant Placement (Module 3, 5 marks)

**Problem:** Place the following 4 campus-project ideas in the correct quadrant of an Impact-Effort matrix.

- P1: Add USB-C charging ports to library desks. (**Low effort, High impact**)
- P2: Build a new central cafeteria. (**High effort, High impact**)
- P3: Replace whiteboard markers with eco pens. (**Low effort, Low impact**)
- P4: Launch a campus-wide AI chatbot using a custom LLM. (**High effort, Low impact**)

### Step-by-Step Placement

| Quadrant | Position | Project |
|---|---|---|
| **Quick Wins** (High Impact, Low Effort) | Top-Left | **P1** |
| **Major Projects** (High Impact, High Effort) | Top-Right | **P2** |
| **Fill-Ins** (Low Impact, Low Effort) | Bottom-Left | **P3** |
| **Money Pit** (Low Impact, High Effort) | Bottom-Right | **P4** |

**Recommended Action Sequence:**
1. Execute **P1** immediately (Quick Win).
2. Schedule **P2** for the next planning cycle (Major Project).
3. Bundle **P3** with routine maintenance (Fill-In).
4. **Defer or abandon P4** (Money Pit).

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Mnemonic:** In the Impact-Effort matrix, *Impact is on the Y-axis* (rising = better) and *Effort is on the X-axis* (rising = more costly). A simple phrase to remember: *"Aim high (Impact) but start left (low Effort)."*

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define divergent thinking. List any four divergent thinking tools used in the Ideation phase of Design Thinking.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Divergent thinking is a cognitive mode in which a designer generates a **large volume of varied, unconventional ideas** by exploring multiple directions from a single problem statement. It expands the solution space without immediate judgment.

**Four Divergent Thinking Tools (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Brainstorming** — A facilitated group session producing 50+ ideas in 30 minutes.
2. **Mind Mapping** — A radial diagram showing relationships between ideas.
3. **SCAMPER** — A seven-prompt checklist (Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put to another use, Eliminate, Reverse).
4. **Brainwriting 6-3-5** — A silent written technique producing up to 90 ideas in 30 minutes.

*(Acceptable alternatives: Worst Possible Idea, Analogies, Storyboarding, Sketching.)*

---

### **Question 2: What is an Empathy Map? Why is it considered an idea-capturing tool rather than a divergent or convergent tool?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition of Empathy Map (1.5 marks):**
An Empathy Map is a **2×2 visual canvas** divided into four quadrants capturing what a user *Says*, *Thinks*, *Does*, and *Feels* during a specific context. It is typically populated with verbatim quotes, observed actions, and inferred thoughts gathered during user interviews or field observations.

**Why it is a capturing tool, not divergent or convergent (1.5 marks):**
- It does **not generate** new ideas (so it is not divergent).
- It does **not evaluate or prioritize** ideas (so it is not convergent).
- It **records and preserves** raw user data as it is observed, acting as a real-time documentation device — the defining role of a capturing tool.
- It supports the Empathize phase and feeds downstream divergent and convergent processes.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the SCAMPER technique in detail. Apply each of its seven prompts to redesign a *conventional water bottle* for school children. (7 marks)**

**(b) Describe the *Weighted Decision Matrix* method. Using a 3-idea × 3-criterion matrix, demonstrate the selection of the best concept for a *campus navigation app*. (7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — SCAMPER Applied to a Water Bottle**

**SCAMPER Definition (2 marks):**
SCAMPER is a divergent thinking checklist developed by **Bob Eberle (1971)**, based on Alex Osborn's work. It applies seven cognitive triggers to an existing product to generate new design possibilities.

**Application to a School Water Bottle (5 marks — ~0.7 each):**

| Prompt | Application |
|---|---|
| **S — Substitute** | Replace the standard plastic body with **bio-degradable bamboo-fiber composite** to reduce environmental impact. |
| **C — Combine** | Combine the bottle with a **collapsible silicone funnel** in the cap for mess-free refilling from taps. |
| **A — Adapt** | Adapt the **fitness tracker lid** concept (smart caps with hydration reminders) into a school-appropriate design. |
| **M — Modify** | Modify the bottle's **diameter** — make it slimmer (cylindrical reduction by 30%) to fit standard school-bag side pockets. |
| **P — Put to another use** | Repurpose the **bottle's base** as a **modular storage compartment** for vitamins or small stationery items. |
| **E — Eliminate** | Eliminate the **metallic screw cap** in favour of a one-hand **push-pull spout** to reduce choking risk for younger students. |
| **R — Reverse / Rearrange** | Reverse the **insulation layer placement** — add a **double-wall vacuum gap** to keep water cool for 8 hours in summer. |

#### **Model Solution (b) — Weighted Decision Matrix for a Campus Navigation App**

**Methodology (2 marks):**
A Weighted Decision Matrix scores each shortlisted idea against weighted criteria. The total weighted score is computed as:

$$
\text{Score}_i \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}
$$

where $w_j$ is the weight of criterion $j$ and $r_{ij}$ is the rating of idea $i$ on criterion $j$.

**Set-up (1 mark):**
- 3 shortlisted ideas:
  - **I1:** Indoor AR wayfinding with phone camera.
  - **I2:** QR-code-based static navigation.
  - **I3:** Bluetooth beacon-based indoor positioning.
- 3 criteria (weights summing to 1.0):
  - User Experience (w = 0.4)
  - Technical Feasibility (w = 0.4)
  - Implementation Cost (w = 0.2, inverse-rated)

**Data Table (1 mark):**

| Criterion | Weight | I1 (AR) | I2 (QR) | I3 (Beacon) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| User Experience | 0.4 | 9 | 6 | 8 |
| Tech Feasibility | 0.4 | 5 | 9 | 7 |
| Cost (inverse) | 0.2 | 4 | 9 | 6 |

**Calculations (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_{I1} &= (0.4 \times 9) + (0.4 \times 5) + (0.2 \times 4) \\
&= 3.6 + 2.0 + 0.8 = 6.4
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_{I2} &= (0.4 \times 6) + (0.4 \times 9) + (0.2 \times 9) \\
&= 2.4 + 3.6 + 1.8 = 7.8
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_{I3} &= (0.4 \times 8) + (0.4 \times 7) + (0.2 \times 6) \\
&= 3.2 + 2.8 + 1.2 = 7.2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision (0.5 mark):**
Ranking — **I2 (7.8) > I3 (7.2) > I1 (6.4)**. The QR-code-based navigation concept is selected for prototyping because it offers the best balance of user experience, technical feasibility, and low cost.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Differentiate between Divergent and Convergent thinking tools. Construct a 2×2 Impact-Effort matrix and place four feature ideas for a *smart attendance system* in the correct quadrants. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the Brainwriting 6-3-5 technique and the Affinity Diagram technique. State one advantage of each. (7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — Divergent vs Convergent & Impact-Effort Matrix**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Divergent Tools | Convergent Tools |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Outward / Expanding | Inward / Filtering |
| Purpose | Generate volume of ideas | Select and prioritize ideas |
| Phase | Ideation (early) | Ideation (late) → Prototyping |
| Judgement | Deferred | Applied rigorously |
| Group Size | 6–10 typical | 4–8 typical |
| Examples | Brainstorming, SCAMPER, Mind Map | Dot Voting, Decision Matrix, SWOT |
| Risk if Skipped | Idea starvation | Premature commitment |
| Output | 50–100 raw ideas | 3–5 shortlisted concepts |

**Impact-Effort Matrix (3 marks):**
**Axes:** X = Effort (Low → High), Y = Impact (Low → High).

**Four Feature Ideas for Smart Attendance System:**

| Feature | Effort | Impact | Quadrant |
|---|---|---|---|
| **F1:** Bluetooth beacon auto-mark via phone | Low | High | **Quick Wins (Top-Left)** |
| **F2:** Facial recognition via campus CCTV | High | High | **Major Projects (Top-Right)** |
| **F3:** Manual roll-call via teacher app | Low | Low | **Fill-Ins (Bottom-Left)** |
| **F4:** AI-driven behavioural pattern attendance (custom ML) | High | Low | **Money Pit (Bottom-Right)** |

**Recommended Action (1 mark):** Execute F1 → Schedule F2 → Bundle F3 with routine class workflow → Defer or abandon F4.

#### **Model Solution (b) — Brainwriting 6-3-5 and Affinity Diagram**

**Brainwriting 6-3-5 (3.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** A silent, written brainstorming variant designed by **Bernd Rohrbach (1969)**.
- **Mechanism:** **6 participants** sit in a circle. Each writes **3 ideas** in **5 minutes** on a worksheet. After 5 minutes, each sheet is passed to the next person, who reads and *adds 3 more ideas*, inspired by or building on the previous ones. This continues for **5 rounds** (the "5" in 6-3-5).
- **Output:** Up to **6 × 3 × 5 = 90 ideas** in 25 minutes.
- **Advantage (0.5 mark):** Eliminates *production blocking* (when faster speakers dominate verbal brainstorming) and *social loafing* (silent contributors feel more equal), yielding higher total idea volume with balanced participation.

**Affinity Diagram (3.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** A convergent synthesis tool developed by **Jiro Kawakita (KJ Method, 1960s)** that groups large volumes of qualitative data into thematic clusters.
- **Mechanism:** Ideas written on sticky notes are silently grouped by participants into **5–10 thematic clusters**. Each cluster is then given a header label that summarizes the theme.
- **Use Case:** Synthesizing user-interview notes, observation logs, and brainstorming outputs into coherent themes.
- **Advantage (0.5 mark):** Reveals *latent patterns* in qualitative data that are not visible in a linear list, enabling design teams to transition from raw observations to actionable themes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Module:**
> 1. **SCAMPER omissions:** Students frequently list only 5–6 of the 7 triggers. Memorize all 7: **S-C-A-M-P-E-R**.
> 2. **Confusing capturing with divergent:** Capturing tools *record* ideas, not *generate* them. Examiners deduct 1 mark for misclassification.
> 3. **Missing the divergent → capturing → convergent sequence:** Many answers jump directly from "generate" to "select" without mentioning how ideas are *documented* in between.
> 4. **Unnormalized weights in Decision Matrix:** Weights should sum to **1.0**. Forgetting this costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Naming tools without explaining their *mechanism*:** A bare list of 10 tool names scores lower than 5 tools explained in detail.
> 6. **No examples:** For every tool mentioned, examiners expect at least one *application* or *example* sentence. Generic definitions earn 50% marks.
> 7. **Forgetting the four Brainstorming rules:** Quantity, No Criticism, Wild Ideas Welcome, Build on Others — listing all four is a guaranteed 1-mark win.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3):**

- ✅ The three families of thinking tools are **Divergent**, **Convergent**, and **Capturing** — they are *not* mutually exclusive in time but operate in different *modes*.
- ✅ **Divergent** = *expand* the idea space. Output is **volume** (50–100+ ideas). Examples: Brainstorming, Mind Mapping, SCAMPER, Brainwriting 6-3-5, Worst Possible Idea, Analogies, Sketching, Storyboarding.
- ✅ **Convergent** = *narrow* the idea space. Output is a **shortlist** (3–5 ideas). Examples: Affinity Diagram, Dot Voting, Impact-Effort Matrix, SWOT, Decision Matrix, Kano Model.
- ✅ **Capturing** = *preserve* ideas continuously. Examples: Sticky Notes, Empathy Map, Customer Journey Map, Audio/Video, Research Repository, Field Notes, User Quotes Log.
- ✅ **SCAMPER = Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put to another use, Eliminate, Reverse** — all **seven** letters required in exams.
- ✅ **Brainwriting 6-3-5 = 6 people × 3 ideas × 5 rounds = up to 90 ideas** in 25 minutes.
- ✅ **Impact-Effort Matrix quadrants:** Quick Wins (Top-Left) | Major Projects (Top-Right) | Fill-Ins (Bottom-Left) | Money Pit (Bottom-Right).
- ✅ **Decision Matrix formula:** $\text{Score}_i = \sum w_j \cdot r_{ij}$, with weights normalized to sum to 1.
- ✅ **Kano Categories:** Must-be, One-dimensional, Attractive, Indifferent, Reverse.
- ✅ **Brainstorming 4 Rules:** No criticism, wild ideas welcome, quantity matters, build on others.
- ✅ **Idea flow:** Divergent → Capturing → Convergent → Capturing → Prototype. Capturing is a *parallel*, continuous process.
- ✅ **Common 14-mark question structure:** Definition (2 marks) + Application (3–4 marks) + Worked Example/Calculation (3–4 marks) + Diagram/Conclusion (2–3 marks).
- ✅ **Affiliation of key founders:** Alex Osborn (Brainstorming), Bob Eberle (SCAMPER), Bernd Rohrbach (Brainwriting 6-3-5), Jiro Kawakita (KJ/Affinity Diagram), Dorothy Leonard-Barton (Sourcing Human Capital — extended analogies).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cross-industry inspiration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cross-industry inspiration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Industry Inspiration (CII)** is a systematic innovation strategy within Design Thinking wherein solutions, technologies, processes, principles, or analogies are deliberately sourced from one industry, discipline, or biological system and creatively transferred to solve problems or create new value in an entirely unrelated industry.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Cross-Industry Inspiration is positioned under **Module 3: Inspiration — Searching for Opportunities** and is treated as a structured **divergent thinking tool** that overcomes the cognitive bias of "industry myopia" (the tendency to look for solutions only within one's own domain).

Mathematically represented as an **Analogical Mapping Function**:

$$
f: D_{\text{source}} \longrightarrow D_{\text{target}}
$$

where $D_{\text{source}}$ is the donor domain and $D_{\text{target}}$ is the recipient problem domain. The mapping $f$ preserves **structure** rather than surface features.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Cross-Industry Inspiration

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU syllabus specifically recognises **three validated sources** of cross-industry inspiration:

| Pillar | Source Domain | Example Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature / Biology** (Biomimicry) | Living organisms, ecosystems, evolution | Velcro from burdock burrs |
| **Other Industries** (Cross-Pollination) | Aerospace, automotive, medical, sports | Ultrasound from sonar |
| **Adjacent Disciplines** (Science & Art) | Physics, mathematics, theatre, music | Agile methodology from software to manufacturing |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Borrowed Recipe" Principle**
>
> Imagine you are cooking **South Indian Dosa** but the batter keeps sticking to the pan. Instead of buying yet another non-stick pan (searching within your own kitchen), you ask your friend who bakes **French Crêpes**: *"What kind of pan do you use? How do you prep the surface?"* You borrow the **cast-iron seasoned pan technique** and apply it to dosa making.
>
> This is exactly Cross-Industry Inspiration — looking **outside your own domain** for a proven solution and adapting it to your own problem.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Students Must Know

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Biomimicry** — Innovation inspired by biological systems (Janine Benyus framework).
> - **Industry Myopia** — Cognitive limitation where problem-solvers only scan their own industry.
> - **Analogical Reasoning** — Cognitive process of transferring knowledge from a known (source) to an unknown (target) based on shared relational structure.
> - **Lateral Thinking** — Term coined by **Edward de Bono** describing problem-solving through indirect, creative approaches.
> - **TRIZ** — Russian acronym (*Teoriya Resheniya Izobretatelskikh Zadatch*) = "Theory of Inventive Problem Solving."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 The Four-Step Cross-Industry Inspiration Process

The KTU 2024 scheme follows a structured four-stage process for cross-industry inspiration:

### Step 1 — Deconstruct the Problem
> Remove all industry-specific jargon and reduce the problem to its **abstract functional core**.

- **Why?** This strips away surface bias and exposes the *real* challenge.
- **How?** Use the **Function-Attribute Matrix** (What must the solution *do*, not *what must it be*).

**Example:** A hospital wants to reduce patient waiting time.
- Surface form: *"We need more receptionists."*
- Abstract function: *"We need a system that prioritises and routes variable inputs in real time."*

### Step 2 — Search Analogous Industries
> Cast a wide net across at least **5–7 unrelated domains** that face a similar abstract function.

- **Why?** Breakthroughs live at the intersection of unrelated fields.
- **How?** Use the **Five-Domain Search Grid** (Nature, Aerospace, Sports, Military, Medicine, Food, Architecture).

### Step 3 — Map the Analogy
> Identify **structural similarities** (not surface similarities) between the source solution and the target problem.

- **Why?** Structural mapping produces **deep, defensible innovation** rather than cosmetic mimicry.
- **How?** Use the **Structure-Mapping Engine** concept (Gentner, 1983): align *relations* between *objects*, not the objects themselves.

### Step 4 — Adapt and Prototype
> Translate the borrowed principle into the target industry's constraints (cost, regulation, user behaviour).

- **Why?** Direct copy rarely works; **adaptation** is where value is created.
- **How?** Apply KTU Design Thinking's **Prototype → Test → Iterate** loop.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important framework** for Module 3 examination answers on this topic.

| # | Concept | Definition | Key Use in KTU Exam |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Biomimicry Levels** | *Form*, *Process*, *System* | Classify any natural-inspired innovation |
| 2 | **Function-Attribute Matrix** | Separates *what* from *how* | Step 1 of the process |
| 3 | **Structure-Mapping** | Aligning relations, not features | Step 3 rationale |
| 4 | **Industry Myopia Index** | Unconscious domain bias | KTU 2-mark short question |
| 5 | **TRIZ 40 Principles** | Inventive problem-solving patterns | Cross-industry conflict resolution |
| 6 | **Lateral Thinking** | de Bono's indirect reasoning | Listed under KTU CO3 |
| 7 | **5-Domain Search Grid** | Nature, Aero, Sports, Mil, Med | Step 2 application |
| 8 | **Analogical Distance** | Closeness between source & target | Determines adaptation effort |

## 2.3 The Biomimicry Taxonomy (Janine Benyus Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently tests this hierarchy — memorise the three levels.**

- **Level 1 — Form (Mimicking Shape):** Inspired by physical form. *Example:* Eiffel Tower's curved base mimicking the human femur's distribution of stress.
- **Level 2 — Process (Mimicking Behaviour):** Inspired by natural processes. *Example:* Self-cleaning paint inspired by lotus leaf's hydrophobic surface tension behaviour.
- **Level 3 — System (Mimicking Ecosystems):** Inspired by whole-system logic. *Example:* Industrial parks where one factory's waste becomes another's input (closed-loop manufacturing mimicking forest nutrient cycles).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cross-industry inspiration is used by:
- **Aerospace engineers** studying bird flight (winglet design).
- **Medical device companies** studying shark skin to reduce bacterial adhesion on hospital surfaces.
- **Automotive designers** studying boxfish for crash-safe ultralight frames.
- **Civil engineers** studying termite mounds for passive cooling skyscrapers (Eastgate Centre, Harare).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Case Study

## 3.1 Case Study: The Shinkansen Bullet Train Nose (Kingfisher Beak Analogy)

This is a **classic KTU textbook example**. We will exhaustively derive how cross-industry inspiration led to this engineering breakthrough.

### Problem Statement
- When the Japanese Shinkansen bullet train exits a tunnel at **270 km/h**, it produces a loud **sonic boom-like thunderclap**.
- This was both a **noise pollution issue** and a **structural wear issue**.
- Engineers needed a redesign of the train nose.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Deconstruct the problem to its abstract function.**

The function is *not* "redesign a train nose." The abstract function is:

$$
F_{\text{abstract}} = \text{Allow a high-speed object to transition smoothly from a confined space to open space with minimum pressure differential shock.}
$$

**Step 2 — Search analogous industries/nature.**

Engineer **Eiji Nakatsu** (birdwatcher and chief engineer) looked at the **kingfisher bird**, which dives from air (low density) into water (high density) at high speed **without splashing**.

**Step 3 — Map the analogy (Structure Mapping).**

We align the **relations** between source and target:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Source (Kingfisher)} &\longrightarrow \text{Target (Train)} \\
\text{Air to water transition} &\longrightarrow \text{Tunnel to open air transition} \\
\text{Beak shape minimises splash} &\longrightarrow \text{Nose shape minimises pressure wave} \\
\text{Bird achieves silent entry} &\longrightarrow \text{Train achieves silent exit}
\end{aligned}
$$

The shared **relational structure** is: *A high-speed object transitioning between two media of differing density requires an elongated, tapered, asymmetric leading edge.*

**Step 4 — Adapt and quantify.**

The kingfisher beak has a length-to-diameter ratio of approximately:

$$
R_{\text{beak}} = \frac{L}{D} \approx 2.5
$$

Applying this to the Shinkansen nose, the redesigned nose had:

$$
L_{\text{nose}} = 15 \text{ m}, \quad D_{\text{train}} = 3.4 \text{ m}
$$

The resulting ratio:

$$
R_{\text{nose}} = \frac{15}{3.4} \approx 4.41
$$

The longer, more streamlined nose reduced:

- Noise level from **100 dB to 70 dB** (a **30% reduction**).
- Energy consumption by **15%**.
- Maximum operating speed increased from **270 to 320 km/h**.

### Outcome Validation

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "NAKA"** for Cross-Industry Process: **N**eed-deconstruct, **A**nalyse-analogies, **K**eep-structure, **A**dapt-context.

## 3.2 Case Study: Velcro (Burdock Burr Analogy)

**Step 1:** Engineer **George de Mestral** examined burrs stuck to his dog's fur under a microscope in 1941.

**Step 2:** He observed that the burrs had tiny **hooks** that latched onto loop-shaped fibres in fur.

**Step 3:** Abstract function = *"Create a reversible, mechanical fastener that requires no glue or metal."*

**Step 4:** Mathematical structure of one hook:

$$
\text{Hook diameter} \approx 200 \, \mu m, \quad \text{Hook density} \approx 50 \, \text{hooks per cm}^2
$$

Mestral patented **VELCRO** (Velours + Crochet) in 1955 — a textbook example of **biomimicry at the Form level**.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical: TRIZ Ideality Calculation

TRIZ defines **Ideality** as:

$$
I = \frac{\sum F_u}{\sum F_h + \sum C}
$$

where:
- $F_u$ = useful functions
- $F_h$ = harmful functions
- $C$ = cost

### Worked Example
- Useful functions of a smartphone battery: $F_u = 10$ (capacity, rechargeability, portability).
- Harmful functions: $F_h = 4$ (explosion risk, degradation, heat).
- Cost factors: $C = 3$.

$$
I = \frac{10}{4 + 3} = \frac{10}{7} \approx 1.43
$$

Cross-industry inspiration (e.g., studying **eel's electric organ biochemistry** for safer electrolytes) aims to **increase $F_u$ and decrease $F_h$**, raising $I$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Cross-Industry Inspiration Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Target Problem"] --> B["Deconstruct to Abstract Function"]
    B --> C["Identify Key Function Keywords"]
    C --> D["Apply Five Domain Search Grid"]
    D --> E1["Domain Nature"]
    D --> E2["Domain Aerospace"]
    D --> E3["Domain Sports"]
    D --> E4["Domain Military"]
    D --> E5["Domain Medicine"]
    E1 --> F["Source Solution Candidates"]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    F --> G["Structure Mapping Engine"]
    G --> H["Relational Alignment Check"]
    H --> I["Adapt to Target Constraints"]
    I --> J["Prototype and Test"]
    J --> K["Validated Cross Industry Innovation"]
```

## 4.2 Biomimicry Levels Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0["Biomimicry Hierarchy"] --> L1["Level 1 Form"]
    L0 --> L2["Level 2 Process"]
    L0 --> L3["Level 3 System"]
    L1 --> L1A["Example Eiffel Tower Femur Bone"]
    L2 --> L2A["Example Lotus Leaf Self Cleaning"]
    L3 --> L3A["Example Industrial Symbiosis Park"]
```

## 4.3 Five-Domain Search Grid (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 3
    block: nature["NATURE BIOLOGY"]
        N1["Bur plant N2 Shark skin N3 Kingfisher beak N4 Termite mound N5 Lotus leaf"]
    end
    block: aero["AEROSPACE MILITARY"]
        A1["Sonar A2 Stealth coating A3 Zero gravity A4 Composite material A5 Night vision"]
    end
    block: sports["SPORTS MEDICINE FOOD"]
        S1["Carbon fiber S2 Ultrasound S3 Spandex fabric S4 MRI S5 Fermentation"]
    end
```

## 4.4 Mapping Analogy Between Kingfisher and Shinkansen

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Source["SOURCE DOMAIN KINGFISHER"]
        S1["Air medium low density"]
        S2["Beak elongated shape"]
        S3["Water medium high density"]
        S4["Silent entry no splash"]
    end
    subgraph Target["TARGET DOMAIN SHINKANSEN"]
        T1["Tunnel confined space"]
        T2["Nose elongated shape"]
        T3["Open air free space"]
        T4["Silent exit no boom"]
    end
    S1 -. structural mapping .-> T1
    S2 -. structural mapping .-> T2
    S3 -. structural mapping .-> T3
    S4 -. structural mapping .-> T4
```

## 4.5 TRIZ Ideality vs Innovation Effort

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Current Ideality I low"] --> B["Apply Cross Industry Inspiration"]
    B --> C["Increase Useful Functions"]
    B --> D["Decrease Harmful Functions"]
    B --> E["Reduce Cost Factors"]
    C --> F["Higher Ideality I prime"]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G["Market Disruptive Innovation"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *Define Cross-Industry Inspiration. List any three of its primary source domains.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Cross-Industry Inspiration is a structured innovation strategy in Design Thinking where solutions, technologies, or principles from one industry or discipline are systematically identified and creatively transferred to solve problems in an entirely different industry.

**Three primary source domains (2 marks — 0.5 + 0.75 + 0.75):**
1. **Nature / Biological systems** (Biomimicry) — e.g., burdock burr → Velcro.
2. **Other industries** (Cross-Pollination) — e.g., aerospace lightweight composites → automotive frames.
3. **Adjacent disciplines** (Science / Art) — e.g., theatre stage lighting → museum product displays.

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> *What is Industry Myopia? How does Cross-Industry Inspiration overcome it?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Industry Myopia (1.5 marks):**
Industry Myopia is a cognitive bias in which designers and engineers unconsciously restrict their search for solutions to their own industry, ignoring potentially superior solutions that already exist in unrelated domains.

**How CII overcomes it (1.5 marks):**
Cross-Industry Inspiration overcomes industry myopia by mandating a **deliberate, structured search** across at least five unrelated domains (the Five-Domain Search Grid) and by translating the problem into an **abstract functional form** before searching. This forces the designer's mind out of its familiar "in-industry" knowledge silo.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
> *a)* Explain the four-step Cross-Industry Inspiration process with a suitable example. *(7 marks)*
> *b)* Discuss the three levels of Biomimicry as proposed by Janine Benyus, giving one engineering example for each level. *(7 marks)*

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

The four-step Cross-Industry Inspiration process is as follows:

**Step 1 — Deconstruct the Problem (2 marks):**
The problem is stripped of industry-specific terms and rephrased as an **abstract functional core**. For example, a hospital facing long patient waiting times does not begin by searching for "more receptionists" but redefines the problem as: *"a system that prioritises and routes variable inputs in real time."* This abstraction is the critical enabler of cross-domain transfer.

**Step 2 — Search Analogous Industries (2 marks):**
Using the **Five-Domain Search Grid** (Nature, Aerospace, Sports, Military, Medicine), the designer surveys solutions in unrelated fields that perform the same abstract function. The hospital example might draw from **air traffic control routing algorithms** to inform patient prioritisation logic.

**Step 3 — Map the Analogy (2 marks):**
The designer applies **Structure-Mapping**: aligning the *relations* between the source solution and the target problem, not the surface features. Air traffic control's separation rules map structurally to patient triage rules because both manage a queue of mixed-priority entities under time pressure.

**Step 4 — Adapt and Prototype (1 mark):**
The borrowed principle is adapted to the target's constraints (medical regulations, cost, user behaviour) and tested via the **Prototype → Test → Iterate** loop.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

Janine Benyus proposed a **three-level biomimicry hierarchy**:

**Level 1 — Form (Mimicking Shape) (2 marks):**
The design copies the physical geometry of a biological structure.
*Engineering Example:* The **Eiffel Tower's curved base** mimics the trabecular distribution of stress in the **human femur bone**, allowing the tower to use less material while supporting greater load.

**Level 2 — Process (Mimicking Behaviour) (2 marks):**
The design copies a *functioning process* found in nature.
*Engineering Example:* **Lotus-Effect self-cleaning paint** mimics the hydrophobic surface tension behaviour of lotus leaves, where water droplets roll off taking dirt with them — used on building facades and solar panels.

**Level 3 — System (Mimicking Ecosystems) (3 marks):**
The design copies the *organising principles* of an entire ecosystem.
*Engineering Example:* **Industrial Symbiosis at Kalundborg, Denmark**, where one factory's waste (steam, gypsum, sludge) becomes another's raw material — directly mimicking a **forest's closed-loop nutrient cycle** at industrial scale.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
> *a)* "Cross-Industry Inspiration is fundamentally a structured form of analogical reasoning." Justify this statement with reference to the Structure-Mapping Engine. *(7 marks)*
> *b)* Analyse the case study of the Shinkansen bullet train redesign, explaining how each step of the Cross-Industry Inspiration process was applied. *(7 marks)*

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Anchoring the statement (2 marks):**
Cross-Industry Inspiration is *not* random copying. It is, at its cognitive core, a **systematic analogical reasoning process** in which a structural correspondence is established between a **known source domain** and an **unknown target problem**. The Structure-Mapping Engine (Gentner, 1983) is the formal cognitive model that describes this process.

**Mechanism of Structure-Mapping (3 marks):**
The Structure-Mapping Engine proposes that human analogy-making operates on **relational structure**, not on surface features. When we see that the kingfisher's beak and the Shinkansen's nose both solve the same abstract function — *smooth transition between media of differing density* — we are performing a **second-order isomorphism**:

$$
\exists \, f: \text{Structure}(D_{\text{source}}) \cong \text{Structure}(D_{\text{target}})
$$

where $f$ preserves *higher-order relations* (purpose, cause, function) rather than *first-order attributes* (colour, size, material).

**Justification (2 marks):**
Because the Structure-Mapping Engine requires the designer to first **abstract the function**, then **search across domains**, then **validate the relational match**, it transforms inspiration from a lucky accident into a **reproducible innovation methodology** — confirming the statement in the question.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Context (1 mark):**
The Shinkansen bullet train experienced a **sonic boom** when exiting tunnels at 270 km/h due to rapid pressure differential release.

**Step 1 — Deconstruction (1.5 marks):**
Chief engineer **Eiji Nakatsu** rephrased the problem abstractly: *"How does a high-speed object transition smoothly from a confined high-pressure space to an open low-pressure space?"*

**Step 2 — Search (1.5 marks):**
Being an avid birdwatcher, Nakatsu looked at the **kingfisher bird**, which dives from air into water at high speed without splashing — a structurally analogous problem in reverse direction.

**Step 3 — Structure-Mapping (1.5 marks):**
He aligned the relations: air-to-water transition ↔ tunnel-to-air transition; tapered beak ↔ elongated nose; silent water entry ↔ silent air exit.

**Step 4 — Adaptation (1.5 marks):**
Applying the kingfisher beak's length-to-diameter ratio to the train's nose yielded a **15 m elongated nose**, reducing noise by 30 dB, cutting energy use by 15%, and increasing speed to 320 km/h.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on this Topic — Read Carefully:**
>
> 1. **Defining Cross-Industry Inspiration as "copying from another industry" (–2 marks).** The correct definition involves *abstract function transfer*, not direct copying. Examiners mark this distinction strictly.
> 2. **Listing Velcro/Shinkansen without explaining the four-step process (–3 marks).** KTU awards process marks, not just example marks. Always pair examples with the step they illustrate.
> 3. **Confusing Biomimicry's three levels (–2 marks).** Form = *shape*, Process = *behaviour*, System = *ecosystem*. Do not interchange.
> 4. **Forgetting to state the abstract function before giving the example (–1 mark).** Examiners expect the student to *deconstruct* the problem first, not jump to a solution.
> 5. **Writing "TRIZ" without expanding the acronym once (–0.5 mark).** Always write "**TRIZ (Theory of Inventive Problem Solving)**" on first mention.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Cross-Industry Inspiration (Module 3):**
>
> - **Definition:** CII = systematic transfer of solutions across unrelated industries based on *abstract function*, not surface features.
> - **Three source pillars:** Nature (Biomimicry), Other Industries (Cross-Pollination), Adjacent Disciplines.
> - **Four-step process:** Deconstruct → Search → Map → Adapt.
> - **Five-Domain Search Grid:** Nature, Aerospace, Sports, Military, Medicine.
> - **Biomimicry levels (Benyus):** Form, Process, System — memorise in order.
> - **Mandatory examples:** Velcro (Form/burrs), Lotus paint (Process), Kalundborg (System), Shinkansen (Process), Eiffel Tower (Form).
> - **Cognitive basis:** Structure-Mapping Engine (Gentner, 1983).
> - **Lateral Thinking:** Edward de Bono.
> - **TRIZ:** Theory of Inventive Problem Solving — Ideality $I = F_u / (F_h + C)$.
> - **Industry Myopia:** The bias CII is designed to overcome.
> - **Golden Rule:** Always write the *abstract function* before naming the example.
> - **Avoid:** Direct copying language, example-without-process, level confusion, unexpanded acronyms.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of research in ideation - Market research - Consumer research - Leveraging research for informed ideation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Role of research in ideation - Market research - Consumer research - Leveraging research for informed ideation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔍 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining the Topic — KTU 2024 Official Terminology

**Role of Research in Ideation** refers to the systematic, evidence-based process of gathering, analyzing, and synthesizing qualitative and quantitative data about users, markets, and the competitive landscape to fuel the generation of innovative, viable, and desirable product concepts.

In the KTU 2024 Design Thinking framework, research is positioned as the **empirical foundation** of the **Empathize** and **Define** stages of the Double Diamond model. It transforms brainstorming from a creative guessing game into a **disciplined discovery process**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (GZEST605, Module 3):**
> Research is *not* a preliminary chore. It is the *connective tissue* between empathy and ideation. Without research, ideation is opinion. With research, ideation is insight.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Detective's Toolkit" 🕵️

Imagine a chef opening a new restaurant in an unfamiliar city.

- **Without research**, the chef cooks whatever *they* love and hopes customers show up. (This is "intuition-driven ideation" — and it fails 70% of the time.)
- **With research**, the chef spends two weeks:
  - Walking through local markets (observing),
  - Talking to neighborhood residents (interviewing),
  - Tasting competitor dishes (benchmarking),
  - Reading local food blogs and Google reviews (secondary research),
  - Running a small pop-up with 3 menu options (prototyping-testing).
  - Only *then* does the chef design the final menu.

**The research IS the recipe for the idea.** The menu (the product idea) is a direct output of the research findings. The chef's ideation is now *informed*, not *assumed*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Truths of Research-Driven Ideation:**
> 1. Customers say what they *think* they want — observation reveals what they *actually* do.
> 2. Market data shows the *playing field* — consumer insights show the *winning moves*.
> 3. Ideation fueled by research is **defensible** to investors, stakeholders, and engineering teams.

---

## 1.3 Three Pillars of Research in the Design Thinking Pipeline

| Pillar | KTU Terminology | Core Question Answered | Primary Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Market Research** | Macro-environmental & competitive scanning | *"Is there a viable market for this idea?"* | TAM/SAM/SOM, SWOT, PESTLE, competitor map |
| **Consumer Research** | Micro-level user & behavioral insight | *"Who is the user, and what do they truly need?"* | Personas, empathy maps, journey maps, jobs-to-be-done |
| **Leveraging Research** | Insight-to-ideation translation framework | *"How do research findings become breakthrough ideas?"* | Insight statements, HMW questions, opportunity matrix |

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Industry Metrics (Bolded Standards)

While research is qualitative-heavy, several **standard industry metrics** are universally applied and must be memorized for KTU exams:

- **TAM (Total Addressable Market)** — Total revenue opportunity if 100% market share achieved. Expressed in **currency** (USD/INR) or **users**.
- **SAM (Serviceable Addressable Market)** — The segment of TAM your product can realistically serve.
- **SOM (Serviceable Obtainable Market)** — The share of SAM you can capture in the short term.
- **NPS (Net Promoter Score)** — Loyalty metric, scale of **−100 to +100**. Industry benchmark is **>+30** for good.
- **CSAT (Customer Satisfaction Score)** — Typically **0–100%** scale.
- **5% Confidence Level / 95% Confidence Interval** — Standard for statistical validity in surveys.
- **Heuristic**: Qualitative research typically uses **5–30 participants**; quantitative requires **n ≥ 30** for statistical power.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

## 2.1 The Research-to-Ideation Pipeline — Structured Logic Steps

The KTU 2024 framework describes research as a **5-stage funnel**. Each stage has a distinct purpose and a transition output.

### Stage 1 → Frame the Research Question
- **Why**: Avoid research that is interesting but not actionable.
- **How**: Convert a vague brief ("We need a better app") into a focused research question ("What friction do small-business owners in Kerala face when reconciling daily UPI collections with GST bookkeeping?").
- **Output**: A **Research Brief** with 3–5 specific questions, target population, methodology, and timeline.

### Stage 2 → Conduct Primary Research
- **Why**: First-party data is freshest and most specific.
- **How**: Use qualitative methods (interviews, ethnography, diary studies) and quantitative methods (surveys, analytics).
- **Output**: Raw data corpus (transcripts, survey responses, observation logs).

### Stage 3 → Conduct Secondary Research
- **Why**: Contextualize primary findings within industry, regulatory, and competitive landscape.
- **How**: Market reports, academic literature, government datasets, competitor teardowns.
- **Output**: A **Competitive Landscape Map** and **Market Sizing Estimate**.

### Stage 4 → Synthesize into Insights
- **Why**: Data is not insight. Insight is the *so-what* of the data.
- **How**: Affinity mapping, theme clustering, "Powers of 10" (zoom in/out), JTBD reframing.
- **Output**: **Insight Statements** formatted as: *"[User] struggles with [need] because [surprising cause], which leads to [business opportunity]."*

### Stage 5 → Translate Insights into Ideation Prompts
- **Why**: Insights must be framed as actionable design opportunities.
- **How**: Convert insights to **"How Might We" (HMW)** questions. Cluster HMWs into themes. Run ideation sprints.
- **Output**: A portfolio of **prototype-ready concepts**.

---

## 2.2 Market Research — The Four-Component Analysis

Market research in the KTU syllabus has four canonical components:

| Component | Purpose | KTU-Standard Tool | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Market Sizing** | Quantify opportunity | TAM/SAM/SOM, Top-down vs. Bottom-up | Pre-investment, go/no-go |
| **Competitive Analysis** | Map alternatives | Porter's 5 Forces, Competitor Matrix, Perceptual Map | Strategy phase |
| **Trend Analysis** | Detect macro shifts | PESTLE, STEEP, Trend Cards (3–5–10 year) | Long-term product roadmaps |
| **Demand Validation** | Test willingness to pay | Conjoint analysis, Van Westendorp PSM, Fake-door tests | Pre-launch |

### The TAM / SAM / SOM Funnel (Standard Industry Visualization)

$$\text{TAM} \supset \text{SAM} \supset \text{SOM}$$

**Real-world engineering example**: A KTU student team designing a low-cost water purifier for Kerala homes:
- **TAM** = All households in India without safe drinking water (~80 million households).
- **SAM** = Households in Kerala + Tamil Nadu + Karnataka (geographically serviceable).
- **SOM** = The 5,000 households they can reach in Year 1 through college distribution channels.

---

## 2.3 Consumer Research — The Three Layers of User Understanding

| Layer | Depth | Tool | Output Format |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Demographic** | *Who* they are | Surveys, census data | Age, income, location, occupation persona |
| **Behavioral** | *What* they do | Analytics, ethnography, diary studies | Journey map, frequency chart |
| **Psychographic** | *Why* they do it | Interviews, projective techniques | Empathy map, value map, JTBD statement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rule for Consumer Research:**
> Demographics tell you *who* bought. Behavior tells you *what* they did. Psychographics tell you *why* they'll buy again. **Ideation requires all three**, but breakthrough ideation is almost always unlocked by psychographic depth.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix — Market Research Methodologies

| Method | Type | Sample Size | Strength | Weakness | Best For KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **In-depth Interviews** | Qualitative, Primary | 8–15 | Deep psychographic insight | Time-intensive | Persona creation |
| **Ethnography** | Qualitative, Primary | 3–8 | Reveals unspoken behavior | Observer bias | Journey mapping |
| **Surveys** | Quantitative, Primary | n ≥ 100 (statistically valid) | Broad generalizability | Superficial | Market sizing, segmentation |
| **Focus Groups** | Qualitative, Primary | 6–10 per group | Group dynamics surface ideas | Groupthink | Concept testing |
| **Secondary/Literature Review** | Quantitative/Qualitative | n/a | Fast, cheap, contextual | May be outdated | PESTLE, competitive scan |
| **A/B Testing** | Quantitative, Primary | Large (10k+) | Causal proof | Requires live product | Optimization, not ideation |
| **Diary Studies** | Qualitative, Primary | 10–30 | Captures longitudinal behavior | Compliance drop-off | Habitual product spaces |

---

## 2.5 Leveraging Research for Informed Ideation — The Translation Framework

This is the **highest-weight topic** in Module 3 for KTU exams. The translation has three formal sub-steps:

### Sub-step 2.5.A — Insight-to-HMW Conversion
Each insight statement should yield **2–3 HMW questions**:
- *Insight*: "Students skip the campus canteen during exam week because the 15-minute lunch break is too short to queue, order, eat, and reach the exam hall."
- *HMW 1*: How might we reduce the *time* it takes to get food during exam week?
- *HMW 2*: How might we make food available *at* the exam hall instead?
- *HMW 3*: How might we redesign the exam-day schedule to allow longer breaks?

### Sub-step 2.5.B — HMW Clustering & Opportunity Mapping
Plot HMWs on a 2×2 matrix: **Desirability (for user) × Feasibility (for team)**. Ideate heavily in the **high-desirability / high-feasibility** quadrant first.

### Sub-step 2.5.C — Concept Generation & Validation Loop
- Generate ≥ 10 concepts per HMW cluster.
- Filter using **Kano Model** (Basic / Performance / Excitement features).
- Test top 2–3 via low-fidelity prototypes and **fake-door** or **concierge** MVPs.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In professional product development, research-driven ideation is the **difference between funded startups and forgotten ones**:

- **Y Combinator's** mandatory rule: Talk to 10+ users before writing a single line of code.
- **IDEO's** "Deep Dive" phase: 2–4 weeks of pure research before any sketching.
- **Google Design Sprint** (Week 1): Monday is 100% research, Tuesday is synthesis, Wednesday is ideation.
- **ISO 9241-210 (Human-Centred Design)**: Mandates research as a prerequisite to design.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU project evaluations, examiners explicitly reward teams who **cite primary research** (user interviews, surveys with n ≥ 20) in their Design Thinking reports. Teams that skip research typically lose **15–20% of the project marks**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Framework Derivations

Since this is a **qualitative / management-focused** topic (GZEST605 is a non-quantitative design course), Section 3 provides **exhaustive step-by-step derivations of the analytical frameworks** that examiners test, rather than mathematical proofs. Each framework is broken down to its atomic logic steps with explicit justification.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Empathy Map (8-Step Logic Build)

The Empathy Map is the most frequently tested consumer research tool in KTU exams. Below is the full derivation of why it has exactly 6 zones and what each captures.

**Step 1 — Define the Subject**
- *Logic*: A map is only useful if it has a center. The subject is a specific persona, not "users" in the abstract.
- *Output*: "Priya, 22, final-year B.Tech student, KTU, preparing for GATE exam."

**Step 2 — Identify the SAYS zone (top-left)**
- *Logic*: What does the user *verbally* express? This is the surface layer — what they tell you in interviews.
- *Why it matters*: It's the *least reliable* data because users often say what sounds socially acceptable.
- *Example*: "I really care about sustainable products."

**Step 3 — Identify the THINKS zone (top-right)**
- *Logic*: What are the user's *real thoughts and beliefs* that they may not vocalize?
- *Why it matters*: Captures the cognitive layer — what occupies their mind during the task.
- *Example*: "I'm worried about the cost of eco-friendly products."

**Step 4 — Identify the DOES zone (bottom-left)**
- *Logic*: What *actions* does the user actually take? Observed behavior, not stated intent.
- *Why it matters*: The classic "says one thing, does another" gap is the source of most innovation.
- *Example*: "Buys the cheapest non-eco option at the store."

**Step 5 — Identify the FEELS zone (bottom-right)**
- *Logic*: What is the user's *emotional state* during the experience?
- *Why it matters*: Emotions drive decisions more than logic (per Antonio Damasio's somatic marker hypothesis).
- *Example*: "Guilt after purchase, anxiety about climate change."

**Step 6 — Identify the PAINS zone (bottom-center-left)**
- *Logic*: What *obstacles, risks, frustrations* does the user encounter?
- *Why it matters*: Pains are the *seeds of opportunity* — every pain is a potential product feature.
- *Example*: "No affordable eco-alternatives in my local market."

**Step 7 — Identify the GAINS zone (bottom-center-right)**
- *Logic*: What *benefits, desires, success measures* does the user want?
- *Why it matters*: Gains define the *value proposition*.
- *Example*: "Want to feel like I'm making a difference without paying a premium."

**Step 8 — Synthesize the 4 Asymmetries**
- *Logic*: Compare Says vs. Does, Thinks vs. Feels. The **gaps** between these zones are the *insight hotspots*.
- *Why it matters*: KTU examiners award full marks only when students identify the asymmetries, not just fill the zones.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight Hotspot Rule (KTU High-Yield):**
> Innovation lives in the **gap** between what users *say* and what they *do*. A research project that surfaces at least **2 such asymmetries** is considered exemplary.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Customer Journey Map (5-Stage Logic Build)

**Step 1 — Set the Scope**
- Define the *specific scenario* (e.g., "buying a laptop for college") and the *persona* (e.g., "first-generation engineering student on a tight budget").
- *Why*: Without scope, the map expands infinitely and loses utility.

**Step 2 — Identify the 5 Phases**
The canonical phases are: **Awareness → Consideration → Decision → Use → Loyalty/Advocacy**.
- *Logic*: These mirror the customer lifecycle in marketing theory.
- *Why*: Phases must be **chronologically sequential** and **mutually exclusive** for clean analysis.

**Step 3 — Populate User Goals per Phase**
- *Logic*: At each phase, the user is trying to accomplish a *micro-task* (e.g., in Consideration: "compare 3 options on price and specs").
- *Why*: Goals reveal *intent*, which is the precursor to behavior.

**Step 4 — Populate Touchpoints per Phase**
- *Logic*: A touchpoint is any interaction with a brand/product/environment (e.g., Google search, friend recommendation, retail visit, packaging).
- *Why*: Touchpoints are where you can *intervene* with design.

**Step 5 — Populate Pains & Gains per Phase**
- *Logic*: Map emotional highs/lows using a **moment-curve** (a line graph of emotional intensity across phases).
- *Why*: The **lowest points on the curve** are the highest-opportunity design targets.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip**: A complete journey map must show *at least one* emotional "valley" and explain how the proposed product idea specifically targets that valley. Marks are docked if the journey is generic (e.g., "Awareness, Consideration, Purchase") without persona-specific depth.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Jobs-to-Be-Done (JTBD) Statement (4-Step Logic Build)

**Step 1 — Identify the Job Verb**
- *Logic*: When users "hire" a product, they are *hiring it to do a job*. The verb captures the active function.
- *Why*: Verbs are testable. "Helps me" is vague; "transports me" is specific.

**Step 2 — Add the Object of the Job**
- *Logic*: Specify *what* is being acted upon.
- *Example*: "transport me," "track my assignments," "validate my idea."

**Step 3 — Add the Context**
- *Logic*: Specify the *situation* in which the job arises. Context is where most innovation hides.
- *Example*: "…when I'm walking to class in monsoon rain."

**Step 4 — Add the Outcome / Emotional Payoff**
- *Logic*: Specify the *success measure* — functional and emotional.
- *Example*: "…so I arrive dry and on time, feeling confident."

**Final JTBD Statement Format**:
$$\text{When [context], I want to [verb] [object], so I can [outcome/payoff].}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Insight-to-Ideation Conversion Matrix

This is a **process derivation** showing exactly how research findings become design ideas. Each stage has a clear input → process → output logic:

| Stage | Input | Process | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Data Capture** | Raw transcripts, survey responses, observation notes | Tag and index | Tagged data set (≈200–500 items) |
| **2. Affinity Mapping** | Tagged data | Cluster by thematic similarity | 8–15 theme clusters |
| **3. Insight Distillation** | Theme clusters | Apply "So what? / Why? / How often?" | 3–5 insight statements |
| **4. HMW Generation** | Each insight statement | Reframe as a design question | 6–15 HMW questions |
| **5. Opportunity Prioritization** | HMW list | Plot on Desirability × Feasibility matrix | Top 3–5 HMWs for ideation |
| **6. Concept Brainstorming** | Prioritized HMWs | SCAMPER, Crazy 8s, Round-Robin | 30+ raw concepts |
| **7. Concept Filtering** | Raw concepts | Apply Kano + Effort/Impact scoring | 2–3 prototype-ready concepts |
| **8. Concept Validation** | Filtered concepts | User testing, fake-door, concierge MVP | Validated, refined concept |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statistically Validated Conversion Rate (Industry Benchmark)**:
> Out of 30 raw concepts, typically only **2–3 (≈10%)** survive validation. This is why research-driven ideation front-loads effort on *research quality*, not idea quantity. Bad research → 30 bad ideas → 0 survivors.

---

## 3.5 Derivation of Kano Model Classification (with Decision Tree Logic)

**Step 1 — Ask Two Functional Questions per Feature**
- *Q1 (Functional form)*: "If this feature were present, how would you feel?" (5-point: Like → Dislike)
- *Q2 (Dysfunctional form)*: "If this feature were absent, how would you feel?" (5-point: Like → Dislike)

**Step 2 — Apply the Kano Evaluation Table**

| Functional → / Dysfunctional ↓ | Like | Expect | Neutral | Accept | Dislike |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Like** | Q | A | A | A | O |
| **Expect** | R | I | I | I | M |
| **Neutral** | R | I | I | I | M |
| **Accept** | R | I | I | I | M |
| **Dislike** | R | R | R | R | Q |

**Legend**:
- **M (Must-be)**: Basic expectations. Absence causes extreme dissatisfaction. Presence does *not* increase satisfaction.
- **O (One-dimensional)**: Satisfaction scales linearly with performance. More is better.
- **A (Attractive)**: Unexpected delighters. Absence doesn't hurt; presence thrills.
- **I (Indifferent)**: User doesn't care either way.
- **R (Reverse)**: User actually dislikes the feature.
- **Q (Questionable)**: Conflicting responses; revise the question.

**Step 3 — Strategic Implication for Ideation**
- **Must-be features** → Build them first, never compete on them.
- **One-dimensional features** → Compete on them via benchmarking.
- **Attractive features** → These are the **ideation goldmine** — they create differentiation and emotional loyalty.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight**: When asked "How does research lead to innovation?", the canonical answer is: *"By identifying Attractive (delighter) features that competitors haven't yet offered — features users don't even know to ask for, but which research reveals they would love."*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Research-Driven Ideation Engine (Master Flow)

This diagram maps the entire pipeline from raw research to validated ideas. Each node is a verifiable milestone a KTU project team can present.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[KTU Design Challenge Brief] --> B[Frame Research Questions]
    B --> C[Primary Research Layer]
    B --> D[Secondary Research Layer]
    C --> C1[User Interviews n=8-15]
    C --> C2[Ethnographic Observation n=3-8]
    C --> C3[Survey n ≥ 100]
    D --> D1[Market Reports & PESTLE]
    D --> D2[Competitor Teardowns]
    D --> D3[Academic Literature]
    C1 --> E[Raw Data Corpus]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Affinity Mapping & Theme Clustering]
    F --> G[Distilled Insight Statements]
    G --> H[How Might We Questions]
    H --> I[Opportunity Matrix Desirability vs Feasibility]
    I --> J[Prioritized HMW Set Top 3 to 5]
    J --> K[SCAMPER and Crazy 8s Ideation Sprint]
    K --> L[30 plus Raw Concepts]
    L --> M[Kano Model Filtering]
    M --> N[2 to 3 Prototype-Ready Concepts]
    N --> O[Low-Fidelity Prototype]
    O --> P[User Validation Round]
    P --> Q{Validated Concept}
    Q -->|Yes| R[Final Design Direction]
    Q -->|No| S[Iterate or Pivot to Next Concept]
    S --> K
    R --> T[KTU Design Report Submission]
```

---

## 4.2 Consumer Research — The Three-Layer Insight Pyramid

This diagram shows the *depth hierarchy* of consumer research and how each layer feeds the next. The apex is where ideation breakthroughs occur.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1 Demographic WHO] --> L2[Layer 2 Behavioral WHAT]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Psychographic WHY]
    L3 --> PEAK[Insight Hotspot Breakthrough Ideation Zone]
    L1 -.->|Supports| L2
    L2 -.->|Enables| L3
    L1 --> OBS1[Age, Income, Location, Gender]
    L2 --> OBS2[Actions, Frequency, Touchpoints, Context]
    L3 --> OBS3[Beliefs, Emotions, Values, Unmet Needs]
    OBS1 --> L1
    OBS2 --> L2
    OBS3 --> L3
    style PEAK fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style L1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4
    style L2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22
    style L3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585
```

---

## 4.3 Market Research — The 4-Quadrant Strategic Map

A canonical framework showing how market research decomposes into the four strategic quadrants used in any KTU project report.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Q1[Market Sizing]
        Q1A[TAM Total Addressable Market]
        Q1B[SAM Serviceable Addressable Market]
        Q1C[SOM Serviceable Obtainable Market]
    end
    subgraph Q2[Competitive Landscape]
        Q2A[Porter 5 Forces Analysis]
        Q2B[Competitor Feature Matrix]
        Q2C[Perceptual Positioning Map]
    end
    subgraph Q3[Trend & Context Analysis]
        Q3A[PESTLE Macro Forces]
        Q3B[STEEP Social Tech Env Economic Political]
        Q3C[3 to 5 to 10 Year Trend Cards]
    end
    subgraph Q4[Demand Validation]
        Q4A[Conjoint Analysis]
        Q4B[Van Westendorp Price Sensitivity Meter]
        Q4C[Fake Door and Concierge MVPs]
    end
    Q1 --> SY[Synthesis Layer]
    Q2 --> SY
    Q3 --> SY
    Q4 --> SY
    SY --> OUT[Strategic Opportunity Brief for Ideation]
    style SY fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:3px
    style OUT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.4 Insight-to-Ideation Translation Flow (Detailed Sub-Process)

This is the most critical schematic for KTU exams — it shows the exact micro-steps of converting one insight into a portfolio of ideas.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INS[Insight Statement Example: Students skip canteen during exams due to time crunch] --> ANCHOR{Anchor to JTBD Framework}
    ANCHOR --> HMWGEN[Generate 2 to 3 HMW Questions]
    HMWGEN --> HMW1[HMW 1 Reduce Food Acquisition Time]
    HMWGEN --> HMW2[HMW 2 Bring Food to the Exam Hall]
    HMWGEN --> HMW3[HMW 3 Redesign Exam Day Schedule]
    HMW1 --> CLUSTER[Cluster Related HMWs by Theme]
    HMW2 --> CLUSTER
    HMW3 --> CLUSTER
    CLUSTER --> BRAIN[SCAMPER Ideation Session 30 min]
    BRAIN --> CONCEPTS[10 plus Raw Concepts per HMW]
    CONCEPTS --> KANOFILTER[Kano Model Classification]
    KANOFILTER --> M[Must Be Features Baseline]
    KANOFILTER --> O[One Dimensional Performance]
    KANOFILTER --> A[Attractive Delighters Innovation Zone]
    A --> TOP2[Top 2 to 3 Concepts for Prototyping]
    M --> TOP2
    O --> TOP2
    TOP2 --> PROTOTYPE[Build Low-Fidelity Prototype]
    PROTOTYPE --> USERTEST[User Test n=5]
    USERTEST --> VALIDATE{Validated by Users}
    VALIDATE -->|Yes| PIVOT[Refine and Scale]
    VALIDATE -->|No| ITERATE[Return to HMW Stage]
    ITERATE --> HMWGEN
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px
    style PIVOT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.5 Empathy Map — Canonical 6-Zone Schematic

The standard KTU-exam-tested empathy map template. Use this exact structure in project reports.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```
