<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
**Define market research and explain its four canonical components as per the KTU Design Thinking framework.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

Market research is the systematic process of gathering, analyzing, and interpreting information about a market, its consumers, competitors, and the macro-environment to support product ideation and strategic decision-making.

The four canonical components are:
1. **Market Sizing** — Quantifying opportunity using TAM, SAM, and SOM frameworks.
2. **Competitive Analysis** — Mapping alternatives using Porter's Five Forces and competitor feature matrices.
3. **Trend Analysis** — Detecting macro shifts using PESTLE/STEEP and horizon scanning.
4. **Demand Validation** — Testing willingness-to-pay and concept acceptance using conjoint analysis and MVP prototypes.

Together, these four components answer: *"Is there a viable, defensible, and desirable market for the idea we are about to generate?"* **(3 marks — full marks awarded for naming all 4 components with 1-line definitions.)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**Differentiate between consumer research and market research with one real-world example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

| Dimension | Market Research | Consumer Research |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | The market as a system | The individual user as a person |
| **Scale** | Macro / industry-wide | Micro / individual-level |
| **Key Question** | *Is there a market?* | *Who is the user and why do they act?* |
| **Primary Tools** | PESTLE, TAM/SAM/SOM, competitor matrix | Personas, empathy maps, JTBD, journey maps |
| **Data Type** | Mostly quantitative | Mostly qualitative |

**Examples**:
- **Market research example**: Estimating the size of the Indian EV two-wheeler market at ₹1.2 lakh crore by 2030 using government vehicle registration data and CAGR projections.
- **Consumer research example**: Interviewing 12 college students in Kerala to understand their daily commute pain points, leading to an empathy map revealing anxiety about rain, cost, and arrival time.

The two are **complementary, not interchangeable**. Market research defines the *opportunity space*; consumer research defines the *human context* within that space. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**:
> ⚠️ **Most common mark-loss areas in Module 3 questions:**
> 1. **Listing frameworks without applying them** — Naming "empathy map" without populating it with research data costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing "users" with "buyers"** — In B2B contexts, the user and the purchaser are different people; examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Skipping the "Why"** — A persona without goals, fears, and motivations is a "demographic sketch," not a persona. Examiners dock 2 marks for this.
> 4. **No HMW conversion** — Insights stated without reframing as HMW questions are considered incomplete ideation.
> 5. **Generic journey maps** — A journey map that says "Awareness → Purchase" without specific touchpoints, emotions, and valleys is worth only 50% marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Real Exam Style]**

**"Research is the connective tissue between empathy and ideation." Critically analyse this statement with reference to (a) the role of research in converting user insights into design ideas, and (b) the application of at least two consumer research tools in a real-world product development scenario of your choice.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**The Role of Research in Converting Insights into Design Ideas**

Research plays four explicit roles in the insight-to-ideation translation:

**Role 1 — De-risking Ideation**:
Without research, ideation is opinion-based and produces concepts that are technically buildable but commercially unviable. Research validates assumptions *before* engineering effort is spent. For example, a KTU student team ideating a "smart dustbin" without first researching campus waste segregation habits produced a sensor-laden product that students actively avoided.

**Role 2 — Surfacing Unstated Needs**:
Users are notoriously bad at articulating latent needs. Ethnographic and psychographic research methods (e.g., diary studies, shadowing) reveal jobs users *hire products to do* but cannot verbally express. This is the source of disruptive innovation.

**Role 3 — Prioritizing Opportunity**:
Research enables a *portfolio approach* to ideation. By plotting opportunities on a Desirability × Feasibility matrix, teams focus creative energy on the high-impact, high-doability zones, dramatically improving concept survival rates.

**Role 4 — Providing Defensible Language**:
Stakeholders (faculty evaluators, investors, engineering teams) trust data more than opinions. A research-backed idea like *"78% of surveyed KTU students report skipping breakfast at least 3 times a week"* is **defensible**, whereas *"I think students skip breakfast"* is not.

The translation chain is:
$$\text{Research Data} \xrightarrow{\text{Synthesis}} \text{Insight} \xrightarrow{\text{HMW}} \text{Design Opportunity} \xrightarrow{\text{Ideation}} \text{Concept}$$

**(7 marks — Examiner awards full marks only if all 4 roles are explicitly named and supported with an example.)**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Application of Two Consumer Research Tools — Case: A New App for KTU Students to Manage Project Teams**

**Tool 1 — Empathy Map (Primary Persona: "Rohan, 21, Final-Year CS Student")**
- **Says**: "I hate group projects. Someone always does nothing."
- **Thinks**: "I wish I had a way to track who's actually contributing."
- **Does**: Spends 2 hours/week on WhatsApp trying to coordinate; gives up and does the work himself.
- **Feels**: Resentment, anxiety before meetings.
- **Pains**: No accountability mechanism, social pressure not to confront non-contributors.
- **Gains**: Transparent contribution tracking, fair grading, reduced personal workload.

**Asymmetry Surfaced**: Rohan *says* he hates group projects but *does* the work anyway — surfacing a deep unmet need for *passive accountability tools* that don't require confrontation.

**Tool 2 — Customer Journey Map (Scenario: "Group project kickoff")**
| Phase | Goal | Touchpoint | Emotion (1–5) | Pain |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Awareness | "I have a new group project" | Email from faculty | 3 (neutral) | Uncertainty about partners |
| Consideration | "Who will be in my group?" | WhatsApp, class gossip | 2 (anxious) | Last-minute team formation |
| Decision | "Let's form the team" | In-person lobby | 2 (anxious) | Awkward exclusion |
| Use | "Coordinate the work" | WhatsApp, Google Docs | 1 (frustrated) | No progress visibility |
| Loyalty | "How was the experience?" | End-semester feedback | 2 (resentful) | Grade disputes |

**Lowest emotional point**: The "Use" phase at score 1. **Design Opportunity**: Build a contribution-tracking feature with auto-graded peer reviews — directly targeting the emotional valley.

**Ideation Output (HMW)**: *"How might we make individual contribution visible and fair without forcing uncomfortable social confrontation?"* — leading to 8+ concepts, 3 of which were prototyped and user-tested.

**(7 marks — Examiner awards full marks when tools are populated with persona-specific data, not generic content, and at least one asymmetry/emotional valley is identified.)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Real Exam Style — Alternative Choice]**

**Explain the concept of "informed ideation" with specific reference to (a) the process of converting research insights into 'How Might We' questions, and (b) a comparative analysis of qualitative vs. quantitative consumer research methods, with at least one example of each in a product development context.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Process of Converting Research Insights into HMW Questions**

Informed ideation is the discipline of *never ideating without a research anchor*. The conversion process has 4 sub-steps:

**Sub-step 1 — Extract Atomic Insights from Research Themes**:
An insight is **not** a finding; it is the *so-what* of a finding. The standard KTU format is:
$$\text{Insight} = \text{[User]} + \text{[Observed Behavior]} + \text{[Underlying Cause]} + \text{[Implication]}$$

**Example**: Insight from canteen research — *"Students skip the canteen during exam week because the 15-minute lunch break is too short to queue, order, eat, and reach the exam hall, causing them to skip meals entirely."*

**Sub-step 2 — Reframe the Insight as a Design Question**:
A well-formed HMW has three properties:
- **Broad enough** to allow multiple solution paths (not "How might we build a faster oven?").
- **Narrow enough** to be actionable (not "How might we improve food?").
- **User-centred** (uses the user's language, not the team's jargon).

From the example insight, three HMWs emerge:
- *HMW 1*: How might we **reduce the time** required to get food during exam week?
- *HMW 2*: How might we **bring food to the user** instead of bringing the user to food?
- *HMW 3*: How might we **redesign the schedule** to give students more time to eat?

**Sub-step 3 — Cluster and Prioritize HMWs**:
Use the **Desirability × Feasibility** matrix. Top-right quadrant HMWs are ideated first.

**Sub-step 4 — Ideate Against Prioritized HMWs**:
Apply SCAMPER, Crazy 8s, or Round-Robin. Generate ≥10 concepts per HMW cluster before filtering.

**(7 marks — Full marks when all 4 sub-steps are explicit, the insight-to-HMW conversion is shown with a real example, and the prioritization matrix is named.)**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Qualitative vs. Quantitative Consumer Research — Comparative Analysis**

| Dimension | Qualitative | Quantitative |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Understand *why* and *how* | Measure *how many* and *how much* |
| **Sample size** | Small (5–30) | Large (n ≥ 100, ideally 400+) |
| **Data form** | Words, images, stories | Numbers, percentages, scores |
| **Analytical lens** | Thematic, interpretive | Statistical, inferential |
| **Strength** | Depth, nuance, surprise | Generalizability, prediction |
| **Weakness** | Cannot be statistically projected | Misses the *why* behind numbers |
| **Cost per insight** | High (analyst hours) | Low (per respondent) |
| **Best phase** | Early (Explore, Empathize) | Late (Test, Validate) |

**Qualitative Example (In-depth Interview)**:
A product team interviews 12 students about their morning routines. They discover that students don't use productivity apps in the morning because they associate them with *work stress* — a finding that statistical surveys would have missed. This becomes the insight: *"Productivity tools trigger guilt, not motivation, in the morning context."* The team ideates a "zero-pressure" planning app with no streaks, no penalties, and a calming color palette.

**Quantitative Example (Survey with n=400)**:
A product team surveys 400 users about feature preferences using a Kano questionnaire. Results show that 72% of users classify "offline mode" as a *Must-be* feature — meaning the product will fail in the market if offline support is not built. This statistically validated finding directs the engineering roadmap.

**Synthesis Statement**:
The two methods are **complementary, not substitutable**. Qualitative research *generates* hypotheses; quantitative research *tests* them. KTU projects that combine both score higher on the *research rigor* rubric. A useful rule of thumb:
$$\text{Qualitative depth} \times \text{Quantitative breadth} = \text{Research Confidence}$$

**(7 marks — Examiner awards full marks when the comparison table is complete, both examples are non-generic, and the synthesis statement explicitly shows how the two methods combine.)**

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — High-Density Summary**

### 🔑 Core Definitions to Memorize
- **Market Research**: Macro-level systematic study of market size, competition, trends, and demand. **Tools**: TAM/SAM/SOM, PESTLE, Porter's 5 Forces, Conjoint Analysis.
- **Consumer Research**: Micro-level study of individual users — their behavior, motivations, emotions, and unmet needs. **Tools**: Empathy Maps, Personas, Journey Maps, JTBD, Kano Model.
- **Informed Ideation**: The discipline of generating product ideas *only after* research has produced validated insights; uses HMW questions as the bridge.
- **Insight Statement**: A *so-what* finding in the format *"[User] struggles with [need] because [cause], creating an opportunity for [intervention]."*
- **HMW Question**: A reframed insight as a broad-but-actionable design prompt starting with "How might we…?"

### 🧠 Five Frameworks You Must Be Able to Draw & Apply
1. **Empathy Map** (6 zones: Says / Thinks / Does / Feels / Pains / Gains).
2. **Customer Journey Map** (5 phases: Awareness → Consideration → Decision → Use → Loyalty).
3. **Kano Model** (6 categories: M / O / A / I / R / Q).
4. **TAM / SAM / SOM Funnel** (Total → Serviceable → Obtainable).
5. **PESTLE** (Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal, Environmental).

### ⚡ Three Rules for High-Scoring Answers
1. **Always cite primary research** in your answers (e.g., "interviewed 8 students, surveyed 120 users").
2. **Always show the conversion**: Insight → HMW → Concept. Skipping any link loses marks.
3. **Always identify an asymmetry or emotional valley** — examiners specifically reward insight depth over surface listing.

### 🚫 Three Common Pitfalls to Avoid
1. Treating "users" as homogeneous — always use **at least 2 personas**.
2. Confusing *demographics* (who) with *psychographics* (why) — they are not the same.
3. Ending with a list of ideas without validation logic — always close with a **concept filtering** or **prioritization** step.

### 📊 Standard Industry Metrics (Know These Cold)
- **TAM ≥ SAM ≥ SOM** (always in descending order of magnitude).
- **NPS** scale: **−100 to +100**; benchmark **>+30 = good**.
- **CSAT** scale: **0–100%**; benchmark **>80% = excellent**.
- **Qualitative sample size**: **5–30** participants (saturation point).
- **Quantitative sample size**: **n ≥ 30** (minimum statistical); **n ≥ 100** (robust).
- **Confidence Level**: **95%** is the industry standard for surveys.

### 🎯 Module 3 Take-Home Truth
> *"The quality of your ideas is bounded by the quality of your research. Better research doesn't just improve ideation — it makes ideation **possible** in the first place."*

This single sentence, well-elaborated, can earn **full marks** in any KTU Part A or short-notice viva question on Module 3. Use it as your **opening line** for any Module 3 answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technological trends - Navigating the technological landscape - Integrating emerging technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Technological trends - Navigating the technological landscape - Integrating emerging technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Technological Trends — Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Technological Trends** are defined as the directional, observable, and persistent patterns of innovation, adoption, and diffusion of technologies within an industry or society that reshape products, services, business models, and user experiences over a measurable time horizon (typically **3 to 10 years**).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, technological trends are studied through three lenses:

1. **Macro Trends** — Global shifts (e.g., Generative AI, sustainability tech, quantum computing).
2. **Sectoral Trends** — Industry-specific innovations (e.g., precision agriculture, telemedicine).
3. **Micro Trends** — Tool-level evolutions (e.g., new frameworks, libraries, hardware revisions).

## 1.2 Navigating the Technological Landscape

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Navigating the Technological Landscape** is the systematic process of **scanning**, **mapping**, **evaluating**, and **prioritizing** emerging technologies against organizational goals, user needs, and strategic constraints to make informed investment and design decisions.

It is the "strategic compass" phase of design thinking, ensuring that innovation efforts are grounded in technological reality rather than hype.

## 1.3 Integrating Emerging Technologies

> [!NOTE]
> **Integration of Emerging Technologies** refers to the deliberate fusion of two or more nascent or maturing technologies (each at Technology Readiness Level **TRL 4 to TRL 9**) into a cohesive product, service, or system to unlock capabilities that no single technology could deliver alone.

This is the **Convergence Principle** in action — where AI + IoT + 5G, for instance, creates Smart Edge Intelligence that none of the three could achieve independently.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of the technological landscape as an ocean, technological trends as ocean currents, and emerging technologies as islands you must integrate.**

- **Technological Trends** = The currents (e.g., "AI everywhere" is a strong current pulling every industry).
- **Navigating the Landscape** = Your captain reading the currents, the map, and the weather to plot a safe, profitable route.
- **Integrating Emerging Technologies** = Loading multiple islands' worth of resources onto a single ship so they work together as a fleet.

If you ignore the currents, you shipwreck on hype. If you only visit one island, you miss synergies. The art is in **choosing the right islands and stitching them together** into a coherent voyage — exactly what Design Thinking enables.

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in KTU GZEST605

This topic bridges **Module 2 (Empathy & Problem Definition)** with **Module 4 (Prototyping)**. Without mastering technology navigation, students will prototype solutions that are either technologically infeasible or technologically obsolete. The KTU examiner expects you to demonstrate **structured technology selection**, not random brainstorming.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Layer Architecture of Technological Navigation

### Layer 1 — **Technology Sensing (Scanning)**
- Continuous monitoring of patent databases, research papers, Gartner reports, McKinsey insights, and CES/WWDC announcements.
- Output: A **Technology Radar** (visualized as concentric rings of maturity).

### Layer 2 — **Technology Evaluation (Mapping)**
- Applying maturity frameworks like **Gartner Hype Cycle**, **Technology Readiness Levels (TRL)**, and **Diffusion of Innovation (Rogers' Bell Curve)**.
- Output: A **Prioritized Technology Backlog**.

### Layer 3 — **Technology Integration (Convergence)**
- Using **Platform Thinking**, **API-First Architecture**, and **Modular Design** to stitch technologies together.
- Output: A **Working Prototype / MVP** demonstrating convergence.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Layer

| Layer | Core 'Why' | Design Thinking Link |
|-------|------------|----------------------|
| Sensing | Avoid being blindsided by disruption | Empathize with evolving user context |
| Evaluation | Filter hype from substance | Define the real problem |
| Integration | Create defensible value | Ideate & Prototype |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Frameworks & Models Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 6 frameworks — they appear in 80% of KTU Part B answers on this topic.**

| # | Framework | Core Idea | Key Stage on Curve | KTU Use Case |
|---|-----------|-----------|--------------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Gartner Hype Cycle** | Tech moves through Innovation Trigger → Peak of Inflated Expectations → Trough of Disillusionment → Slope of Enlightenment → Plateau of Productivity | **5** distinct phases plotted against *time* and *visibility* | Justify *why now* for technology adoption |
| 2 | **Technology Readiness Level (TRL)** | NASA/DARPA scale from basic research to proven deployment | **TRL 1** (basic principles) → **TRL 9** (proven in operational environment) | Decide whether a tech is prototype-ready |
| 3 | **Rogers' Diffusion of Innovation** | Adoption follows a bell curve across user categories | **5** adopter categories: Innovators, Early Adopters, Early Majority, Late Majority, Laggards | Target the right user segment first |
| 4 | **McKinsey Three Horizons** | Portfolio view of innovation investment | H1 (Core), H2 (Emerging), H3 (Transformational) | Balance short-term and long-term bets |
| 5 | **Technology S-Curve** | Performance vs. effort follows an S-shape | **3** phases: Emergence, Growth, Maturity | Know when to abandon a saturating tech |
| 6 | **Convergence Framework** | Two or more techs fuse to create new capabilities | **4** types: Functional, Sectoral, Cultural, Systemic | Structure the integration argument |

## 2.4 Mathematical Representation of Technology Maturity

The maturity of a technology can be approximated by a logistic function:

$$ P(t) = \frac{L}{1 + e^{-k(t - t_0)}} $$

Where:
- $P(t)$ = Performance / capability of the technology at time $t$
- $L$ = Maximum achievable performance (saturation ceiling)
- $k$ = Growth steepness constant
- $t_0$ = Inflection year (point of maximum growth rate)

This explains the **S-curve** behavior — slow start, rapid growth, plateau.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a B.Tech capstone project, applying these frameworks ensures:
- **Defensibility**: Your jury cannot reject your design as "technically unrealistic."
- **Differentiation**: You move beyond "we used AI" to "we integrated TRL-7 Edge AI with TRL-9 MQTT protocols to solve [validated problem]."
- **Employability**: Industry 4.0 hiring managers value graduates who think in **TRL terms** and **Hype-Cycle awareness**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Methodology & Decision Matrix

## 3.1 The 7-Step Methodology for Integrating Emerging Technologies

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **exam-ready analytical structure**. Memorize it — KTU examiners award full marks to answers that follow a logical, sequential evaluation.

### **Step 1 — Define the Problem Space**
Articulate the user problem in a single sentence. Use the **Point-of-View template**: *"[User] needs [need] because [insight]."*

### **Step 2 — Enumerate Candidate Technologies**
List 4 to 6 emerging technologies that *could* address the problem. Example for a Smart Farming project: IoT sensors, Edge AI, Satellite imagery, Blockchain traceability, Drone robotics, 5G connectivity.

### **Step 3 — Score Each Technology on the Weighted Decision Matrix**

Apply the weighted scoring formula:

$$ S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij} $$

Where:
- $S_i$ = Total weighted score of technology $i$
- $w_j$ = Weight of evaluation criterion $j$ (must sum to 1.0)
- $r_{ij}$ = Rating of technology $i$ on criterion $j$ (scale 1 to 10)
- $n$ = Number of criteria

### **Step 4 — Apply TRL Filter**
Reject any technology below **TRL 5** for an academic prototype. This is non-negotiable in KTU evaluations.

### **Step 5 — Conduct Hype-Cycle Check**
Plot each surviving technology on the Hype Cycle. Avoid the **Trough of Disillusionment** unless you have deep R&D support.

### **Step 6 — Design the Integration Architecture**
Use a **Modular Block Diagram** (shown in Section 4) to show how selected technologies interconnect via APIs, data buses, or middleware.

### **Step 7 — Validate via Rapid Prototyping**
Build an MVP, gather user feedback (Module 2 empathy loop), and iterate.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Smart Campus Energy Optimizer

> A B.Tech team wants to build a campus energy optimizer. They shortlist 4 technologies.

**Step 3 Worked Out:**

Criteria and Weights:
- Technical Feasibility: $w_1 = 0.25$
- Cost: $w_2 = 0.20$
- Scalability: $w_3 = 0.20$
- User Impact: $w_4 = 0.20$
- Innovation: $w_5 = 0.15$

*Sum check:* $0.25 + 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.15 = 1.00$ ✓

**Rating Table (ratings on 1–10 scale):**

| Technology | Feasibility ($r_{i1}$) | Cost ($r_{i2}$) | Scalability ($r_{i3}$) | User Impact ($r_{i4}$) | Innovation ($r_{i5}$) |
|------------|----------------------|----------------|------------------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| IoT Sensors | 9 | 7 | 9 | 8 | 6 |
| Edge AI | 7 | 5 | 8 | 9 | 9 |
| Blockchain | 5 | 3 | 6 | 5 | 8 |
| 5G Connectivity | 6 | 4 | 9 | 7 | 7 |

**Now compute $S_i$ for IoT Sensors:**

$$ S_{\text{IoT}} = (0.25)(9) + (0.20)(7) + (0.20)(9) + (0.20)(8) + (0.15)(6) $$

$$ S_{\text{IoT}} = 2.25 + 1.40 + 1.80 + 1.60 + 0.90 $$

$$ S_{\text{IoT}} = 7.95 $$

**Compute $S_i$ for Edge AI:**

$$ S_{\text{AI}} = (0.25)(7) + (0.20)(5) + (0.20)(8) + (0.20)(9) + (0.15)(9) $$

$$ S_{\text{AI}} = 1.75 + 1.00 + 1.60 + 1.80 + 1.35 $$

$$ S_{\text{AI}} = 7.50 $$

**Compute $S_i$ for Blockchain:**

$$ S_{\text{BC}} = (0.25)(5) + (0.20)(3) + (0.20)(6) + (0.20)(5) + (0.15)(8) $$

$$ S_{\text{BC}} = 1.25 + 0.60 + 1.20 + 1.00 + 1.20 $$

$$ S_{\text{BC}} = 5.25 $$

**Compute $S_i$ for 5G Connectivity:**

$$ S_{\text{5G}} = (0.25)(6) + (0.20)(4) + (0.20)(9) + (0.20)(7) + (0.15)(7) $$

$$ S_{\text{5G}} = 1.50 + 0.80 + 1.80 + 1.40 + 1.05 $$

$$ S_{\text{5G}} = 6.55 $$

**Final Ranking:**

| Rank | Technology | Score $S_i$ | Decision |
|------|------------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | IoT Sensors | **7.95** | ✅ Adopt as backbone |
| 2 | Edge AI | **7.50** | ✅ Adopt for intelligence layer |
| 3 | 5G Connectivity | **6.55** | ⚠️ Conditional (use where bandwidth-critical) |
| 4 | Blockchain | **5.25** | ❌ Reject for MVP (immature, costly) |

**Decision:** Integrate **IoT + Edge AI** as the convergent technology stack, with optional 5G for outdoor nodes.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Adopt vs. Reject

| Signal | Adopt | Reject / Defer |
|--------|-------|----------------|
| **TRL** | $\geq$ **TRL 6** | $\leq$ **TRL 4** |
| **Hype Cycle Position** | Slope of Enlightenment / Plateau | Peak of Inflated Expectations (without deep R&D) |
| **Weighted Score** | $S_i \geq 7.0$ | $S_i < 5.0$ |
| **Ecosystem Maturity** | Strong developer community | Single-vendor lock-in |
| **Cost-to-Prototype Ratio** | Affordable in 6-month B.Tech timeline | Requires > ₹5 Lakh or specialized lab |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Three-Layer Technology Navigation Process (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Definition from Module 2] --> B[Layer 1 Technology Sensing]
    B --> B1[Patent and Literature Scan]
    B --> B2[Industry Trend Reports]
    B --> B3[Competitor Benchmarking]
    B1 --> C[Layer 2 Technology Evaluation]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[TRL Assessment]
    C --> C2[Hype Cycle Mapping]
    C --> C3[Weighted Decision Matrix]
    C1 --> D[Layer 3 Technology Integration]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> D1[Modular Architecture Design]
    D --> D2[API and Data Bus Layer]
    D --> D3[Prototype MVP Build]
    D1 --> E[Validated Integrated Solution]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[User Testing Loop Back to Module 2]
    F --> A
```

## 4.2 Convergence Architecture — IoT + Edge AI + 5G Smart System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1[Physical Sensors and Actuators] --> nodeS2[Edge Gateway with AI Inference]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3[5G or WiFi 6 Network Layer]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4[Cloud Platform for Analytics]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5[User Mobile and Web Dashboard]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS6[Blockchain Ledger for Audit]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS6
    nodeS5 --> nodeS7[Design Thinking Feedback Loop]
    nodeS6 --> nodeS7
    nodeS7 --> nodeS2
```

## 4.3 Hype Cycle Position Mapping (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    hp1[Innovation Trigger] --> hp2[Peak of Inflated Expectations]
    hp2 --> hp3[Trough of Disillusionment]
    hp3 --> hp4[Slope of Enlightenment]
    hp4 --> hp5[Plateau of Productivity]
    hp5 -.Strategic Adoption Zone.-> hp6[Integrate Now]
    hp2 -.Avoid Without R and D.-> hp7[Defer]
```

## 4.4 McKinsey Three Horizons Innovation Balance

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph H1[Horizon 1 Core Business]
        h1a[Incremental Improvements]
        h1b[Existing Tech Optimization]
    end
    subgraph H2[Horizon 2 Emerging]
        h2a[Adjacent Tech Adoption]
        h2b[Pilots and MVPs]
    end
    subgraph H3[Horizon 3 Transformational]
        h3a[Disruptive R and D]
        h3b[Futuristic Tech Bets]
    end
    H1 --> H2
    H2 --> H3
```

## 4.5 Modular Integration Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    blkU[User Interface Layer] --> blkA[Application and Logic Layer]
    blkA --> blkD[Data and AI Layer]
    blkD --> blkC[Connectivity Layer]
    blkC --> blkP[Perception and Hardware Layer]
    blkP --> blkS[Sensors and IoT Devices]
    blkS --> blkR[Real World Environment]
    blkR --> blkS
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Block Diagram:** Data flows from the physical world (bottom) upward through perception, connectivity, intelligence, and application layers, ultimately reaching the user — and design feedback loops back downward to refine the system. This is the **canonical pattern** for any integrated emerging-tech product.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Q1. Define the Gartner Hype Cycle and list its five phases in order.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Gartner Hype Cycle is a graphical representation of the maturity, adoption, and social application of emerging technologies. Its five phases in order are:
1. **Innovation Trigger** — initial breakthrough and media interest.
2. **Peak of Inflated Expectations** — hype-driven over-expectation.
3. **Trough of Disillusionment** — interest wanes as implementations fail.
4. **Slope of Enlightenment** — practical use cases emerge.
5. **Plateau of Productivity** — mainstream adoption and stability.

*(Award: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for five phases in correct sequence.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q2. What is Technology Readiness Level (TRL)? Why is TRL 6 considered the minimum threshold for B.Tech prototype projects?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
TRL is a 1-to-9 scale used to assess the maturity of a technology, from basic principles (TRL 1) to proven operational deployment (TRL 9). TRL 6 represents "system/subsystem model or prototype demonstration in a relevant environment." For B.Tech projects, TRL 6 is the minimum threshold because it guarantees the technology has moved beyond laboratory validation and can be realistically demonstrated within the limited 6-month academic timeline, ensuring both feasibility and examiner credibility.

*(Award: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for TRL 6 reasoning.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model | Module 3]**

### **Question A (14 Marks):** 
*"A regional hospital wants to reduce patient wait times by 40%. As a design thinking team, propose how you would (a) navigate the technological landscape to identify suitable emerging technologies and (b) integrate at least three emerging technologies into a coherent solution. Justify your technology selection using TRL and Hype Cycle frameworks."*

#### **Part (a) — Navigating the Technological Landscape (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Define the Problem Space (1 mark):**
Using the POV template — *"Hospital administrators and patients need reduced wait times because current queuing systems ignore real-time patient acuity and resource availability."*

**Step 2 — Conduct Technology Sensing (2 marks):**
Scan emerging technologies via Gartner reports, IEEE Xplore, and healthcare journals. Candidate list: IoT wearables for vitals, Edge AI for triage prediction, Computer Vision for queue analysis, 5G for low-latency communication, Digital Twins for hospital simulation.

**Step 3 — Apply Hype Cycle Mapping (2 marks):**

| Technology | Hype Cycle Position | Strategic Verdict |
|------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| IoT Wearables | Plateau of Productivity | ✅ Mature, adopt |
| Edge AI | Slope of Enlightenment | ✅ Mature enough |
| Computer Vision | Peak of Inflated Expectations | ⚠️ Defer unless strong team |
| 5G | Trough of Disillusionment | ⚠️ Conditional |
| Digital Twins | Innovation Trigger | ❌ Reject (TRL too low) |

**Step 4 — TRL Filter (2 marks):**
IoT Wearables (TRL 9) ✅, Edge AI (TRL 7) ✅, 5G (TRL 8) ✅ — all above TRL 6 threshold.

#### **Part (b) — Integrating Three Emerging Technologies (7 Marks)**

**Step 5 — Convergence Architecture (3 marks):**
Integrate **IoT Wearables + Edge AI + 5G** into a Smart Triage System:

1. **IoT Wearables** continuously monitor patient vitals (heart rate, SpO₂) — Perception Layer.
2. **Edge AI** at the hospital gateway processes vitals in real-time and predicts deterioration risk — Intelligence Layer.
3. **5G connectivity** ensures sub-10 ms latency between ambulances, wearables, and the central dashboard — Connectivity Layer.

**Step 6 — Implementation Pathway (2 marks):**
Build a 3-month MVP using Raspberry Pi + Edge Impulse + mock 5G. Test with simulated patient data. Iterate via Design Thinking feedback loop.

**Step 7 — Justification (2 marks):**
The integrated system reduces wait times by dynamically re-prioritizing patients based on real-time vitals, achieving the 40% reduction goal while remaining TRL-compliant and cost-effective.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** *[Internal Choice Alternative]*
*"For a smart agriculture solution targeting smallholder farmers in Kerala, (a) build a weighted decision matrix to select two emerging technologies and (b) design a modular integration architecture showing how they work together. Apply McKinsey's Three Horizons framework to justify portfolio balance."*

#### **Part (a) — Weighted Decision Matrix (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Problem Definition (1 mark):**
*"Smallholder farmers in Kerala need affordable, real-time crop health monitoring because current solutions require expensive drones and high-bandwidth connectivity."*

**Step 2 — Criteria and Weights (2 marks):**
- Affordability: $w_1 = 0.30$
- Technical Feasibility: $w_2 = 0.25$
- Scalability: $w_3 = 0.20$
- Innovation: $w_4 = 0.15$
- Local Ecosystem Support: $w_5 = 0.10$
- *Sum check:* $0.30 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.10 = 1.00$ ✓

**Step 3 — Rating Table (2 marks):**

| Technology | Affordability | Feasibility | Scalability | Innovation | Ecosystem |
|------------|---------------|-------------|-------------|------------|-----------|
| IoT Soil Sensors | 9 | 8 | 9 | 6 | 8 |
| Edge AI on Mobile | 7 | 8 | 8 | 9 | 7 |
| Satellite Imagery | 5 | 6 | 9 | 8 | 4 |
| Blockchain Traceability | 3 | 5 | 6 | 8 | 3 |

**Step 4 — Compute Scores (2 marks):**

$$ S_{\text{IoT}} = (0.30)(9) + (0.25)(8) + (0.20)(9) + (0.15)(6) + (0.10)(8) $$

$$ S_{\text{IoT}} = 2.70 + 2.00 + 1.80 + 0.90 + 0.80 = 8.20 $$

$$ S_{\text{AI}} = (0.30)(7) + (0.25)(8) + (0.20)(8) + (0.15)(9) + (0.10)(7) $$

$$ S_{\text{AI}} = 2.10 + 2.00 + 1.60 + 1.35 + 0.70 = 7.75 $$

**Result:** IoT Sensors (8.20) ✅ and Edge AI (7.75) ✅ are selected. Blockchain and Satellite Imagery are rejected.

#### **Part (b) — Modular Architecture & Three Horizons (7 Marks)**

**Step 5 — Modular Architecture (3 marks):**
- **Layer 1 (Perception):** IoT Soil Sensors (moisture, pH, NPK).
- **Layer 2 (Edge Intelligence):** Mobile-based Edge AI for offline disease detection via TensorFlow Lite.
- **Layer 3 (Connectivity):** LoRaWAN (low-cost, long-range — fits Kerala's rural context).
- **Layer 4 (User Interface):** Multilingual Malayalam-English mobile app.

**Step 6 — Three Horizons Justification (2 marks):**

| Horizon | Allocation | Technology Choice |
|---------|-----------|-------------------|
| H1 (Core) | 60% | IoT Sensors (proven, immediate ROI) |
| H2 (Emerging) | 30% | Edge AI (pilots and MVPs) |
| H3 (Transformational) | 10% | Reserved for future quantum-sensor R&D |

**Step 7 — Concluding Integration Argument (2 marks):**
The IoT + Edge AI + LoRaWAN stack achieves convergence at low cost, respects Kerala's rural connectivity constraints, and balances innovation across all three horizons — a textbook example of integrated emerging-tech design thinking.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls That Cost Students 3–5 Marks:**
>
> 1. **Listing technologies without frameworks** — Saying "we used AI and IoT" without TRL or Hype Cycle justification will cap your answer at 60% of marks. Always anchor in a framework.
> 2. **Ignoring weights in the decision matrix** — Simply averaging ratings without applying weights shows shallow analytical depth.
> 3. **No convergence argument** — Picking two technologies and describing them separately is *not* integration. You must show how they interoperate (data flow, API, middleware).
> 4. **Forgetting the Design Thinking loop** — The examiner expects a feedback arrow from Prototype back to Empathy. Skip it, lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Using undated examples** — Always cite the year (e.g., "as of 2024") when referencing Hype Cycle position. Position changes annually.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

- ☐ **Three Layers**: Sensing → Evaluation → Integration. Memorize the order.
- ☐ **Six Core Frameworks**: Gartner Hype Cycle, TRL, Rogers' Diffusion, McKinsey 3 Horizons, S-Curve, Convergence Framework.
- ☐ **TRL 6** is the B.Tech minimum threshold for prototype adoption.
- ☐ **Weighted Decision Matrix formula**: $S_i = \sum w_j \cdot r_{ij}$, where weights sum to **1.0** and ratings are on a **1–10** scale.
- ☐ **Convergence ≠ Coexistence**: True integration requires demonstrated interoperation (API, data bus, shared protocol).
- ☐ **McKinsey 3 Horizons allocation**: 70/20/10 or 60/30/10 rule of thumb.
- ☐ **S-Curve equation**: $P(t) = \frac{L}{1 + e^{-k(t - t_0)}}$ — know the variables and meaning.
- ☐ **Always close with the Design Thinking feedback loop** in your answer diagram.
- ☐ **Cite the year** (2024) when discussing Hype Cycle positions.
- ☐ **Affordable, TRL-mature, ecosystem-supported** are the three filters for B.Tech project tech selection.

---

> **END OF NOTES — GZEST605 Module 3 | Technological Trends, Navigation & Integration**

topics.title: Feasibility studies - Technical, economic, market, operational, legal, and ethical feasibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Feasibility studies - Technical, economic, market, operational, legal, and ethical feasibility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Feasibility Study?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **Feasibility Study** is a comprehensive, systematic, and analytical evaluation conducted during the early phases of product development to determine whether a proposed project, product, or system is **viable, practical, justifiable, and worth pursuing**. It investigates the project's potential for success by assessing its ability to meet objectives within defined constraints of time, budget, technology, and human resources.

In the **Design Thinking and Product Development** framework, the feasibility study acts as a **decision-gate** between the *Ideation/Concept Generation* phase and the *Detailed Design & Prototyping* phase. It answers the critical question: *"Should we build this?"*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Planning a House Construction:**
> Imagine a family in Kerala deciding to build a new house. Before laying a single brick, they would naturally ask:
> - Can we **technically** build it on this plot (soil testing, layout)?
> - Can we **economically** afford it (loan eligibility, total cost)?
> - Is there a **market** for resale if needed (location value)?
> - Can we **operationally** run it daily (water, electricity, road access)?
> - Is it **legally** permissible (building permits, zoning laws)?
> - Is it **ethically** and socially acceptable (no harm to neighbors, environmental concerns)?
>
> A feasibility study is exactly this — but applied to a **product, software system, or engineering project** before committing massive resources.

### 1.2 Purpose of Feasibility Study in Product Development

The feasibility study serves **four primary objectives**:

1. **Risk Reduction** — Identifies potential failures *before* heavy investment.
2. **Resource Optimization** — Ensures efficient use of capital, time, and manpower.
3. **Strategic Alignment** — Confirms the project aligns with business goals and user needs.
4. **Stakeholder Confidence** — Provides objective evidence for investors and management approval.

### 1.3 The Six Pillars of Feasibility Study

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly mandates **six dimensions** of feasibility analysis. Every product idea must be evaluated across these lenses:

| # | Feasibility Type | Core Question |
|---|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Technical Feasibility** | *Can we build it with available technology?* |
| 2 | **Economic Feasibility** | *Is it financially justifiable?* |
| 3 | **Market Feasibility** | *Will customers buy it?* |
| 4 | **Operational Feasibility** | *Will it work in our organization?* |
| 5 | **Legal Feasibility** | *Is it compliant with the law?* |
| 6 | **Ethical Feasibility** | *Is it morally and socially responsible?* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always remember the acronym **"TEMOLE"** (Technical → Economic → Market → Operational → Legal → Ethical) for sequencing your answers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Feasibility Dimension

### 2.1.1 Technical Feasibility

**Definition:** Technical feasibility evaluates whether the proposed product can be **designed, developed, and implemented** using the currently available technology, tools, and engineering expertise.

**Key Evaluation Parameters:**
- Availability of required **hardware and software** infrastructure
- Maturity of underlying **technology stack**
- Skill set and **technical competency** of the development team
- **Scalability** potential of the architecture
- **Integration compatibility** with existing systems
- Performance benchmarks (speed, accuracy, reliability)

**Engineering Real-World Utility:**
In a startup designing an AI-based medical diagnosis tool, technical feasibility checks whether the team has GPU compute resources, access to medical datasets, ML model expertise, and whether the model's accuracy meets clinical-grade thresholds (>95%).

---

### 2.1.2 Economic (Financial) Feasibility

**Definition:** Economic feasibility — also called **financial feasibility** or **cost-benefit analysis** — determines whether the proposed product will generate enough **financial returns** to justify the investment.

**Key Evaluation Parameters:**
- Total **Capital Expenditure (CAPEX)** and **Operational Expenditure (OPEX)**
- **Return on Investment (ROI)**
- **Net Present Value (NPV)** of future cash flows
- **Payback Period**
- **Break-Even Point (BEP)**
- **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**

---

### 2.1.3 Market Feasibility

**Definition:** Market feasibility assesses whether there exists a **viable customer base** and **demand-pull** for the proposed product in the target market.

**Key Evaluation Parameters:**
- **Target Audience** identification and **TAM-SAM-SOM** analysis
- **Competitor landscape** and market share
- **Customer pain points** and willingness to pay
- Market **growth trends** and CAGR
- **Pricing strategy** and revenue model
- **Marketing and distribution channels**

---

### 2.1.4 Operational Feasibility

**Definition:** Operational feasibility examines whether the product will be **effectively adopted, used, and maintained** within the organization's day-to-day operations and end-user environment.

** Key Evaluation Parameters:**
- **User-friendliness** and learning curve
- Compatibility with **existing workflows**
- Availability of trained **personnel for operation**
- **Maintenance** and support infrastructure
- **Cultural and organizational** acceptance
- **Training requirements** for staff

---

### 2.1.5 Legal Feasibility

**Definition:** Legal feasibility ensures the product complies with all **applicable laws, regulations, intellectual property rights, and contractual obligations**.

**Key Evaluation Parameters:**
- **Intellectual Property (IP)** — patents, trademarks, copyrights
- **Data protection laws** (e.g., India's DPDP Act 2023, GDPR for EU)
- **Industry-specific regulations** (FDA for medical, BIS for electronics)
- **Licensing and permits**
- **Liability and warranty** considerations
- **Contractual obligations** with vendors and partners

---

### 2.1.6 Ethical Feasibility

**Definition:** Ethical feasibility evaluates whether the product adheres to **moral principles, social responsibility, environmental sustainability, and fairness standards** — even when not legally mandated.

**Key Evaluation Parameters:**
- **User privacy** and data consent
- **Algorithmic bias** and fairness
- **Environmental impact** (carbon footprint, e-waste)
- **Inclusivity** and accessibility
- **Transparency** and accountability
- **Long-term societal impact**

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Board Exams — Memorize these!**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description of Variables |
|---|--------------|----------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $ROI = \dfrac{Net\ Profit}{Total\ Investment} \times 100$ | Net Profit = Revenue − Cost; in **%** |
| 2 | **Payback Period** | $Payback\ Period = \dfrac{Initial\ Investment}{Annual\ Cash\ Inflow}$ | Result in **years** |
| 3 | **Break-Even Point (Units)** | $BEP_{units} = \dfrac{Fixed\ Costs}{Selling\ Price - Variable\ Cost\ per\ Unit}$ | Units to recover cost |
| 4 | **Break-Even Point (Revenue)** | $BEP_{revenue} = \dfrac{Fixed\ Costs}{1 - \dfrac{Variable\ Cost}{Revenue}}$ | In monetary units |
| 5 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - Initial\ Investment$ | $CF_t$ = Cash flow at time $t$; $r$ = discount rate |
| 6 | **Contribution Margin** | $CM = Selling\ Price - Variable\ Cost$ | Per unit contribution |
| 7 | **Profit Margin** | $Profit\ Margin = \dfrac{Net\ Profit}{Revenue} \times 100$ | In **%** |
| 8 | **Market Share** | $Market\ Share = \dfrac{Company\ Sales}{Total\ Market\ Sales} \times 100$ | In **%** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for Markdown:** In the formula table above, notice that absolute value bars are replaced with $\vert$ for safety. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` inside tables to avoid breaking syntax.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are the **backbone of product management** in industries ranging from automotive (Tata Motors evaluating EV production feasibility) to IT services (Infosys assessing new AI service lines) to startups (Kerala-based agri-tech startups evaluating drone-based farming solutions).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Economic Feasibility of a Smart Irrigation Product

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A Kerala-based agri-tech startup proposes a **Smart IoT-based Irrigation System** for small farmers. The following data is given:
> - Initial Investment (CAPEX): ₹20,00,000
> - Annual Operating Cost: ₹3,00,000
> - Annual Revenue from Subscriptions: ₹8,00,000
> - Project Life: 5 years
> - Discount Rate: 10%
>
> Annual Cash Inflow = Revenue − Operating Cost = ₹8,00,000 − ₹3,00,000 = **₹5,00,000**

---

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step ROI Calculation

$$
ROI = \frac{Net\ Profit\ (Annual)}{Total\ Investment} \times 100
$$

**Step 1:** Compute Net Profit per year.

$$
Net\ Profit = Revenue - Operating\ Cost = 8{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}00{,}000\ INR
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the ROI formula.

$$
ROI = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} \times 100
$$

**Step 3:** Final simplification.

$$
ROI = 0.25 \times 100 = 25\%
$$

**Interpretation:** A **25% annual ROI** is considered excellent for a social-impact agri-tech product.

---

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Payback Period Calculation

$$
Payback\ Period = \frac{Initial\ Investment}{Annual\ Cash\ Inflow}
$$

**Step 1:** Identify values.

$$
Initial\ Investment = 20{,}00{,}000;\quad Annual\ Cash\ Inflow = 5{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute and compute.

$$
Payback\ Period = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = 4\ years
$$

**Interpretation:** The startup will recover its full investment in **4 years**, leaving 1 year of pure profit within the 5-year project life.

---

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Break-Even Point Calculation

Given: Fixed Cost = ₹20,00,000, Selling Price per unit = ₹5,000, Variable Cost per unit = ₹2,000.

$$
BEP_{units} = \frac{Fixed\ Cost}{Selling\ Price - Variable\ Cost}
$$

**Step 1:** Compute contribution margin per unit.

$$
CM = 5{,}000 - 2{,}000 = 3{,}000\ INR\ per\ unit
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into BEP formula.

$$
BEP_{units} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{3{,}000}
$$

**Step 3:** Final value.

$$
BEP_{units} = 666.67 \approx 667\ units
$$

**Interpretation:** The startup must sell at least **667 units** to cover all fixed costs.

---

### 3.1.4 Step-by-Step NPV Calculation

$$
NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - Initial\ Investment
$$

**Step 1:** Compute discount factors for each year.

$$
\begin{aligned}
DF_1 &= \frac{1}{(1.10)^1} = 0.9091 \\
DF_2 &= \frac{1}{(1.10)^2} = 0.8264 \\
DF_3 &= \frac{1}{(1.10)^3} = 0.7513 \\
DF_4 &= \frac{1}{(1.10)^4} = 0.6830 \\
DF_5 &= \frac{1}{(1.10)^5} = 0.6209
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute present value of cash flows (CF = ₹5,00,000 each year).

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.9091 = 4{,}54{,}545 \\
PV_2 &= 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8264 = 4{,}13{,}223 \\
PV_3 &= 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7513 = 3{,}75{,}657 \\
PV_4 &= 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6830 = 3{,}41{,}507 \\
PV_5 &= 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6209 = 3{,}10{,}461
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Sum the present values.

$$
Total\ PV = 4{,}54{,}545 + 4{,}13{,}223 + 3{,}75{,}657 + 3{,}41{,}507 + 3{,}10{,}461
$$

$$
Total\ PV = 18{,}95{,}393\ INR
$$

**Step 4:** Subtract the initial investment.

$$
NPV = 18{,}95{,}393 - 20{,}00{,}000 = -1{,}04{,}607\ INR
$$

**Interpretation:** A **negative NPV (-₹1,04,607)** indicates the project, as designed, is **NOT financially viable** at a 10% discount rate. The startup must either:
- Increase subscription revenue to ₹6,00,000/year, or
- Reduce CAPEX, or
- Extend project life to 7 years.

---

## 3.2 Feasibility Evaluation Matrix (Analytical Tool)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** This weighted scoring matrix is the most common 14-mark question format.

| Feasibility Type | Weight (W) | Score (1–5) | Weighted Score = W × S |
|------------------|:----------:|:-----------:|:---------------------:|
| Technical        | 0.20       | 4           | 0.80                  |
| Economic         | 0.25       | 3           | 0.75                  |
| Market           | 0.20       | 5           | 1.00                  |
| Operational      | 0.15       | 4           | 0.60                  |
| Legal            | 0.10       | 5           | 0.50                  |
| Ethical          | 0.10       | 4           | 0.40                  |
| **Total**        | **1.00**   | —           | **4.05 / 5.00**      |

**Decision Rule:**
- **Total ≥ 3.5** → Project is **FEASIBLE** — proceed to detailed design.
- **Total 2.5 – 3.5** → Project is **MARGINAL** — refine before proceeding.
- **Total < 2.5** → Project is **NOT FEASIBLE** — reject or pivot.

In this example, score **4.05/5.00 → FEASIBLE**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Feasibility Study Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Project Idea / Concept"] --> B["Define Scope and Objectives"]
    B --> C["Conduct Preliminary Analysis"]
    C --> D["Evaluate Six Feasibility Dimensions"]
    D --> E1["Technical Feasibility"]
    D --> E2["Economic Feasibility"]
    D --> E3["Market Feasibility"]
    D --> E4["Operational Feasibility"]
    D --> E5["Legal Feasibility"]
    D --> E6["Ethical Feasibility"]
    E1 --> F["Aggregate Feasibility Report"]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    E6 --> F
    F --> G{"Feasibility Score"}
    G -->|"Score >= 3.5"| H["GO - Proceed to Design Phase"]
    G -->|"Score 2.5 to 3.5"| I["REFINE - Iterate the Concept"]
    G -->|"Score < 2.5"| J["STOP - Reject or Pivot"]
    H --> K["Detailed Design and Prototyping"]
    I --> B
    J --> L["Document Lessons Learned"]
```

## 4.2 Six Pillars of Feasibility — Hierarchical Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R["Feasibility Study Framework"] --> T["Technical Feasibility"]
    R --> Ec["Economic Feasibility"]
    R --> M["Market Feasibility"]
    R --> O["Operational Feasibility"]
    R --> L["Legal Feasibility"]
    R --> Et["Ethical Feasibility"]

    T --> T1["Hardware and Software Readiness"]
    T --> T2["Team Expertise"]
    T --> T3["Scalability and Performance"]

    Ec --> Ec1["ROI and NPV Analysis"]
    Ec --> Ec2["Payback Period"]
    Ec --> Ec3["Break-Even Point"]

    M --> M1["TAM SAM SOM Analysis"]
    M --> M2["Competitor Mapping"]
    M --> M3["Customer Validation"]

    O --> O1["Workflow Compatibility"]
    O --> O2["User Acceptance"]
    O --> O3["Maintenance Plan"]

    L --> L1["IPR and Patents"]
    L --> L2["Regulatory Compliance"]
    L --> L3["Data Protection Laws"]

    Et --> Et1["Privacy and Consent"]
    Et --> Et2["Environmental Impact"]
    Et --> Et3["Bias and Fairness"]
```

## 4.3 Decision Gate Logic — Go / Refine / Stop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Weighted Score Calculation"] --> B{"Score Range"}
    B -->|"0.0 to 2.4"| C["STOP Project"]
    B -->|"2.5 to 3.4"| D["REFINE Concept"]
    B -->|"3.5 to 5.0"| E["GO to Next Phase"]
    C --> C1["Archive Analysis"]
    C --> C2["Communicate to Stakeholders"]
    D --> D1["Iterate Design"]
    D --> D2["Re-evaluate Feasibility"]
    E --> E1["Allocate Resources"]
    E --> E2["Begin Prototyping"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q: Define feasibility study. List any four types of feasibility analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

A feasibility study is a systematic evaluation conducted to determine whether a proposed project or product is viable, practical, and worth pursuing in terms of available technology, finances, market demand, operations, law, and ethics.

The four types of feasibility analysis are:

1. **Technical Feasibility** — assesses technology readiness
2. **Economic Feasibility** — assesses financial viability
3. **Market Feasibility** — assesses customer demand
4. **Operational Feasibility** — assesses organizational readiness

*(Two additional types for full syllabus coverage: Legal Feasibility and Ethical Feasibility.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: Differentiate between market feasibility and operational feasibility.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Market Feasibility | Operational Feasibility |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------------|
| **Focus** | External — customers and demand | Internal — organization and users |
| **Core Question** | Will customers buy the product? | Can the organization operate it? |
| **Key Parameters** | TAM-SAM-SOM, competitor analysis, pricing | Workflow fit, user training, maintenance |
| **Outcome** | Validates revenue potential | Validates day-to-day usability |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` (14 Marks)

**Q: Explain the different types of feasibility studies with suitable examples. Discuss their significance in product development.**

**Model Answer Structure:**

**(a) Introduction to Feasibility Studies [3 marks]**

Feasibility study is the analytical phase of product development that determines whether a proposed idea is worth investing resources into. It acts as a **decision filter** between concept ideation and prototype development.

**(b) Detailed Explanation of Six Types [7 marks]**

1. **Technical Feasibility** — Evaluates if the product can be built with existing technology.
   - *Example:* A startup developing a VR-based education app must verify availability of VR headsets, Unity/Unreal engines, and 5G bandwidth.

2. **Economic Feasibility** — Evaluates if the product is financially viable.
   - *Example:* Calculating ROI, NPV, and payback period for a new electric vehicle model.

3. **Market Feasibility** — Evaluates market demand and customer acceptance.
   - *Example:* Tata Motors conducting market research before launching the Punch EV.

4. **Operational Feasibility** — Evaluates organizational readiness.
   - *Example:* A hospital implementing an EHR system must train staff and redesign workflows.

5. **Legal Feasibility** — Evaluates regulatory and IP compliance.
   - *Example:* A fintech app ensuring RBI compliance and PCI-DSS certification.

6. **Ethical Feasibility** — Evaluates moral and social responsibility.
   - *Example:* An AI hiring tool being audited for gender and caste bias.

**(c) Significance in Product Development [4 marks]**

- **Risk Mitigation** — Identifies failure points early.
- **Resource Efficiency** — Prevents wastage of capital on unviable ideas.
- **Stakeholder Buy-in** — Provides evidence-based justification.
- **Strategic Alignment** — Ensures alignment with business goals.
- **Improved Success Rate** — Industry data shows feasibility-tested projects have **40% higher success rate**.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**Q: A company proposes to launch a new mobile-based food delivery app with an initial investment of ₹15,00,000. The expected annual revenue is ₹6,00,000 and annual operating cost is ₹2,00,000. The project life is 5 years with a discount rate of 10%. Perform the economic feasibility analysis using ROI, Payback Period, Break-Even Point, and NPV. Assume selling price per order is ₹100 and variable cost per order is ₹40.**

**Model Answer Structure:**

**(a) ROI and Payback Period [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Compute Annual Cash Inflow**

$$
Annual\ Cash\ Inflow = 6{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}00{,}000\ INR
$$

**Step 2: ROI Calculation**

$$
ROI = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 26.67\%
$$

**Step 3: Payback Period Calculation**

$$
Payback\ Period = \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} = 3.75\ years
$$

**Interpretation:** The project yields a **26.67% annual ROI** and recovers its investment in **3.75 years**.

**(b) Break-Even Point and NPV [7 marks]**

**Step 1: BEP Calculation**

Fixed Cost = ₹15,00,000, Selling Price = ₹100, Variable Cost = ₹40.

$$
BEP_{units} = \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{100 - 40} = \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{60} = 25{,}000\ orders
$$

**Step 2: NPV Calculation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.9091 = 3{,}63{,}636 \\
PV_2 &= 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8264 = 3{,}30{,}578 \\
PV_3 &= 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7513 = 3{,}00{,}525 \\
PV_4 &= 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6830 = 2{,}73{,}205 \\
PV_5 &= 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6209 = 2{,}48{,}369 \\
Total\ PV &= 15{,}16{,}313 \\
NPV &= 15{,}16{,}313 - 15{,}00{,}000 = 16{,}313\ INR
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- **BEP = 25,000 orders** must be delivered to cover fixed costs.
- **NPV = ₹16,313 (Positive)** → Project is **marginally viable**. A slight increase in revenue or reduction in cost would make it strongly feasible.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ROI with Profit Margin** — ROI uses *Total Investment* in denominator; Profit Margin uses *Revenue*.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract Initial Investment in NPV** — Many students stop at sum of discounted cash flows.
> 3. **Wrong units in BEP** — BEP result is in **units sold**, not revenue (unless using the revenue formula).
> 4. **Omitting examples** — KTU examiners award marks for **real-world examples** in long answers.
> 5. **Listing without explaining** — Merely listing the six types without brief explanation loses 4–5 marks.
> 6. **No flowchart/diagram** — A simple block diagram in long answers earns 1–2 extra marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ Feasibility Study = **Gate-keeper** between Ideation and Detailed Design.
- ✅ **Six Types** — Technical, Economic, Market, Operational, Legal, Ethical (Mnemonic: **TEMOLE**).
- ✅ **Technical Feasibility** answers: *"Can we build it?"*
- ✅ **Economic Feasibility** answers: *"Should we build it for profit?"* — uses ROI, NPV, Payback, BEP.
- ✅ **Market Feasibility** answers: *"Will customers buy it?"* — uses TAM-SAM-SOM.
- ✅ **Operational Feasibility** answers: *"Will our team run it daily?"* — focuses on user adoption.
- ✅ **Legal Feasibility** answers: *"Are we allowed to build it?"* — covers IP, compliance, data laws.
- ✅ **Ethical Feasibility** answers: *"Is it the right thing to build?"* — covers privacy, bias, sustainability.
- ✅ **Weighted Scoring Matrix** is the standard KTU evaluation tool.
- ✅ **Decision Rule** — ≥ 3.5 Go | 2.5–3.4 Refine | < 2.5 Stop.
- ✅ **ROI Formula** — (Net Profit / Investment) × 100.
- ✅ **NPV > 0** → Project is financially viable.
- ✅ **Payback Period** shorter than project life → Feasible.
- ✅ Always include a **flowchart or diagram** for full marks in 14-mark answers.
- ✅ Always provide **real-world examples** to score beyond average.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ideation session - Techniques and tips
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ideation session - Techniques and tips

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Ideation Session** is a structured, collaborative, and time-bound design thinking activity in which a multidisciplinary team generates a large volume of diverse, innovative, and actionable ideas aimed at solving a previously defined problem statement (the "How Might We" question emerging from the Define/Empathize stages of the Design Thinking framework).

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for GZEST605, ideation is treated as the **third core phase of the Stanford d.school's 5-stage Design Thinking model** (Empathize → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test), and it is the only stage where the explicit goal is *quantity over quality*, *divergent thinking over convergent judgment*, and *postponement of critical evaluation*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**
> "Ideation is the process of generating a broad set of ideas on a given topic — with no attempt to judge, evaluate, or filter them. The goal is to push beyond the obvious solutions and explore a wide solution space."

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of an Ideation Session

| Pillar | Description | KTU Examiner's Keyword |
|---|---|---|
| **Diverge** | Expand the solution space; aim for 100+ raw ideas per session | "Go for quantity" |
| **Defer Judgment** | No criticism, no "yes-but", no feasibility filtering during generation | "Defer judgment" |
| **Build on Ideas** | Use "Yes, and..." instead of "No, because..." | "Hitchhike / Piggyback" |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Cooking Pot of Ideas" 🍲**
>
> Imagine you are hosting a family dinner. To make a good curry, you cannot put just one spice into the pot. You need to throw in *many* ingredients, some weird, some traditional, some experimental — even chocolate, even coffee — and only *after* the pot is full do you taste, season, and refine.
>
> The **Ideation Session is exactly that cooking pot**. You throw every possible idea in — even silly, even impossible — because one "weird" idea might combine with another "ordinary" idea to create the final recipe. If you judge too early (taste while still adding), the curry never forms.

## 1.4 Why Ideation Sessions are *Sessions* and not Meetings

A regular meeting focuses on *deciding*. An ideation session focuses on *creating*. This distinction is fundamental and is often tested in KTU exams.

**Key Difference:**

$$\text{Meeting} = \text{Convergence} + \text{Decision-Making} + \text{Single Winner}$$

$$\text{Ideation Session} = \text{Divergence} + \text{Creation} + \text{Many Co-existing Ideas}$$

## 1.5 Pre-Requisites Before an Ideation Session Begins

Before an ideation session can be conducted (KTU 2024 Module 3 explicitly mandates this), the following outputs from earlier stages must be ready:

1. **Empathy Maps** (from Empathize stage)
2. **Point-of-View (POV) Statement** (from Define stage)
3. **How-Might-We (HMW) Questions** — typically 3 to 5 framed HMWs

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse *Brainstorming* with *Ideation Session*. Brainstorming is **one technique** used *within* an ideation session. Ideation is the **broader phase** that may include brainstorming, SCAMPER, Mind Mapping, Worst Possible Idea, and many other techniques.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Anatomy of an Ideation Session

An ideation session, as prescribed in the KTU 2024 Design Thinking curriculum, follows a strict 6-step operational sequence:

### Step 1: Frame the Problem (Re-confirm the HMW)
The facilitator re-states the **How-Might-We question** written on a visible board. All ideation is anchored to this single question. Without a clear HMW, the session devolves into unstructured chatter.

### Step 2: Set the Rules of Engagement
Five golden rules are usually established:

$$\text{Rule Set} = \{\text{Defer Judgment},\ \text{Quantity},\ \text{Build on Ideas},\ \text{Stay on Topic},\ \text{Visualize}\}$$

### Step 3: Warm-Up Exercise (5–10 min)
A short, unrelated creative warm-up is run to "prime" divergent thinking. Examples:
- "List 30 uses of a brick in 4 minutes"
- "Sketch your morning routine using only triangles"

### Step 4: The Generation Phase (30–60 min)
The bulk of the session. Multiple techniques (Section 2.3) are used to generate ideas. Sticky notes / digital equivalents are used liberally.

### Step 5: The Clustering / Synthesis Phase
Raw ideas are **clustered thematically** using Affinity Diagrams. This converts quantity into navigable structure.

### Step 6: The Voting / Selection Phase
Each participant is given **3 dots / votes** to mark the most promising ideas. High-vote ideas proceed to the Prototype stage.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step — Pedagogical Reasoning

| Step | Cognitive Purpose | Bloom's Taxonomy Level |
|---|---|---|
| Frame the Problem | Constrains the search space to prevent off-topic ideation | Remembering / Understanding |
| Set Rules | Removes social anxiety and idea-killing behavior | Applying |
| Warm-Up | Disrupts mental fixation (functional fixedness) | Applying |
| Generation | Activates divergent lateral thinking | Creating |
| Clustering | Initiates the *start* of convergent thinking | Analyzing |
| Voting | Filters ideas by group consensus & impact | Evaluating |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Ideation Techniques — Master Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important study artifact** for this topic. KTU 2024 examiners routinely ask 14-mark questions asking students to *"Explain any 4 ideation techniques"* or *"Compare brainstorming and SCAMPER"*.

| # | Technique | Origin / Authority | Core Trigger | Best Used When | KTU Keyword |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Brainstorming** | Alex Osborn (1953) | "Generate as many ideas as possible" | Wide, open-ended problems | Quantity over quality |
| 2 | **Mind Mapping** | Tony Buzan (1970s) | Central topic → radial branches | Visual learners; complex domains | Radial association |
| 3 | **SCAMPER** | Bob Eberle (1971) | 7 letter prompts (Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put to other use, Eliminate, Reverse) | Improving an existing product | 7-letter mnemonic |
| 4 | **Worst Possible Idea** | d.school Stanford | "How could we make this WORSE?" | Breaking creative blocks | Inversion technique |
| 5 | **Sketching / Visual Ideation** | d.school Stanford | "Draw, don't write" | UI/UX, spatial, product design | Visual fluency |
| 6 | **Storyboarding** | Walt Disney Studios | Sequential frames of user journey | Service design, experience design | Sequential visualization |
| 7 | **Analogies / Biomimicry** | Biologists + Designers | "How does nature solve this?" | Engineering, sustainability problems | Nature as mentor |
| 8 | **6 Thinking Hats** | Edward de Bono (1985) | 6 colored hats = 6 thinking modes | Group decisions, balanced view | Parallel thinking |
| 9 | **How-Might-We Reframing** | d.school Stanford | Restate problem as opportunity | Post-Empathy, pre-Ideation | Reframing |
| 10 | **Bodystorming** | d.school Stanford | Physically act out the scenario | Physical products, environments | Embodied simulation |

## 2.4 The SCAMPER Formula — Deeper Structure

SCAMPER is the most *algorithm-like* technique and is highly testable. The framework is:

$$I_{\text{new}} = f_{\text{SCAMPER}}(I_{\text{existing}})$$

Where each letter generates a specific cognitive prompt:

$$\begin{aligned}
\textbf{S} &\rightarrow \text{Substitute:} \quad \text{What can be substituted?} \\
\textbf{C} &\rightarrow \text{Combine:} \quad \text{What can be merged?} \\
\textbf{A} &\rightarrow \text{Adapt:} \quad \text{What can be adjusted?} \\
\textbf{M} &\rightarrow \text{Modify:} \quad \text{What can be made bigger/smaller?} \\
\textbf{P} &\rightarrow \text{Put to other use:} \quad \text{Can it serve another purpose?} \\
\textbf{E} &\rightarrow \text{Eliminate:} \quad \text{What can be removed?} \\
\textbf{R} &\rightarrow \text{Reverse:} \quad \text{What can be flipped or reordered?}
\end{aligned}$$

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

| Industry | Ideation Technique Most Used | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Software / IT** | SCAMPER, Sketching | Feature upgrades of existing apps |
| **Automotive** | Biomimicry, Worst Possible Idea | Lightweight chassis, crash safety |
| **Healthcare** | Storyboarding, Bodystorming | Patient journey redesign |
| **Furniture** | SCAMPER (Modify), Mind Mapping | Modular furniture design |
| **Architecture** | Sketching, Analogies | Sustainable building facades |

## 2.6 Time Boxing Math (Useful for KTU Numerical Questions)

> [!NOTE]
> Although this course is qualitative, KTU sometimes asks 3-mark questions involving basic time/resource allocation. The standard formula is:

$$N_{\text{ideas}} = R_{\text{rate}} \times T_{\text{time}} \times P_{\text{participants}}$$

**Example (KTU-Style):** If a team of 5 generates 8 ideas per minute on average, in a 30-minute session, total raw ideas produced:

$$N_{\text{ideas}} = 8 \times 30 \times 5 = 1200 \text{ ideas}$$

This calculation is a KTU-favorite and may appear in **Part A** numerical questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough — Conducting a Brainstorming Session

Because *Brainstorming* is the most frequently tested technique in KTU 2024 papers, here is a complete 7-step procedure that students must be able to reproduce verbatim in the exam hall.

### Step-by-Step Procedure (Brainstorming)

**Step 1: Define the HMW Question**
The facilitator writes the question on a board.

$$\text{HMW: How might we reduce student food waste in campus canteens?}$$

**Step 2: Appoint a Facilitator and a Scribe**
- Facilitator: Manages time, energy, and rules
- Scribe: Captures every idea verbatim (no editing)

**Step 3: State the 4 Rules Clearly**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Rule 1:} \quad &\text{Defer Judgment — no criticism, no evaluation} \\
\text{Rule 2:} \quad &\text{Quantity Counts — aim for 50+ ideas} \\
\text{Rule 3:} \quad &\text{Build on Ideas — use "Yes, and..."} \\
\text{Rule 4:} \quad &\text{Encourage Wild Ideas — the wilder, the better}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Run a 2-minute Warm-Up**
Example: "List 25 uses of a paperclip."

**Step 5: Begin the Generation Phase (e.g., 30 minutes)**

**Step 6: Capture ideas on Sticky Notes / Whiteboard**
Each idea = one sticky note, max 6 words.

**Step 7: Cluster & Vote**
- Group similar ideas into themes (Affinity Mapping)
- Each member gets 3 dot-votes
- Top 3–5 ideas proceed to prototyping

## 3.2 Exhaustive Walkthrough — SCAMPER Applied to an Existing Product

Let's apply SCAMPER to a **regular school backpack** to demonstrate to KTU examiners how to articulate the technique in an answer.

**Starting Product:** Standard school backpack with two straps, one main compartment.

$$\begin{aligned}
\textbf{S — Substitute:} \quad &\text{Replace cotton with recycled PET fabric.} \\
\textbf{C — Combine:} \quad &\text{Combine the backpack with a foldable raincoat.} \\
\textbf{A — Adapt:} \quad &\text{Adapt the strap design from a hiking rucksack (padded waist belt).} \\
\textbf{M — Modify:} \quad &\text{Modify the size — make it modular (expandable for books, contractible for lunch).} \\
\textbf{P — Put to other use:} \quad &\text{Use it as a portable solar charging station.} \\
\textbf{E — Eliminate:} \quad &\text{Eliminate zippers; use magnetic closures instead.} \\
\textbf{R — Reverse:} \quad &\text{Reverse the opening — top-loading to side-loading for easier access.}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When writing SCAMPER in a 14-mark KTU answer, students must explain **each of the 7 letters explicitly**. A common mistake is to write only 4 or 5 letters — this loses 2–3 marks.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Brainstorming vs. Mind Mapping vs. SCAMPER

> [!NOTE]
> Comparison questions of this kind are a **guaranteed 14-marker** in KTU 2024 papers. Memorize this matrix.

| Comparison Parameter | Brainstorming | Mind Mapping | SCAMPER |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type of Thinking** | Divergent | Divergent + Associative | Transformative (acts on existing) |
| **Output Volume** | Very High (50–200) | Medium (1 large map) | Medium (7–21) |
| **Format** | Linear list / sticky notes | Radial / tree structure | Prompted-by-letter |
| **Input Required** | HMW question | Central concept | Existing product/idea |
| **Time Required** | 20–45 min | 15–30 min | 10–20 min |
| **Group Size** | 4–12 | 1–4 | 1–4 |
| **Best for** | Open problems | Complex multi-domain topics | Product improvement |
| **Risk** | Idea redundancy | Tangent drift | Limited to 7 prompts |
| **Inventor** | Alex Osborn (1953) | Tony Buzan (1974) | Bob Eberle (1971) |

## 3.4 The 6 Thinking Hats — Detailed Expansion

De Bono's 6 Hats method is structured parallel thinking. Each hat forces a specific cognitive mode:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{White Hat} &\rightarrow \text{Facts, data, information (objective)} \\
\text{Red Hat} &\rightarrow \text{Emotions, intuition, gut feeling} \\
\text{Black Hat} &\rightarrow \text{Cautious, critical, risk-focused} \\
\text{Yellow Hat} &\rightarrow \text{Optimistic, benefits-focused} \\
\text{Green Hat} &\rightarrow \text{Creative, new ideas, alternatives} \\
\text{Blue Hat} &\rightarrow \text{Process control, meta-thinking}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> A common student error is to confuse *Yellow Hat* (positive benefits) with *Green Hat* (creative new ideas). Yellow evaluates existing ideas positively; Green generates new ones.

## 3.5 Convergence-Divergence Cycle in Ideation

Design Thinking's iconic visualization:

$$\text{Diverge} \longrightarrow \text{Converge} \longrightarrow \text{Diverge} \longrightarrow \text{Converge}$$

In a single 60-minute session:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Minute 0–5:} \quad &\text{Converge (clarify HMW)} \\
\text{Minute 5–40:} \quad &\text{Diverge (generate ideas)} \\
\text{Minute 40–55:} \quad &\text{Converge (cluster, vote)} \\
\text{Minute 55–60:} \quad &\text{Diverge (reframe, restart if stuck)}
\end{aligned}$$

This **double diamond** cycle is a **guaranteed high-yield KTU 2024 topic**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Anatomy of an Ideation Session

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Empathize and Define outputs] --> B[Frame the HMW Question]
    B --> C[Set Rules: Defer Judgment, Quantity, Build]
    C --> D[Warm-Up Exercise 5 to 10 min]
    D --> E[Generate Ideas 30 to 60 min]
    E --> F[Cluster via Affinity Diagram]
    F --> G[Dot Voting 3 votes per member]
    G --> H{Top Ideas Selected}
    H -->|Yes| I[Move to Prototype Stage]
    H -->|No| J[Reframe HMW and Re Ideate]
    J --> B
    I --> K[End of Ideation Session]
    style A fill:#E0F2F1,stroke:#00695C,color:#000000
    style B fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000000
    style C fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000000
    style D fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000000
    style E fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000000
    style F fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000000
    style G fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000000
    style H fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000000
    style I fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000000
    style J fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000000
    style K fill:#E0F2F1,stroke:#00695C,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Taxonomy of Ideation Techniques

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Ideation Techniques))
    Verbal Techniques
      Brainstorming
      Brainwriting 6 3 5
      Round Robin
      Silent Brainstorming
    Visual Techniques
      Mind Mapping
      Sketching
      Storyboarding
      Bodystorming
    Structured Prompts
      SCAMPER
      Six Thinking Hats
      How Might We Reframing
    Inversion Techniques
      Worst Possible Idea
      Reverse Assumptions
    Cross Domain Techniques
      Biomimicry
      Analogies
      Synectics
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — SCAMPER Letter Expansion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Existing Product or Idea] --> S[Substitute: What if we replace X with Y]
    P --> C1[Combine: What if we merge with Z]
    P --> A1[Adapt: What if we borrow from domain W]
    P --> M1[Modify: What if we change size or shape]
    P --> P1[Put to Other Use: What if used for purpose V]
    P --> E1[Eliminate: What if we remove component U]
    P --> R1[Reverse: What if we flip the order]
    S --> OUT[7 New Improved Ideas]
    C1 --> OUT
    A1 --> OUT
    M1 --> OUT
    P1 --> OUT
    E1 --> OUT
    R1 --> OUT
    style P fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF6F00,color:#000000
```
