```mermaid
    style OUT fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000000
    style S fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000000
    style C1 fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000000
    style A1 fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000000
    style M1 fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000000
    style P1 fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000000
    style E1 fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000000
    style R1 fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000000
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Convergence-Divergence Pulse of Ideation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SessionBlock[60 Minute Ideation Session]
        direction LR
        A1[Diverge: Clarify HMW] --> B1[Converge: Lock the Question]
        B1 --> C1[Diverge: Generate Wildly]
        C1 --> D1[Converge: Cluster and Vote]
        D1 --> E1[Diverge: Build on Top 3]
    end
    style A1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000000
    style B1 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000000
    style C1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000000
    style D1 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000000
    style E1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define an Ideation Session in Design Thinking. List any two rules to be followed during an ideation session.**

**Model Answer:**

An **Ideation Session** is a structured, time-bound, collaborative workshop in which a multidisciplinary team generates a large quantity of diverse and innovative ideas in response to a previously defined "How-Might-We" question, with the explicit goal of exploring a wide solution space **before** any judgment or filtering is applied.

**Two rules of an ideation session:**

1. **Defer Judgment:** No criticism, evaluation, or "yes-but" responses are allowed during the generation phase. All ideas, even wild or impractical ones, are recorded without filtering.
2. **Quantity over Quality:** The team must aim to produce a very high volume of ideas (typically 50–200+ in a single session) because a larger pool statistically increases the probability of breakthrough solutions.

*(Acceptable alternate rules: Build on ideas using "Yes, and…", Stay on topic, Encourage wild ideas, Visualize every idea on a sticky note.)*

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Expand the acronym SCAMPER. Why is it called a "structured" ideation technique?**

**Model Answer:**

SCAMPER stands for:

$$\text{SCAMPER} = \{\text{Substitute},\ \text{Combine},\ \text{Adapt},\ \text{Modify},\ \text{Put to other use},\ \text{Eliminate},\ \text{Reverse}\}$$

SCAMPER is called a **structured** ideation technique because, unlike free-flow brainstorming, the participant is **guided by 7 fixed letter-based prompts**. The thinker is forced to apply a specific cognitive lens to an existing product or idea, ensuring a comprehensive, repeatable, and exhaustive exploration of modifications. Because the structure is mechanical (7 prompts), the technique is also highly suitable for individuals working alone and for producing an evenly distributed idea set.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Brainstorming technique** of ideation. Describe its rules, procedure, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Mind Mapping technique** and compare it with Brainstorming using any four parameters. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — Brainstorming Technique (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Brainstorming, introduced by **Alex Osborn in 1953**, is a group-based divergent ideation technique in which participants verbally generate a large number of ideas in response to a single How-Might-We question, with explicit rules preventing judgment.

**Rules of Brainstorming (2 marks):**
1. **Defer Judgment** — No criticism or evaluation during the session.
2. **Quantity Counts** — Aim for as many ideas as possible.
3. **Build on Ideas** — Use "Yes, and…" to extend peers' ideas.
4. **Encourage Wild Ideas** — Wild ideas often trigger practical breakthroughs.

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Frame the HMW question.
2. Appoint a facilitator and a scribe.
3. Warm-up exercise (2 min).
4. Generate ideas (20–45 min).
5. Cluster via Affinity Diagram.
6. Dot-vote to select top ideas.

**Advantages (1 mark):** Generates high volume, leverages group synergy, breaks conventional thinking, equalizes participation.

**Limitations (1 mark):** Production blocking (waiting turn), social loafing, dominance by extroverts, idea-fixation on first solutions.

#### Model Solution to (b) — Mind Mapping vs. Brainstorming (7 marks)

**Mind Mapping Explanation (3 marks):** Mind Mapping, developed by **Tony Buzan in the 1970s**, is a visual-radial ideation technique in which a central concept is written at the center of a page, and associated ideas branch out organically in all directions using keywords, colors, and images. The non-linear structure mirrors the brain's natural associative thinking and allows single participants to generate 30–100 related ideas in 15–30 minutes.

**Comparison using 4 parameters (4 marks — 1 per parameter):**

| Parameter | Brainstorming | Mind Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| **Format** | Linear list / sticky notes | Radial / tree structure |
| **Best for** | Open-ended, broad problems | Complex multi-domain topics |
| **Group Use** | Highly group-oriented (4–12 people) | Works best individually or in pairs |
| **Output Volume** | Very high (50–200 ideas) | Medium (1 large map with 30–100 nodes) |

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain the **SCAMPER technique** of ideation. List all 7 prompts with one example each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Worst Possible Idea** technique and the **6 Thinking Hats** technique. Why are both considered "anti-blocking" techniques? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — SCAMPER with Example (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):** SCAMPER, formulated by **Bob Eberle (1971)**, is a structured ideation technique that applies 7 letter-based prompts to an existing product, service, or idea, generating 7 distinct streams of improvement.

**Applied Example: A Standard Electric Kettle** (6 marks — roughly 1 mark per letter):

$$\begin{aligned}
\textbf{S — Substitute:} &\quad \text{Replace the metal body with bamboo composite.} \\
\textbf{C — Combine:} &\quad \text{Combine the kettle with a built-in tea infuser.} \\
\textbf{A — Adapt:} &\quad \text{Adapt smartphone app-control technology for remote boiling.} \\
\textbf{M — Modify:} &\quad \text{Modify the spout to a wide-mouth pour for easier cleaning.} \\
\textbf{P — Put to Other Use:} &\quad \text{Use the kettle as a humidifier by adding an atomizer.} \\
\textbf{E — Eliminate:} &\quad \text{Eliminate the power cord by adding induction heating.} \\
\textbf{R — Reverse:} &\quad \text{Reverse the heating — cool liquids faster using a Peltier chip.}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Model Solution to (b) — Worst Possible Idea + 6 Hats (7 marks)

**Worst Possible Idea (2.5 marks):** Originated at the **Stanford d.school**, this technique deliberately inverts the brainstorming goal. Participants are asked: *"How might we make this problem worse?"* By exploring the absolute worst, impractical, unethical, or absurd solutions, participants break through creative blocks and surface hidden assumptions. The humor and absurdity lower psychological defensiveness, often surfacing "good" ideas as the inverse of a deliberately bad one.

**Example:** HMW reduce exam anxiety? Worst Ideas → 1) Tell students results are public, 2) Conduct exams at 3 AM. **Inverse of these** → private result display, exams at student-chosen slots. Both are real, viable ideas.

**6 Thinking Hats (3.5 marks):** Edward de Bono's parallel-thinking method. The group wears one "hat" (cognitive mode) at a time:

$$\text{White} = \text{Facts} \quad \vert \quad \text{Red} = \text{Emotions} \quad \vert \quad \text{Black} = \text{Risks}$$
$$\text{Yellow} = \text{Benefits} \quad \vert \quad \text{Green} = \text{Creativity} \quad \vert \quad \text{Blue} = \text{Process}$$

**Why both are anti-blocking (1 mark):** Both techniques deliberately break the *functional fixedness* that prevents traditional brainstorming. Worst Possible Idea bypasses the psychological fear of proposing "bad" ideas; 6 Hats prevents conversational conflict by allowing only one thinking mode at a time, ensuring every participant processes the problem from the same angle.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here):**
> 1. **Confusing Brainstorming with Ideation** — Brainstorming is *one* technique; Ideation is the *phase*. Always clarify this in your introduction.
> 2. **Omitting the 7 SCAMPER letters** — KTU strict grading: if you list only 4–5 letters, you lose 2–3 marks. Write all 7.
> 3. **Forgetting the HMW prerequisite** — Always state: *"Before ideation, a clear How-Might-We question is required."*
> 4. **Mixing Yellow and Green Hats** — Yellow = *evaluate positively*; Green = *generate new*. Examiners catch this.
> 5. **No real example** — For any technique, write at least 1 application example. Without it, the answer is incomplete.
> 6. **Skipping advantages/limitations** — Most 7-mark questions explicitly ask. Always include both.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 60-Second Revision List — Memorize This:**

- **Ideation** = Divergent generation phase; **3rd** stage of Design Thinking.
- **Brainstorming** is a *technique*; **Ideation** is a *phase*. Do not equate them.
- **5 Core Rules of Ideation:** Defer Judgment, Quantity, Build on Ideas, Stay on Topic, Visualize.
- **Always begin** with a clear **How-Might-We (HMW)** question.
- **SCAMPER** has exactly **7 letters**; remember with mnemonic: *"Some Cats Always Meow, Purring Extremely Rarely."*
- **6 Thinking Hats:** White (data), Red (feeling), Black (caution), Yellow (optimism), Green (creativity), Blue (control).
- **Worst Possible Idea** = Inversion technique; breaks creative block.
- **Mind Mapping** = radial, visual, best for individuals.
- **Storyboarding** = sequential frames, best for service design.
- **Bodystorming** = physical enactment, best for spatial/physical products.
- **Biomimicry** = nature as design mentor, best for engineering problems.
- **Dot-voting** is the standard KTU-recommended convergence method.
- **Time-boxing formula:** $N_{\text{ideas}} = R \times T \times P$ — keep it ready for numerical 3-markers.
- **Double Diamond** model applies: Diverge → Converge → Diverge → Converge.
- **Alex Osborn (1953)** → Brainstorming. **Tony Buzan (1974)** → Mind Mapping. **Bob Eberle (1971)** → SCAMPER. **Edward de Bono (1985)** → 6 Hats. Memorize these names and years for 1-mark sub-questions.
- **Output goal:** **100+ raw ideas** in a 60-min session is considered healthy.
- **Transition:** Selected ideas flow to the **Prototype** stage — never skip prototyping after ideation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proof of Concept (PoC): Setting objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Proof of Concept (PoC): Setting objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🧭 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Proof of Concept (PoC)** is a rigorous, evidence-based demonstration undertaken in the early stages of a product development lifecycle to **validate the technical feasibility**, **practical viability**, and **functional soundness** of a proposed idea, design, or theoretical concept before committing significant resources toward full-scale development.

Within the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for *Design Thinking and Product Development*, the PoC sits at the critical intersection of **ideation** and **prototyping**, acting as a structured checkpoint that answers the fundamental question:

> *"Can this idea actually be built, and will it work in a real-world context?"*

**Setting Objectives** for a Proof of Concept is the deliberate, structured process of defining **specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound (SMART)** goals that the PoC must satisfy. These objectives act as the **acceptance criteria** against which the PoC is judged successful or unsuccessful.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> In the context of the Design Thinking process, "Setting Objectives" for a PoC is NOT the same as the product's final business goals. PoC objectives are *technical validation milestones* — they focus on demonstrating feasibility, not commercial success.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a chef who has invented a new recipe in their head — a fusion of South Indian dosa and Italian pizza. Before investing in a commercial kitchen, hiring staff, and printing menus, the chef does a **small test batch** in a home kitchen.

That test batch is the **Proof of Concept**.

Now, the chef doesn't just randomly cook. Before testing, they set objectives:
- *"Will the batter hold the toppings without tearing?"* → **Functional feasibility**
- *"Will it taste good within 5 minutes of preparation?"* → **User acceptance**
- *"Can the ingredients be sourced locally under ₹100?"* → **Economic feasibility**

Just like the chef, a product designer sets PoC objectives to **answer specific, narrow questions** before going big. If the test dosa-pizza works, great — full development begins. If it fails, the chef learns *why* and iterates **without burning massive resources**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:**
> A PoC is a *small, controlled experiment* to prove an idea isn't just a daydream. **Setting objectives** means writing down *exactly what success looks like* for that experiment — what must be true, by when, and how we will know.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Standardized for KTU 2024

| Term | Definition | KTU Context |
|------|------------|-------------|
| **PoC** | Proof of Concept | Early-stage feasibility validation |
| **MVP** | Minimum Viable Product | Smallest version with core features |
| **Prototype** | Working model for testing | Closer to final product than PoC |
| **Acceptance Criteria** | Pre-defined conditions for success | Direct output of "Setting Objectives" |
| **Feasibility** | Practical possibility of execution | Technical + Economic + Operational |
| **Scope Creep** | Uncontrolled expansion of objectives | Key risk during PoC planning |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Many students confuse PoC with Prototype. Remember — a **PoC validates the idea**, a **Prototype validates the design**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 The Strategic Role of PoC in Design Thinking

In the KTU 2024 prescribed Design Thinking framework (typically mapped as **Empathize → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test**), the Proof of Concept emerges during the transition between **Ideation** and **Prototyping**. It acts as a **technical risk filter**.

### 2.1.1 Why PoC is Non-Negotiable

- **Resource Protection:** A failed full-scale product can cost **lakhs to crores**; a failed PoC costs a fraction.
- **Stakeholder Buy-in:** Investors and management committees demand tangible evidence — not PowerPoint promises.
- **Technical De-risking:** Identifies unknown unknowns (latent technical barriers) early.
- **Decision Gate:** Provides a data-driven **go / no-go** signal for further investment.

---

## 2.2 The "Setting Objectives" Process — Deconstructed

Setting objectives for a PoC is a **5-stage structured methodology**. Each stage must be completed before moving to the next.

### **Stage 1: Identify the Core Hypothesis**
Every PoC exists to test a single (or small set of) **hypothesis**. The hypothesis is the statement of *belief* that the team holds about the proposed solution.

> Example: *"An AI-based chatbot can resolve 70% of customer queries without human intervention."*

### **Stage 2: Define Success Metrics (KPIs)**
The objective must declare **how** the hypothesis will be measured. These are the **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)** — quantitative or qualitative benchmarks.

> Example KPIs: *accuracy %, response time, user satisfaction score, cost per query*.

### **Stage 3: Apply the SMART Framework**
Every PoC objective must pass the **SMART** test:

| Letter | Stands For | Question It Answers |
|:------:|:----------:|:-------------------:|
| **S** | Specific | *What exactly will be tested?* |
| **M** | Measurable | *How will success be quantified?* |
| **A** | Achievable | *Is it realistic given current resources?* |
| **R** | Relevant | *Does it align with the core product vision?* |
| **T** | Time-bound | *By when must the result be available?* |

### **Stage 4: Establish Scope Boundaries**
Explicitly state **what is in-scope** and **what is out-of-scope**. This prevents **scope creep**, the #1 killer of PoC projects.

> Example: *"In-scope: text-based queries in English. Out-of-scope: voice input, regional languages, payment integration."*

### **Stage 5: Define Stop / Go / Iterate Criteria**
The objective must predefine the **decision logic** that follows the PoC:

- ✅ **Go:** All KPIs met → proceed to full prototype
- 🟡 **Iterate:** Partial success → refine hypothesis, run PoC v2
- ❌ **No-Go:** KPIs not met → abandon or pivot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:**
> In viva and 14-mark questions, students are expected to explicitly mention **SMART criteria** and the **Go / Iterate / No-Go decision framework**. Omitting these signals superficial understanding.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Framework Cheat Sheet

| Framework / Tool | Purpose | When Applied in PoC | Key Output |
|:-----------------|:--------|:-------------------:|:-----------|
| **SMART Objectives** | Structuring the PoC goal | Stage 3 | Well-defined objective statement |
| **Hypothesis Statement** | Identifying what to test | Stage 1 | "If-Then" or belief statement |
| **KPI Matrix** | Measuring success | Stage 2 | Quantitative success metrics |
| **Scope Box (In/Out)** | Boundary definition | Stage 4 | Scope document |
| **Go / Iterate / No-Go** | Decision-making | Stage 5 | Predefined decision logic |
| **Risk Register** | Tracking unknowns | Throughout | List of risks + mitigations |
| **MoSCoW Prioritization** | Ranking PoC features | Optional | Must / Should / Could / Won't |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU 2024:**
> PoC objectives are **technical** in nature. They differ from **business objectives** (revenue, market share) and **learning objectives** (what the team wants to discover). Mixing these is a common student error.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In real engineering practice, setting PoC objectives is foundational in sectors like:

- **Hardware Product Development:** Validating that a new sensor can detect the target parameter within tolerance limits before PCB fabrication.
- **Software / AI Products:** Confirming that a machine learning model achieves a target **F1-score** or **precision** before integration.
- **Biomedical Devices:** Demonstrating that a diagnostic device meets **sensitivity / specificity** thresholds before clinical trials.
- **Automotive EV Systems:** Proving that a battery pack delivers the required **range and thermal stability** under simulated conditions.

The discipline of *setting objectives first* transforms PoC from a vague experiment into a **decision-support instrument**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Objective-Setting Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Adaptation Notice:**
> Since "Proof of Concept: Setting Objectives" is a **design / management topic** rather than a pure mathematical subject, the standard algebraic derivation has been replaced with a **rigorous step-by-step analytical methodology walkthrough**, presented in alignment with KTU 2024's demand for structured, process-oriented answers.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example: Setting PoC Objectives for a Smart Irrigation System

**Background Context (Given Scenario):**
> A startup is developing a **smart irrigation system** for small-scale Kerala farmers. The system uses **soil moisture sensors**, **weather forecast APIs**, and an **AI-based decision engine** to automate watering. The team must define PoC objectives before building a full prototype.

---

### **Step 1: Formulate the Core Hypothesis**

The team must articulate the single most important belief they want to test.

> *"If soil moisture sensors and a weather API are integrated with a rule-based decision engine, then the system can reduce water consumption by 25% compared to manual irrigation while maintaining crop yield within ±5%."*

**Why this matters:**
The hypothesis is **falsifiable** — it can be proven true or false through measurement. A PoC objective built on a vague belief (e.g., *"farmers will like it"*) is unmeasurable and academically weak.

---

### **Step 2: Convert the Hypothesis into SMART Objectives**

Using the SMART framework, the hypothesis is decomposed into **3-5 distinct objectives**:

| # | Objective | SMART Check |
|:-:|-----------|:-----------:|
| O1 | Reduce water consumption by **≥ 25%** vs. manual baseline within a **30-day** trial | ✅ S M T |
| O2 | Maintain crop yield within **±5%** of manual irrigation baseline | ✅ S M R |
| O3 | Sensor network must operate with **< 10%** data loss over 30 days | ✅ S M A |
| O4 | Total PoC cost must remain **below ₹50,000** | ✅ S M A R T |
| O5 | Decision engine response time **< 2 seconds** per zone | ✅ S M A T |

> [!TIP]
> Notice how each objective is **individually measurable**. KTU 14-mark answers should always present objectives in a **tabular SMART-checked format** for examiner clarity.

---

### **Step 3: Define the Measurement Methodology**

For each objective, the PoC must specify **how** the KPI will be measured.

$$\text{Water Savings (\%)} = \frac{V_{\text{manual}} - V_{\text{automated}}}{V_{\text{manual}}} \times 100$$

Where $V_{\text{manual}}$ is the volume of water used in the manual irrigation baseline, and $V_{\text{automated}}$ is the volume used by the PoC system.

$$
\text{Crop Yield Deviation (\%)} = \frac{\vert Y_{\text{automated}} - Y_{\text{manual}} \vert}{Y_{\text{manual}}} \times 100
$$

> *Note:* The vertical bar notation $\vert \cdot \vert$ represents absolute value (magnitude of deviation).

**Decision engine response time** is measured as the wall-clock time between sensor data ingestion and actuation signal output, averaged across all zones over the trial period.

---

### **Step 4: Establish Scope Boundaries (In-Scope vs. Out-of-Scope)**

| In-Scope (PoC) | Out-of-Scope (Deferred to Prototype) |
|:----------------|:--------------------------------------|
| 1-acre test plot | Multi-farm / multi-crop deployment |
| Coconut + banana crops | All Kerala crop varieties |
| English-language mobile alerts | Regional language support |
| Rule-based engine (no ML) | Full AI/ML model |
| Solar + battery power | Grid-powered systems |

---

### **Step 5: Define Stop / Go / Iterate Decision Rules**

$$
\text{Decision} =
\begin{cases}
\textbf{GO} & \text{if all 5 objectives are met (100\% pass)} \\[4pt]
\textbf{ITERATE} & \text{if 3 or 4 objectives are met} \\[4pt]
\textbf{NO-GO} & \text{if 2 or fewer objectives are met}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Insight:**
> This pre-defined decision rule eliminates **post-hoc rationalization**. Without it, teams tend to declare a PoC "successful" based on subjective feelings, which defeats the purpose of evidence-based product development.

---

### **Step 6: Risk Register (Linked to Objectives)**

| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
|:-----|:----------:|:------:|:-----------|
| Sensor failure in monsoon humidity | High | High | Use IP67-rated sensors; spare units |
| API downtime (weather data) | Medium | Medium | Local 3-day weather cache |
| Farmer non-adoption of alerts | Medium | High | Bilingual training + voice alerts |
| Power outage | High | Medium | Solar + 48-hr battery backup |

---

## 3.2 Summary: Analytical Matrix of PoC Objective Setting

| Step | Output Artifact | KTU-Exam Weight |
|:-----|:----------------|:---------------:|
| 1. Hypothesis | Falsifiable belief statement | High |
| 2. SMART Objectives | 3-5 measurable goals | **Very High** |
| 3. Measurement Plan | KPIs + formulas / methods | High |
| 4. Scope Boundaries | In/Out list | Medium |
| 5. Decision Rules | Go / Iterate / No-Go logic | **Very High** |
| 6. Risk Register | Risk + mitigation table | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Strategy:**
> Since "Setting PoC Objectives" is a process-driven concept, the diagrams below are rendered as **functional flowcharts and block-level architecture diagrams** in Mermaid. These visually represent the relationships between the steps and decision points.

---

## 4.1 Diagram 1: PoC Objective-Setting Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Problem Statement"] --> node2["Identify Core Hypothesis"]
    node2 --> node3["Draft Raw Objectives"]
    node3 --> node4["Apply SMART Filter"]
    node4 --> node5{"SMART Compliant?"}
    node5 -- No --> node6["Refine Objective"]
    node6 --> node3
    node5 -- Yes --> node7["Define Measurement KPIs"]
    node7 --> node8["Set Scope Boundaries"]
    node8 --> node9["Define Decision Rules"]
    node9 --> node10["Build Risk Register"]
    node10 --> node11["Final PoC Objective Document"]
    node11 --> node12["Execute PoC"]
    node12 --> node13{"Objectives Met?"}
    node13 -- All Met --> node14["GO to Prototype"]
    node13 -- Partial --> node15["ITERATE PoC v2"]
    node13 -- None --> node16["NO-GO or PIVOT"]
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: SMART Objective Decomposition Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Core Hypothesis"] --> nodeB["S - Specific"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["M - Measurable"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["A - Achievable"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["R - Relevant"]
    nodeA --> nodeF["T - Time Bound"]
    nodeB --> nodeG["Refined PoC Objective"]
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: PoC Position in the Design Thinking Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1["Empathize"] --> nodeP2["Define"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3["Ideate"]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4["SET PoC OBJECTIVES"]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5["Execute PoC"]
    nodeP5 --> nodeP6{"Validated?"}
    nodeP6 -- Yes --> nodeP7["Prototype"]
    nodeP6 -- No --> nodeP3
    nodeP7 --> nodeP8["Test"]
    nodeP8 --> nodeP9["Iterate / Launch"]
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Go / Iterate / No-Go Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeX["PoC Execution Complete"] --> nodeY["Evaluate KPIs"]
    nodeY --> nodeZ{"Pass Rate"}
    nodeZ -- "100% Met" --> nodeY1["GO: Proceed to Prototype"]
    nodeZ -- "60-80% Met" --> nodeY2["ITERATE: Refine and Re-test"]
    nodeZ -- "Below 60%" --> nodeY3["NO-GO: Abandon or Pivot"]
    nodeY1 --> nodeY4["Document Learnings"]
    nodeY2 --> nodeY4
    nodeY3 --> nodeY4
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Including **at least one flowchart** in a 14-mark answer can significantly boost visual presentation marks, which KTU examiners are explicitly trained to reward.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📚 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**Define Proof of Concept (PoC). Why is "Setting Objectives" considered the most critical step in a PoC exercise?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Proof of Concept (PoC)** is a small-scale, controlled demonstration conducted in the early stage of product development to **validate the technical and practical feasibility** of a proposed idea before full-scale investment.

**Setting objectives** is the most critical step because:
1. **(1 Mark)** It defines the **acceptance criteria** — without objectives, there is no way to judge success or failure.
2. **(1 Mark)** It prevents **scope creep** and keeps the PoC focused, time-bound, and resource-efficient.
3. **(1 Mark)** It enables **evidence-based decision-making** (Go / Iterate / No-Go), aligning stakeholders and justifying further investment.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**List and briefly explain the SMART framework as applied to PoC objective setting.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **SMART framework** ensures that PoC objectives are well-structured and testable:

| Element | Meaning (1 Mark Total for Identification) |
|:--------|:--------------------------------------------|
| **S** | Specific — Clear, unambiguous scope of testing |
| **M** | Measurable — Quantifiable success metrics / KPIs |
| **A** | Achievable — Realistic given budget, time, and skills |
| **R** | Relevant — Aligned to the core product hypothesis |
| **T** | Time-bound — Defined deadline for PoC completion |

*(1 Mark for explaining the relevance of any 2 elements in PoC context.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Expected Pattern, Module 3]**

> *"A team is developing a wearable health-monitoring device for elderly patients. Before building a full prototype, they plan to execute a Proof of Concept. As the product design lead:"*
>
> *(a)* Formulate the **core hypothesis** and derive **at least 4 SMART objectives** for the PoC. **(7 Marks)**
>
> *(b)* Define the **scope boundaries**, **measurement methodology**, and **Go / Iterate / No-Go decision rules** for the PoC. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Hypothesis & SMART Objectives (7 Marks)**

**Core Hypothesis (2 Marks):**
> *"If a wrist-worn device integrates a heart-rate sensor, SpO2 sensor, and fall-detection accelerometer with a cloud alert system, then it can accurately detect abnormal health events in elderly users with < 5% false alarm rate over a 60-day trial."*

**SMART Objectives (5 Marks — 1.25 each, tabular presentation recommended):**

| # | Objective | SMART Element |
|:-:|-----------|:-------------:|
| O1 | Detect abnormal heart-rate events with **≥ 90% sensitivity** | Specific + Measurable |
| O2 | Fall detection must trigger alerts within **10 seconds** of impact | Specific + Time-bound |
| O3 | False alarm rate must remain **below 5%** | Measurable + Achievable |
| O4 | Device battery life must support **≥ 48 hours** continuous use | Relevant + Time-bound |
| O5 | PoC must be completed within **60 days** and budget **₹2,00,000** | Achievable + Time-bound |

---

**(b) Scope, Measurement & Decision Rules (7 Marks)**

**Scope Boundaries (2 Marks):**
- **In-Scope:** 10 elderly volunteers (age 60+), single location (Kerala care home), wrist-worn form, basic vitals only.
- **Out-of-Scope:** ECG-grade cardiac monitoring, multi-device sync, regional language app, insurance integration.

**Measurement Methodology (3 Marks):**

$$
\text{Sensitivity (\%)} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{False Alarm Rate (\%)} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} \times 100
$$

Where $TP$ = true positives, $FN$ = false negatives, $FP$ = false positives, $TN$ = true negatives.

- **Heart-Rate Accuracy:** Compared against a clinically validated reference device (gold standard).
- **Fall Detection Latency:** Timestamp difference between impact event and cloud alert.
- **Battery Life:** Continuous-operation test under simulated daily use.

**Decision Rules (2 Marks):**
- ✅ **GO:** All 5 SMART objectives passed → proceed to full prototype.
- 🟡 **ITERATE:** 3-4 objectives passed → refine algorithm, PoC v2.
- ❌ **NO-GO:** 2 or fewer objectives passed → abandon or pivot to alternate sensor architecture.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Expected Pattern, Module 3]**

> *"An EdTech startup proposes an AI-based personalized learning platform for KTU-affiliated engineering colleges. Before full development, they want to run a PoC."*
>
> *(a)* Explain the **role of a PoC** in the design thinking lifecycle and justify why **setting clear objectives** is essential for this EdTech PoC. **(7 Marks)**
>
> *(b)* Design a **PoC objective document** for this scenario, including hypothesis, SMART objectives, KPIs, and a **risk register** with at least 4 risks. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Role of PoC & Importance of Objectives (7 Marks)**

**Role of PoC in Design Thinking (3.5 Marks):**
1. The PoC bridges **ideation and prototyping** — it is the *first physical or digital validation* of the concept.
2. It **de-risks the product** by surfacing technical, user, and economic unknowns early.
3. It serves as a **stakeholder communication tool**, replacing assumptions with evidence.
4. It provides a **decision gate** — a structured checkpoint for **Go / Iterate / No-Go**.

**Why Setting Objectives is Essential (3.5 Marks):**
1. **Prevents scope creep** — the EdTech domain is vast; without boundaries, the PoC will balloon.
2. **Ensures measurability** — vague goals like "improve learning" cannot be tested; specific KPIs (e.g., *"improve test scores by 15%"*) can.
3. **Aligns the cross-functional team** — engineering, pedagogy, and business teams share a common target.
4. **Enables objective Go/No-Go** — protects investor capital and prevents emotional decision-making.

---

**(b) PoC Objective Document (7 Marks)**

**Hypothesis (1 Mark):**
> *"If an AI engine personalizes question difficulty based on a student's real-time performance, then average test scores in engineering subjects can improve by ≥ 15% over a 12-week PoC across 50 students."*

**SMART Objectives & KPIs (3 Marks):**

| Objective | KPI | Target | Timeline |
|:----------|:----|:------:|:--------:|
| Personalized difficulty adaptation | Score improvement | ≥ 15% | 12 weeks |
| Student engagement | Daily active usage | ≥ 70% of cohort | 12 weeks |
| AI recommendation accuracy | Precision @ top-5 | ≥ 80% | 12 weeks |
| Platform uptime | System availability | ≥ 99% | 12 weeks |
| PoC budget adherence | Cost variance | ≤ 10% over budget | 12 weeks |

**Risk Register (3 Marks):**

| # | Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
|:-:|:-----|:----------:|:------:|:-----------|
| 1 | Low student adoption / login fatigue | High | High | Gamification + reminders + faculty nudges |
| 2 | AI model bias across student demographics | Medium | High | Balanced training data + fairness audit |
| 3 | Internet connectivity issues in rural colleges | High | Medium | Offline-mode sync + low-bandwidth fallback |
| 4 | Data privacy / FERPA-like compliance | Medium | Critical | Anonymized data + local compliance review |
| 5 | Faculty resistance to AI replacing teaching | Medium | Medium | Position AI as *assistant*, not replacement |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on "PoC Setting Objectives" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PoC with Prototype** — Many students describe prototype building (3D models, UI mockups) instead of PoC validation. KTU examiners deduct 2-3 marks for this.
> 2. **Vague / Non-SMART Objectives** — Writing *"improve user experience"* or *"make it faster"* instead of quantified KPIs. Always quantify.
> 3. **No Decision Rule** — Forgetting to define Go/Iterate/No-Go criteria. KTU 14-mark answers **must** include this.
> 4. **Missing Scope Boundaries** — A PoC answer without an explicit In-Scope / Out-of-Scope table is considered incomplete.
> 5. **No Risk Awareness** — Risk register is often skipped; examiners expect at least 3-4 risks with mitigations in 14-mark answers.
> 6. **Ignoring Time and Budget** — Time-bound and budget-constrained objectives are hallmarks of mature PoC planning. Omitting them costs marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist — Proof of Concept: Setting Objectives**

- **PoC ≠ Prototype** — PoC validates the *idea*; prototype validates the *design*.
- **Setting Objectives** is a **5-stage process**: Hypothesis → SMART Goals → KPIs → Scope → Decision Rules.
- **SMART** = Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound. Always use this in KTU answers.
- **Hypothesis must be falsifiable** — must be testable true *or* false through measurement.
- **KPIs must be quantified** — avoid vague terms; use formulas like sensitivity, accuracy, F1-score, time, cost.
- **Scope Boundaries** are mandatory — In-Scope vs. Out-of-Scope table expected.
- **Go / Iterate / No-Go** decision rules must be **predefined**, not decided after the PoC.
- **Risk Register** with at least 3-4 risks + mitigations boosts marks in 14-mark answers.
- PoC objectives are **technical validation goals**, NOT business/revenue goals.
- A PoC is **resource-protective** — it prevents costly full-scale failures.
- **Time and budget constraints** are integral to SMART PoC objectives.
- KTU 14-mark answers should ideally include: a **table of objectives**, a **scope table**, and a **decision rule** — this visual structure is examiner-friendly.

> 🎯 **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:**
> When a 14-mark question asks about PoC, always structure your answer in this order: **(1) Definition & Purpose → (2) Hypothesis → (3) SMART Objectives Table → (4) Scope Boundaries → (5) KPIs & Measurement → (6) Go/Iterate/No-Go → (7) Risk Register.** This order signals structured, examiner-aligned thinking.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risk assessment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Risk assessment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Risk Assessment** in the context of Design Thinking and Product Development is a systematic, structured, and iterative process used to **identify, analyze, evaluate, and prioritize potential risks** that could negatively impact the success of a new product, service, or design solution throughout its lifecycle. It is a critical phase of the broader *Risk Management* framework and forms the analytical backbone of informed decision-making during prototyping, testing, and market launch.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Risk Assessment is defined as a **two-stage analytical process** comprising:
1. **Risk Analysis** – the systematic use of available information to determine how often specified events may occur and the magnitude of their consequences.
2. **Risk Evaluation** – the process of comparing the level of risk against predetermined acceptance criteria to determine the significance of the risk.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"Risk assessment is the overall process of risk identification, risk analysis, and risk evaluation. It is an integral part of design thinking because every iteration of prototyping carries uncertainty that must be measured and mitigated before proceeding to the next stage."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — The Mountain Climbing Expedition**

Imagine a team of mountaineers planning to climb a peak. Before starting, they don't just "wing it." They:
- **Identify risks**: Avalanches, crevasses, altitude sickness, equipment failure, sudden weather changes.
- **Analyze risks**: They rate *how likely* each risk is (Probability) and *how bad* it would be if it happened (Impact).
- **Evaluate risks**: They decide which risks are *tolerable* (e.g., mild sunburn) and which are *show-stoppers* (e.g., avalanche warning).
- **Plan mitigation**: They carry oxygen tanks, ropes, satellite phones, and avalanche beacons.

**Product development is the mountain.** Each feature, supplier, technology, and user group is a possible crevasse. **Risk Assessment is the map, the weather forecast, and the safety gear — all rolled into one disciplined process.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examiners:** Students who frame risk assessment as merely "listing things that could go wrong" lose marks. The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to articulate the **systematic, measurable, and decision-oriented** nature of the process — i.e., risk = **f(probability, impact)**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants in Risk Assessment

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Scale | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Probability | **P** | **1 – 5** | Likelihood of risk occurring |
| Impact / Severity | **I** or **S** | **1 – 5** | Consequence magnitude if risk occurs |
| Risk Score / RPN | **R** | **1 – 25** (or **1 – 125** for FMEA) | Combined measure of risk exposure |
| Detection (FMEA only) | **D** | **1 – 10** | Ability to detect risk before it reaches user |
| Risk Appetite | — | Qualitative | Maximum risk organization is willing to accept |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Board Exam Memorization:** Always quote the **Risk Score formula** as $R = P \times I$ and the **FMEA Risk Priority Number** as $RPN = S \times O \times D$. These two are high-yield formulas.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Risk Assessment

Risk assessment, as taught under the KTU 2024 Design Thinking curriculum, rests on three operational pillars:

#### **Pillar 1 — Risk Identification**
The qualitative stage where the project team surfaces *what* could go wrong. The objective is **completeness**, not quantification.

**Common techniques (memorize these for KTU):**
- **Brainstorming sessions** — free-flowing ideation of failure modes.
- **SWOT Analysis** — *Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats*. The "T" directly lists external risks.
- **Checklists** — based on past projects and industry standards.
- **Expert Interviews / Delphi Method** — soliciting expert judgment in iterative rounds.
- **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** — structured bottom-up analysis of component-level failure modes.
- **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** — top-down deductive analysis using Boolean logic.
- **Cause-and-Effect (Ishikawa / Fishbone) Diagrams** — categorizing causes into *Man, Machine, Method, Material, Measurement, Environment (5M + E)*.

#### **Pillar 2 — Risk Analysis**
The quantitative or semi-quantitative stage where identified risks are scored.

- **Qualitative Analysis**: Uses categorical scales (Low / Medium / High).
- **Semi-Quantitative Analysis**: Uses numerical scales (e.g., **1 – 5**) to compute a Risk Score.
- **Quantitative Analysis**: Uses numerical probability distributions, Monte Carlo simulation, Expected Monetary Value (EMV).

#### **Pillar 3 — Risk Evaluation**
Decides whether each risk is **acceptable, treatable, or unacceptable** by comparing the Risk Score against a pre-agreed **Risk Matrix** (also called Heat Map or Risk Grid).

- Risks in the **Red Zone** → Must be mitigated or the project is halted.
- Risks in the **Yellow Zone** → Monitor and apply contingency planning.
- Risks in the **Green Zone** → Accept and proceed.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Basic Risk Score | $R = P \times I$ | Probability multiplied by Impact | Most KTU numerical problems |
| 2 | FMEA Risk Priority Number | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Severity × Occurrence × Detection | FMEA-based product design questions |
| 3 | Expected Monetary Value | $EMV = P \times C$ | Probability multiplied by Cost/Consequence | Quantitative cost-risk analysis |
| 4 | Risk Exposure | $RE = P \times L \times C$ | Probability × Loss × Criticality | Enterprise risk frameworks |
| 5 | Probability (normalized) | $P_i = \dfrac{\text{Favorable Outcomes}}{\text{Total Outcomes}}$ | Classical probability | Statistical risk modeling |
| 6 | Composite Project Risk | $R_{project} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot I_i$ | Sum of all weighted risks | Portfolio-level assessment |
| 7 | Mitigation Effectiveness | $R_{residual} = R_{original} \times (1 - M_{eff})$ | Residual risk post-mitigation | Post-mitigation analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** In FMEA, the **Severity (S)** scale is typically **1 – 10**, while in the basic risk matrix the Impact is typically **1 – 5**. Mixing these scales is a common student error that costs marks. Always check the **scale range** stated in the question.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Risk assessment is not an academic exercise — it underpins engineering decisions across industries:

- **Automotive Industry**: ISO 26262 mandates FMEA for every Electronic Control Unit (ECU) before vehicle homologation.
- **Medical Devices**: ISO 14971 requires documented risk assessment for every Class II/III device (pacemakers, insulin pumps).
- **Software / IT**: NIST SP 800-30 governs cybersecurity risk assessment for federal systems.
- **Civil Engineering**: Probabilistic risk assessment informs dam safety, bridge load tolerances, and earthquake design.
- **Aerospace**: NASA uses FTA and Probabilistic Risk Assessment (PRA) for every launch decision.

In **Design Thinking**, the iterative prototyping loop inherently generates uncertainty — and risk assessment provides the **decision gate** to decide whether to iterate further, pivot, or proceed to commercialization.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Basic Risk Score Formula

The fundamental risk equation used universally in KTU 2024 scheme coursework is:

$$R = P \times I$$

**Derivation logic — step by step:**

**Step 1:** Define the *probability* $P$ of a risk event occurring. For semi-quantitative scales, we use a normalized index on a **1 – 5** scale:

$$P \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$$

where $P = 1$ means *Rare* and $P = 5$ means *Almost Certain*.

**Step 2:** Define the *impact* $I$ (or severity $S$) of the risk event, also normalized on a **1 – 5** scale:

$$I \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$$

where $I = 1$ means *Negligible* and $I = 5$ means *Catastrophic*.

**Step 3:** The risk $R$ is a **joint function** of the two. Since both are non-negative integers, a simple multiplicative aggregation is most intuitive (and is the industry standard).

**Step 4:** Bounds of $R$:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{min} &= P_{min} \times I_{min} = 1 \times 1 = 1 \\
R_{max} &= P_{max} \times I_{max} = 5 \times 5 = 25
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Therefore the risk score lies in:

$$R \in [1, 25]$$

**Step 6 (Classification):** Risks are bucketed into zones:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Green Zone:} \quad & 1 \leq R \leq 4 \\
\text{Yellow Zone:} \quad & 5 \leq R \leq 12 \\
\text{Red Zone:} \quad & 13 \leq R \leq 25
\end{aligned}$$

This gives the **5 × 5 Risk Matrix** which is the standard heat-map used in KTU board problems.

### 3.2 Derivation of FMEA Risk Priority Number (RPN)

The FMEA framework uses three independent variables:

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

where each variable is rated on a **1 – 10** scale.

**Step 1:** Define **Severity (S)** — how severe the failure's effect is on the customer.

$$S \in \{1, 2, \ldots, 10\}, \quad S=10 \text{ being hazard without warning}$$

**Step 2:** Define **Occurrence (O)** — how frequently the failure cause occurs.

$$O \in \{1, 2, \ldots, 10\}, \quad O=10 \text{ being very high, almost inevitable}$$

**Step 3:** Define **Detection (D)** — the likelihood that the current process control will *detect* the failure mode *before* the customer is affected. Note: **higher D = worse detection = higher risk**.

$$D \in \{1, 2, \ldots, 10\}, \quad D=10 \text{ being almost impossible to detect}$$

**Step 4:** Compute RPN:

$$\begin{aligned}
RPN_{min} &= 1 \times 1 \times 1 = 1 \\
RPN_{max} &= 10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1000
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$RPN \in [1, 1000]$$

**Step 5 (Action Priority):**

$$\begin{aligned}
RPN \leq 50 \quad &\rightarrow \text{Low priority (monitor)} \\
50 < RPN \leq 200 \quad &\rightarrow \text{Medium priority (action recommended)} \\
RPN > 200 \quad &\rightarrow \text{High priority (mandatory corrective action)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why multiply and not add?** Multiplication ensures that a single critically bad factor (e.g., $S=10$ but $O=1$) is still scored appropriately, while addition would dilute it. Multiplication is **conservative and safety-first**, which is why automotive and aerospace standards mandate it.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A new drone product has three identified risks during prototype testing.

| Risk ID | Description | Probability (P) | Impact (I) |
|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | Battery overheating | 3 | 5 |
| R2 | GPS signal loss in urban canyons | 4 | 3 |
| R3 | Propeller blade fatigue failure | 2 | 5 |

**Step 1 — Compute each Risk Score:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= P_1 \times I_1 = 3 \times 5 = 15 \quad \text{(Red Zone)} \\
R_2 &= P_2 \times I_2 = 4 \times 3 = 12 \quad \text{(Yellow Zone)} \\
R_3 &= P_3 \times I_3 = 2 \times 5 = 10 \quad \text{(Yellow Zone)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Rank by Risk Score (descending):**

$$R_1 (15) > R_2 (12) > R_3 (10)$$

**Step 3 — Decision:**

- **R1 (Battery overheating)** → **Red Zone** → Must be mitigated *before* market launch. Redesign battery management system.
- **R2 (GPS loss)** → **Yellow Zone** → Apply contingency (visual positioning fallback) and monitor.
- **R3 (Propeller fatigue)** → **Yellow Zone** → Schedule periodic inspection in user manual.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Board Exams:** Always end a numerical risk problem with a **prioritized action list**. Examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for the *decision* or *recommendation* phase, not just the calculation.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Risk Tool

| Stage of Design Thinking | Most Suitable Risk Tool | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Empathize / Define | Risk Register, Stakeholder Analysis | External & user-related risks dominate |
| Ideate | Brainstorming, Delphi | Creative, divergent risk surfacing |
| Prototype | FMEA, Cause-and-Effect | Component-level failure modes emerge |
| Test | FTA, Quantitative Simulation | Field-test data feeds probabilistic models |
| Launch | Risk Matrix, Monte Carlo | Portfolio-level, financial & market risks |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Risk Assessment Process Flow

The following Mermaid block renders the **end-to-end risk assessment workflow** as a sequential flow aligned with the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: Define Project Scope] --> A2[Identify Stakeholders and Context]
    A2 --> A3[Risk Identification Techniques]
    A3 --> A31[Brainstorming]
    A3 --> A32[SWOT Analysis]
    A3 --> A33[FMEA]
    A3 --> A34[Expert Delphi Method]
    A3 --> A35[Checklists from Past Projects]
    A31 --> A4[Compile Risk Register]
    A32 --> A4
    A33 --> A4
    A34 --> A4
    A35 --> A4
    A4 --> A5[Risk Analysis: Assign P and I Scores]
    A5 --> A6[Compute Risk Score R equals P times I]
    A6 --> A7[Plot on Risk Matrix Heat Map]
    A7 --> A8{Zone Classification}
    A8 -->|R between 1 and 4| A81[Green: Accept Risk]
    A8 -->|R between 5 and 12| A82[Yellow: Monitor and Plan]
    A8 -->|R between 13 and 25| A83[Red: Mandatory Mitigation]
    A81 --> A9[Document and Review Quarterly]
    A82 --> A9
    A83 --> A10[Design Mitigation Strategy]
    A10 --> A11[Recompute Residual Risk]
    A11 --> A12{Residual Acceptable?}
    A12 -->|Yes| A9
    A12 -->|No| A13[Escalate to Steering Committee]
    A13 --> A10
    A9 --> A14[Continuous Risk Monitoring Loop]
    A14 --> A3
```

### 4.2 5 × 5 Risk Matrix Heat Map (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph B1[Impact Scale 1 to 5]
        B11[1 Negligible]
        B12[2 Minor]
        B13[3 Moderate]
        B14[4 Major]
        B15[5 Catastrophic]
    end
    subgraph B2[Probability Scale 1 to 5]
        B21[1 Rare]
        B22[2 Unlikely]
        B23[3 Possible]
        B24[4 Likely]
        B25[5 Almost Certain]
    end
    B2 --> B3[Cross Tabulation R equals P times I]
    B1 --> B3
    B3 --> B4[Color Coded Zones]
    B4 --> B41[Green Low: 1 to 4]
    B4 --> B42[Yellow Medium: 5 to 12]
    B4 --> B43[Red High: 13 to 25]
```

### 4.3 FMEA Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Component or Subsystem Identified] --> C2[List Potential Failure Modes]
    C2 --> C3[List Effects on Customer and System]
    C3 --> C4[List Potential Causes]
    C4 --> C5[Assign Severity Score S 1 to 10]
    C4 --> C6[Assign Occurrence Score O 1 to 10]
    C4 --> C7[Assign Detection Score D 1 to 10]
    C5 --> C8[Compute RPN equals S times O times D]
    C6 --> C8
    C7 --> C8
    C8 --> C9{RPN Threshold}
    C9 -->|Above 200| C10[Critical: Mandatory Redesign]
    C9 -->|50 to 200| C11[Recommended Action]
    C9 -->|Below 50| C12[Acceptable: Monitor]
    C10 --> C13[Recommend Corrective Action]
    C11 --> C13
    C12 --> C14[Document in FMEA Register]
    C13 --> C15[Recompute New RPN]
    C15 --> C9
    C14 --> C16[Periodic FMEA Review]
```

### 4.4 Risk Treatment Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Identified Risk with Score R] --> D2{Is R in Red Zone?}
    D2 -->|Yes| D3[Choose Risk Treatment]
    D2 -->|No| D4[Accept and Document]
    D3 --> D31[Avoid: Eliminate the Cause]
    D3 --> D32[Transfer: Insurance or Outsourcing]
    D3 --> D33[Mitigate: Reduce P or I]
    D3 --> D34[Exploit: For Positive Risks Opportunities]
    D31 --> D5[Residual Risk Recalculation]
    D32 --> D5
    D33 --> D5
    D34 --> D5
    D5 --> D6{Residual Acceptable?}
    D6 -->|Yes| D7[Close Risk and Archive]
    D6 -->|No| D3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Board Diagrams:** Examiners often award **1–2 marks** for correctly drawn risk process flow diagrams. The above Mermaid blocks are rendered using the Mermaid `flowchart TD` and `flowchart LR` syntax with alphanumeric-only node IDs, complying with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**
**Define Risk Assessment. List any four techniques used for risk identification in product development.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Risk assessment is the systematic process of identifying, analyzing, and evaluating risks associated with a product development project. It involves determining the probability and impact of potential risk events to support informed decision-making across the design thinking lifecycle.

**Four Risk Identification Techniques (1.5 Marks — 0.5 each for any two, or 0.375 each for four):**
1. **Brainstorming Sessions** – Group ideation of potential failure modes.
2. **SWOT Analysis** – Examination of *Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats*; the "T" captures external risks.
3. **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** – Structured bottom-up analysis of component-level failure modes.
4. **Delphi Method** – Iterative expert consultation rounds to converge on critical risks.

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Distinguish between Qualitative and Quantitative Risk Analysis with two points each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Qualitative Analysis | Quantitative Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Used** | Categorical descriptors (Low/Medium/High) | Numerical probabilities and cost values |
| **Computation** | No formula; uses judgment and scoring | Uses formulas like EMV, Monte Carlo |
| **Output** | Risk Matrix zones (Green/Yellow/Red) | Numerical risk exposure in monetary terms |
| **Example** | Scoring $P \times I$ on 1–5 scale | $EMV = P \times C$ simulation |

**Examiner's Allocation:** 1.5 marks for the comparison table + 1.5 marks for a correct example of each.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **Risk Matrix (Heat Map)** method of risk evaluation in detail. Construct a 5 × 5 risk matrix with Probability (1 – 5) on one axis and Impact (1 – 5) on the other, marking Green, Yellow, and Red zones. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A startup is launching a smart wearable health monitor. The product development team has identified the following four risks with their Probability (P) and Impact (I) scores on a 1–5 scale:

| Risk | Description | P | I |
|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | Inaccurate heart-rate sensor | 4 | 4 |
| R2 | Battery drains in 4 hours | 3 | 3 |
| R3 | App crashes on Android 12 | 2 | 4 |
| R4 | Strap skin allergy | 1 | 5 |

For each risk, compute the Risk Score $R = P \times I$, classify the zone, and recommend an action. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Risk Matrix Explanation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 Mark):**
A Risk Matrix, also called a Heat Map, is a semi-quantitative visual tool that plots **Probability** on one axis against **Impact** on the other to classify risks into predefined zones for decision-making.

**Step 2 — Construction Logic (2 Marks):**
The matrix is constructed by:
- Defining the **Probability scale** (1 = Rare to 5 = Almost Certain).
- Defining the **Impact scale** (1 = Negligible to 5 = Catastrophic).
- Computing the product $R = P \times I$ for each cell.

**Step 3 — The 5 × 5 Matrix (3 Marks):**

| P \ I | 1 Negligible | 2 Minor | 3 Moderate | 4 Major | 5 Catastrophic |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **5 Almost Certain** | 5 Y | 10 Y | 15 R | 20 R | 25 R |
| **4 Likely** | 4 G | 8 Y | 12 Y | 16 R | 20 R |
| **3 Possible** | 3 G | 6 Y | 9 Y | 12 Y | 15 R |
| **2 Unlikely** | 2 G | 4 G | 6 Y | 8 Y | 10 Y |
| **1 Rare** | 1 G | 2 G | 3 G | 4 G | 5 Y |

**Zone Classification (1 Mark):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Green Zone (Low):} \quad & 1 \leq R \leq 4 \quad \rightarrow \text{Accept risk} \\
\text{Yellow Zone (Medium):} \quad & 5 \leq R \leq 12 \quad \rightarrow \text{Monitor and plan contingency} \\
\text{Red Zone (High):} \quad & 13 \leq R \leq 25 \quad \rightarrow \text{Mandatory mitigation before proceeding}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Risk Computation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute Risk Scores (2 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= P_1 \times I_1 = 4 \times 4 = 16 \\
R_2 &= P_2 \times I_2 = 3 \times 3 = 9 \\
R_3 &= P_3 \times I_3 = 2 \times 4 = 8 \\
R_4 &= P_4 \times I_4 = 1 \times 5 = 5
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Zone Classification (2 Marks):**

| Risk | Score | Zone |
|---|---|---|
| R1 | 16 | Red |
| R2 | 9 | Yellow |
| R3 | 8 | Yellow |
| R4 | 5 | Yellow |

**Step 3 — Recommended Actions (3 Marks — 0.75 each):**

- **R1 (Inaccurate heart-rate sensor — Red Zone):** **High priority.** Redesign the sensor module with medical-grade accuracy and add calibration software. This is a *patient-safety* risk and cannot be shipped.
- **R2 (Battery drain — Yellow Zone):** Optimize firmware for power efficiency and publish a "battery saving mode" feature. Monitor in field tests.
- **R3 (App crashes on Android 12 — Yellow Zone):** Issue a software patch pre-launch and add automated compatibility testing to CI/CD pipeline.
- **R4 (Strap skin allergy — Yellow Zone):** Although rare, the impact is high (medical). Switch to hypoallergenic silicone strap and label materials clearly.

**Final Prioritized Action List:**

$$R_1 \;(16) > R_2 \;(9) > R_3 \;(8) > R_4 \;(5)$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** methodology. Describe the three rating parameters — Severity (S), Occurrence (O), and Detection (D) — and compute the RPN for the following failure modes in a new electric vehicle (EV) battery pack. *(7 Marks)*

| Failure Mode | S | O | D |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cell thermal runaway | 9 | 4 | 6 |
| BMS communication failure | 7 | 5 | 8 |
| Connector corrosion | 6 | 6 | 5 |
| Cooling pump seizure | 8 | 3 | 7 |

**(b)** Discuss the **four strategies of risk treatment** — Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate, and Accept — with one engineering example for each. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — FMEA Methodology (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition of FMEA (1 Mark):**
FMEA is a systematic, bottom-up analytical technique used to identify potential failure modes of a product, assess their effects, and prioritize corrective actions using the Risk Priority Number.

**Step 2 — Three Rating Parameters (3 Marks — 1 each):**

- **Severity (S) [1 – 10]:** Measures the seriousness of the failure's effect on the customer. $S = 10$ indicates a safety hazard without warning.
- **Occurrence (O) [1 – 10]:** Measures the frequency of the failure cause. $O = 10$ indicates the failure is almost inevitable.
- **Detection (D) [1 – 10]:** Measures the ability of the process control to detect the failure *before* reaching the customer. $D = 10$ indicates detection is almost impossible.

**Step 3 — RPN Computation (3 Marks — 0.75 each):**

$$\begin{aligned}
RPN_1 &= S \times O \times D = 9 \times 4 \times 6 = 216 \\
RPN_2 &= 7 \times 5 \times 8 = 280 \\
RPN_3 &= 6 \times 6 \times 5 = 180 \\
RPN_4 &= 8 \times 3 \times 7 = 168
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Prioritization (1 Mark):**

| Failure Mode | RPN | Priority |
|---|---|---|
| BMS communication failure | 280 | **Critical** (highest) |
| Cell thermal runaway | 216 | **Critical** |
| Connector corrosion | 180 | Medium |
| Cooling pump seizure | 168 | Medium |

The **BMS communication failure** is the top priority since its RPN exceeds 200, indicating mandatory corrective action.

**Part (b) — Four Risk Treatment Strategies (7 Marks):**

| Strategy | Definition (1.25 Marks) | Engineering Example (0.5 Marks) |
|---|---|---|
| **Avoid** | Eliminate the risk entirely by removing the cause or changing the design. | Replace a flammable battery chemistry with a non-flammable solid-state electrolyte. |
| **Transfer** | Shift the financial or operational consequence to a third party. | Purchase product liability insurance or outsource component manufacturing to a certified supplier. |
| **Mitigate** | Reduce either the probability or the impact to an acceptable level. | Add redundant sensors, software watchdog timers, and a thermal cutoff switch to reduce EV battery failure rate. |
| **Accept** | Consciously retain the risk because the cost of mitigation exceeds the consequence. | Allow minor cosmetic scratches on a low-cost consumer device; no mitigation, but document in the risk register. |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Scale Confusion (–2 Marks):** Using a 1–5 scale for FMEA's Severity instead of 1–10. Always verify the scale stated in the question.
> 2. **Missing Action / Recommendation Step (–2 Marks):** Computing the Risk Score or RPN without stating the *decision* or *mitigation*. KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests decision-making under CO3 (Apply).
> 3. **Confusing Risk with Issue (–1 Mark):** A risk is a *potential* future event. An issue is an event that has *already* occurred. Examiners deduct marks if these are used interchangeably.
> 4. **Skipping the Zone Classification (–1 Mark):** Numerical answers must always end with a Green/Yellow/Red tag or RPN threshold statement.
> 5. **Failing to Rank / Prioritize (–1 Mark):** If multiple risks are computed, you must rank them in descending order. A list of unranked scores is incomplete.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Risk Assessment (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

- ✅ **Risk Assessment** = **Risk Identification + Risk Analysis + Risk Evaluation**.
- ✅ **Core Formula:** $R = P \times I$ (basic), $RPN = S \times O \times D$ (FMEA).
- ✅ **Risk Matrix (5 × 5):** Green Zone 1–4, Yellow Zone 5–12, Red Zone 13–25.
- ✅ **FMEA RPN Range:** 1 to 1000; Action threshold: RPN > 200.
- ✅ **Risk Treatment Strategies:** **Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate, Accept** (and **Exploit** for opportunities).
- ✅ **Common Identification Tools:** Brainstorming, SWOT, FMEA, Delphi, Checklists, Ishikawa Fishbone, Fault Tree Analysis.
- ✅ **Risk = Future uncertainty with consequence** — distinguish from *Issue* (current problem).
- ✅ **Probability & Impact are always on the same scale** in any single problem.
- ✅ **Always end a numerical answer with a ranked priority list + actionable decision.**
- ✅ **Real-world standards:** ISO 14971 (medical), ISO 26262 (automotive), NIST SP 800-30 (cybersecurity), ISO 31000 (general risk management).
- ✅ **In Design Thinking context:** Risk Assessment is the **decision gate** between prototype iterations and final commercialization.
- ✅ **Detection (D) is inversely intuitive in FMEA** — a *higher* D score means *worse* detection ability, hence *higher* risk.
- ✅ **Expected Monetary Value:** $EMV = P \times C$ — used for cost-benefit justification of mitigation.
- ✅ **Residual Risk:** $R_{residual} = R_{original} \times (1 - M_{eff})$ — risk remaining after mitigation.
- ✅ **KTU CO Mapping:** This topic primarily maps to **CO3 (Apply risk tools to real product scenarios)** and **CO4 (Evaluate design decisions using risk metrics)** under the Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

---

> **End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes — Module 3, Topic: Risk Assessment**
> *Course: GZEST605 — Design Thinking and Product Development | KTU 2024 Scheme*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technology scouting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Technology scouting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Technology Scouting** is the systematic, structured, and proactive process of identifying, capturing, evaluating, and acquiring emerging technologies, innovative solutions, intellectual property (IP), and external knowledge assets from the global innovation ecosystem to accelerate an organization's internal innovation pipeline, bridge capability gaps, and sustain long-term competitive advantage.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of **Design Thinking and Product Development**, technology scouting acts as the **external intelligence arm** that feeds the *Ideation* and *Prototyping* stages with validated, market-ready or near-market technological possibilities. It transforms an organization from a closed, in-house R&D model to an **Open Innovation** paradigm, where ideas and technologies flow across organizational boundaries.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Highlight:** Technology scouting is positioned as a critical enabler of the *Define–Ideate–Prototype–Test* loop, ensuring that design decisions are grounded not only in user empathy but also in **technological feasibility** and **external opportunity mapping**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **head chef** designing a new fusion dish. You have a clear sense of what your customers (the *users*) want — perhaps a spicy, tangy dessert. Inside your kitchen, you have flour, sugar, and eggs (your *core competencies*). However, you are missing the exotic "kick" — say, *yuzu citrus from Japan* or *smoked chili from Mexico*.

**Technology scouting is the process by which the chef steps OUT of the kitchen, walks through global spice markets, food expos, supplier catalogs, university food labs, and competitor menus, to scout for the perfect exotic ingredient that can elevate the dish.**

- The *global spice markets* = Universities, public research labs, incubators
- The *food expos* = Trade fairs, conferences, hackathons
- The *supplier catalogs* = Patent databases, technology marketplaces
- The *university food labs* = Open-source communities, R&D centers

Once found, the chef doesn't just grab it; she **evaluates** its flavor profile, cost, sustainability, compatibility with her recipe, and legal safety (IP rights) before adding it to her menu.

**In engineering terms:** Technology scouting helps a product team find the right *technology component* to merge with their *user insight* and *business viability* — completing the Design Thinking sweet spot.

## 1.3 Why Technology Scouting Matters for KTU Engineers

- **Reduces R&D cost and time-to-market** by leveraging pre-existing innovations.
- **Mitigates risk** of investing in dead-end in-house research.
- **Enables leapfrogging** — adopting disruptive tech instead of incremental improvements.
- **Builds innovation ecosystems** with startups, academia, and industry partners.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Core Metric to Remember (Bold Standard):** A mature technology scouting function typically aims for a **Technology Pipeline Conversion Rate of 10–15%** — meaning only about 1 in every 10 scouted technologies successfully transitions into a commercialized product.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Technology Scouting

Technology scouting operates on three strategic pillars, each answering a fundamental question:

### Pillar 1: **Identification (The "Where?")**
- Locating promising technologies across global innovation landscapes.
- Sources include: patent filings, scientific publications, startup ecosystems, university tech-transfer offices, competitor product launches, open-source platforms, government labs.

### Pillar 2: **Evaluation (The "So What?")**
- Assessing relevance, maturity, feasibility, and strategic fit.
- Common frameworks: **TRL (Technology Readiness Level)** scale, SWOT, McKinsey 3-Horizons, Gartner Hype Cycle.

### Pillar 3: **Acquisition (The "How?")**
- Securing the technology through licensing, joint development, venture investment, M&A, or in-house absorption.

## 2.2 Types of Technology Scouting

| **Type** | **Direction** | **Description** | **Typical Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Active Scouting** | Push (need-driven) | Driven by a clearly defined internal technology gap; scouts hunt for specific solutions. | Solving a known engineering bottleneck. |
| **Passive Scouting** | Pull (opportunity-driven) | Continuous monitoring of the external environment for emergent tech, without a pre-defined need. | Building an opportunistic innovation pipeline. |
| **Reactive Scouting** | Trigger-based | Activated by external events (competitor patent, market disruption, regulatory change). | Crisis response or sudden market shift. |
| **Proactive (Strategic) Scouting** | Future-focused | Long-horizon scanning for 5–10 year disruptive technologies. | Corporate foresight & R&D roadmap planning. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction:** The KTU syllabus emphasizes *Active Scouting* during the *Define* phase (when user pain points reveal tech needs) and *Passive Scouting* during ongoing *Empathize* and *Test* phases (to keep the innovation funnel alive).

## 2.3 The Technology Scouting Process — Stepwise Logic

1. **Define Scouting Objective:** Align with corporate/product strategy. *Why* are we scouting?
2. **Map the Search Field:** Identify relevant domains, geographies, and technology areas.
3. **Set Search Criteria:** Define TRL range, IP status, cost ceiling, integration complexity.
4. **Source Identification:** Engage scouts (internal employees, external consultants, AI tools).
5. **Filtering & Triage:** Apply criteria to shortlist relevant technologies.
6. **Deep Evaluation:** Technical due diligence, market validation, IP analysis.
7. **Decision & Acquisition Mode:** License? Partner? Acquire? Build?
8. **Integration & Knowledge Transfer:** Embed into product development workflow.
9. **Monitoring & Feedback Loop:** Track post-acquisition performance.

## 2.4 KTU Framework Sheet — Cheat Table for Examination

| **Framework / Tool** | **Purpose** | **Key Output** |
|---|---|---|
| **TRL Scale (1–9)** | Measures technology maturity from basic research (TRL 1) to proven deployment (TRL 9). | TRL score of a scouted tech. |
| **Technology Landscape Map** | Visual mapping of technologies vs. maturity vs. relevance. | Strategic positioning chart. |
| **SWOT Analysis** | Internal-external evaluation of a scouted technology. | Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats matrix. |
| **McKinsey 3 Horizons** | Balances core (H1), adjacent (H2), and transformational (H3) tech bets. | Portfolio diversification strategy. |
| **Gartner Hype Cycle** | Maps technologies across Innovation Trigger, Peak of Inflated Expectations, Trough of Disillusionment, Slope of Enlightenment, Plateau of Productivity. | Right-time-to-adopt signals. |
| **Patent Analytics** | Mines patent databases (WIPO, USPTO, EPO) for emerging tech. | IP white-space & competitor mapping. |
| **Open Innovation Funnel** | Manages inflow of external ideas to internal teams. | Conversion metrics & ROI on scouting. |
| **Scorecard / Weighted Matrix** | Quantitative ranking of scouted technologies against weighted criteria. | Ranked shortlist. |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Tesla scouted lithium-ion cell technology from Panasonic and battery-management IP to leapfrog legacy automakers.
- **Healthcare:** Philips scouted MRI imaging algorithms from academic labs to enhance its diagnostic equipment line.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Apple maintains a dedicated **Technology Scouting Group (TSG)** that monitors semiconductor, display, and AI startups globally.
- **Startups:** Use scouting to identify licensable university IP through **Office of Technology Transfer (OTT)** offices.

## 2.6 Common Pitfalls in Technology Scouting

| **Pitfall** | **Consequence** | **Mitigation** |
|---|---|---|
| "Not Invented Here" (NIH) syndrome | Rejection of valuable external tech. | Foster an open-innovation culture. |
| Over-reliance on TRL without market fit | Adopting immature tech with no commercial demand. | Combine TRL with **MRL (Market Readiness Level)**. |
| Ignoring IP landscape | Infringement lawsuits, blocked commercialization. | Conduct freedom-to-operate (FTO) analysis early. |
| Scouting without strategic alignment | "Shiny object" distraction. | Tie every scout to a product roadmap gap. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Decision Framework

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Technology Evaluation Scorecard

Since technology scouting is fundamentally a **decision-making exercise**, KTU examiners frequently test the student's ability to construct and apply a **weighted scoring matrix**. Below is the complete, exhaustive construction of such a matrix, presented step-by-step.

### Step 1: Define Evaluation Criteria

The first step is to identify the *dimensions* along which every scouted technology will be judged. For a typical KTU product development case, these criteria are:

- **Technical Feasibility (TF):** Can we integrate this with our existing stack?
- **Market Potential (MP):** Will customers pay for this?
- **Intellectual Property Strength (IP):** Are the patents defensible?
- **Cost of Acquisition (CA):** Licensing fee, partnership cost, or acquisition price.
- **Strategic Fit (SF):** Alignment with the company's 3-year product roadmap.
- **Time to Integration (TTI):** Months needed to embed into our product.

### Step 2: Assign Weights

Each criterion is given a **weight** based on its strategic importance. Weights must sum to **1.00**.

$$
w_{TF} = 0.25, \quad w_{MP} = 0.20, \quad w_{IP} = 0.15, \quad w_{CA} = 0.15, \quad w_{SF} = 0.15, \quad w_{TTI} = 0.10
$$

$$
\sum_{i} w_i = 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.15 + 0.15 + 0.10 = 1.00
$$

### Step 3: Score Each Scouted Technology

Each scouted technology is rated on a **1-to-5 scale** (1 = Poor, 5 = Excellent) for every criterion. Let us evaluate three hypothetical scouted technologies:

| **Criterion (Weight)** | **Tech A: Solid-State Battery** | **Tech B: AI Co-Pilot** | **Tech C: Flexible Display** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Technical Feasibility (0.25) | 3 | 5 | 2 |
| Market Potential (0.20) | 5 | 4 | 3 |
| IP Strength (0.15) | 2 | 4 | 3 |
| Cost of Acquisition (0.15) | 2 | 5 | 4 |
| Strategic Fit (0.15) | 5 | 4 | 3 |
| Time to Integration (0.10) | 2 | 4 | 3 |

### Step 4: Compute the Weighted Score

The **Weighted Score (WS)** for each technology is calculated as:

$$
WS = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i
$$

where $s_i$ is the score for criterion $i$ and $w_i$ is its weight.

**Computation for Tech A (Solid-State Battery):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WS_A &= (0.25 \times 3) + (0.20 \times 5) + (0.15 \times 2) + (0.15 \times 2) + (0.15 \times 5) + (0.10 \times 2) \\
&= 0.75 + 1.00 + 0.30 + 0.30 + 0.75 + 0.20 \\
&= 3.30
\end{aligned}
$$

**Computation for Tech B (AI Co-Pilot):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WS_B &= (0.25 \times 5) + (0.20 \times 4) + (0.15 \times 4) + (0.15 \times 5) + (0.15 \times 4) + (0.10 \times 4) \\
&= 1.25 + 0.80 + 0.60 + 0.75 + 0.60 + 0.40 \\
&= 4.40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Computation for Tech C (Flexible Display):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WS_C &= (0.25 \times 2) + (0.20 \times 3) + (0.15 \times 3) + (0.15 \times 4) + (0.15 \times 3) + (0.10 \times 3) \\
&= 0.50 + 0.60 + 0.45 + 0.60 + 0.45 + 0.30 \\
&= 2.90
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Rank and Decide

| **Rank** | **Technology** | **Weighted Score** | **Decision Threshold (≥ 3.5)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tech B: AI Co-Pilot | **4.40** | **PROCEED — Pursue licensing/partnership.** |
| 2 | Tech A: Solid-State Battery | 3.30 | HOLD — Re-evaluate in 6 months as TRL improves. |
| 3 | Tech C: Flexible Display | 2.90 | REJECT — Low strategic fit and high integration cost. |

### Step 6: Sensitivity Analysis (Examiner's Favorite)

A KTU examiner may ask: *"What if Strategic Fit becomes more important than Cost of Acquisition?"* In such cases, recompute by re-weighting:

$$
w_{SF} = 0.25, \quad w_{CA} = 0.05 \quad (\text{others unchanged})
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
WS_A^{'} &= (0.25 \times 3) + (0.20 \times 5) + (0.15 \times 2) + (0.05 \times 2) + (0.25 \times 5) + (0.10 \times 2) \\
&= 0.75 + 1.00 + 0.30 + 0.10 + 1.25 + 0.20 \\
&= 3.60
\end{aligned}
$$

Notice how Tech A now crosses the **3.5 decision threshold**, demonstrating how weighting changes priorities. This is a **classic KTU valuation trick**.

## 3.2 TRL-Based Decision Logic

The **Technology Readiness Level (TRL)** provides a 1-to-9 ordinal scale. A typical KTU decision rule:

$$
\text{Scouted Technology Status} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Reject}, & \text{if } TRL < 4 \\
\text{Conditional Pilot}, & \text{if } 4 \leq TRL \leq 6 \\
\text{Full Adoption}, & \text{if } TRL > 6
\end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb (Bold Standard):** Technologies at **TRL 4–6** are the sweet spot for technology scouting — mature enough to de-risk integration, yet early enough to provide a competitive edge.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Technology Scouting Workflow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Define Scouting Objective Aligned to Product Strategy] --> B[Step 2: Map Search Field Domains Geographies Sources]
    B --> C[Step 3: Set Search Criteria TRL Cost IP Fit]
    C --> D[Step 4: Active Source Identification Internal Scouts External Networks AI Tools]
```
