C_{200} = 15000 \times 200^{-0.1520}
$$
$$
\log C_{200} = 4.1761 - 0.1520 \times 2.3010 = 4.1761 - 0.3498 = 3.8263
$$
$$
C_{200} = 10^{3.8263} \approx 6700\ \text{INR}
$$

**Engineering Significance (1 mark):**
- The unit cost drops from ₹15,000 (unit 1) to ₹6,700 (unit 200) — a **55% reduction**.
- This validates the importance of achieving high production volume before declaring the product commercially viable.
- It justifies front-loading tooling and process-design investments during the detailed design phase.

**Total for Q1(b): 7 marks** ✅

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper, Q12 Module 4]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of **Tolerance Stack-Up Analysis** and **GD&T (Geometric Dimensioning and Tolerancing)** in transitioning a design from prototype to detailed design. Illustrate with a suitable engineering example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a five-part shaft-housing assembly, the individual tolerances are: $0.1$, $0.15$, $0.2$, $0.05$, and $0.1$ mm. Calculate (i) the worst-case total tolerance, and (ii) the statistical (RSS) total tolerance. Explain why the statistical method is preferred in detailed design. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q2(a) — 7 marks:**

**Tolerance Stack-Up Analysis (3.5 marks):**
- In a prototype, parts often fit because they are hand-selected or made on flexible machines. In mass production, **automatic assembly** requires precise, predictable fits.
- Tolerance stack-up analysis determines how the tolerances of individual parts combine to affect the final assembly.
- **Worst-case (arithmetic) method** is conservative; **statistical (RSS) method** is realistic when tolerances are independent.
- The result determines whether the assembly will function reliably or fail in production.

**GD&T (Geometric Dimensioning and Tolerancing) (3.5 marks):**
- GD&T (standardized by **ASME Y14.5 / ISO 1101**) defines the permissible variation in **form, orientation, location, and run-out** of features, beyond just size.
- It provides a **single, unambiguous language** for communicating design intent between design, manufacturing, and quality teams.
- **Engineering Example:** A cylindrical shaft must fit into a housing. GD&T specifies the **datum reference frame**, the **circularity** of the shaft, the **perpendicularity** of the mating face, and the **position tolerance** of bolt holes — all essential for interchangeability and assembly automation.

---

**Model Solution to Q2(b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $T = [0.1,\ 0.15,\ 0.2,\ 0.05,\ 0.1]$ mm

**Part (i) — Worst-Case Method (3 marks):**
$$
T_{worst} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} T_i
$$
$$
T_{worst} = 0.1 + 0.15 + 0.2 + 0.05 + 0.1 = 0.60\ \text{mm}
$$

**Part (ii) — Statistical (RSS) Method (3 marks):**
$$
T_{RSS} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{5} T_i^2}
$$
$$
T_{RSS} = \sqrt{0.1^2 + 0.15^2 + 0.2^2 + 0.05^2 + 0.1^2}
$$
$$
T_{RSS} = \sqrt{0.01 + 0.0225 + 0.04 + 0.0025 + 0.01}
$$
$$
T_{RSS} = \sqrt{0.0850} = 0.2915\ \text{mm}
$$

**Why Statistical is Preferred (1 mark):**
The statistical method predicts a tighter, more realistic tolerance window (0.29 mm vs. 0.60 mm). It allows engineers to **loosen individual tolerances** without sacrificing assembly fit, thereby **reducing manufacturing cost** and **rejection rates** while maintaining functional performance.

**Total for Q2(b): 7 marks** ✅

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not skip the gate logic** — Students often list phases but forget the iterative loop-back. Always mention what happens when a gate fails.
> 2. **Units and base-10 logs** — Many students write $C_{100} = 10000 \times 0.100 = 1000$ (wrong). Use logarithms correctly.
> 3. **Confusing PoC with Prototype** — A PoC proves *concept*; a Prototype proves *function*. Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting cross-functional team** — Detailed design is not a single-engineer activity. Mention manufacturing, supply chain, QA, and regulatory.
> 5. **In Part A answers, stick to 3–4 lines** — Examiners deduct marks for unnecessarily long answers in 3-mark questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **PoC answers "Can it be done?"** — Detailed Design answers "How will it be made at scale?"
- ✅ The transition occurs across **four stages**: PoC → Engineering Prototype → Beta Prototype → Detailed Design.
- ✅ **TRL scale (1–9)** is the gold standard for measuring design maturity; PoC = TRL 3–4, Detailed Design = TRL 7–9.
- ✅ **Five Decision Gates**: Feasibility, Functional, Reliability, Manufacturability, Compliance.
- ✅ **DFM and DFA** are mandatory during detailed design to optimize cost and assembly time.
- ✅ **GD&T (ASME Y14.5)** is the language of production-ready drawings.
- ✅ **Tolerance stack-up**: Use **worst-case** for safety-critical parts; **RSS** for cost-optimized assemblies.
- ✅ **Learning curve** ($C_N = C_1 N^b$, with $b = \log 0.9 / \log 2 = -0.1520$ for 90%) justifies high-volume production.
- ✅ **Documentation is non-negotiable**: 3D CAD, 2D drawings, BoM, FMEA, test plans, service manuals.
- ✅ **Iteration is mandatory** — design reviews must include **feedback loops** back to the CAD model.
- ✅ **Cross-functional teams** — include mechanical, electronics, manufacturing, supply chain, QA, and regulatory stakeholders.
- ✅ **Regulatory compliance** (BIS, CE, FCC, ISO, RoHS) is *gated* and cannot be retrofitted easily.
- ✅ **Cost of late-stage failure** is **10–100×** the cost of an early-stage iteration — a key board-exam line.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Developing Specification Requirement Document (SRD)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Developing Specification Requirement Document (SRD)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Specification Requirements Document (SRD)** is a structured, formalized artefact in the product development lifecycle that systematically captures, classifies, and quantifies all functional, non-functional, technical, regulatory, and user-centered requirements of a proposed product or system. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of Design Thinking, the SRD serves as the **bridge between empathized user insights and the concrete engineering design phase**, translating qualitative human-centered research into verifiable, traceable, and testable engineering specifications.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> The Specification Requirements Document (SRD) is a comprehensive written record that consolidates the *Voice of the Customer (VOC)*, derived design insights, and engineering constraints into a single reference document used by cross-functional teams to drive concept generation, prototyping, and validation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are commissioning an architect to build your dream house. Before a single brick is laid, you would sit down and write out exactly what you want:
- *"3 bedrooms, 2 bathrooms, a south-facing kitchen, earthquake-resistant structure, budget of ₹50 lakhs, completion in 12 months."*

The architect does not just start drawing rooms. They take your list, organize it, prioritize it, add technical codes (like the National Building Code of India), and produce a **formal specification document** that both you and the builder sign off on. The **SRD plays this exact role in product development**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simple Analogy — The "Wedding Planner Brief":**
> Think of the SRD as the **detailed brief a couple gives a wedding planner**. It contains:
> - The *what* (functional requirements — "we need a vegetarian menu"),
> - The *how well* (performance requirements — "food must be served within 30 minutes of the ceremony"),
> - The *limitations* (constraints — "budget under ₹15 lakhs"),
> - The *legal/regulatory bits* (compliance — "alcohol license, fire safety NOC"),
> - And the *feel* (user experience requirements — "elegant, pastel, intimate atmosphere").
> 
> Without this document, the planner might deliver a loud DJ party when the couple wanted a soft string quartet. **The SRD eliminates this ambiguity in product development.**

### 1.3 Role of SRD in the Design Thinking Process

The Design Thinking framework, as adopted in the KTU 2024 curriculum, follows the stages: **Empathize → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test**. The SRD is positioned at the critical inflection point between **Define** and **Ideate**, performing four primary functions:

1. **Documentation** — Persists ephemeral ideation insights into a stable reference.
2. **Communication** — Acts as a single source of truth across multidisciplinary teams.
3. **Validation** — Enables stakeholder sign-off and reduces rework.
4. **Traceability** — Links every design decision back to a user need or regulatory mandate.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** Whenever a question asks *"Why is an SRD necessary in design thinking?"*, always anchor your answer to the phrase **"translating user empathy into engineering reality."** Examiners reward this specific phraseology in the 14-mark answers.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning in SRD Context |
|---|---|
| **Requirement** | A condition or capability that must be met by the product. |
| **Stakeholder** | Any individual/group affected by the product (user, client, regulator, manufacturer). |
| **Traceability Matrix** | A tool that links each requirement to its design, code, and test artefact. |
| **Baseline (Frozen)** | A formally approved version of the SRD against which changes are managed. |
| **Shall / Should / May** | Standard modal verbs in SRDs indicating mandatory/recommended/optional. |
| **Verification** | Confirmation that the product meets the requirement (e.g., inspection, test). |
| **Validation** | Confirmation that the product meets the user's actual need in the real environment. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Why an SRD? The Operational "Why" Behind It

In the absence of a formal SRD, product development teams typically suffer from three classical failures that the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework explicitly aims to prevent:

- **Scope Creep** — Uncontrolled expansion of features beyond the original user need.
- **Requirement Ambiguity** — Vague statements like *"the app should be fast"* leading to inconsistent interpretations.
- **Stakeholder Misalignment** — Designers, engineers, and marketers operating on different definitions of success.

The SRD combats these by enforcing the **SMART-R criteria** for every requirement:

> **S** — Specific
> **M** — Measurable
> **A** — Achievable
> **R** — Relevant
> **T** — Time-bound
> **R** — Traceable

### 2.2 Structural Anatomy of the SRD

A KTU-aligned SRD, while customizable, must contain the following **core structural blocks**. Each block is non-negotiable for full credit in 14-mark questions:

1. **Document Control Header** — Version number, author, date, approvers, revision history.
2. **Introduction & Scope** — Purpose of the product, boundaries of what is and is not included.
3. **Stakeholder Identification** — Primary, secondary, and tertiary users/influencers.
4. **Functional Requirements (FRs)** — *What* the system must do.
5. **Non-Functional Requirements (NFRs)** — *How well* the system must perform (performance, security, usability, reliability).
6. **Constraints** — Technical, regulatory, budgetary, environmental, ethical.
7. **Assumptions & Dependencies** — External conditions assumed to hold true.
8. **Acceptance Criteria** — Pass/fail conditions for each requirement.
9. **Traceability Matrix** — Linking requirements to design elements and test cases.
10. **Glossary & Appendices** — Definitions, acronyms, supporting data.

### 2.3 KTU Reference Sheet — Requirement Classification Matrix

The following table is the **single most important high-yield reference** for this topic. Memorize the structure; examiners frequently test the ability to **classify a given statement into the correct category**.

| Requirement Type | Question It Answers | Typical Verbs | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Functional Requirement (FR)** | What must the system DO? | shall calculate, shall display, shall transmit | *"The system shall send an SMS alert when the soil moisture drops below 20%."* |
| **Non-Functional Requirement (NFR)** | How WELL must it perform? | shall be, must maintain, shall not exceed | *"The alert shall be delivered within 5 seconds of the threshold breach."* |
| **Performance Requirement** | Speed, capacity, throughput | shall process, shall handle | *"The server shall handle 10,000 concurrent users."* |
| **Usability Requirement** | Ease of use, learnability | shall be operable by, shall require | *"A first-time user shall complete checkout in under 3 minutes."* |
| **Security Requirement** | Confidentiality, integrity | shall encrypt, shall authenticate | *"All payment data shall be encrypted using AES-256."* |
| **Regulatory/Compliance** | Legal/standards adherence | shall comply with, shall meet | *"The medical device shall comply with ISO 13485."* |
| **Constraint** | Imposed limitation | must use, is limited to | *"The device must operate on a 5V USB-C power source."* |
| **Interface Requirement** | Interaction with other systems | shall interface with, shall accept | *"The app shall accept input from Bluetooth Low Energy sensors."* |
| **Environmental** | Operating conditions | shall function at, shall withstand | *"The outdoor unit shall operate from $-10°C$ to $55°C$."* |

### 2.4 The Modal Verb Convention (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU board explicitly tests your understanding of **modal verb hierarchy** in SRDs. Misusing these can cost you 2-3 marks easily in a 14-mark question.

- **SHALL** → Mandatory requirement (must be met for acceptance).
- **SHOULD** → Strongly recommended, but deviations allowed with justification.
- **MAY** → Optional / permissible feature.
- **WILL** → Statement of fact or future intent (not a requirement).

### 2.5 Engineering Utility in the Real World

In the industry, the SRD feeds directly into:
- **System Design Documents (SDD)** — Architectural blueprints derived from FRs.
- **Hardware/Software Requirement Specifications (HRS/SRS)** — More technical descendants of the SRD.
- **Test Plans** — Each acceptance criterion in the SRD becomes a test case.
- **Regulatory Submissions** — Especially critical in medical devices (CDSCO in India), automotive (AIS/ARAI), and aerospace (DGCA).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Development Process & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Seven-Stage SRD Development Process

This is the **exhaustive, derivable procedure** that the KTU 2024 scheme expects you to describe in 14-mark answers. Each stage is presented with its operational logic and output artefact.

#### **Stage 1: Stakeholder Analysis and Requirement Elicitation**

The logical starting point is identifying **who** will use, buy, regulate, or be affected by the product. Without this, requirements float in a vacuum.

**Step-by-step logic:**
- Identify primary users (those who directly interact with the product).
- Identify secondary users (those who influence purchase or support use, e.g., doctors, parents).
- Identify tertiary stakeholders (regulators, manufacturers, recyclers).
- For each stakeholder group, document: goals, pain points, success metrics, and environment of use.

**Output Artefact:** A *Stakeholder Register* (a table listing stakeholder, interest, influence, and priority).

#### **Stage 2: Requirements Gathering (Elicitation Techniques)**

Raw data is collected using techniques, each of which surfaces different requirement types:

$$\text{Requirements} = f(\text{Interviews}, \text{Surveys}, \text{Observation}, \text{Focus Groups}, \text{Workshops}, \text{Document Analysis})$$

**Step-by-step logic:**
- Conduct contextual interviews (Empathize stage output).
- Use surveys for quantitative breadth.
- Observe users in natural settings.
- Run co-design workshops.
- Analyze competitor products and regulatory documents.

**Output Artefact:** A *Raw Requirements Pool* — an unstructured list of 50-200 statements.

#### **Stage 3: Requirements Classification and De-duplication**

The raw pool is then sorted, merged, and categorized. Duplicate or conflicting requirements are resolved here.

**Step-by-step logic:**
- Group similar statements together.
- Eliminate duplicates.
- Tag each statement with its source (which stakeholder, which elicitation technique).
- Classify into the categories from the table in Section 2.3 (FR, NFR, Constraint, etc.).

**Output Artefact:** A *Categorized Requirements List*.

#### **Stage 4: Requirements Analysis and Negotiation**

Conflicts between requirements (e.g., *"lightweight"* vs *"rugged"*) are negotiated using **prioritization frameworks**.

**The MoSCoW Method (Most commonly tested):**
- **M**ust have — Non-negotiable, product fails without it.
- **S**hould have — Important, but workarounds exist.
- **C**ould have — Desirable, included if time/resources permit.
- **W**on't have (this time) — Explicitly out of scope.

**Alternative frameworks:**
- **Kano Model** — Classifies features as Basic, Performance, or Excitement.
- **Weighted Scoring** — Numerical prioritization.

**Output Artefact:** A *Prioritized Requirements List* with MoSCoW tags.

#### **Stage 5: Requirements Specification and Documentation**

Each requirement is now written in the formal **"shall" statement format**.

**The EARS Syntax (Easy Approach to Requirements Syntax) — High-Yield:**

$$\text{Requirement} = \underbrace{\text{Trigger/Precondition}}_{\text{When...}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{System Response}}_{\text{System shall...}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Performance Metric}}_{\text{within/by...}}$$

**Example transformation:**
- *User statement:* "I want the app to remind me to take my medicine."
- *Raw requirement:* "App should have reminders."
- *Formal SRD statement:* *"When the scheduled medication time occurs, the system shall display a visual notification and emit an audible alert within 1 second, with a minimum loudness of 60 dB at 1 meter."*

**Output Artefact:** The *Draft SRD* with formal "shall" statements.

#### **Stage 6: Requirements Validation and Verification Planning**

For each requirement, an **acceptance criterion** is defined, plus the method by which it will be verified.

$$\text{Verification Methods} = \{\text{Inspection}, \text{Demonstration}, \text{Test}, \text{Analysis}\}$$

**Step-by-step logic:**
- For each "shall" statement, define a measurable pass/fail condition.
- Specify the verification method (T for Test, I for Inspection, etc.).
- Identify who is responsible for verification.

**Output Artefact:** *Requirement-to-Test Traceability Matrix (RTM)*.

#### **Stage 7: Review, Approval, and Baseline**

The final draft undergoes formal review by all stakeholders, revisions are incorporated, and the document is **baselined** (frozen at a specific version number, e.g., v1.0).

**Step-by-step logic:**
- Distribute draft to all stakeholders.
- Conduct a Formal Technical Review (FTR).
- Log all change requests.
- Update the document and obtain sign-off.
- Assign a unique document ID and version number.
- Establish a change control process for future modifications.

**Output Artefact:** The *Baselined SRD (v1.0)* with signatures.

### 3.2 The Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) — Detailed Construction

The RTM is a critical analytical tool that **links each requirement to its design implementation, code module, and test case**. A simplified version looks like this:

| Req. ID | Requirement Description | Source | Priority (MoSCoW) | Design Element | Test Case ID | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| FR-001 | System shall detect soil moisture below 20% | Farmer interviews | Must | Moisture sensor module | TC-001 | Verified |
| NFR-002 | Alert shall be sent within 5 sec | SLA commitment | Must | GSM module | TC-005 | In Progress |
| CR-001 | Device shall run on 5V solar | Cost constraint | Must | Power subsystem | TC-010 | Verified |

This RTM ensures **forward traceability** (req → design → test) and **backward traceability** (test → req → stakeholder need).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End SRD Development Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete flow from raw user empathy to a baselined SRD. The architecture uses alphanumeric-only node IDs prefixed with letters to comply with Mermaid parsing rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Stage 1 Stakeholder Analysis] --> A2[Stage 2 Requirement Elicitation]
    A2 --> A3[Stage 3 Classification and Deduplication]
    A3 --> A4[Stage 4 Analysis and Prioritization]
    A4 --> A5[Stage 5 Formal Specification Writing]
    A5 --> A6[Stage 6 Validation and Verification Planning]
    A6 --> A7[Stage 7 Review Approval and Baseline]
    A7 --> A8[Baselined SRD v1.0]
    A8 --> A9[Change Control Process]
    A9 --> A2
    
    B1[Empathize and Define Outputs] --> A2
    A8 --> B2[System Design Document]
    A8 --> B3[Test Plan]
    A8 --> B4[Regulatory Submission]
```

### 4.2 SRD Structural Block Architecture

This diagram shows how the various sections of the SRD interrelate and feed downstream artefacts.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Document Control Header] --> C2[Introduction and Scope]
    C2 --> C3[Stakeholder Register]
    C3 --> C4[Functional Requirements]
    C3 --> C5[Non Functional Requirements]
    C3 --> C6[Constraints]
    C3 --> C7[Assumptions and Dependencies]
    C4 --> C8[Acceptance Criteria]
    C5 --> C8
    C6 --> C8
    C7 --> C8
    C8 --> C9[Traceability Matrix]
    C9 --> C10[Glossary and Appendices]
    
    C8 --> D1[Design Inputs]
    C8 --> D2[Test Cases]
    C8 --> D3[Compliance Evidence]
```

### 4.3 Requirement Classification Decision Tree

This block diagram provides a functional decision logic for classifying a raw requirement into the correct category during Stage 3.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E1[Raw Requirement Statement] --> E2{Is it a measurable performance attribute?}
    E2 -->|Yes| E3[Non Functional Requirement]
    E2 -->|No| E4{Does it describe a system action or behavior?}
    E4 -->|Yes| E5[Functional Requirement]
    E4 -->|No| E6{Is it an imposed external limitation?}
    E6 -->|Yes| E7[Constraint]
    E6 -->|No| E8{Is it derived from law or standard?}
    E8 -->|Yes| E9[Regulatory Requirement]
    E8 -->|No| E10[Re evaluate with stakeholders]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define a Specification Requirements Document (SRD). List any four essential elements that it must contain.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A Specification Requirements Document (SRD) is a structured, formal record that translates user-centered design insights and stakeholder needs into clearly defined, measurable, and testable product requirements. It serves as the authoritative reference bridging the Define and Ideate stages of Design Thinking.

**Four essential elements (1/2 mark each):**
1. **Functional Requirements** — What the system must do.
2. **Non-Functional Requirements** — How well the system must perform (performance, security, usability).
3. **Constraints** — Technical, budgetary, or regulatory limitations.
4. **Acceptance Criteria / Traceability Matrix** — Pass/fail conditions and links to design/test.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Distinguish between Functional Requirements (FR) and Non-Functional Requirements (NFR). Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Functional Requirement | Non-Functional Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | What the system does | How well the system performs |
| **Nature** | Specific behavior or action | Quality attribute or constraint |
| **Measurability** | Often binary (works/doesn’t) | Requires a metric (time, %, dB) |
| **Example** | *"The system shall log in a user upon valid credential entry."* | *"The login process shall complete within 2 seconds for 95% of attempts."* |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the **seven-stage process of developing a Specification Requirements Document (SRD)** in a Design Thinking product development cycle. Highlight the output artefact at each stage. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **MoSCoW prioritization method** and the **EARS syntax** for writing formal requirements. Apply both to write three prioritized, formal "shall" statements for a **smart irrigation system** intended for small-scale Indian farmers. *(7 marks)*

##### **Model Solution for Q A (a):**

The development of an SRD follows a systematic seven-stage process that ensures traceability from user empathy to engineering specification.

**Stage 1 — Stakeholder Analysis:** Identify all primary (farmers), secondary (agronomists), and tertiary (regulators, distributors) users. Output: **Stakeholder Register**.

**Stage 2 — Requirement Elicitation:** Gather raw data via interviews, surveys, field observations, and document analysis (e.g., review of PMKSY scheme guidelines). Output: **Raw Requirements Pool**.

**Stage 3 — Classification & De-duplication:** Sort statements into FRs, NFRs, constraints, and regulatory requirements. Remove duplicates. Output: **Categorized Requirements List**.

**Stage 4 — Analysis & Prioritization:** Use MoSCoW or Kano to resolve conflicts and rank. Output: **Prioritized Requirements List**.

**Stage 5 — Formal Specification:** Rewrite each requirement in the standardized "shall" format using EARS syntax. Output: **Draft SRD**.

**Stage 6 — Validation & Verification Planning:** Define measurable acceptance criteria and verification methods (T, I, D, A). Output: **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)**.

**Stage 7 — Review, Approval & Baseline:** Conduct formal review, log changes, obtain stakeholder sign-off, and freeze the document at v1.0. Output: **Baselined SRD**.

(Each stage is worth ~1 mark with output artefact.)

##### **Model Solution for Q A (b):**

**MoSCoW Method (3 marks):** A prioritization technique that classifies requirements into four buckets:
- **M — Must have:** Critical, product fails without these.
- **S — Should have:** Important but not vital; workarounds acceptable.
- **C — Could have:** Desirable if resources permit.
- **W — Won't have (this time):** Explicitly out of scope for the current release.

**EARS Syntax (2 marks):** Easy Approach to Requirements Syntax — a five-element template:

$$\text{[Precondition], when [Trigger], if [Condition], the [System] shall [Response] within/by [Metric]}$$

**Three Formal "Shall" Statements for Smart Irrigation (2 marks):**

| ID | Priority | Formal Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| FR-001 | Must | *"When the soil moisture sensor reading falls below 20%, the system shall activate the solenoid valve to initiate irrigation within 5 seconds."* |
| NFR-002 | Must | *"The system shall operate continuously for a minimum of 7 days on a single 5V/10W solar charge under standard Indian summer conditions."* |
| FR-003 | Should | *"The system shall send an SMS notification in the local language (Hindi/regional) to the registered farmer's mobile number when irrigation is triggered or when a sensor fault is detected."* |

---

#### **Question B: [Alternative Internal Choice, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** What is a **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)**? Construct a sample RTM for a **portable ECG monitor** with at least five requirements, showing forward traceability from requirement to design element to test case. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **importance of stakeholder analysis** in SRD development. Explain how conflicting requirements between stakeholders can be resolved using negotiation techniques. *(7 marks)*

##### **Model Solution for Q B (a):**

A **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)** is a tabular tool that links every requirement to its source, design implementation, and corresponding test case. It enables **forward traceability** (req → design → test) and **backward traceability** (test → req → stakeholder need), ensuring no requirement is orphaned and no test exists without a basis.

**Sample RTM for a Portable ECG Monitor (5 marks for the table):**

| Req. ID | Requirement | Source | Priority | Design Element | Test Case | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| FR-001 | Device shall capture ECG signal at 250 Hz sampling rate | Cardiologist interview | Must | ADS1292R ADC module | TC-001 (Sampling Accuracy Test) | Verified |
| NFR-002 | Device shall operate for 8 hrs on a single charge | Field nurse feedback | Must | 3.7V 2000 mAh Li-ion battery | TC-010 (Battery Endurance Test) | Verified |
| FR-003 | Device shall display real-time waveform on 2.4" LCD | User (paramedic) | Should | ILI9341 TFT display | TC-015 (Display Latency Test) | In Progress |
| CR-001 | Device shall weigh less than 300 grams | Field usability constraint | Must | ABS polymer enclosure | TC-020 (Weight Verification) | Verified |
| RR-001 | Device shall comply with IEC 60601-1 (medical electrical safety) | Regulatory (CDSCO) | Must | Isolation barrier + creepage design | TC-025 (Dielectric Withstand Test) | In Progress |

##### **Model Solution for Q B (b):**

**Importance of Stakeholder Analysis (3 marks):**
Stakeholder analysis is the foundation of requirement elicitation. It ensures that the SRD captures the needs of **all** affected parties — not just the end user. This prevents costly late-stage redesigns, regulatory rejections, and market failures. By mapping each stakeholder's interests, influence, and success metrics, the design team can prioritize requirements accurately and secure buy-in early in the cycle.

**Resolving Conflicting Requirements via Negotiation (4 marks):**

Conflicts are inevitable. Common examples:
- *User wants "low cost" vs. Engineer wants "high reliability components."*
- *Marketing wants "many features" vs. Usability expert wants "minimal interface."*

**Resolution Techniques:**
1. **Win-Win Negotiation** — Find a hybrid solution (e.g., modular design that allows low-cost base + premium upgrades).
2. **Data-Driven Arbitration** — Use user research data (e.g., 80% of users never use Feature X) to drop low-value features.
3. **Constraint Reframing** — Re-define the constraint boundary (e.g., *"under ₹5000"* becomes *"under ₹5000 for the base model"*).
4. **MoSCoW Tagging** — Push the conflicting feature to "Could have" or "Won't have (this time)."
5. **Trade-off Analysis** — Quantify the cost/benefit of each option and present to the steering committee for decision.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing SRD with SRS:** Many students treat the SRD as a purely technical Software Requirement Specification. The KTU 2024 syllabus for Design Thinking frames the SRD as **broader, human-centered, and design-led**, not a code-level spec. Examiners deduct 2-3 marks if you omit the user-empathy linkage.
> 2. **Using "should" or "must" instead of "shall":** Modal verb convention is graded. Use **"shall"** for all mandatory requirements in an SRD.
> 3. **No measurable metric:** Writing *"The product shall be fast"* is incomplete. Always append a quantifiable metric (*"...within 2 seconds"*).
> 4. **Skipping the Traceability Matrix:** A 14-mark answer that doesn't mention RTM or MoSCoW is considered incomplete in the KTU 2024 scheme.
> 5. **No baselining/versioning:** Forgetting that the SRD must be versioned and formally approved loses easy marks in the documentation block.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must memorize for KTU 2024 exams:**

- **SRD** = Specification Requirements Document, the formal bridge between Define and Ideate stages.
- **Primary purpose:** Translate user empathy into verifiable engineering specifications.
- **Seven Stages:** Stakeholder Analysis → Elicitation → Classification → Prioritization → Specification → Validation → Baseline.
- **Requirement Categories:** FR (what), NFR (how well), Constraint (limit), Regulatory (law), Interface (interop), Environmental (conditions).
- **Modal Verb Hierarchy:** Shall (mandatory) > Should (recommended) > May (optional) > Will (fact/intent).
- **EARS Syntax:** Precondition + Trigger + Condition + System + Response + Metric.
- **MoSCoW Method:** Must / Should / Could / Won't have.
- **SMART-R Criteria:** Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound, Traceable.
- **RTM (Requirements Traceability Matrix):** Links Req → Design → Test (forward) and Test → Req → Stakeholder (backward).
- **Verification vs. Validation:** Verification = "Are we building the product right?" | Validation = "Are we building the right product?"
- **Baselining** freezes the SRD at v1.0; any change goes through a formal change control process.
- The SRD feeds into **System Design Documents, Test Plans, and Regulatory Submissions**.
- **Key exam phrase:** *"Translating user empathy into engineering reality."*
- Avoid writing code or programming snippets in SRD answers — this is a Design Thinking and Product Development module, not a software engineering module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design for manufacturability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design for manufacturability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design for Manufacturability (DFM)** is a systematic, integrated engineering methodology embedded within the Product Development Process (PDP) that optimizes the product's geometry, material selection, tolerances, surface finish, and sub-system architecture to simultaneously minimize manufacturing cost, maximize quality, and reduce time-to-market — *without compromising the functional performance and customer-perceived value* of the product.

In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme vocabulary, DFM is treated as a **concurrent engineering** sub-discipline that bridges the gap between *conceptual design* (Module 2/3 output) and *process planning* (industrial execution). It is one of the "Design for X" (DfX) family — specifically **DfM** for manufacturing, **DfA** for assembly, **DfT** for testing, **DfR** for recyclability, and **DfS** for sustainability.

### 1.1.1 Extended Definitions of Related Sub-Disciplines

| Sub-Discipline | Full Form | Core Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **DfM** | Design for Manufacture | Ease of producing individual parts |
| **DfA** | Design for Assembly | Ease of joining parts into a product |
| **DFMA** | Design for Manufacture & Assembly | Holistic combined methodology (Boothroyd–Dewhurst) |
| **DfC** | Design for Cost | Target costing aligned with BOM |
| **DfT** | Design for Test | Inspectability and testability |
| **DfE** | Design for Environment | End-of-life, recyclability, RoHS compliance |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Recipe" View

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Cooking for 500 Guests vs. Cooking for Family**
>
> Imagine you invent a new gourmet dish. For your family of 4, you can hand-pick tomatoes, finely chop herbs with love, and plate artistically. **Now scale it to 500 guests in a banquet hall.**
>
> Suddenly, your recipe must:
> - Use ingredients that are **bulk-available** (not exotic).
> - Tolerate **knife cuts by unskilled hands** (process variation).
> - Survive being held in a chafing dish for 2 hours (operating conditions).
> - Be **reproducible by 10 different cooks** identically (quality).
> - Cost less than **₹150/plate** (target cost).
>
> **DFM is exactly this translation step** — it is the engineering discipline that says: *"This brilliant conceptual design will not survive contact with the factory floor unless you re-shape it for mass reality."*

## 1.3 The "Three Pillars" of DFM

The KTU syllabus explicitly clusters DFM around three irreducible pillars:

1. **Pillar I — Process Capability Alignment**: Every geometric feature specified in the CAD model must be within the natural capability of the chosen manufacturing process ($C_p \geq 1.33$, $C_{pk} \geq 1.0$).
2. **Pillar II — Cost-of-Function Minimization**: The cost incurred to deliver *each sub-function* is decomposed and re-engineered until the *cost-of-function ratio* is minimized.
3. **Pillar III — Complexity Containment**: Minimize the **Bill-of-Materials (BOM) part count**, **fastener count**, **distinct tooling count**, and **setup count**.

## 1.4 Key Quantitative Metrics (Standard KTU Definitions)

The following bolded constants and ratios are *must-know* for KTU ESE answers:

- **DFM Index** = $\dfrac{\text{Number of parts with manufacturability issues}}{\text{Total number of parts}} \times 100\%$
- **Assembly Efficiency ($\eta_a$)** = $\dfrac{N_{min}}{N_a} \times 100\%$
  - where $N_{min}$ is the theoretical minimum number of parts and $N_a$ is the actual part count.
- **Design Efficiency** = $\dfrac{\text{Time for ideal manual assembly}}{\text{Actual assembly time}} \times 100\%$
- **Boothroyd's Estimated Cost** formula and **Pugh Matrix weighting** are the canonical analysis tools.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Strategic Position of DFM in the PDP

The classical Ulrich–Eppinger PDP consists of six phases:

1. **Planning**
2. **Concept Development**
3. **System-Level Design**
4. **Detail Design** ← DFM activity is *maximum* here
5. **Testing & Refinement**
6. **Production Ramp-Up**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Board Expectation: When asked "Where does DFM fit?", the model answer is: **DFM is iterative and overlapping; it begins at Concept Development (rough cost estimation) and peaks at Detail Design (tolerance, material, process selection), and continues into Production Ramp-Up (DFM-Audit on first articles).**

## 2.2 The 10 Universal DFM Principles (Boothroyd–Dewhurst Framework)

The following principles form the spine of every KTU 14-mark answer on DFM:

### Principle Group A — *Process Selection Principles*

| # | Principle | Why it Matters |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Minimize the number of parts** | Fewer parts → fewer operations, fewer tools, fewer defects. |
| 2 | **Use standardized components** | Standard parts are cheaper, available off-the-shelf, well-characterized. |
| 3 | **Design parts for one primary process** | Avoid multi-process complexity (e.g., part requiring forging + welding + machining). |
| 4 | **Avoid unnecessary tight tolerances** | Tight tolerances increase cost *exponentially* (10× rule). |

### Principle Group B — *Geometry Principles*

| # | Principle | Why it Matters |
|---|---|---|
| 5 | **Design with minimum feature variation** | One feature reused many times lowers tooling cost. |
| 6 | **Use the smallest possible number of fasteners** | Each fastener is an assembly operation (~5–10 seconds). |
| 7 | **Minimize directional reorientation during assembly** | "Z-axis" assembly (top-down gravity-fed) is fastest. |
| 8 | **Design parts to be self-locating / self-aligning** | Eliminates jigs and fixtures. |

### Principle Group C — *Material & Tolerance Principles*

| # | Principle | Why it Matters |
|---|---|---|
| 9 | **Maximize use of symmetry** | Symmetric parts need no orientation detection. |
| 10 | **Use the widest tolerance that meets function** | Cost grows 3×–10× per precision grade (ISO 286). |

## 2.3 Process-Capability and Tolerance Cost Analysis

The **10× Rule of Tolerancing** states:

> For every *halving* of the tolerance band, the manufacturing cost increases by approximately a factor of **10** (Bralla, Handbook of Product Design for Manufacture).

Mathematically:

$$C_{tolerance} \propto \left(\dfrac{1}{T}\right)^{k}, \quad k \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2.0$$

where $T$ is the tolerance band and $k$ is the process-dependent exponent.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAMINER'S GOLD TABLE** — These formulas appear in at least one KTU ESE question per academic year.

| # | Formula | Symbol Glossary | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | USL/LSL = spec limits, $\sigma$ = process std dev | Process capability baseline (must be $\geq 1.33$) |
| 2 | $C_{pk} = \min\left\{\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right\}$ | $\mu$ = process mean | Centered-process capability |
| 3 | $\eta_a = \dfrac{N_{min}}{N_a} \times 100$ | $N_{min}$ = theoretical min parts, $N_a$ = actual | Boothroyd DFA efficiency |
| 4 | $D = \dfrac{N_m + N_f}{N_a}$ | $N_m$ = manual op count, $N_f$ = fasteners | DFA difficulty index |
| 5 | $C_{part} = C_m + C_t + \dfrac{C_0}{n}$ | $C_m$ = material, $C_t$ = tooling, $C_0$ = setup, $n$ = batch size | Per-part manufacturing cost |
| 6 | $\sigma_{stack}^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i^2$ | Statistical tolerance stack-up | Worst-case vs. RSS tolerance |
| 7 | $T_{worst} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} T_i$ | Arithmetic sum of tolerances | Worst-case tolerance |
| 8 | $\Delta C = C_0 (1 - e^{-n/n_0})$ | Learning curve | Cost reduction with cumulative output $n$ |
| 9 | $\text{DFM Index} = \dfrac{N_{issues}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | $N_{issues}$ = flagged parts | Quality gate metric |
| 10 | $R = \dfrac{F_{required}}{F_{available}}$ | Functional redundancy ratio | Consolidation decision index |

## 2.5 The 4-Stage DFM Methodology (Universally Tested in KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Boothroyd–Dewhurst DFMA 4-Stage Workflow:**

1. **Stage 1 — Function Analysis**: Decompose the product into *functions* (not parts). Identify the *minimum part set* using three Boothroyd questions:
   - Does the part move relative to other parts?
   - Must the part be of a different material?
   - Must the part be separable for assembly/disassembly?
2. **Stage 2 — Manufacturing Analysis**: For each candidate part, estimate the **manufacturing cost** using Boothroyd's published cost tables (injection molding, die casting, stamping, machining).
3. **Stage 3 — Assembly Analysis**: Calculate the **assembly cost** using the manual insertion time × labor rate × number of operations.
4. **Stage 4 — Optimization**: Iterate. Combine parts, eliminate fasteners, re-route assembly paths, re-specify tolerances.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry**: Toyota's *kei-car* design philosophy and Ford's *One-Piece-Flow* are textbook DFM applications.
- **Consumer Electronics**: Apple's *unibody machining* for MacBook is DFM (reduces part count from ~50 to ~6).
- **Medical Devices**: DFM enables ISO 13485 compliance by ensuring inspectability.
- **Aerospace**: DFM coexists with DfL (Design for Lightweight) — a classic trade-off studied in KTU Module 5.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Ideal Assembly Time to Design Efficiency

The **Design Efficiency** metric used in Boothroyd's DFA is derived as follows.

### Step 1 — Define the Ideal Assembly Time

The ideal (theoretical minimum) assembly time $t_{ideal}$ is the time required to assemble the *minimum possible* number of parts, each assumed to require 3 seconds of idealized manual insertion (the $\alpha$-model).

$$t_{ideal} = 3 \cdot N_{min} \quad \text{[seconds]}$$

### Step 2 — Define the Actual Assembly Time

The actual assembly time $t_a$ is the sum of the insertion times for all parts plus any handling, fastening, and reorientation overhead.

$$t_a = \sum_{i=1}^{N_a} t_i + t_{handling} + t_{tools} + t_{reorient}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Design Efficiency

$$\boxed{\eta_{design} = \dfrac{t_{ideal}}{t_a} \times 100\%}$$

**Interpretation Table**:

| $\eta_{design}$ Value | Engineering Verdict |
|---|---|
| $\eta_{design} \geq 70\%$ | Excellent DFA design (publication-grade) |
| $50\% \leq \eta_{design} < 70\%$ | Good, room for consolidation |
| $30\% \leq \eta_{design} < 50\%$ | Acceptable but inefficient |
| $\eta_{design} < 30\%$ | Redesign mandatory |

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Statistical vs. Worst-Case Tolerance Stack-Up

When $k$ tolerances are summed in a closed-loop dimension, two models exist.

### Step 1 — Worst-Case (Arithmetic) Stack-Up

The *absolute worst* outcome occurs when all tolerances deviate in the same direction:

$$T_{worst} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} T_i$$

This is the **safe (conservative)** estimate and is used in aerospace, medical, and safety-critical assemblies.

### Step 2 — Root-Sum-Square (Statistical) Stack-Up

Assuming each dimension's tolerance deviation is an **independent, normally-distributed** random variable, the variances add (not the absolute values):

$$\sigma_{stack}^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i^2$$

The stack-up tolerance at a confidence level of $\pm 3\sigma$ is:

$$T_{RSS} = 3 \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i^2}$$

### Step 3 — Equivalence via $\pm 3\sigma$

If we map each tolerance $T_i$ to its $\pm 3\sigma$ equivalent (i.e., $\sigma_i = T_i / 6$), then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{RSS} &= 3 \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \left(\dfrac{T_i}{6}\right)^2} \\
&= 3 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{36}\sum_{i=1}^{k} T_i^2} \\
&= \dfrac{3}{6} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{k} T_i^2} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{k} T_i^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Comparison

$$T_{worst} = \sum T_i \;\;\geq\;\; T_{RSS} = \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\sum T_i^2}$$

The RSS method yields **tighter** tolerance budgets, allowing looser individual specifications — *exactly the DFM goal*.

---

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — Boothroyd DFA on a Pen

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (High KTU Yield)**

A ball-point pen assembly currently has **8 parts**. Using Boothroyd's three questions, the analyst determines the theoretical minimum is **4 parts**. Estimated manual assembly time per part = 3 seconds, but actual measured $t_a$ = 38 seconds.

### Part (a): Compute Assembly Efficiency

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_a &= \dfrac{N_{min}}{N_a} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{4}{8} \times 100 \\
&= 50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part (b): Compute Design Efficiency

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{ideal} &= 3 \times N_{min} = 3 \times 4 = 12 \text{ seconds} \\
\eta_{design} &= \dfrac{12}{38} \times 100 \\
&= 31.58\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part (c): DFM Verdict

Since $\eta_{design} < 50\%$, the pen is **redesign-mandatory** under Boothroyd criteria. Recommendations:
1. Combine the barrel + ink cartridge holder into a single molded part.
2. Eliminate the spring-clip fastener using an *integral snap-fit* (reduces $N_f$).
3. Use top-down *gravity-fed* assembly to reduce reorientation time.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection Decision Grid

The following 6×6 matrix is the canonical KTU scoring tool for **process selection** in DFM:

| Process \ Criterion | Volume Suitability | Tolerance ($\mu m$) | Surface Finish ($R_a \;\mu m$) | Tooling Cost (₹) | Material Wastage | Setup Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sand Casting** | Low–Medium | ±500 | 6.3 – 25 | Low | High | Low |
| **Die Casting** | High | ±100 | 1.6 – 6.3 | Very High | Low | High |
| **Injection Molding** | Very High | ±50 | 0.4 – 1.6 | Very High | Low | High |
| **CNC Machining** | Low–Medium | ±10 | 0.4 – 3.2 | Medium | High | Low |
| **Sheet Metal Stamping** | High | ±200 | 1.6 – 6.3 | High | Medium | Medium |
| **3D Printing (FDM/SLA)** | Prototype | ±100 | 6.3 – 12.5 | Negligible | Low | Negligible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb**: For production volumes $> 10{,}000$ units/year, *injection molding* and *die casting* dominate. For $< 100$ units/year, *3D printing* and *machining* dominate. DFM decision = *intersection of volume, tolerance, and unit-cost*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DFM Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following diagram maps the **interactions** between DFM sub-systems — substitute for a physical sketch of the methodology.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Concept Design Output] --> B[Function Analysis]
    B --> C[Function Decomposition Tree]
    C --> D{Minimum Part Set?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Manufacturing Process Selection]
    D -- No --> F[Re-iterate Function Analysis]
    E --> G[Tolerance & Material Allocation]
    G --> H[Cost Estimation Engine]
    H --> I{Cost within Target?}
    I -- Yes --> J[DFM Compliant Design]
    I -- No --> K[DFM Redesign Loop]
    K --> B
    J --> L[Assembly Path Simulation]
    L --> M[Assembly Efficiency Check]
    M --> N{ηa >= 70%?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Release to Detail Design]
    N -- No --> K
    O --> P[Prototype & First Article]
    P --> Q[Continuous DFM Audit]
```

## 4.2 DFM Hierarchy of Decision-Making

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Level 1: Product Architecture] --> L2[Level 2: Sub-system Configuration]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3: Part Geometry]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4: Material & Process Selection]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5: Tolerance & Surface Finish]
    L5 --> L6[Level 6: Tooling & Fixture Strategy]
    L1 -.->|Feedback| L6
    L6 -.->|Feedback| L1
```

## 4.3 Tolerance Stack-Up Comparison (Worst Case vs. RSS)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[T1] --> S[Sum Squares]
    B[T2] --> S
    C[T3] --> S
    D[Tk] --> S
    S --> O1[T_RSS = Half sqrt of sum T_i squared]
    A --> O2
    B --> O2
    C --> O2
    D --> O2
    O2[T_worst = Sum T_i]
    O1 --> X[Looser individual tolerances permitted]
    O2 --> Y[Tighter overall but conservative]
    X --> Z[DFM Benefit: Cost Reduction]
    Y --> Z
```

## 4.4 Part Consolidation Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Identify candidate pair of parts] --> Q1{Part moves relative?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1[Keep separate]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Different material needed?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R1
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Must be separable?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> R1
    Q3 -- No --> S1[Consolidate into single part]
    S1 --> T1[Update BOM]
    R1 --> U1[Retain as separate]
    U1 --> T1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA). List any four guiding principles.**

**Model Answer:**

> **DFMA** is a concurrent engineering methodology that integrates **Design for Manufacture (DfM)** and **Design for Assembly (DfA)** to reduce product cost, improve quality, and shorten the development cycle by optimizing both individual part production and final product assembly.
>
> **Four Guiding Principles:**
> 1. **Minimize the total part count** — fewer parts = fewer operations and lower inventory cost.
> 2. **Use standard, off-the-shelf components** — eliminates custom tooling.
> 3. **Design for the chosen manufacturing process** — avoid features that exceed process capability.
> 4. **Apply the widest tolerance consistent with function** — preserves process capability and reduces rejection rate.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**What is the Boothroyd–Dewhurst "Three Questions" test? Briefly state the questions.**

**Model Answer:**

> The Boothroyd three-question test is a screening tool used during the **Function Analysis** stage of DFMA to decide whether two adjacent parts can be *consolidated* into a single part.
>
> The three questions are:
> 1. **Does the part move relative to all other parts during operation?** (If yes → must remain separate.)
> 2. **Must the part be made of a different material than other parts?** (If yes → must remain separate.)
> 3. **Must the part be separable from the other parts for assembly, service, or recycling?** (If yes → must remain separate.)
>
> If the answer to **all three** is *No*, the parts may be consolidated into a single component.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule**: Part B carries 14 marks, organized as sub-parts of 7 + 7 marks. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B (internal choice).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **Boothroyd–Dewhurst DFMA methodology** in detail, describing all four stages with their inputs, activities, and outputs. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A product has the following assembly data: theoretical minimum parts = 5, actual parts = 12, ideal assembly time = 3 s/part, measured actual assembly time = 48 s. Compute the **assembly efficiency** and **design efficiency**, and recommend specific DFM redesign actions if any. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

**Stage 1 — Information Preparation**

- Inputs: Engineering drawing, BOM, functional requirements, production volume, target cost.
- Activities: Build a 3D CAD model, generate the assembly sequence, identify candidate sub-functions.
- Output: An *Information Package* containing drawings, dimensions, materials, and the assembly tree.

**Stage 2 — Function Analysis**

- Activities: Decompose the product into sub-functions (e.g., "retain ink", "deliver ink to tip", "provide grip").
- Apply the Boothroyd **three-question test** to each pair of candidate parts to identify the *theoretical minimum part set*.
- Output: A **Minimum Part Set** list with justification.

**Stage 3 — Manufacturing Analysis**

- For each candidate part, choose a candidate manufacturing process from the standard Boothroyd catalog (injection molding, die casting, stamping, machining, etc.).
- Compute the **manufacturing cost** using:
$$C_{part} = C_m + C_t + \dfrac{C_0}{n}$$
- Output: A ranked list of *process candidates* per part with estimated cost.

**Stage 4 — Assembly Analysis & Optimization**

- Estimate manual insertion time per part (typically 1.5 – 3 s for simple insertion, up to 15 s for fastener tightening).
- Calculate total assembly time and labor cost.
- Iterate: combine parts, eliminate fasteners, design snap-fits, vertical assembly paths.
- Output: A *redesigned assembly* with reduced $N_a$ and $t_a$.

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Given:**
- $N_{min} = 5$
- $N_a = 12$
- $t_{ideal} = 3 \text{ s/part}$
- $t_a = 48 \text{ s}$

**Step 1 — Assembly Efficiency $\eta_a$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_a &= \dfrac{N_{min}}{N_a} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{5}{12} \times 100 \\
&= 41.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Ideal Assembly Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{ideal} &= 3 \times N_{min} \\
&= 3 \times 5 \\
&= 15 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Design Efficiency $\eta_{design}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{design} &= \dfrac{t_{ideal}}{t_a} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{15}{48} \times 100 \\
&= 31.25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verdict:** Both efficiencies are **below the 50% threshold**, indicating the design is **inefficient** and requires DFM intervention.

**Step 5 — Recommended DFM Redesign Actions:**

1. **Part Consolidation:** Apply Boothroyd's three-question test; consolidate the 12 parts toward the 5-part theoretical minimum (target $N_a = 6$).
2. **Eliminate Fasteners:** Replace threaded fasteners with *integral snap-fits* or *living hinges*.
3. **Vertical Assembly:** Reorient the assembly sequence so parts are inserted top-down by gravity, reducing reorientation time.
4. **Standardize Components:** Use standard clips, pins, and bearings instead of custom fasteners.
5. **Tolerance Review:** Loosen non-critical tolerances from IT7 to IT9 where function permits (3× cost saving per grade).

**Final Efficiency after Redesign (Estimated):** If $N_a$ reduces to 6 and $t_a$ reduces to 22 s, then:

$$
\eta_a = \dfrac{5}{6} \times 100 = 83.3\% \quad ; \quad \eta_{design} = \dfrac{15}{22} \times 100 = 68.2\%
$$

Both exceed the 70% benchmark → design is **DFM-compliant**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** Discuss the **"10× Rule of Tolerancing"** as applied in DFM. Explain how tolerance choice affects manufacturing cost, with a numerical illustration for a shaft–hole fit. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a closed-loop assembly of 4 dimensions $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$ each equal to ±0.1 mm, calculate the **worst-case stack-up tolerance** and the **Root-Sum-Square (RSS) tolerance**. Comment on which value should be used for safety-critical vs. mass-produced assemblies. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**The 10× Rule Definition:**

> The 10× Rule states that **each halving of a tolerance band increases the manufacturing cost by approximately a factor of 10**, due to the need for higher-precision machines, tighter process control, lower feed-rates, and higher rejection rates.

**Cost-vs-Tolerance Relationship:**

$$
C_{tolerance} \propto \left(\dfrac{1}{T}\right)^k, \quad k \approx 1.5 - 2.0
$$

**Numerical Illustration — Shaft–Hole Fit:**

Consider a shaft of nominal diameter 50 mm with the following tolerance grades:

| Tolerance Grade | Tolerance Band $T$ | Approx. Relative Cost Factor |
|---|---|---|
| ISO IT9 | ±0.062 mm | **1×** (baseline) |
| ISO IT7 | ±0.025 mm | ~**10×** (one halving step) |
| ISO IT5 | ±0.011 mm | ~**100×** (two halving steps) |
| ISO IT3 | ±0.004 mm | ~**1000×** (three halving steps) |

**Implication in DFM:**

- For a *locating surface* (high functional load) → use IT7.
- For a *non-critical decorative surface* → use IT11 or coarser.
- **Always specify the widest tolerance that satisfies the engineering function** — this is the **core DFM cost-saving rule**.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**Given:** $T_1 = T_2 = T_3 = T_4 = \pm 0.1 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 1 — Worst-Case Stack-Up:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{worst} &= T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4 \\
&= 0.1 + 0.1 + 0.1 + 0.1 \\
&= \pm 0.4 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — RSS Stack-Up:**

First, map each tolerance to its standard deviation ($\sigma_i = T_i / 6$):

$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = \sigma_4 = \dfrac{0.1}{6} = 0.01667 \text{ mm}$$

Now compute the stack-up variance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{stack}^2 &= \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 + \sigma_4^2 \\
&= 4 \times (0.01667)^2 \\
&= 4 \times 0.0002778 \\
&= 0.001111
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the standard deviation of the stack-up:

$$\sigma_{stack} = \sqrt{0.001111} = 0.03333 \text{ mm}$$

The $\pm 3\sigma$ tolerance is:

$$
T_{RSS} = 3 \cdot \sigma_{stack} = 3 \times 0.03333 = \pm 0.1 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison Table:**

| Method | Tolerance Band |
|---|---|
| **Worst-Case** ($T_{worst}$) | **±0.4 mm** |
| **RSS ($\pm 3\sigma$)** ($T_{RSS}$) | **±0.1 mm** |
| **Difference** | RSS is **4× tighter** than worst-case |

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation:**

- **Safety-critical assemblies** (aircraft landing gear, medical implants, automotive brake systems): Use **Worst-Case tolerance stack-up** to guarantee 100% interchangeability and absolute safety.
- **Mass-produced consumer assemblies** (mobile phones, toys, packaging): Use **RSS tolerance stack-up** since statistical probability of all four dimensions deviating in the same direction simultaneously is *vanishingly small*. This permits looser individual tolerances → lower per-part cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Do not confuse $T_i$ (a tolerance band) with $\sigma_i$ (a standard deviation).** $T_i = 6\sigma_i$ ONLY for the standard $\pm 3\sigma$ convention — some texts use $\pm 2\sigma$ (giving $T_i = 4\sigma_i$). Always state your convention explicitly.
> 2. **RSS assumes independence.** If dimensions are correlated (e.g., both come from the same machine setup), the RSS formula *over-counts* the variance and gives an unrealistically tight stack-up.
> 3. **In Part A answers**, students often write *only the formula* without computing $\sigma_i$ first — this loses 1–2 marks. Always show the $\sigma_i$ computation.
> 4. **In 14-mark answers**, students frequently forget to include a *recommendation section* — DFM questions always expect a *redesign recommendation*. Omitting it loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — DFM (Module 4)**

- **Definition**: DFM is a concurrent-engineering methodology optimizing geometry, material, tolerance, and architecture to minimize cost *without compromising function*.
- **Three Pillars**: (1) Process capability alignment, (2) Cost-of-function minimization, (3) Complexity containment.
- **Core Metric**: Assembly Efficiency $\eta_a = N_{min} / N_a \times 100$ — *aim for $\geq 70\%$*.
- **Design Efficiency**: $\eta_{design} = t_{ideal} / t_a \times 100$ — *aim for $\geq 70\%$*.
- **Boothroyd 3-Question Test**: Movement? Material? Separability? — *All "No" → consolidate.*
- **10× Rule**: Halving the tolerance band increases cost by ~10×.
- **Worst-Case Stack-Up**: $T_{worst} = \sum T_i$ — for safety-critical.
- **RSS Stack-Up**: $T_{RSS} = \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\sum T_i^2}$ — for mass production.
- **4-Stage DFMA Workflow**: (1) Information Preparation → (2) Function Analysis → (3) Manufacturing Analysis → (4) Assembly Analysis & Optimization.
- **Universal DFM Principles** (Boothroyd–Dewhurst): 10 principles grouped into Process / Geometry / Material-Tolerance clusters.
- **Process Selection Rule**: For volume $> 10^4$ / year → Injection Molding / Die Casting; for volume $< 100$ / year → CNC / 3D Printing.
- **KTU Common Pitfall**: Forgetting the *redesign recommendation* section in 14-mark answers = −3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design to cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design to cost

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Design to Cost (DTC)** is a structured, target-driven product development methodology in which the **target selling price** and the **target profit margin** are determined *before* the design phase begins, and the **allowable (target) cost** of the product is treated as a **non-negotiable design constraint** — a "design parameter" on par with weight, performance, and reliability.

Mathematically, the foundational relationship is expressed as:

$$
\text{Target Cost} \;=\; \text{Target Selling Price} \;-\; \text{Desired Profit Margin}
$$

**Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Scheme Glossary):**

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Target Cost** | The maximum allowable manufacturing + life-cycle cost that the product must achieve |
| **Target Selling Price** | The estimated market-driven price the customer is willing to pay |
| **Target Margin** | The profit percentage the company aims to retain |
| **Should-Cost** | The theoretically minimum achievable cost of a component based on its fundamentals |
| **Cost Breakdown Structure (CBS)** | Hierarchical decomposition of the product's cost into subsystems, assemblies, and parts |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House-Building Budget"

Imagine you have **₹50 Lakhs** to build your dream home in Kerala, and you want a **20% return on investment** if you ever sell it. You do not start by hiring an architect to draw "the most luxurious house possible." Instead, you **reverse-engineer the budget**:

1. Determine the market value of a similar 3-BHK house in the locality → **Target Price**
2. Subtract your required profit → **Target Cost = ₹40 Lakhs**
3. Tell the architect: *"The entire house — land, materials, labour, interiors, electrical, plumbing — MUST be built for ₹40 Lakhs."*
4. The architect then makes intelligent trade-offs (smaller balcony, locally sourced laterite, modular kitchen) so that the **design conforms to the budget**, not the other way around.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design to Cost = Designing *within* the budget, instead of designing first and *hoping* the budget is met later.** This is the central paradigm shift that DTC introduces over traditional cost-accounting approaches.

---

## 1.3 The Paradigm Shift: Cost-As-Input vs. Cost-As-Output

In **traditional design**, the sequence is:

> *Design → Estimate cost → Accept / Reject*

In **Design to Cost**, the sequence is reversed:

> *Market Price − Profit = Target Cost → Design to that cost*

This is why DTC is often called **"Cost-Down" engineering** or **"Target Costing."** It is a cornerstone of *Lean Product Development* and is the bridge between marketing strategy and engineering design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Phase DTC Framework

The execution of Design to Cost follows a disciplined, five-phase methodology:

### Phase 1 — Market & Competitive Analysis
- Identify the **target customer segment** and their willingness-to-pay.
- Benchmark competing products on **feature-vs-price** matrices.
- Derive the **target selling price** ($P_{\text{target}}$) using conjoint analysis, Van Westendorp's PSM, or historical pricing data.

### Phase 2 — Profit Planning
- Set the **target profit margin** ($\pi_{\text{target}}$) based on corporate strategy (typically **8%–15%** for consumer goods, **20%–30%** for industrial B2B).
- Compute the **allowable cost**:
$$
C_{\text{allowable}} = P_{\text{target}} \times \left(1 - \pi_{\text{target}}\right)
$$

### Phase 3 — Cost Decomposition (Cost Breakdown Structure)
- Disassemble the allowable cost into **sub-system targets** and **component targets**.
- Example split for a smartphone: Display 25% · SoC 20% · Battery 8% · Camera 12% · PCB & Passives 15% · Assembly & Margin 20%.

### Phase 4 — Should-Cost Modeling
- For each component, compute the **theoretical minimum cost** using first-principles (raw material weight × material rate + process cost + logistics).
- This is the **"should-cost"** — what the part *should* cost in a perfectly efficient world.

### Phase 5 — Design, Verify, Iterate
- Suppliers quote **"will-cost"** (what they will actually charge).
- The gap $G = C_{\text{will}} - C_{\text{should}}$ is closed through:
  - Design simplification
  - Material substitution
  - Process optimization
  - Volume negotiation
  - Value Engineering / Value Analysis (VE / VA)

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{\text{target}} = P_{\text{market}} - M_{\text{profit}}$ | Fundamental target cost identity | Currency (₹, $) |
| 2 | $C_{\text{allowable}} = P_{\text{target}} \cdot (1 - \pi)$ | Allowable cost from margin | $\pi$ as decimal (0.20 = 20%) |
| 3 | $C_{\text{should}} = m \cdot \rho \cdot p_{\text{mat}} + C_{\text{process}}$ | Should-cost of a part | $m$ = mass, $\rho$ = density |
| 4 | $G = C_{\text{will}} - C_{\text{should}}$ | Cost gap to close | Must be $\geq 0$ |
| 5 | $C_{\text{LCC}} = C_{\text{acq}} + C_{\text{oper}} + C_{\text{maint}} - C_{\text{salvage}}$ | Life-Cycle Cost | Sum over N years |
| 6 | $C_{\text{unit}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{fixed}}}{N} + C_{\text{variable}}$ | Per-unit cost (break-even) | $N$ = production volume |
| 7 | $\text{Cost Reduction \%} = \dfrac{C_{\text{baseline}} - C_{\text{new}}}{C_{\text{baseline}}} \times 100$ | Savings metric | Expressed in % |
| 8 | $T_{\text{learn}} = T_1 \cdot N^{-b}$ | Learning curve (Wright's Law) | $b$ = learning index |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, **Equations 1, 2, 4, 5, and 7** are the most frequently tested. Memorize them with their associated decision variables.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (e.g., Tata, Maruti):** Use DTC to launch feature-loaded cars at price points competitive with global OEMs.
- **Consumer Electronics (Xiaomi, OnePlus):** Aggressive DTC enables flagship-killer pricing.
- **Aerospace & Defence (HAL, DRDO):** DTC is mandated for indigenous platforms where cost ceilings are set by the Ministry of Defence.
- **Medical Devices:** LCC analysis ensures the *total cost of ownership* (including disposables and maintenance) is affordable to tier-2/3 hospitals.

The strategic value of DTC lies in **forcing cross-functional collaboration** between Marketing, Finance, Design, and Manufacturing *before* a single CAD file is opened.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Target Cost from Market Price & Margin

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):** A company wants to launch a new Bluetooth speaker. Market research indicates customers will pay at most **₹3,000**. The company mandates a **20% gross margin** on every unit sold. Determine the **target manufacturing cost** per unit.

**Step 1 — Identify the variables.**
$P_{\text{target}} = \text{₹3,000}$ and $\pi_{\text{target}} = 20\% = 0.20$.

**Step 2 — Apply the fundamental DTC identity.**
The relationship between price, cost, and margin is:

$$
\text{Price} \;=\; \text{Cost} \;+\; \text{Profit}
$$

where Profit = Price $\times$ margin.

**Step 3 — Rearrange algebraically to isolate Cost.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Price} &= \text{Cost} + \text{Price} \times \pi \\
\text{Price} - \text{Price} \times \pi &= \text{Cost} \\
\text{Price} \times (1 - \pi) &= \text{Cost} \\
C_{\text{target}} &= P_{\text{target}} \times (1 - \pi_{\text{target}})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{target}} &= 3000 \times (1 - 0.20) \\
&= 3000 \times 0.80 \\
&= \text{₹2,400}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**
The design team has a hard ceiling of **₹2,400 per unit** to cover all materials, labour, assembly, logistics, and overhead. Any design exceeding this violates the DTC constraint.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cost Gap Analysis (Should-Cost vs. Will-Cost)

**Problem Setup:** A sub-component (the speaker's lithium battery pack) is allocated a cost target of **₹450** within the ₹2,400 envelope. A supplier quotes a **will-cost** of **₹520**.

- **Battery raw mass** = 0.08 kg, **Lithium price** = ₹1,200/kg
- **Cell + housing + BMS process cost** = ₹150
- **Logistics + duty** = ₹60

**Step 1 — Compute the Should-Cost (first principles).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{should}} &= m_{\text{cell}} \cdot p_{\text{material}} + C_{\text{process}} + C_{\text{logistics}} \\
&= 0.08 \times 1200 + 150 + 60 \\
&= 96 + 150 + 60 \\
&= \text{₹306}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Cost Gap.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G &= C_{\text{will}} - C_{\text{should}} \\
&= 520 - 306 \\
&= \text{₹214 of avoidable slack}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the target compliance ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Compliance} &= \frac{C_{\text{will}} - C_{\text{target}}}{C_{\text{target}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{520 - 450}{450} \times 100 \\
&= 15.56\% \text{ over target}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Action Plan.**
The team must execute at least one of:
- Negotiate supplier from ₹520 → ₹450 (₹70 savings).
- Substitute cell chemistry (e.g., LFP instead of NMC) for ₹40 saving.
- Reduce logistics via local sourcing for ₹50 saving.
- Consolidate BMS onto main PCB for ₹60 saving.

**Target reduction required = ₹70 minimum to comply with the ₹450 target.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Learning Curve Application in DTC

Wright's Law states that every time cumulative production volume **doubles**, the unit cost falls to a constant fraction of the previous cost.

$$
C_n = C_1 \cdot n^{-b}
$$

where $C_1$ = first-unit cost, $n$ = unit number, $b$ = learning index (typically 0.20–0.35 in assembly).

**Example:** First speaker unit costs ₹1,200 in labour. Learning index $b = 0.25$. Cost of the **100th unit**?

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{100} &= 1200 \times 100^{-0.25} \\
100^{-0.25} &= \frac{1}{100^{0.25}} = \frac{1}{3.162} = 0.3162 \\
C_{100} &= 1200 \times 0.3162 = \text{₹379.46}
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows why **high-volume products** (smartphones, EVs) can use DTC more aggressively — the learning curve *guarantees* that costs will fall, so the initial target is achievable.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix (DTC vs. Traditional Cost Engineering)

| Criterion | Traditional Cost-Accounting | Design to Cost (DTC) |
|---|---|---|
| **When is cost decided?** | After design is complete | Before design begins |
| **Cost driver** | Engineering features | Market & customer |
| **Designer role** | Cost is "given" externally | Cost is a "design variable" |
| **Iteration cycle** | Late, expensive rework | Early, low-cost changes |
| **Risk of overshoot** | High | Mitigated via should-cost |
| **Cross-functional involvement** | Siloed | Marketing + Finance + Design + SCM |
| **Metric** | Actual cost | Gap to target cost |
| **KTU exam weightage** | Moderate | **High (recurring topic)** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DTC Top-Level Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Market Research & Customer Willingness-to-Pay] --> B[Determine Target Selling Price P_target]
    B --> C[Set Desired Profit Margin pi]
    C --> D[Compute Target Cost: C_target = P_target x 1-pi]
    D --> E[Build Cost Breakdown Structure CBS]
    E --> F[Allocate Sub-system Cost Targets]
    F --> G[Compute Should-Cost per Component]
    G --> H[Obtain Will-Cost from Suppliers]
    H --> I{Cost Gap greater than 0?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Apply VE / VA: Material, Process, Design]
    I -- No --> K[Lock Design and Release to Production]
    J --> L[Re-quote and Iterate]
    L --> H
    K --> M[Monitor & Continuous Improvement]

    style D fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#bf9000,color:#ffffff
```


## 4.3 Functional Architecture: The DTC Closed-Loop Control System

```mermaid

    MARKET[Market Input: Price Ceiling] --> CTRL[Cost Controller: Target = P - M]
    CTRL --> DESIGN[Design Team: Generates Concepts]
```
