# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Design to Cost (DTC) and state its primary objective.**

**Model Answer:**
Design to Cost is a product development strategy in which the target cost of a product is determined *before* the design phase, by subtracting the desired profit margin from the market-driven target selling price. Its primary objective is to **treat cost as an independent design variable (a constraint)**, forcing the engineering team to design *to* the cost target rather than estimating cost *after* design. This eliminates late-stage cost overruns and ensures market viability from day one.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for the equation, 1 for the objective)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between "Should-Cost" and "Will-Cost" with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Should-Cost | Will-Cost |
|---|---|---|
| **Meaning** | The theoretical minimum cost of a part based on raw material, mass, and process fundamentals | The actual cost quoted by the supplier or calculated by current process |
| **Approach** | First-principles, ideal-world estimation | Bottom-up, real-world quote |
| **Example** | Should-cost of an aluminium bracket = mass (0.5 kg) × Al rate (₹400/kg) + machining (₹60) = **₹260** | The same bracket quoted by a vendor at **₹340** |
| **Use in DTC** | Sets the *floor*; reveals cost gap | Sets the *current reality*; the gap = improvement opportunity |

*(3 marks: 1 mark each for distinction + 1 mark for examples)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

**(a)** A startup plans to launch a smart fitness band. Market research shows customers are willing to pay a maximum of **₹4,500**. The company targets a **gross margin of 25%**. The product is to be manufactured in a batch of **50,000 units**.

Compute:
(i) The target cost per unit.
(ii) The total allowable cost for the entire production run.
(iii) If the company achieves the target on the first 30,000 units but the next 20,000 units cost ₹200 more per unit due to logistics, what is the **average effective cost per unit**?

**(b)** With the help of a neat flowchart, explain the **five phases of Design to Cost (DTC)** methodology. For each phase, mention the **key deliverable** and the **stakeholder responsible**.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a):

**(i) Target cost per unit:**
$$
C_{\text{target}} = P_{\text{target}} \times (1 - \pi) = 4500 \times (1 - 0.25) = 4500 \times 0.75 = \text{₹3,375}
$$

**(ii) Total allowable cost for 50,000 units:**
$$
C_{\text{total}} = 3375 \times 50{,}000 = \text{₹16,87,50,000} = \text{₹16.875 Crores}
$$

**(iii) Weighted average cost per unit:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{(30{,}000 \times 3375) + (20{,}000 \times 3575)}{50{,}000} \\
&= \frac{10{,}12{,}50{,}000 + 7{,}15{,}00{,}000}{50{,}000} \\
&= \frac{17{,}27{,}50{,}000}{50{,}000} \\
&= \text{₹3,455 per unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

The overrun is ₹3,455 − ₹3,375 = **₹80 per unit**, eroding the margin by 80/4500 = 1.78 percentage points (now margin is ~23.22%).

*(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 3 = 7)*

#### Model Solution to Question A (b):

The five phases of DTC are:

| Phase | Key Deliverable | Responsible Stakeholder |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Market & Competitive Analysis | Target Selling Price (₹4,500) | Marketing & Sales |
| 2. Profit Planning | Target Margin (25%) → Allowable Cost | Finance / Top Management |
| 3. Cost Decomposition (CBS) | Sub-system & component cost allocation | Cost Engineering / PMO |
| 4. Should-Cost Modeling | Theoretical minimum cost per part | Manufacturing Engineering |
| 5. Design, Verify, Iterate | Locked design within target + VE/VA report | Cross-functional DTC team |

The flowchart shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes should be redrawn in the answer booklet, with each box labelled with the phase number and stakeholder.

*(7 marks: 5 marks for the five-phase explanation in table form + 2 marks for the flowchart sketch)*

---

### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

**(a)** "Design to Cost is not merely cost reduction; it is a **strategic alignment** of engineering, marketing, and finance." Critically analyse this statement with **four supporting arguments**. For each argument, cite one real-world industry example.

**(b)** A washing machine manufacturer sets a target cost of **₹12,000** for a new front-load model. The motor sub-system is allocated **₹2,400 (20%)**. A supplier quotes a will-cost of **₹2,750** for a 1.2 kW BLDC motor.

Compute:
(i) The **cost gap** between will-cost and target.
(ii) The **should-cost**, given: copper mass = 0.5 kg, copper rate = ₹900/kg, steel mass = 2 kg, steel rate = ₹150/kg, magnets and bearings = ₹350, assembly labour = ₹180.
(iii) Recommend **two specific design actions** to close the gap.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a):

**Argument 1 — Customer-Centricity:** DTC begins with the *customer's wallet*, ensuring the product is *affordable* by design. **Example:** Xiaomi's Redmi series uses DTC to deliver 90 Hz AMOLED displays at ₹10,000 — a segment where competitors assumed customers would only accept LCD.

**Argument 2 — Cross-Functional Integration:** DTC forces Marketing (price ceiling), Finance (margin), and Engineering (design) to collaborate from day zero. **Example:** Toyota's product development system uses *target costing* with cross-functional *obeya* (big room) teams to align all three functions.

**Argument 3 — Waste Elimination (Lean Thinking):** By comparing should-cost vs. will-cost, DTC exposes every rupee of *muda* (waste) in the supply chain. **Example:** Boeing's 787 programme applied should-cost analysis globally to reduce part counts by 30%.

**Argument 4 — Early Risk Mitigation:** Cost decisions made in the *concept* stage cost **1×** to change; the same decision in production costs **100× to 1000×**. DTC forces decisions early. **Example:** Tesla re-engineered the Model 3 dashboard multiple times in CAD — changes that cost dollars on screen would have cost millions on the assembly line.

*(7 marks: 4 arguments × 1.5 marks each = 6 marks + 1 mark for conclusion/synthesis)*

#### Model Solution to Question B (b):

**(i) Cost Gap:**
$$
G = C_{\text{will}} - C_{\text{target}} = 2750 - 2400 = \text{₹350 (over target)}
$$

**(ii) Should-Cost (first principles):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{should}} &= m_{\text{Cu}} \cdot p_{\text{Cu}} + m_{\text{Fe}} \cdot p_{\text{Fe}} + C_{\text{magnets}} + C_{\text{labour}} \\
&= (0.5 \times 900) + (2 \times 150) + 350 + 180 \\
&= 450 + 300 + 350 + 180 \\
&= \text{₹1,280}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cost slack available = ₹2,400 − ₹1,280 = ₹1,120** of theoretical room (indicates either over-engineering or supplier inefficiency).

**(iii) Two Design Actions:**

1. **Material Optimisation:** Switch to aluminium rotor housing (2 kg × ₹250 = ₹500) instead of steel (2 kg × ₹150 = ₹300) — wait, aluminium is *more* expensive; instead, reduce steel mass by 30% through topology optimization → savings of 0.6 kg × ₹150 = **₹90** + reduced shipping.

2. **Design for Manufacturing (DFM):** Integrate the motor controller PCB into the main appliance PCB to eliminate the separate housing, connectors, and assembly steps — saving approximately **₹150–200** in BOM consolidation.

3. *(Bonus)* Volume negotiation: Committing to 50,000 units/year typically fetches a 10–12% supplier discount → saves **₹275–330** on the will-cost.

*(7 marks: 2 + 3 + 2 = 7)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in DTC Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "Cost" with "Price":** Price is what the customer pays; Cost is what the company spends. The difference is *profit*. Many students write $C = P$ — this loses 1–2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Using the wrong margin base:** Some textbooks use *markup on cost* (Cost × 1.20), while DTC uses *margin on price* (Price × 0.80). KTU 2024 Scheme expects **margin on price**.
> 3. **Ignoring the should-cost step:** A common error is to jump from "target cost" to "design" without computing should-cost. Examiners award 2 marks specifically for the first-principles breakdown.
> 4. **Treating DTC as synonymous with "cost-cutting":** DTC is a *proactive upstream* strategy, not a reactive downstream one. Use the phrase *"market-driven cost target"* in your answer.
> 5. **Forgetting units in the learning curve:** $C_n = C_1 \cdot n^{-b}$ — the exponent $-b$ is on the *unit number*, not the cost. Stating it as $C_n^b$ loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **DTC = designing *to* a cost, not estimating cost *of* a design.**
- [x] Core equation: $C_{\text{target}} = P_{\text{target}} \times (1 - \pi_{\text{target}})$.
- [x] **Five phases:** Market Analysis → Profit Planning → Cost Decomposition → Should-Cost → Design & Iterate.
- [x] **Should-Cost** = theoretical minimum; **Will-Cost** = supplier quote; **Gap** = improvement opportunity.
- [x] **Life-Cycle Cost (LCC)** = Acquisition + Operation + Maintenance − Salvage.
- [x] **Learning curve (Wright's Law):** $C_n = C_1 \cdot n^{-b}$ — every doubling of volume reduces cost.
- [x] **Cost Breakdown Structure (CBS)** is the *bridge* between system-level target and component-level engineering.
- [x] DTC integrates with **Value Engineering (VE)**, **Design for Manufacturing (DFM)**, and **Design for Assembly (DFA)**.
- [x] For KTU 2024 exams: memorize the **fundamental equation**, the **five phases**, and the **should-cost methodology**.
- [x] Real-world anchors: Toyota (origin of target costing), Xiaomi (DTC at scale), Boeing 787 (global should-cost), HAL/DRDO (defence DTC).
- [x] Always interpret the result — don't just compute; explain the *engineering or business consequence* of the number.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pre-compliance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pre-compliance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Pre-compliance** is the proactive, in-house engineering practice of evaluating, testing, and verifying a product against applicable regulatory, safety, electromagnetic, environmental, and industry-specific standards **before** it is submitted to an accredited third-party certification body for formal compliance testing. It forms a critical "gate" within the Design Thinking and Product Development lifecycle, bridging the gap between conceptual design and market launch.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (GZEST605), pre-compliance is positioned as a **risk-mitigation and iterative validation** activity that aligns with the **Verify** and **Iterate** stages of the Stanford d.school's Design Thinking model and supports the ISO 9001 / ISO 14001 / IEC 61508 design-control philosophies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Pre-compliance is a non-negotiable checkpoint. It is the bridge between "we think it works" and "the regulator certifies it works." Skipping it is the single most common reason for **product launch delays, recalls, and penalties**.

### The Three Pillars of Pre-compliance

1. **Regulatory Pre-compliance** – Local and international statutory requirements (e.g., BIS for India, FCC for USA, CE for EU).
2. **Technical Pre-compliance** – EMC, EMI, Safety, RF, and environmental performance.
3. **Documentation Pre-compliance** – Technical files, declarations of conformity, risk assessment reports, and test plans.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Driving Learner's License Test**
>
> Imagine learning to drive. Before appearing for the **RTO driving test** (the formal compliance test), you practice in your colony lanes, on quiet roads, and with a driving instructor in the car (pre-compliance). You are NOT yet on the official test track, but you are checking mirrors, signaling, braking, and parking.
>
> - If you fail in your colony practice → you fix it cheaply at home.
> - If you reach the RTO and fail → you pay fees, lose time, and may damage the car.
>
> **Pre-compliance is the "colony practice" of product development.** The formal compliance test is the "RTO test."

### Key Intuition Points
- **Pre-compliance is a *process*, not a *certificate***. It produces internal confidence and data, not regulatory approval.
- It is performed using **in-house or rented equipment**, not necessarily accredited labs.
- It is iterative — each design revision is re-validated through pre-compliance loops.
- It saves **time, money, and reputation** by surfacing failures early.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

The following terms are used throughout the KTU board examination for this topic:

| Term | Standard Definition | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **EMC** | Electromagnetic Compatibility | dBµV, dBµV/m |
| **EMI** | Electromagnetic Interference | dB |
| **CE Marking** | Conformité Européenne | Mandatory for EU market |
| **FCC Part 15** | US RF emissions standard | Class A / Class B |
| **RoHS** | Restriction of Hazardous Substances | 10 substance limits (ppm) |
| **REACH** | EU chemical safety regulation | SVHC list |
| **BIS** | Bureau of Indian Standards | ISI mark |
| **MTBF** | Mean Time Between Failures | Hours |
| **DFMEA** | Design Failure Mode and Effects Analysis | RPN score (1–1000) |
| **Pre-compliance Test Report** | Internal document validating readiness | Page count: 20–100 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Designer's Mandate:** Every B.Tech student graduating under KTU 2024 must understand that pre-compliance is **not optional** in modern product development. It is a core competency expected of every design engineer, regardless of branch.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework

Pre-compliance is governed by a **layered verification philosophy**. At every stage of product development, the engineering team performs progressively rigorous checks. The theoretical structure can be decomposed into the following six logical blocks:

### Block 1: Regulatory Landscape Mapping
- **Why:** The engineer must first identify *which* regulations apply to the product. A Bluetooth speaker sold in India needs **BIS, WPC, and TEC**; the same product sold in the EU needs **CE, RoHS, REACH, and RED**.
- **How:** A **Regulatory Matrix** is created mapping each regulation to product features, components, and lifecycle stages.

### Block 2: Requirement Decomposition
- Each regulation is broken into **testable engineering specifications** (e.g., FCC Part 15 Class B → radiated emissions ≤ 40 dBµV/m at 3 m between 30 MHz–230 MHz).
- These become **Design Input Requirements (DIRs)** in the product specification document.

### Block 3: Design-Time Pre-compliance
- Performed *during* the design phase using simulation tools (e.g., CST Studio, ANSYS, Altium EMC simulators) and design-rule checks.
- Output: **Predictive compliance report** indicating areas of likely failure.

### Block 4: Bench-Level Pre-compliance
- Performed on **engineering prototypes** using near-field probes, spectrum analyzers, LISNs, and TEM cells.
- Output: **Quantitative test data** compared against regulatory limits.

### Block 5: Documentation & Traceability
- Every test, every design decision, every deviation is logged in a **Technical File** or **Design History File (DHF)**.
- Required for ISO 13485 (medical), FDA 21 CFR 820 (US medical), and CE Technical File.

### Block 6: Pre-compliance-to-Compliance Handover
- The pre-compliance package is submitted to a NABL-accredited / ILAC-recognized lab.
- A **Pre-compliance Readiness Score** is computed to estimate the probability of first-pass compliance.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** While pre-compliance is qualitative-heavy, several *quantitative metrics* are heavily tested. Memorize the table below.

| # | Concept | Formula / Metric | Variables & Units | Real-World Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Safety Margin (SM)** | $$ SM = \dfrac{Limit - Measured}{Limit} \times 100\% $$ | Limit (dBµV), Measured (dBµV) | Quantifies how far a product is from the regulatory threshold |
| 2 | **EMC Margin** | $$ M_{EMC} = E_{limit} - E_{measured} \quad [\text{dB}] $$ | $E_{limit}$ in dBµV, $E_{measured}$ in dBµV | KTU favourite; 6 dB margin is industry standard |
| 3 | **RPN (Risk Priority Number)** | $$ RPN = S \times O \times D $$ | Severity (1–10), Occurrence (1–10), Detection (1–10) | Used in DFMEA during pre-compliance risk analysis |
| 4 | **Field Strength Conversion (far-field)** | $$ E = \dfrac{\sqrt{30 \cdot P \cdot G}}{r} \quad [\text{V/m}] $$ | $P$ = power (W), $G$ = gain, $r$ = distance (m) | Used in radiated emission pre-compliance |
| 5 | **Duty Cycle Correction** | $$ L_{dc} = 20 \cdot \log_{10}(\delta) \quad [\text{dB}] $$ | $\delta$ = duty cycle (0–1) | Applied to pulsed signals in EMI testing |
| 6 | **Conducted Emission Limit (CISPR 22 Class B)** | $$ L_{QP} = 56 - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{f}{MHz}\right) \quad [\text{dBµV}] $$ | $f$ = frequency | Quasi-peak limit for AC mains |
| 7 | **Shielding Effectiveness** | $$ SE = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_1}\right) \quad [\text{dB}] $$ | $E_0$ = incident, $E_1$ = transmitted field | Validates enclosure shielding pre-compliance |
| 8 | **Pre-compliance Cost Ratio** | $$ C_{ratio} = \dfrac{C_{pre}}{C_{post}} $$ | $C_{pre}$ = in-house cost, $C_{post}$ = post-launch recall cost | Usually $C_{ratio} \approx 1{:}100$ to $1{:}1000$ |
| 9 | **First-Pass Yield (FPY)** | $$ FPY = \dfrac{N_{pass}}{N_{total}} \times 100\% $$ | $N_{pass}$ = passes, $N_{total}$ = units tested | Pre-compliance should drive FPY > 90% |
| 10 | **Thermal Derating Factor** | $$ \theta_{JA, derated} = \theta_{JA} \cdot \dfrac{T_{J,max} - T_{A,expected}}{T_{J,max} - T_{A,rated}} $$ | Junction-to-ambient thermal resistance | Pre-compliance for safety/thermal standards |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Board Tip:** KTU often asks, *"What safety margin is industry-acceptable in pre-compliance?"* The standard answer is **6 dB (i.e., 50% headroom)** for EMC, and **20% derating** for thermal/safety.

## 2.3 Why Pre-compliance is an Engineering Imperative

1. **Cost Asymmetry:** Recall costs are **100×–1000×** higher than fixing issues at the design stage. The **1-10-100 Rule** (1 to fix at concept, 10 to fix at design, 100 to fix at production, 1000 to fix in the field) is a KTU favourite.
2. **Time-to-Market:** Each compliance failure delays launch by 6–12 weeks. Pre-compliance compresses iteration cycles.
3. **Brand Protection:** Failures in the field are public; pre-compliance failures are private.
4. **Legal Shield:** Documented pre-compliance efforts are a defense against product liability suits in many jurisdictions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (KTU Examinable):** "If you have to ask whether you need pre-compliance, you need pre-compliance."

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: EMC Safety Margin Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern):** A Bluetooth headset is being pre-compliance tested for radiated emissions at **156 MHz**. The CISPR 22 Class B quasi-peak limit is **30 dBµV/m at 10 m**. The spectrum analyzer reads **22 dBµV/m**. Compute the safety margin and determine if the design is pre-compliance ready.

### Step 1: Identify Given Quantities

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= 156 \text{ MHz} \\
E_{limit} &= 30 \text{ dBµV/m} \\
E_{measured} &= 22 \text{ dBµV/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

These values are extracted from the test setup documentation. The frequency lies in the FM broadcast band, which is a known sensitive region for the Bluetooth 2.4 GHz harmonic at the **n = 16** sub-harmonic.

### Step 2: Recall the Safety Margin Formula

$$
SM = \dfrac{Limit - Measured}{Limit} \times 100\%
$$

This formula expresses the percentage headroom of the measured emission below the regulatory limit. A higher SM means a safer design.

### Step 3: Substitute Numerical Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
SM &= \dfrac{30 - 22}{30} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{8}{30} \times 100\% \\
&= 0.2667 \times 100\% \\
&= 26.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Convert to dB Margin (Alternative Form)

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{EMC} &= E_{limit} - E_{measured} \\
&= 30 - 22 \\
&= 8 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Apply the Industry Acceptance Criterion

The KTU / industry standard requires a **6 dB margin** for first-pass compliance certainty. Since 8 dB > 6 dB, the design is pre-compliance ready at this frequency.

### Step 6: Final Interpretation

> The Bluetooth headset's radiated emission at 156 MHz is **26.67% below the limit** and has an **8 dB safety margin**, exceeding the 6 dB benchmark. **Verdict: PRE-COMPLIANCE READY.**

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: DFMEA RPN Computation

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU Pattern):** A lithium-ion battery pack is being pre-compliance assessed. For the failure mode "cell over-voltage during charging", the engineering team assigns: Severity = 9, Occurrence = 4, Detection = 5. Compute the RPN and recommend action.

### Step 1: Recall the RPN Formula

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

Where $S$ is severity (1 = negligible, 10 = catastrophic), $O$ is occurrence (1 = rare, 10 = very frequent), and $D$ is detection difficulty (1 = very detectable, 10 = undetectable).

### Step 2: Substitute Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
RPN &= 9 \times 4 \times 5 \\
&= 36 \times 5 \\
&= 180
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Apply the KTU / Industry Threshold Table

| RPN Range | Action Level |
|---|---|
| 1 – 50 | **Low risk** — accept with monitoring |
| 51 – 150 | **Moderate risk** — recommend mitigation |
| 151 – 300 | **High risk** — must mitigate |
| > 300 | **Critical** — stop production, redesign |

### Step 4: Interpret the Result

Since $RPN = 180$, the failure mode falls in the **High Risk** band. Action is mandatory.

### Step 5: Recommend Pre-compliance Mitigation

1. Add a redundant battery management system (BMS) with over-voltage cut-off at 4.25 V/cell.
2. Improve detection by adding a per-cell voltage monitor with alarm.
3. Re-score: New $S = 9$, new $O = 2$, new $D = 2$ → New $RPN = 9 \times 2 \times 2 = 36$ (Low risk).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pre-compliance vs Compliance Testing

This matrix is a **high-yield KTU comparison** that is frequently asked as a 7-mark question.

| Parameter | Pre-compliance Testing | Formal Compliance Testing |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Internal risk reduction | Regulatory certification |
| **Performed by** | In-house R&D team | NABL / ILAC accredited third-party lab |
| **Equipment** | Engineering-grade, near-field probes, spectrum analyzers | Calibrated, traceable, accredited |
| **Cost** | Low (₹10K – ₹5L) | High (₹2L – ₹50L) |
| **Time** | Days to weeks | Weeks to months |
| **Test environment** | Lab / bench / anechoic chamber (optional) | Fully anechoic chamber (FAR) / OATS |
| **Output** | Internal report | Certificate of Conformity |
| **Legal status** | Non-binding | Legally binding |
| **Tolerance / margin** | 3–6 dB acceptable | 0 dB (exact limit) |
| **Iteration cycle** | Fast (design loop) | Slow (re-submission loop) |
| **Documentation** | Internal logbook | Technical File, Declaration of Conformity |
| **Failure consequence** | Design revision | Product rejection, launch delay, recall |

## 3.4 Pre-compliance Test Procedure (Algorithmic Flow as Math)

The pre-compliance test procedure can be modeled as a decision tree. Let $S$ be the system state:

$$
S = \{S_0, S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4\}
$$

Where:
- $S_0$: Requirements identified
- $S_1$: Prototype built
- $S_2$: Bench tests executed
- $S_3$: Data analyzed
- $S_4$: Ready / Iterate

The transition function $T$ is:

$$
T(S_i) = 
\begin{cases}
S_1 & \text{if } S = S_0 \text{ and prototype available} \\
S_2 & \text{if } S = S_1 \text{ and prototype powered-on} \\
S_3 & \text{if } S = S_2 \text{ and data captured} \\
S_4 & \text{if } S = S_3 \text{ and } M_{EMC} \geq 6 \text{ dB} \\
S_0 & \text{if } S = S_3 \text{ and } M_{EMC} < 6 \text{ dB (iterate)} \\
\emptyset & \text{if data invalid}
\end{cases}
$$

This mathematical model is a useful representation when explaining pre-compliance to auditors or in technical interviews.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Define pre-compliance testing. Why is it essential in product development?"**

**Model Answer:**
Pre-compliance testing is the in-house, preliminary evaluation of a product against applicable regulatory and technical standards, performed before submission to an accredited third-party certification lab. It is essential because it (i) identifies design defects early, (ii) reduces time-to-market, (iii) lowers overall certification cost, and (iv) provides documentation that supports a higher first-pass yield at the formal compliance lab. It forms an iterative feedback loop inside the design phase itself.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"List any four standards typically verified during pre-compliance testing."**

**Model Answer:**
The four standards are:
1. **CISPR 22 / EN 55032** – Information technology equipment radio disturbance.
2. **IEC 61000-4-2** – Electrostatic discharge immunity.
3. **IEC 60950-1 / IEC 62368-1** – Safety of information technology equipment.
4. **RoHS 2 (2011/65/EU)** – Restriction of hazardous substances.

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) – Internal Choice Option 1

### Question A [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]
**(a)** Explain the layered regulatory framework that a product must satisfy before market launch. Discuss the role of international, regional, industry-specific, and customer-specific standards. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the pre-compliance test workflow for an electronic product with a neat block diagram. Highlight the typical equipment used and the decision criterion for declaring the design pre-compliance ready. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution (a):
The regulatory framework for any product is **multi-layered**:

1. **International Standards (Layer 1):** Issued by bodies like **IEC, ISO, CISPR, ITU**. These are *voluntary harmonized standards* that provide a technical baseline. Example: IEC 62368-1 for audio/video/ICT safety.
2. **Regional Regulations (Layer 2):** Enforced by geographic jurisdictions. Example: **CE marking (EU), FCC Part 15 (USA), BIS (India), MIC (Japan)**. These are *legally mandatory* before sale.
3. **Industry-Specific Standards (Layer 3):** Tailored to a sector. Example: **FDA 21 CFR (medical), EASA / FAA (aviation), UL 60950 (IT safety), IEC 60601 (medical electrical equipment)**.
4. **Customer-Specific Requirements (Layer 4):** Imposed by OEMs, defense buyers, or B2B clients. Example: Google's Android Compatibility Definition Document (CDD), or automotive OEMs requiring IATF 16949 supply chain compliance.

**Role of pre-compliance:** Pre-compliance ensures the product is verified against all four layers *before* the formal certification process begins. The deliverable is a **Pre-compliance Traceability Matrix (PTM)** mapping each clause to the test evidence.

### Model Solution (b):
The pre-compliance workflow for an electronic product is:

1. **Regulatory Mapping** – Identify all applicable standards (EMC, Safety, RF, Environmental).
2. **Test Plan Preparation** – Define test points, limits, instruments, and acceptance criteria (≥ 6 dB EMC margin, ≥ 20% thermal derating).
3. **Prototype Preparation** – Use engineering samples, not pre-production units.
4. **Bench Test Execution** – Conducted emissions via LISN, radiated emissions via near-field probes or GTEM cell, ESD via gun, surge via coupling network.
5. **Data Acquisition** – Spectrum analyzer, oscilloscope, and EMC software capture emissions.
6. **Comparison with Limits** – Apply safety margin formula and compare against CISPR / FCC / IEC limits.
7. **Decision** – If $M_{EMC} \geq 6$ dB AND all other tests pass, mark **PRE-COMPLIANCE READY**. Else, return to design.

**Typical Equipment:** Spectrum analyzer (9 kHz–26 GHz), LISN, near-field probe set, GTEM cell, ESD gun, surge generator, oscilloscope, power analyzer, thermal camera.

**Decision Criterion:** A product is pre-compliance ready if **all** measured parameters are below regulatory limits with an **industry-standard 6 dB safety margin**, **all** DFMEA risks are in the "Low" or "Moderate" band (RPN < 150), and **all** documentation is traceable.

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) – Internal Choice Option 2

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]
**(a)** Define the Risk Priority Number (RPN) used in DFMEA. A failure mode has Severity = 8, Occurrence = 6, Detection = 7. Compute the RPN and recommend a pre-compliance action. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast pre-compliance testing with formal compliance testing across ten parameters. Why is the industry-acceptable safety margin set at 6 dB for EMC pre-compliance? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution (a):
The **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** is a quantitative metric used in **Design Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (DFMEA)** to prioritize failure modes. It is computed as:

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

Where $S$ = Severity (1–10), $O$ = Occurrence (1–10), $D$ = Detection difficulty (1–10).

**Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RPN &= 8 \times 6 \times 7 \\
&= 48 \times 7 \\
&= 336
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** RPN = 336 falls in the **Critical** band (> 300). This is a *stop-ship* condition.

**Recommended Pre-compliance Action:**
- **Redesign the circuit** to reduce severity (e.g., add protection circuitry).
- **Add detection** (e.g., sensors, alarms, self-test routines).
- **Reduce occurrence** via tighter process control and supplier qualification.
- Re-score the RPN until it falls below 150 (preferably below 50).

### Model Solution (b):
The detailed ten-parameter comparison is presented in **Section 3.3 (Analytical Matrix)** of these notes. Key highlights:

1. Pre-compliance is internal; compliance is third-party.
2. Pre-compliance uses engineering-grade equipment; compliance uses calibrated accredited equipment.
3. Pre-compliance accepts a **3–6 dB margin**; compliance is checked at the **exact 0 dB limit**.
4. Pre-compliance is fast and iterative; compliance is slow and re-submission based.
5. Pre-compliance has no legal standing; compliance yields a legally binding certificate.

**Why 6 dB for EMC?**
The 6 dB safety margin is industry-accepted because:
- **6 dB represents a factor of 2** in voltage (or factor of 4 in power). Measurement uncertainty in radiated emissions is typically **±3 to ±5 dB**, so a 6 dB margin protects against the upper bound of uncertainty.
- **It is the standard practice in CISPR and ANSI test reports.**
- **It accounts for production variance** between pre-compliance prototypes and mass-produced units.
- KTU and industry textbooks (e.g., Henry Ott's *Noise Reduction Techniques in Electronic Systems*, Tim Williams' *EMC for Product Designers*) recommend ≥ 6 dB.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Students often **confuse pre-compliance with compliance** — read the question carefully.
> - Marks are lost for **not mentioning the 6 dB margin** explicitly in EMC questions.
> - In RPN questions, always **interpret the range**, not just compute the number.
> - Never write "pre-compliance is optional" — it is **mandatory** in modern product development.
> - Diagram questions require a **flowchart with feedback loop**, not just a linear list.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pre-compliance** is *internal, iterative, in-house testing* before formal certification.
- It is the **bridge between design and market launch**.
- The **1-10-100-1000 Rule** quantifies the cost of fixing defects at each stage.
- **EMC safety margin of 6 dB** is the KTU / industry benchmark.
- **RPN thresholds:** 1–50 (Low), 51–150 (Moderate), 151–300 (High), > 300 (Critical).
- **Equipment set:** spectrum analyzer, LISN, near-field probes, GTEM cell, ESD gun.
- **Documentation:** Technical File, Declaration of Conformity, DFMEA, Test Reports.
- **Frameworks:** CISPR, FCC, CE, RoHS, REACH, BIS, IEC, ISO.
- **Pre-compliance ≠ Compliance** — pre-compliance is a *preparatory act*, compliance is a *legal outcome*.
- **Iterate, document, and trace** every step.
- **Always re-test** after any design change.
- **In KTU answers**, structure your response as: *Definition → Why → How → Example → Diagram → Conclusion*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (DFMEA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (DFMEA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Design Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (DFMEA)** is a structured, systematic, and proactive analytical engineering methodology used to identify, evaluate, prioritize, and mitigate potential failure modes in a product or system *before* it is released to manufacturing or the customer. The word "Design" qualifies the standard FMEA to focus specifically on failures caused by the **design decisions** of the product, rather than manufacturing process variations (which is the focus of PFMEA).

According to the standard reference framework adopted globally (originating from the automotive industry's **AIAG-VDA** methodology and the older **AIAG QS-9000** standard), DFMEA is a living document maintained throughout the design life-cycle. It maps every design function, enumerates what could go wrong (the *failure mode*), evaluates the downstream consequence (the *effect*), traces the engineering root-cause (the *cause*), and assigns three numerical ratings — **Severity (S)**, **Occurrence (O)**, and **Detectability (D)** — from which the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** is derived.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 4):**
> DFMEA is the central quantitative risk-management tool taught under *Product Development* and is a direct application of the *Empathize/Define/Prototype/Test* loop of Design Thinking — it transforms qualitative "what could go wrong" brainstorming into a defensible, mathematically prioritized action list.

## 1.2 The "Why" — The Three Pillars of DFMEA

| Pillar | Purpose | Design-Thinking Link |
|---|---|---|
| **Severity (S)** | Measures the seriousness of the *consequence* to the end customer. | Defining the user's pain (Empathy) |
| **Occurrence (O)** | Measures how frequently the *cause* of failure will actually happen. | Mapping the scenario where failure emerges |
| **Detection (D)** | Measures the *probability that the existing design controls will fail to detect* the issue before reaching the customer. | Testing the prototype before launch |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car-Buying" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:**
> Imagine you are designing a new **electric kettle** (just like the KTU project-based labs often use as a benchmark product).
>
> 1. **Severity** → *How bad is it if the kettle handle melts?* — Catastrophic (rating 9–10). The user gets burned.
> 2. **Occurrence** → *How likely is the handle to melt?* — If we use cheap ABS plastic at 100 °C, it is *very likely* (rating 8).
> 3. **Detection** → *Would our prototype testing have caught this?* — If our tests only ran at room-temperature water, we would *not* catch it (rating 9).
>
> **RPN = 9 × 8 × 9 = 648** → A screaming red flag, demanding an immediate design change (switch to a high-temperature polymer). This is exactly the kind of risk the DFMEA worksheet forces the engineering team to confront on paper *before* a single physical prototype is built.

The single most important takeaway: **DFMEA is a *preventive* tool, not a *reactive* one.** It is the engineering embodiment of the Design Thinking motto — *"Fail early, fail cheap, fail on paper."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The DFMEA Workflow — Six Logical Steps

The DFMEA is not a single calculation; it is a **six-step structured workflow** that the design team walks through for every component, sub-assembly, and system.

1. **Step 1 — Define the Scope & Boundaries**
   The team must first write down *exactly* what is being analyzed (e.g., the *Heating Element Subsystem* of a kettle), the analysis *level* (system / sub-system / component), the *customer* of that level (end user? next assembly? regulatory body?), and *assumptions* (e.g., "Mains voltage = 230 V, 50 Hz, Indian grid").
2. **Step 2 — Construct the Block Diagram (Function Tree)**
   A visual decomposition of the product into functional blocks. Each block represents one engineering function (e.g., "Boil Water", "Indicate Status", "Auto Shut-off").
3. **Step 3 — List the Design Functions**
   For every block, state the *intended* function in measurable engineering terms. Example: "Deliver 1500 W ± 5 % for the first 30 s after switch-on."
4. **Step 4 — Identify Potential Failure Modes**
   Brainstorm (using past warranty data, customer complaints, benchmarking) all the ways the function in Step 3 can fail. Example: "Element does not heat", "Element overheats", "Element takes 5 min to boil".
5. **Step 5 — Determine Effects, Causes & Current Controls**
   - *Effect* = the impact on the customer (e.g., "User scalds hand")
   - *Cause* = the engineering root cause (e.g., "Thermostat spring fatigue")
   - *Current Control* = the existing detection/prevention method (e.g., "100 % production Hi-Pot test")
6. **Step 6 — Rate, Calculate RPN, Prioritize Actions**
   Apply the **S, O, D** scales, compute RPN, sort the worksheet, and assign **recommended actions** with target dates and owners for every high-risk row.

## 2.2 The Three Rating Scales (AIAG-VDA Standard, 1–10)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These scales are *the* scoring system the industry — and KTU — expects you to use. **Memorize the anchor definitions**, especially for Severity, as it is the only scale that is *independent* of design controls.

### 2.2.1 Severity (S) — The Consequence Rating
- **10** — Hazard without warning (loss of life, limb, or vision)
- **9** — Hazard with warning (severe injury)
- **8** — Major loss of primary function (vehicle inoperable)
- **7** — Significant degradation of primary function
- **6** — Loss of secondary function
- **5** — Degraded secondary function
- **4** — Marginal inconvenience to customer
- **3** — Minor inconvenience
- **2** — No effect, noticed only by discriminating customer
- **1** — No effect

### 2.2.2 Occurrence (O) — The Cause Frequency Rating
- **10** — Very High: failure almost inevitable (> 1 in 2)
- **7–8** — High: persistent failures
- **5–6** — Moderate: occasional failures
- **3–4** — Low: relatively few failures
- **2** — Remote: failure unlikely
- **1** — Extremely unlikely: failure nearly impossible

### 2.2.3 Detection (D) — The Probability of *Not* Detecting
> [!WARNING]
> **Crucial Inversion:** A high D number is **bad**. D = 10 means the existing control has *almost no chance* of catching the failure before it reaches the customer. A D = 1 means there is an almost-certain detection control (e.g., automated end-of-line test with 100 % coverage).

- **10** — Absolute uncertainty: no control exists
- **7–8** — Low detection capability
- **4–6** — Moderate detection capability
- **2–3** — High detection capability
- **1** — Almost certain detection: control prevents/defects 100 % of the time

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Term** | **Engineering Meaning** | **Formula / Definition** | **Range / Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S$ | Severity of the failure's *effect* on the customer | Assigned per the AIAG Severity table | 1 – 10 (dimensionless integer) |
| $O$ | Likelihood of the *cause* occurring | Assigned per the AIAG Occurrence table | 1 – 10 (dimensionless integer) |
| $D$ | Probability that existing controls *fail to detect* the failure mode before release | Assigned per the AIAG Detection table | 1 – 10 (dimensionless integer) |
| $\text{RPN}$ | Risk Priority Number | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | 1 – 1000 (dimensionless integer) |
| $\text{AP}$ | Action Priority (AIAG-VDA 2019) | Replaces the hard RPN threshold logic (H / M / L) | High / Medium / Low |
| $S_{\max}$ | Maximum theoretical RPN | $S_{\max} = 10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1000$ | 1000 |
| $R_{\text{threshold}}$ | Conventional KTU/industry "must-act" cutoff | $\text{RPN} > 100$ **or** $S \geq 9$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In regulated industries (automotive, medical devices, aerospace under AS9100), the DFMEA is a *legal due-diligence* document. If a product fails in the field and the DFMEA shows a previously-identified high-RPN row with no action taken, the OEM faces severe product-liability and recall exposure.

## 2.4 The Modern Shift: From RPN to Action Priority (AP)

A critical piece of KTU 2024 currency is recognizing that the **legacy 1970s RPN methodology has well-known flaws** (e.g., RPN of 4×1×5 = 20 looks "low" but a Severity of 4 is still visible to the customer). The **AIAG-VDA 2019** update introduced a **risk-matrix–based Action Priority (AP)** that uses an explicit mapping of (S, O) → AP and then adjusts by D.

$$\text{AP Level} = f(S, O, D) \quad \text{where} \quad f \in \{\text{High (H), Medium (M), Low (L)}\}$$

Under the AP logic, the team *must* act on every **High (H)** action priority regardless of raw RPN.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — The KTU "Smart Helmet" DFMEA

> [!NOTE]
> The following is a *complete* worked example suitable for a 14-mark university problem. A KTU student should be able to reproduce the table, the math, and the final action list.

**Product:** A university-project-grade *Smart Helmet* (ECE/Mech design lab) intended to detect accidents and auto-dial an emergency number.
**Subsystem Under Analysis:** The **3-Axis Accelerometer (MPU-6050) and its mechanical mounting** in the helmet shell.

### Step 1 — Scope and Customer

- **Scope:** Crash-detection sub-system (sensor + mounting + firmware threshold).
- **Customer:** End rider (must receive the SOS call within 60 s of impact).
- **Assumption:** 3G/4G network coverage is *not* in scope (covered by a separate Communication DFMEA).

### Step 2 — Block Diagram (Function Tree)

We decompose the sub-system into the following engineering functions:

1. Sense linear acceleration on 3 axes.
2. Transmit digital data to MCU via I²C bus.
3. Withstand drop impact of 1.5 m onto concrete.
4. Operate on helmet battery for ≥ 6 months standby.

### Step 3 & 4 — Functions and Failure Modes

| # | Design Function | Potential Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|
| F1 | Sense acceleration | No signal generated during impact |
| F2 | Sense acceleration | Spurious signal during a normal ride |
| F3 | Withstand impact | Sensor detaches from the shell |
| F4 | I²C communication | Data corrupted by EMI from the motor |

### Step 5 & 6 — Effects, Causes, Current Controls and Ratings

We now build the full DFMEA worksheet. The student must show the *complete* row derivation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For Row F1, Failure: "No signal during impact"}: & \\
\text{Effect on customer} &= \text{No SOS call sent} \quad \Rightarrow S = 10 \text{ (safety hazard, no warning)} \\
\text{Cause} &= \text{Sensor mounting too soft, dampens the impulse} \\
\text{Occurrence} &= \text{Very High: every crash that is below the sensor's $g$-threshold will be missed} \quad \Rightarrow O = 7 \\
\text{Current control} &= \text{Visual mounting check; no drop-test verification} \quad \Rightarrow D = 9 \\
\text{RPN}_1 &= S \times O \times D = 10 \times 7 \times 9 = \mathbf{630}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For Row F2, Failure: "Spurious signal during normal ride"}: & \\
\text{Effect} &= \text{False SOS triggered, embarrassing & draining battery} \quad \Rightarrow S = 6 \\
\text{Cause} &= \text{Firmware threshold set too low} \\
\text{Occurrence} &= \text{Occasional on rough roads} \quad \Rightarrow O = 5 \\
\text{Current control} &= \text{Lab bench test only; no field trial} \quad \Rightarrow D = 8 \\
\text{RPN}_2 &= 6 \times 5 \times 8 = \mathbf{240}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For Row F3, Failure: "Sensor detaches on impact"}: & \\
\text{Effect} &= \text{Same as F1: no SOS} \quad \Rightarrow S = 10 \\
\text{Cause} &= \text{Adhesive (3M VHB) fails on oily EPS foam} \\
\text{Occurrence} &= \text{Possible} \quad \Rightarrow O = 5 \\
\text{Current control} &= \text{Hand-press adhesive; no peel test} \quad \Rightarrow D = 9 \\
\text{RPN}_3 &= 10 \times 5 \times 9 = \mathbf{450}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For Row F4, Failure: "I²C data corrupted by EMI"}: & \\
\text{Effect} &= \text{MCU reads garbage; no SOS} \quad \Rightarrow S = 10 \\
\text{Cause} &= \text{Motor-driver PWM switching noise couples into the I²C lines} \\
\text{Occurrence} &= \text{Frequent (every motor start)} \quad \Rightarrow O = 7 \\
\text{Current control} &= \text{None (no ferrite bead, no shielding)} \quad \Rightarrow D = 10 \\
\text{RPN}_4 &= 10 \times 7 \times 10 = \mathbf{700}
\end{aligned}
$$

### The Final DFMEA Worksheet (Compiled)

| Item | Function | Failure Mode | Effect | S | Cause | O | Current Control | D | **RPN** | AP (AIAG-VDA) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | Sense acceleration | No signal on impact | No SOS, rider dies | 10 | Soft mount dampens impulse | 7 | Visual check only | 9 | **630** | **H** |
| F2 | Sense acceleration | Spurious signal | False SOS, embarrassment | 6 | Threshold too low | 5 | Bench test only | 8 | **240** | **M** |
| F3 | Withstand impact | Sensor detaches | No SOS | 10 | Adhesive fails on EPS | 5 | Hand-press | 9 | **450** | **H** |
| F4 | I²C comm. | Data corrupted | No SOS | 10 | Motor EMI | 7 | None | 10 | **700** | **H** |

### Step 7 — Recommended Actions (DFMEA Action Closure)

| Item | Recommended Action | Responsibility | Target Date | New S / O / D | New RPN |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | Replace foam mount with rigid 3D-printed ABS bracket, add 1.5 m drop test | Mech Lead | 15-Mar | 10 / 2 / 3 | 60 |
| F2 | Field-tune threshold to 4 $g$, add 7-day rider trial | Firmware Lead | 22-Mar | 6 / 2 / 3 | 36 |
| F3 | Use 3M DP-8010 structural adhesive + 100 % peel-test on production | Mech Lead | 30-Mar | 10 / 1 / 2 | 20 |
| F4 | Add ferrite beads on I²C lines + shielded twisted pair + shield to GND | Hardware Lead | 25-Mar | 10 / 3 / 4 | 120 |

### Step 8 — Analytical Matrix: The Mathematical Derivation of the Worst-Case Bound

For exam purposes, the KTU marker expects you to be able to *derive* the maximum possible RPN and to *prove* the dominance of certain rows.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Theoretical maximum RPN} &= S_{\max} \times O_{\max} \times D_{\max} \\
&= 10 \times 10 \times 10 \\
&= 1000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mean RPN of our 4 rows} &= \frac{630 + 240 + 450 + 700}{4} = \frac{2020}{4} = 505 \\
\text{Threshold for "Must-Act"} &= \text{RPN} > 100 \quad \text{(industry convention)} \\
\text{Rows exceeding threshold} &= \{F1, F2, F3, F4\} \quad \text{all 4 must have an action plan} \\
\text{Rows with } S \geq 9 \text{ (safety hazard, no action can be deferred)} &= \{F1, F3, F4\} \quad \text{must have action regardless of RPN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that the *lowest* RPN in our worksheet (F2 = 240) is *still* 2.4× the action threshold. This is why experienced DFMEA facilitators *never* rely on RPN alone; the **Action Priority (AP)** label and the **Severity hard-floor (S ≥ 9)** are the *legally* binding triggers, not RPN. The KTU 2024 examiner will award full marks only to answers that mention this nuance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End DFMEA Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagrams the complete **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of a DFMEA — from product scoping to action closure and re-rating. This is the "DFMEA value stream" that the KTU examiner expects to see drawn in any 14-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Step 0 Initiate DFMEA Project] --> A1[Step 1 Define Scope and Customer]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2 Build Block Diagram and Function Tree]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3 List Design Functions per Block]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4 Brainstorm Failure Modes for each Function]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5 Determine Effect Cause and Current Control]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6 Assign Ratings S O D from AIAG VDA Tables]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7 Compute RPN equals S times O times D]
    A7 --> A8[Step 8 Determine Action Priority AP from S O D Matrix]
    A8 --> A9{Decision: AP equals High or RPN greater than 100}
    A9 -->|Yes| A10[Step 9a Assign Recommended Action with Owner and Date]
    A10 --> A11[Step 9b Implement Action and Re rate S O D]
    A11 --> A12[Step 9c Confirm New RPN is below Threshold]
    A9 -->|No| A13[Step 10 Log and Monitor Low Risk Row]
    A12 --> A14[Step 11 Close DFMEA Row and Move to Next Item]
    A13 --> A14
    A14 --> A15[Step 12 Final Review and Lessons Learned Archive]
```

## 4.2 DFMEA Worksheet Logic — Row-by-Row Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Function] --> F2[Failure Mode]
    F2 --> F3[Effect on Customer]
    F3 --> F4[Cause Root]
    F4 --> F5[Current Design Control]
    F5 --> F6[Rate Severity S]
    F5 --> F7[Rate Occurrence O]
    F5 --> F8[Rate Detection D]
    F6 --> F9[RPN equals S times O times D]
    F7 --> F9
    F8 --> F9
    F9 --> F10[Compare to AP Matrix and Threshold]
    F10 --> F11{Action Required}
    F11 -->|Yes| F12[Recommended Action and Owner and Date]
    F11 -->|No| F13[Accept and Document]
    F12 --> F14[Implement and Re calculate RPN]
    F14 --> F15[Row Closed]
    F13 --> F15
```

## 4.3 The RPN Decision Matrix (Mermaid Risk Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S_Domain[Severity Domain]
        S1[S 1 to 3 Minor]
        S2[S 4 to 6 Moderate]
        S3[S 7 to 8 High]
        S4[S 9 to 10 Hazardous]
    end
    subgraph O_Domain[Occurrence Domain]
        O1[O 1 to 3 Remote]
        O2[O 4 to 6 Low]
        O3[O 7 to 8 High]
        O4[O 9 to 10 Very High]
    end
    subgraph D_Domain[Detection Domain]
        D1[D 1 to 2 Almost Certain Detection]
        D2[D 3 to 5 Moderate]
        D3[D 6 to 8 Low]
        D4[D 9 to 10 Almost No Detection]
    end
    S4 --> RPN_High[Result RPN often 500 to 1000 AP equals High]
    S3 --> RPN_Med[Result RPN often 100 to 400 AP equals Medium or High]
    S2 --> RPN_Low[Result RPN often 30 to 200 AP equals Medium or Low]
    S1 --> RPN_Nil[Result RPN often below 50 AP equals Low]
    O4 --> RPN_High
    O3 --> RPN_Med
    D4 --> RPN_High
    D3 --> RPN_Med
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Design Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (DFMEA)". Why is it performed at the *design* stage rather than only at the production stage?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> DFMEA is a structured, proactive engineering methodology used to systematically identify, evaluate, and document potential failure modes of a product at the *design* level, including their effects on the end customer, their engineering root causes, and the adequacy of existing design controls.
>
> **(1 mark)** for the definition.
>
> It is performed at the design stage because, by the Pareto principle, **~70 % of a product's total life-cycle cost is locked in at the design phase**. A change to a CAD model costs ₹1,000; the same change after tooling costs ₹10,00,000. Identifying failures on paper (or in a simulation) before any physical tooling is built is the most cost-effective risk-reduction strategy. **(2 marks)** for the design-stage justification.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the Risk Priority Number (RPN) in a DFMEA? Write its formula and state the conventional threshold for action.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Risk Priority Number (RPN) is a single numerical index that ranks the relative risk of each identified failure mode by combining the three DFMEA ratings — **Severity (S)**, **Occurrence (O)** and **Detection (D)**.
>
> **(1 mark)** for the concept.
> **(1 mark)** for the formula:
> $$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$
> **(1 mark)** for the conventional threshold: a row must be actioned when **RPN > 100** *or* when **S ≥ 9** (regardless of RPN).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-4 Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Note:** Module 4 in the GZEST605 paper carries one full 14-mark question, and you will typically be given an internal choice between two sub-questions. The two alternatives below are designed to give you genuine *either/or* options, just as the KTU question paper would.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *DFMEA Computation on a Real Product*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

You are part of the design team for a **cordless handheld electric screwdriver** (used in a final-year project by a Mechanical batch). The product specifications are:
- 12 V DC brushless motor, 1.5 N·m peak torque
- 3.7 V, 2000 mAh Li-ion battery with a USB-C charging port
- LED work-light and forward/reverse selector

**(a)** Construct a DFMEA worksheet for the **forward/reverse selector switch sub-system** with at least **three distinct failure modes**. For each, derive the Severity (S), Occurrence (O) and Detection (D) ratings, and compute the RPN. Use the AIAG-VDA 1–10 scales and the formula $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Identify the row with the highest RPN. Propose a concrete *recommended action* that would reduce its RPN to below 100, and re-derive the new (S, O, D) and new RPN to prove the action's effectiveness. Discuss briefly why the modern AIAG-VDA framework would also flag a row with RPN < 100 if its Severity is 9 or 10. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) DFMEA Worksheet Construction (7 marks):**

We begin by identifying the design functions of the forward/reverse selector switch: (i) electrically reverse the motor polarity, (ii) provide tactile feedback of position, (iii) survive 10,000 cycles of operation.

Failure modes identified: (1) Switch does not reverse polarity (stuck in forward), (2) Switch reverses while motor is running (mid-cycle reversal), (3) Switch detaches from the housing.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Failure Mode 1: "Stuck in forward, no reverse"} & \\
\text{Effect: User cannot remove a jammed screw} \Rightarrow S &= 6 \\
\text{Cause: Solder joint on the SPDT switch cracks} & \\
\text{Occurrence: Likely after 5,000 cycles} \Rightarrow O &= 6 \\
\text{Current control: No cycle test, only continuity probe} \Rightarrow D &= 8 \\
\text{RPN}_1 &= 6 \times 6 \times 8 = \mathbf{288} \quad (AP = \text{Medium})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Failure Mode 2: "Mid-cycle reversal under load"} & \\
\text{Effect: Motor draws stall current, MOSFET blows, fire risk} \Rightarrow S &= 9 \\
\text{Cause: No firmware interlock; mechanical switch can be toggled any time} & \\
\text{Occurrence: Possible} \Rightarrow O &= 5 \\
\text{Current control: None} \Rightarrow D &= 10 \\
\text{RPN}_2 &= 9 \times 5 \times 10 = \mathbf{450} \quad (AP = \text{High})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Failure Mode 3: "Switch detaches from housing"} & \\
\text{Effect: Open circuit, tool inoperable} \Rightarrow S &= 7 \\
\text{Cause: Snap-fit claws too thin, break under thumb pressure} & \\
\text{Occurrence: Low to moderate} \Rightarrow O &= 4 \\
\text{Current control: Visual inspection only} \Rightarrow D &= 7 \\
\text{RPN}_3 &= 7 \times 4 \times 7 = \mathbf{196} \quad (AP = \text{Medium})
\end{aligned}
$$

**(Full marks: 2 for the worksheet structure, 3 for the three RPN derivations, 2 for the correct application of the 1–10 scales.)**

**(b) Highest RPN Action and AIAG-VDA Nuance (7 marks):**

The highest RPN is **Failure Mode 2 = 450**, dominated by the *safety* effect of an in-rush fire.

**Recommended Action:** Add a *firmware interlock* in the motor-controller MCU that reads the RPM sensor and refuses to toggle the H-bridge MOSFETs if the motor is spinning above 1000 rpm. Reinforce the SPDT switch with a $t = 1.2$ mm rib.

Re-derivation after action:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{New S} &= 9 \quad (\text{safety effect unchanged; action does not reduce severity}) \\
\text{New O} &= 2 \quad (\text{firmware blocks 99 \% of unintended toggles}) \\
\text{New D} &= 3 \quad (\text{automated 100 \% end-of-line cycle test catches regressions}) \\
\text{New RPN}_2 &= 9 \times 2 \times 3 = \mathbf{54} \quad (< 100, \text{ action closed})
\end{aligned}
$$

**AIAG-VDA Nuance (3 marks):** Even though the *numeric* RPN can be brought below 100, the **AIAG-VDA Action Priority matrix** classifies any combination of $S \geq 9$ with $O \geq 4$ as **Action Priority = High (H)**, demanding an action *regardless* of the post-action RPN. This avoids the historical flaw of the legacy RPN-only logic, where a row with $S = 9$, $O = 1$, $D = 2$ (RPN = 18) might be wrongly "accepted" despite being a latent safety hazard. In the case of our Failure Mode 2, the post-action AP remains H until the firmware interlock is *field-validated*, at which point AP can be downgraded to M.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:**
> The single most common error in DFMEA computation questions is **re-using the original S, O, D for the post-action RPN**. If the action only adds *detection* (e.g., a test), D drops but S and O do **not**. If the action only adds a *guard* (e.g., a firmware interlock), O drops but S and D do **not**. The student must justify *each* rating change explicitly, with a one-line engineering reason, to score full marks on the re-derivation sub-part.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Conceptual & Process-Oriented*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Alternative]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **complete six-step DFMEA workflow** from product scoping to action closure. In your diagram, clearly mark the step at which the AIAG-VDA ratings S, O and D are assigned, and the step at which the Action Priority is determined. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **traditional RPN-based DFMEA** with the **modern AIAG-VDA Action Priority (AP) approach**. Use a small numerical counter-example (e.g., two failure modes) to demonstrate a scenario in which the legacy RPN logic would *wrongly* accept a high-severity failure that the AP approach would correctly *reject*. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Six-Step DFMEA Workflow (7 marks):**

*(Full marks require both a labeled block diagram and a one-sentence description per step. Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the canonical Mermaid flow.)*

The workflow is: (1) Define scope and customer → (2) Build function tree → (3) List failure modes per function → (4) Determine effect, cause and current control → (5) Rate S, O, D using the AIAG tables and compute RPN → (6) Determine AP using the (S, O, D) risk matrix, and assign recommended actions for any **AP = High** row, with named owners and target dates, followed by implementation and re-rating until the row can be closed. **(1 mark per step explained, 1 mark for the rating-step identification, 1 mark for the AP-step identification, total 7.)**

**(b) RPN vs. AP Comparison (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Legacy RPN | AIAG-VDA AP |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Single integer 1 – 1000 | Categorical H / M / L |
| Aggregation | Multiplies three ordinals (questionable math) | Uses a pre-validated risk matrix |
| Severity floor | Ignored if RPN is small | $S \geq 9$ always forces H |
| Action trigger | Subjective threshold (typically 100) | Deterministic matrix lookup |
| Industry adoption | Older QS-9000 manuals | Mandatory for new automotive programs |

**Counter-example:** Consider two failure modes:
- **Mode X:** $S = 9$, $O = 1$, $D = 2$ → $\text{RPN}_X = 18$ (low!)
- **Mode Y:** $S = 4$, $O = 8$, $D = 8$ → $\text{RPN}_Y = 256$ (high!)

Legacy RPN logic would prioritize **Y** and *accept* **X** as low-risk. But Mode X is a *latent safety hazard* (severity 9 = "hazard without warning") whose cause simply has not been observed yet — a *single* field failure is unacceptable. The AIAG-VDA AP matrix, in contrast, classifies Mode X as **AP = High (H)** (because $S = 9$ forces H regardless of O and D), correctly demanding an immediate action, while Mode Y is downgraded to **AP = Medium (M)**.

**(4 marks for the table comparison, 3 marks for the counter-example calculation and conclusion.)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> Students frequently describe the *steps* of DFMEA correctly but **omit the iterative action-closure loop**. The KTU marker specifically looks for the words *"re-rate"* and *"action closure"* — DFMEA is a *living* document; rows are not "completed" the first time, they are *closed* only after the new (S, O, D) is proven by evidence (test report, field trial). Forgetting this loop will cost you 2 marks in part (a).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — DFMEA**

- **DFMEA = Design FMEA.** The "D" restricts scope to *design-induced* failures, *not* manufacturing process variation (that is **PFMEA**).
- **Three ratings, all 1 – 10:** **S** (effect severity, never changes with design controls), **O** (cause frequency), **D** (probability of *not* detecting — high D = bad).
- **RPN Formula:** $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$, bounded in $[1,\ 1000]$.
- **Conventional Action Thresholds:** $\text{RPN} > 100$ **OR** $S \geq 9$ **OR** (under AIAG-VDA) $\text{AP} = \text{High}$.
- **Six-Step Workflow:** Scope → Function Tree → Failure Modes → Effect/Cause/Control → Rate S/O/D → Action & Re-rate.
- **AIAG-VDA 2019 Upgrade:** Replaces the "RPN-only" logic with the **Action Priority (H / M / L)** risk matrix; safety-critical rows (high S) can *never* be closed on RPN alone.
- **DFMEA is a *living* document** — owned by the design lead, updated at every design review, and re-issued whenever the design baseline changes.
- **Cost rationale:** Catching a failure at the design stage costs $\sim 10$–$100\times$ less than catching it on the production line, and $\sim 1000\times$ less than catching it in the field (warranty + recall cost).
- **DFMEA worksheet columns (in order):** Item / Function / Failure Mode / Effect / S / Cause / O / Current Control / D / RPN / AP / Recommended Action / Owner / Target Date / Action Taken / New S / New O / New D / New RPN.
- **Common exam traps:** (i) Forgetting that D measures *non-detection*, not detection; (ii) Re-using the same (S, O, D) for the post-action row; (iii) Not listing *current* controls; (iv) Saying "DFMEA is used to fix failures" (it is a *prevention* tool, not a corrective one).
- **Design-Thinking Link:** DFMEA is the *Test* phase of the iterative loop — it is the systematic way to *predict* what would fail in the user test, *before* spending money to actually test it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forecasting future design changes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Forecasting future design changes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forecasting Future Design Changes** is the systematic, evidence-based discipline within Design Thinking and Product Development that employs qualitative, quantitative, and hybrid methodologies to anticipate, model, and prepare for the evolution of product form, function, technology, and user experience across short, medium, and long-term horizons.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (NEP 2020 aligned, Outcome-Based Education), forecasting future design changes is positioned as the strategic foresight capability that bridges the **Empathize–Define–Ideate–Prototype–Test** loop with the **future state of the market, technology, and society**. It empowers designers and product teams to move from *reactive design* to **anticipatory design** and **adaptive design**.

The discipline is grounded in three foundational pillars:

| Pillar | Description | KTU CO Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| **Horizon Scanning** | Detecting weak signals of change in the socio-technical environment | CO4 – Analyze |
| **Scenario Planning** | Constructing multiple plausible futures to test design robustness | CO4 – Analyze |
| **Design Foresight** | Translating forecasts into actionable design strategy | CO5 – Create |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Design Meteorologist**
> Imagine you are a meteorologist, but instead of predicting rain, you are predicting the next generation of a smartphone. Just as meteorologists study atmospheric pressure, ocean currents, and historical patterns to forecast tomorrow's weather, design forecasters study technology trends, user behaviour shifts, cultural movements, and economic signals to forecast tomorrow's products. The umbrella here is the *design strategy* you prepare *before* the storm of disruption arrives.

Another intuitive framing: **Forecasting future design changes is to product design what a GPS rerouting feature is to driving** — it does not control the traffic (market), but it tells you the smartest lane change (design pivot) to take *before* you are stuck in a dead end (obsolescence).

## 1.3 Why It Matters in KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 — GZEST605):**
> The 2024 scheme explicitly links forecasting to **sustainable product development**, **inclusive design futures**, and **AI-augmented design pipelines**. Students are expected to demonstrate the ability to *analyze trend matrices*, *apply scenario planning frameworks*, and *propose future-ready design interventions*.

Key terminology the KTU examiner expects:

- **Anticipatory Design** — Design choices made *before* the user explicitly requests them.
- **Speculative Design** — Critical design that explores possible futures through prototypes and narratives.
- **Backcasting** — Working *backward* from a desired future to define present actions.
- **Design Horizon** — The time window (typically **1–3 years (Short)**, **3–7 years (Medium)**, **7+ years (Long)**) over which a forecast applies.
- **Weak Signals** — Early, ambiguous indicators of potentially significant change.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Architecture of Design Forecasting

The discipline rests on a layered architecture. Each layer feeds the next, ensuring forecasts are not speculation but **structured anticipation**.

### Layer 1 — Environmental Scanning
Collects raw data from the **macro-environment** surrounding the product.

### Layer 2 — Trend Analysis
Filters and clusters signals into coherent patterns (megatrends, microtrends).

### Layer 3 — Scenario Construction
Synthesizes trends into **2x2 or 2x3 scenario matrices**.

### Layer 4 — Design Translation
Converts scenarios into design briefs, prototypes, and product roadmaps.

### Layer 5 — Continuous Re-calibration
Uses feedback loops to revise forecasts as new data emerges.

## 2.2 Core Forecasting Methodologies

### A. STEEP / PESTLE Analysis
A *qualitative environmental scanning* tool that examines change drivers across six dimensions:

| Dimension | Focus | Example Driver (2024) |
|---|---|---|
| **S**ocial | Demographics, values, lifestyle | Ageing populations, remote work culture |
| **T**echnological | Innovation, R&D, automation | Generative AI, quantum computing |
| **E**conomic | Markets, trade, income | Gig economy, subscription models |
| **E**nvironmental | Climate, resources, sustainability | Circular economy mandates |
| **P**olitical | Regulation, policy, geopolitics | Data sovereignty laws (e.g., DPDP Act 2023) |
| **L**egal | Compliance, IP, liability | EU AI Act, accessibility mandates |
| **E**thical | Moral frameworks, inclusivity | Algorithmic bias, dark patterns |

### B. Trend Impact Analysis (TIA)
Quantifies the *probability* and *impact* of identified trends on the product.

$$
\text{Trend Impact Score (TIS)} = P_{\text{trend}} \times I_{\text{trend}} \times T_{\text{time horizon}}
$$

Where:
- $P_{\text{trend}}$ = Probability of trend materialization (**0 ≤ P ≤ 1**)
- $I_{\text{trend}}$ = Impact intensity on product (**scale 1–5**)
- $T_{\text{time horizon}}$ = Time-decay factor (**0.2 to 1.0**)

### C. Scenario Planning (Schwartz 2x2 Matrix)
Peter Schwartz's classic framework uses **two critical uncertainties** as axes, generating **four distinct scenarios**.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU examiner frequently tests scenario logic: students must be able to *name the four quadrants* and *place a design intervention* within each.

### D. Backcasting
Reverse logic: define a **desired future** (e.g., "carbon-neutral product by 2035") and walk backward to identify the design decisions required *today*.

### E. Morphological Forecasting
Systematically combines *technology sub-solutions* to generate novel product configurations. Used heavily in patent-strategy and disruptive innovation studies.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Framework Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table: Forecasting Frameworks — At a Glance**

| Framework | Time Horizon | Output Type | Best For | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **STEEP / PESTLE** | 5–25 years | Qualitative driver list | Macro-context mapping | High |
| **Trend Impact Analysis (TIA)** | 3–10 years | Numerical score (0–5) | Prioritization of trends | High |
| **Scenario Planning (2x2)** | 5–20 years | Four narrative futures | Strategic robustness testing | Very High |
| **Backcasting** | 10–30 years | Reverse roadmap | Sustainability & vision-led design | High |
| **Morphological Analysis** | 3–10 years | Combinatorial matrix | Technology substitution | Medium |
| **Horizon Scanning** | 1–25 years | Weak signal catalogue | Early-warning detection | Medium |
| **Delphi Method** | 5–15 years | Expert consensus | Reducing forecasting bias | Medium |
| **Design Fiction** | 10–50 years | Provocative prototypes | Disruptive exploration | Low–Medium |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry** — Forecasting the transition from ICE to EV (Electric Vehicles) to autonomous mobility. Tesla's design pivot in the 2010s was a direct output of this discipline.
- **Consumer Electronics** — Apple uses scenario planning to decide between incremental (iPhone S-series) and radical (Vision Pro) design bets.
- **Healthcare Devices** — Forecasting ageing-population trends drives design of assistive wearables.
- **Architecture & Urban Design** — Backcasting from 2050 net-zero goals reshapes today's material choices.
- **KTU Capstone Projects** — The 2024 scheme mandates that capstone projects include a *future-readiness section* that is directly fed by this forecasting step.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Process Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Canonical 6-Step Forecasting Workflow

This is the **operational process** students must be able to reproduce in the KTU ESE. It is derived from the integrated practices of **RAND Corporation, IDEO, and the UK Government Office for Science**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for the Examiner:** **S-C-A-S-T-F** → **S**can, **C**luster, **A**nalyze, **S**cenario, **T**ranslate, **F**eedback.

### Step 1 — SCAN (Horizon Scanning)
Gather weak signals, emerging issues, and drivers of change.

**Activity:** Conduct STEEP/PESTLE, scan academic journals, patents, CES/IFA launches, social media sentiment, and policy white papers.

**Deliverable:** A *signal log* of **30–100 entries**.

### Step 2 — CLUSTER (Trend Identification)
Group related signals into discrete *trends* and rank them by **novelty, persistence, and reach**.

**Analytical check — Trend Triangle:**

$$
\text{Trend Strength} = \frac{N + Pe + R}{3}
$$

Where:
- $N$ = Novelty score (**1–5**)
- $Pe$ = Persistence score (**1–5**)
- $R$ = Reach / breadth score (**1–5**)

**Deliverable:** A ranked *Trend Catalogue* (typically 8–12 trends).

### Step 3 — ANALYZE (Impact Mapping)
Apply **Trend Impact Analysis (TIA)** to compute the *Trend Impact Score*:

$$
\text{TIS} = P_{\text{trend}} \times I_{\text{trend}} \times T_{\text{decay}}
$$

Detailed calculation steps (KTU frequently tests this):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3a:} \quad & \text{Estimate } P_{\text{trend}} \text{ from historical base rates.} \\
\text{Step 3b:} \quad & \text{Score } I_{\text{trend}} \text{ on the 1--5 impact scale (1 = cosmetic, 5 = existential).} \\
\text{Step 3c:} \quad & \text{Apply } T_{\text{decay}} = e^{-\lambda t} \text{ where } \lambda \text{ = 0.1/year, } t \text{ = years to horizon.} \\
\text{Step 3d:} \quad & \text{Compute TIS and rank trends.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example Numerical Walkthrough:**

Suppose a product team evaluates **three trends** for a wearable fitness device on a **5-year horizon**:

| Trend | $P_{\text{trend}}$ | $I_{\text{trend}}$ | $t$ (years) | $T_{\text{decay}} = e^{-0.1t}$ | TIS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AI-personalized coaching | 0.85 | 5 | 5 | 0.6065 | **2.578** |
| Solid-state batteries | 0.60 | 4 | 5 | 0.6065 | **1.456** |
| Haptic emotion sensing | 0.30 | 3 | 5 | 0.6065 | **0.546** |

**Interpretation:** AI-personalized coaching is the **highest-priority** trend and should dominate the design roadmap.

### Step 4 — SCENARIO (Build Futures)
Select the **two most uncertain and most impactful** trends as axes of the **2x2 scenario matrix**. Develop four narratives (e.g., *Growth, Decline, Discipline, Collapse* in the original Shell terminology; or *Optimist, Pessimist, Conservative, Revolutionary* in design-futures vocabulary).

**Naming convention for KTU answers:**
- **Q1 (Top-Right):** High trend A × High trend B → *Idealized future*
- **Q2 (Top-Left):** Low trend A × High trend B → *Transformation future*
- **Q3 (Bottom-Left):** Low trend A × Low trend B → *Stagnation future*
- **Q4 (Bottom-Right):** High trend A × Low trend B → *Fragmented future*

### Step 5 — TRANSLATE (Design Brief Generation)
For **each** scenario, produce a *Design Brief* that addresses: form, function, technology stack, user experience, and sustainability.

**Template (exam-friendly):**

$$
\text{Design Brief} = \{ \text{User}, \text{Need}, \text{Insight}, \text{Solution Idea}, \text{Success Metric} \}
$$

### Step 6 — FEEDBACK (Re-calibration Loop)
After prototyping, feed user-testing data back into the **signal log** for the next forecasting cycle. This implements the *double-loop learning* principle central to OBE.

## 3.2 Worked Derivative: Backcasting for a Sustainable Smartwatch

A KTU favourite — backcasting derivation in 5 explicit steps.

> **Target Future (2050):** A fully circular, biodegradable smartwatch with zero e-waste.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step A:} \quad & \text{Define the 2050 vision:} \\
& V_{2050} = \{\text{Modular PCB}, \text{Mycelium casing}, \text{Energy: body-heat harvesting}\} \\
\text{Step B:} \quad & \text{Define the 2035 milestone:} \\
& V_{2035} = \{\text{Modular PCB (50\% repairable)}, \text{Bio-polymer casing (TBC)}\} \\
\text{Step C:} \quad & \text{Define the 2027 milestone:} \\
& V_{2027} = \{\text{Open-standard screws}, \text{Replaceable battery}, \text{Recycled aluminium}\} \\
\text{Step D:} \quad & \text{Define the 2024 immediate action:} \\
& V_{2024} = \{\text{Redesign charging port as universal}, \text{Publish repair manual}\} \\
\text{Step E:} \quad & \text{Validate reverse path consistency:} \\
& V_{2024} \rightarrow V_{2027} \rightarrow V_{2035} \rightarrow V_{2050} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix (for the Examiner)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **When to use which method? — Decision Matrix**

| If your design challenge is... | Then use... | Because... |
|---|---|---|
| Long-term strategic (>10 yrs) | Backcasting | Vision-led, less prone to short-termism |
| Mid-term disruptive (5–10 yrs) | Scenario Planning | Handles deep uncertainty |
| Short-term product iteration (1–3 yrs) | Trend Impact Analysis | Data-driven, quick |
| Exploring radical alternatives | Design Fiction / Speculative Design | Frees imagination from constraints |
| Cross-industry technology fusion | Morphological Analysis | Combinatorial power |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The S-C-A-S-T-F Forecasting Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[stepA: SCAN Horizon] --> B[stepB: CLUSTER Trends]
    B --> C[stepC: ANALYZE Impact TIS]
    C --> D[stepD: SCENARIO 2x2 Matrix]
    D --> E[stepE: TRANSLATE to Design Brief]
    E --> F[stepF: FEEDBACK Loop]
    F -.->|Re-enter| A
    E --> G[artifact1: Future-Ready Design Roadmap]
    C --> H[artifact2: Trend Catalogue Ranked]
    D --> I[artifact3: Four Scenario Narratives]
```

**Reading Guide:** The solid arrows denote the linear flow; the dashed arrow denotes the *continuous re-calibration* feedback loop. The three artefacts on the right are the **deliverables** expected from a KTU Module 4 assignment.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — STEEP Environmental Scanning Layer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph macroLayer[Macro Environment]
        direction TB
        nodeS[Social]
        nodeT[Technological]
        nodeE1[Economic]
        nodeE2[Environmental]
        nodeP[Political]
        nodeL[Legal]
        nodeE3[Ethical]
    end
    macroLayer --> scanner[Signal Aggregator]
    scanner --> catalogue[Trend Catalogue]
    catalogue --> forecast[Forecasting Engine]
    forecast --> brief[Design Brief Output]
```

**Reading Guide:** The *Macro Environment* (left) is the unbounded source of change. The *Signal Aggregator* filters noise. The *Forecasting Engine* applies TIA, scenario, and backcasting. The output is a *Design Brief*.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — 2x2 Scenario Planning Matrix (Schwartz Framework)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph qmatrix[Scenario Quadrants]
        direction LR
        nodeQ1[Q1 IDEALIZED High A High B]
        nodeQ2[Q2 TRANSFORMATION Low A High B]
        nodeQ3[Q3 STAGNATION Low A Low B]
        nodeQ4[Q4 FRAGMENTED High A Low B]
    end
    axisX[AXIS X Critical Uncertainty A] --- qmatrix
    axisY[AXIS Y Critical Uncertainty B] --- qmatrix
    qmatrix --> robust[ROBUST DESIGN STRATEGY]
```
