**(b)** Explain the **OKR (Objectives and Key Results) methodology**. With reference to the same mobile application, write **one Objective and three Key Results**, and compute the OKR score given hypothetical achievement values. *(7 Marks)*

### **Model Answer for (a):**

The SMART framework was proposed by **George T. Doran (1981)** as a five-attribute filter for setting effective objectives. Each attribute is explained below in the context of the *KTU Fresher Campus Navigation App*:

**1. Specific (S):** The objective must be unambiguous, addressing *what* will be done, *for whom*, and *where*.
*Example:* "Reduce time taken by freshers to locate classrooms, labs, and key facilities inside the KTU main campus."

**2. Measurable (M):** A numerical metric must be attached.
*Example:* "From a baseline of 15 minutes to a target of under 4 minutes per journey."

**3. Achievable (A):** The target must be realistic given the team's resources, skills, and timeline.
*Example:* Achievable with 4 team members, a 2-month development window, and access to the existing campus Wi-Fi infrastructure for indoor positioning.

**4. Relevant (R):** The objective must align with an actual user need identified in the *Empathize* and *Define* phases.
*Example:* Directly relevant because user interviews revealed that 87% of freshers reported getting lost in the first two weeks.

**5. Time-bound (T):** A hard deadline must be defined.
*Example:* "Achieve by the end of Week 8 of the development cycle."

**Final SMART Objective Set:**

- **O1:** Reduce average time-to-locate-room from 15 minutes to $\leq 4$ minutes within 8 weeks of pilot launch.
- **O2:** Cover all 50+ academic and administrative buildings of KTU with verified location pins by Week 6.
- **O3:** Achieve $\geq 80\%$ task-success rate (user reaches destination correctly) during 25 supervised trial runs by Week 8.

**Self-Check Matrix (as part of the answer):**

| Objective | S | M | A | R | T |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| O1 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| O2 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| O3 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |

### **Model Answer for (b):**

**OKR Methodology — Definition and Origin:**
OKR stands for **Objectives and Key Results**. It was developed by **Andy Grove at Intel** in the 1970s and later popularised by **John Doerr at Google**. It is now the world's most widely adopted objective-setting framework in technology companies.

**Structure:**
- **Objective:** A *qualitative*, ambitious, time-bound, and inspiring statement of *what* must be achieved.
- **Key Results:** A small set (typically **3–5**) of *quantitative*, measurable outcomes that *prove* the objective was achieved. Key Results are **measurable, not activities**.

**Writing OKRs — Golden Rules:**
1. Objectives are **short, memorable, qualitative**, and inspiring.
2. Key Results are **aggressive but realistic**, and are **verifiable**.
3. Key Results are **outputs / outcomes**, not tasks.
4. There are usually **2 to 5** key results per objective.

**Example OKR for the KTU Campus Navigation App:**

- **Objective:** *Deliver a delightful first-day campus-navigation experience for KTU freshers.*

- **Key Result 1:** Average time-to-find-room reduced from 15 min to $\leq 4$ min.
- **Key Result 2:** Task-success rate (correct arrival at intended destination) $\geq 90\%$ in pilot trials.
- **Key Result 3:** Day-7 retention of the app $\geq 60\%$ among 50 pilot freshers.

**Hypothetical Achieved Values:**

- **KR1 achieved:** Time reduced to 5 minutes (target was 4 min).
- **KR2 achieved:** Task-success rate of 78% (target was 90%).
- **KR3 achieved:** Day-7 retention of 54% (target was 60%).

**Computation of OKR Score (Arithmetic Mean Method):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_1 &= \frac{4 \text{ min target} - 5 \text{ min achieved improvement}}{4 - 15} = \frac{11}{11} = 1.00 \\
&\text{(Wait, recompute carefully.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Let us re-compute using the **achievement ratio** method against the target.

**KR1 — Time reduction:**
Target time = 4 min, achieved = 5 min. *Lower is better.*

$$
r_1 = \frac{\text{Baseline} - \text{Achieved}}{\text{Baseline} - \text{Target}} = \frac{15 - 5}{15 - 4} = \frac{10}{11} \approx 0.91
$$

**KR2 — Task-success rate:**
Target = 90%, achieved = 78%. *Higher is better.*

$$
r_2 = \frac{78}{90} \approx 0.867
$$

**KR3 — Day-7 retention:**
Target = 60%, achieved = 54%. *Higher is better.*

$$
r_3 = \frac{54}{60} = 0.90
$$

**Arithmetic OKR Score:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score} &= \frac{r_1 + r_2 + r_3}{3} \times 10 \\
            &= \frac{0.91 + 0.867 + 0.90}{3} \times 10 \\
            &= \frac{2.677}{3} \times 10 \\
            &\approx 8.92 \;/\; 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:**
A score of **8.92** lies in the **0.8 – 1.0 range**, which is the **over-achievement zone** in Google's framework. This signals that the team's targets for the *next cycle* should be **raised** to maintain the 0.7 "sweet spot."

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Confusing **Objective** (qualitative) with **Key Result** (quantitative). The Objective must NOT contain a number.
> 2. Listing **activities** (e.g., "design the UI") as Key Results. KRs are **outcomes**, not tasks.
> 3. Writing **more than 5 KRs** per objective — this dilutes focus.
> 4. Forgetting to **state the deadline** in the Time-bound attribute.
> 5. Forgetting to verify the **Iron Triangle** alignment (Desirability / Viability / Feasibility).

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** What is the **Iron Triangle of Objectives** in Design Thinking? Explain each pillar with a product example. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **6-step process of setting objectives** in a product-development project. Apply it to formulate objectives for a **solar-powered water purifier designed for Kerala flood-relief camps.** *(7 Marks)*

### **Model Answer for (a):**

The **Iron Triangle of Objectives** (also called the **Three Lenses of Innovation** by IDEO) is a foundational concept in Design Thinking. It states that **any successful product objective must simultaneously satisfy three constraints**:

**1. Desirability (the Human Lens):**
*Does the user genuinely want or need this?* It is measured through empathy, user interviews, and observed behaviour.
*Example:* A user-research study reveals that 80% of Kerala flood-relief camp residents boil water using firewood, which takes 45 minutes. A water purifier objective must address this *real* need, not an assumed one.

**2. Viability (the Business Lens):**
*Does it make business sense?* It must generate sustainable revenue, reduce cost, or fulfil a sponsor's mandate.
*Example:* The solar-powered purifier must cost $\leq$ ₹8,000 per unit to be funded by state disaster-relief budgets.

**3. Feasibility (the Technology Lens):**
*Can it be built with current technology, skills, and time?* It must be technically possible within constraints.
*Example:* A 50-watt solar panel, a UV + ceramic filter combo, and a 20-litre tank are technically feasible with off-the-shelf parts.

**Why "Iron Triangle"?** Because the three pillars are **interdependent and rigid** — weakening one collapses the other two. An objective that is *desirable* but *not feasible* (e.g., a teleporter) is impossible; *feasible* but *not viable* (e.g., ₹10 lakh purifier) is unsellable; *viable* but *not desirable* (e.g., a forced-usage product) fails in the market.

### **Model Answer for (b):**

The **6-step process of setting objectives** is a structured, repeatable methodology that converts a defined problem into measurable goals.

**Step 1 — Re-read the Point-of-View (POV) Statement:**
*Example POV:* "Families in Kerala flood-relief camps need a self-sustaining source of clean drinking water because municipal supply is delayed by 48–72 hours during floods."

**Step 2 — Identify Stakeholders and Their Success Criteria:**

| Stakeholder | Success Criterion |
|---|---|
| Camp residents | Safe drinking water within 2 hours of camp setup |
| Relief operators | Easy to deploy, no daily fuel cost |
| Sponsors (NGOs) | Cost per unit $\leq$ ₹10,000 |

**Step 3 — Brainstorm Candidate Objectives (no filtering):**
- Reduce water-borne disease incidence.
- Eliminate boiling dependency.
- Provide 20 litres/day per family.
- Operate without grid power.

**Step 4 — Cluster and Refine Using SMART Filter:**

| Candidate | S | M | A | R | T | Pass |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reduce disease incidence | ✓ | ✓ | ? | ✓ | ✓ | Refine metric |
| Eliminate boiling | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | **YES** |
| 20 L/day per family | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | **YES** |
| Off-grid operation | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | **YES** |

**Step 5 — Map to the Iron Triangle:**

- *Desirability:* ✓ Addresses a life-critical need.
- *Viability:* ✓ Sponsor-funded relief distribution model.
- *Feasibility:* ✓ Solar + UV + ceramic filter, 3-month build cycle.

**Step 6 — Define Key Results and Measurement Cadence:**

- **O1:** Deliver 20 litres of purified water per family per day within 2 hours of camp setup.
  - **KR1.1:** Purifier output $\geq$ 20 L/day under direct sunlight.
  - **KR1.2:** Bacterial count $\leq$ 0 CFU/100 mL (BIS standard).
  - **KR1.3:** Setup time $\leq$ 15 minutes by a single untrained person.
- **O2:** Operate fully off-grid for 7 continuous days.
  - **KR2.1:** Battery autonomy $\geq$ 72 hours without sun.
  - **KR2.2:** Solar recharge time $\leq$ 6 hours under Kerala peak sun.
- **O3:** Reduce per-unit cost to $\leq$ ₹10,000.
  - **KR3.1:** Bill of Materials (BOM) cost $\leq$ ₹6,500 at 1,000-unit scale.

**Measurement Cadence:**
- *Daily:* Output volume and bacterial count (operational log).
- *Weekly:* Cost-reduction progress review with manufacturing partner.
- *Quarterly:* Field-trial review in 3 flood-relief camps.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. Skipping the **POV re-reading** step — examiners expect you to start from a defined problem.
> 2. Writing **operational tasks** ("assemble the circuit") as objectives — they must be **outcomes**.
> 3. Failing to **map to the Iron Triangle** — losing 2 easy marks.
> 4. Not specifying the **measurement cadence** — a 1-mark deduction.
> 5. Omitting **units** in KPIs (e.g., writing "20 litres" vs. "20 L/day/family").
> 6. Writing objectives that are **technically infeasible** (violates Feasibility pillar).

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- [x] **Setting Objectives** is the *bridge* between the *Define* phase and the *Ideate* phase of Design Thinking.
- [x] Every objective MUST pass the **SMART** filter — Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound.
- [x] Every objective MUST satisfy the **Iron Triangle** — Desirability, Viability, Feasibility.
- [x] The **OKR** framework pairs a *qualitative Objective* with **2 to 5** *quantitative Key Results*.
- [x] Google's scoring sweet spot is **0.7**; a score of **1.0** means targets were too easy.
- [x] The progress formula is $P(t) = \dfrac{M(t) - B}{T - B} \times 100\%$.
- [x] The classical OKR score formula is $\text{Score} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{A_i}{T_i} \times 10$.
- [x] Use the **V – M – O – K – A** mnemonic for Vision, Mission, Objective, Key Result, Action.
- [x] **Stakeholder mapping** is mandatory before drafting objectives.
- [x] **Strategy maps** (Kaplan-Norton) link Learning → Process → Customer → Financial.
- [x] Key Results are **outcomes**, never activities or tasks.
- [x] **Refreshing objectives** every quarter is the industry best practice.
- [x] The 6-step process: *POV → Stakeholders → Brainstorm → Cluster + SMART → Iron-Triangle Map → KRs + Cadence*.
- [x] **Vanity metrics** (downloads, page-views) are not objectives — they are *activity* metrics.
- [x] Always specify the **deadline** and the **measurement unit** in every objective.
- [x] OKR originates from **Andy Grove (Intel)**; SMART originates from **George T. Doran (1981)**.
- [x] For KTU 14-mark answers, always include a **self-check table** (SMART pass/fail).
- [x] Always **justify** an objective's relevance with a *user insight* from the Empathize phase.
- [x] For a **Kerala-context** product (KTU's regional emphasis), always anchor to **local user data** (Kerala flood relief, campus life, coir/handloom industries, etc.).
- [x] When scoring OKRs, **state the zone** (Red / Yellow / Green / Over-achieved) — examiners reward interpretation, not just computation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Identification and selection of manufacturing partner for pilot build
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Identification and selection of manufacturing partner for pilot build

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> **Manufacturing Partner Identification and Selection for Pilot Build** is the structured, multi-stage strategic procurement process by which a product development team (typically a startup, design studio, or OEM R&D division) identifies, evaluates, qualifies, and contracts an external or internal manufacturing entity to produce a **limited-quantity pilot batch** (typically **50–500 units**) of a newly designed product. The objective is to validate Design for Manufacturability (DFM), freeze the Bill of Materials (BOM), verify tooling, and de-risk mass production prior to commercial scale-up.

In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned framework for **GZEST605 – Design Thinking and Product Development**, this topic sits at the critical interface between the **Prototype Validation Phase** and the **Commercialization Phase** of the Design Thinking funnel (Empathize → Define → Ideate → Prototype → **Pilot Build** → Test → Launch).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wedding Venue" Intuition

Imagine you have invented a new dish (your product prototype). Before opening a full-scale restaurant (mass production), you want to host a **private tasting dinner for 80 food critics** (your pilot build). You now need a **commercial kitchen** (manufacturing partner) to actually cook your dish.

You would NOT just pick the first kitchen you find on Google Maps. Instead, you would:

1. **Shortlist kitchens** that have the right equipment (ovens, tandoor, sous-vide) — this is **Technical Capability Matching**.
2. **Visit and inspect** their hygiene, storage, and past food safety records — this is the **Audit / Facility Walk-through**.
3. **Ask for a sample cook** (a small paid trial batch) — this is the **Pilot Run / Trial Build**.
4. **Negotiate cost, exclusivity of your recipe, and what happens if they leak it** — this is the **Commercial, IP, and Legal Due Diligence**.
5. **Sign a contract** before the actual dinner — this is the **Master Service Agreement (MSA) + NDA + SOW**.

The kitchen you finally choose is your **Manufacturing Partner for the Pilot Build**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:**
> The pilot build is the **first time your design meets real production economics**. The partner chosen here will often determine whether your product can be scaled profitably. A bad choice at this stage kills more startups than bad ideas do.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in the Design Thinking Lifecycle

The **Design Thinking process** stresses *empathy* and *iteration*. The pilot-build partner selection step is where the rubber meets the road because:

| Design Thinking Stage | Role of Manufacturing Partner Selection |
|---|---|
| **Prototype Stage** | Hand-built, low fidelity — internal team only |
| **Pilot Build Stage** | Partner-fabricated, medium fidelity — **external partner needed** |
| **Mass Production** | Same or scaled partner — continuity is critical |

Selecting the wrong partner at the pilot stage introduces **latent DFM defects**, **tooling rework**, **IP leakage**, and **time-to-market slippage** — all of which violate the agile, customer-validated ethos of Design Thinking.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The KTU 2024 syllabus lists this as a key competency under the *Product Development Strategy* sub-module. Expected cognitive level: **Apply / Analyze (Bloom Level 3–4)**. You may be asked to justify a selection framework, list evaluation criteria, or critique a given vendor profile.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Stage Selection Architecture

The identification-and-selection process is not a single decision — it is a **funnel**. You start with a broad market of potential partners and progressively narrow down through structured gates.

```
Stage 1 → Universe Mapping          (Market scan, trade shows, referrals)
Stage 2 → Long List Generation     (15–30 potential partners)
Stage 3 → RFI Issuance             (Request for Information)
Stage 4 → Capability Assessment    (Technical + Commercial scoring)
Stage 5 → Short List & Site Audit  (3–5 finalists, on-site verification)
Stage 6 → Pilot Contract Award     (NDA, SOW, MSA, PO issued)
```

## 2.2 Core Selection Criteria — The "5C + 3R" Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5C + 3R Framework (High-Yield for KTU ESE):**
> This is the most examination-relevant framework in this module. Memorize it.

### The **5C** — *What you evaluate*

1. **C1 — Capability (Technical Fit)**
   - Does the partner possess the specific process technology required (e.g., CNC machining, injection molding, SMT assembly, sheet-metal fabrication)?
   - **Tolerances held** (typical: ±0.05 mm for precision; ±0.5 mm for general).
   - **Material compatibility** (aluminum, ABS, PC-ABS, FR4 PCB, etc.).
   - **Tooling capacity** (press tonnage, bed size, spindle speed).

2. **C2 — Capacity (Volume Headroom)**
   - Can they handle the pilot batch **plus future scale-up** without re-tooling?
   - Rule of thumb: partner's spare capacity should be **≥ 30%** of your pilot volume.

3. **C3 — Cost (Unit Economics)**
   - Quoted piece price + tooling amortization + NRE (Non-Recurring Engineering) charges.
   - Payment terms (typically **Net 30** or **Net 45** for established vendors).
   - Cost-reduction roadmap over 3 years.

4. **C4 — Compliance (Quality & Regulatory)**
   - Certifications: **ISO 9001**, **IATF 16949** (automotive), **AS9100** (aerospace), **ISO 13485** (medical), **CE / RoHS / REACH** (electronics).
   - In-house **APQP / PPAP** capability.

5. **C5 — Culture & Communication (Soft Fit)**
   - Responsiveness, English proficiency, time-zone overlap, design-engineering maturity, willingness to share DFM feedback.

### The **3R** — *What you de-risk*

1. **R1 — Risk (Operational & Financial)**
   - Vendor financial health (check D&B rating, annual filings).
   - Single-source vs. dual-source dependency.
   - Geopolitical / natural-disaster exposure.

2. **R2 — Reliability (Quality Track Record)**
   - First-Pass Yield (FPY) history — target **≥ 95%** for a pilot build.
   - Reject rate, on-time delivery (OTD) score, RMA history.

3. **R3 — Rights (IP & Legal Protection)**
   - Willingness to sign an **NDA**, **IP assignment clause**, and **tooling-ownership clause**.
   - Non-compete on your product category.

## 2.3 The Weighted Scoring Matrix — KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **central analytical tool** of this topic. Every KTU Part B question on vendor selection will expect either the matrix itself or a weighted-score calculation.

Let the **Weighted Score (WS)** for a vendor $V_i$ be defined as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
WS(V_i) \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot s_{ij}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $w_j$ = weight of criterion $j$ (such that $\sum w_j = 1$),
- $s_{ij}$ = normalized score of vendor $i$ on criterion $j$ (typically on a **1 to 5** or **1 to 10** scale).

A vendor is **selected** if and only if:

$$
\begin{aligned}
WS(V_i) \;\geq\; WS_{\text{threshold}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $WS_{\text{threshold}}$ is the minimum acceptable weighted score, conventionally set at **70% of the maximum possible score**.

### KTU Cheat-Sheet Table — Criteria, Weights, Rationale

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** I am using `\vert` instead of the vertical pipe `|` to prevent markdown table breakage.

| Criterion $j$ | Symbol | Typical Weight $w_j$ | Pilot-Build Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| Technical capability | $w_{\text{tech}}$ | 0.20 | Pilot must prove the process works. |
| Cost competitiveness | $w_{\text{cost}}$ | 0.15 | Pilot economics foreshadow scale economics. |
| Quality / Compliance | $w_{\text{qual}}$ | 0.20 | Defects are expensive to retrofit at pilot. |
| Capacity / Scalability | $w_{\text{cap}}$ | 0.15 | Avoid re-tooling at mass-production gate. |
| Lead time | $w_{\text{lt}}$ | 0.10 | Time-to-market is a startup survival metric. |
| IP protection | $w_{\text{ip}}$ | 0.10 | Protects the Design Thinking IP generated upstream. |
| Communication / Culture | $w_{\text{com}}$ | 0.05 | Smaller weight but a hard gate (knockout). |
| Location / Logistics | $w_{\text{loc}}$ | 0.05 | Affects freight, customs, agility. |
| **Total** | — | **1.00** | — |

### Worked Example of the Scoring Formula

Suppose three vendors are evaluated on five criteria (out of 5) with weights $w_1 = 0.25$, $w_2 = 0.20$, $w_3 = 0.20$, $w_4 = 0.20$, $w_5 = 0.15$.

| Criterion (Weight) | Vendor A | Vendor B | Vendor C |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capability (0.25) | 4 | 5 | 3 |
| Cost (0.20) | 5 | 3 | 4 |
| Quality (0.20) | 4 | 4 | 4 |
| Capacity (0.20) | 3 | 5 | 4 |
| Lead Time (0.15) | 4 | 4 | 5 |

**Step 1** — Compute the weighted score for Vendor A:

$$
\begin{aligned}
WS(A) &= (0.25)(4) + (0.20)(5) + (0.20)(4) + (0.20)(3) + (0.15)(4) \\
&= 1.00 + 1.00 + 0.80 + 0.60 + 0.60 \\
&= 4.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2** — Compute the weighted score for Vendor B:

$$
\begin{aligned}
WS(B) &= (0.25)(5) + (0.20)(3) + (0.20)(4) + (0.20)(5) + (0.15)(4) \\
&= 1.25 + 0.60 + 0.80 + 1.00 + 0.60 \\
&= 4.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — Compute the weighted score for Vendor C:

$$
\begin{aligned}
WS(C) &= (0.25)(3) + (0.20)(4) + (0.20)(4) + (0.20)(4) + (0.15)(5) \\
&= 0.75 + 0.80 + 0.80 + 0.80 + 0.75 \\
&= 3.90
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4** — Apply the selection rule. Threshold:

$$
\begin{aligned}
WS_{\text{threshold}} &= 0.70 \times 5.00 = 3.50
\end{aligned}
$$

All three pass the threshold, but **Vendor B wins** because $WS(B) = 4.25$ is the maximum.

## 2.4 Documents Governing the Selection

| Document | Purpose | Issued When |
|---|---|---|
| **RFI** — Request for Information | Cast a wide net, gather vendor capability data | Stage 3 |
| **RFQ** — Request for Quotation | Get hard pricing for a defined BOM | Stage 4 |
| **NDA** — Non-Disclosure Agreement | Protect IP before sharing drawings | Before Stage 3 |
| **SOW** — Statement of Work | Define deliverables, acceptance criteria | Stage 6 |
| **MSA** — Master Service Agreement | Set long-term commercial & legal terms | Stage 6 |
| **PO** — Purchase Order | Legally commit to the pilot batch | Stage 6 |
| **Tooling Ownership Agreement** | Clarify who owns jigs/fixtures/stamp dies | Stage 6 |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Companies like **Foxconn, Flex, Jabil, Tata Electronics, and Bharat Fritz Werner** all use variations of this exact funnel. The 5C+3R framework is taught in IIM courses on Strategic Sourcing and is also embedded in **Stage-Gate® processes** used by innovation-driven firms.

---

## 2.5 Common Pitfalls in Partner Selection

- **Lowest-bidder trap**: choosing purely on quoted piece price, ignoring tooling, NRE, and quality costs (the **Total Cost of Ownership** fallacy).
- **Ignoring soft skills**: a vendor with great machines but zero engineering responsiveness is a pilot-stage disaster.
- **No dual sourcing**: putting all eggs in one basket before the design is frozen.
- **Forgetting the tooling clause**: vendors often retain ownership of jigs you paid for, locking you in.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough of the Selection Methodology

## 3.1 End-to-End Selection Process (Derivation-Style Walkthrough)

The KTU 2024 ESE expects you to be able to **justify, derive, and defend** the selection logic step by step. Below is the exhaustive derivation of the partner-selection decision.

---

### **Step 1 — Define the Pilot Build Requirements (Inputs)**

Before searching for any vendor, articulate the **technical specifications** of the pilot build:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pilot Inputs} &= \big\{\, Q_{\text{pilot}},\; T_{\text{tol}},\; M_{\text{material}},\; C_{\text{compliance}},\; t_{\text{deadline}},\; B_{\text{budget}} \,\big\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $Q_{\text{pilot}}$ = quantity (typical range **50–500 units**),
- $T_{\text{tol}}$ = geometric tolerance (e.g., $\pm 0.1\,\text{mm}$),
- $M_{\text{material}}$ = material grade (e.g., Al 6061-T6, ABS, FR4),
- $C_{\text{compliance}}$ = required certifications (ISO 9001 minimum),
- $t_{\text{deadline}}$ = delivery deadline,
- $B_{\text{budget}}$ = budget envelope.

This step is the **Design-for-Manufacturability (DFM) freeze** — until this is done, vendor comparison is meaningless.

---

### **Step 2 — Universe Mapping (Long List Generation)**

Generate a candidate set $V_{\text{long}} = \{V_1, V_2, \ldots, V_k\}$ where $k \geq 15$.

Sources:
- Trade shows (e.g., **IMTS, Hannover Messe, electronica**).
- Industry directories (ThomasNet, IndiaMART, Alibaba verified suppliers).
- Referrals from peers and mentors.
- Government industrial parks (e.g., **SIDBI clusters, Coimbatore pump cluster**).

---

### **Step 3 — Issue the RFI (Information Gathering)**

Send a structured RFI to each $V_i \in V_{\text{long}}$. Required data points:

$$
\begin{aligned}
RFI_{i} &= \big\{\, \text{ProcessList}_i,\; \text{MachineList}_i,\; \text{CertList}_i,\; \text{RefCust}_i,\; \text{FPY}_i \,\big\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Knockout Filter** — Eliminate any vendor with:
- No relevant process on their machine list,
- Missing mandatory certifications,
- Negative reference checks.

The remaining set is the **short list** $V_{\text{short}}$ with $3 \leq \vert V_{\text{short}} \vert \leq 5$.

---

### **Step 4 — Issue the RFQ (Quotation Gathering)**

Send a sealed RFQ to each $V_j \in V_{\text{short}}$ with:
- Final 3D CAD pack,
- BOM with approved makes,
- Quality clauses (e.g., AQL 1.0, sampling per ISO 2859-1),
- Required delivery date.

Each vendor returns:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_j &= \big\{\, P_{\text{unit}},\; C_{\text{tool}},\; C_{\text{NRE}},\; t_{\text{lead}},\; \text{PPAP}\,=\,\text{Y/N} \,\big\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute the **Total Landed Cost (TLC)** for each vendor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TLC_j &= P_{\text{unit},j} \cdot Q_{\text{pilot}} \;+\; C_{\text{tool},j} \;+\; C_{\text{NRE},j} \;+\; F_{\text{freight},j} \;+\; D_{\text{duty},j}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $F_{\text{freight},j}$ is freight cost and $D_{\text{duty},j}$ is customs/duty. Use TLC — not unit price alone — for the cost sub-score.

---

### **Step 5 — Apply the Weighted Scoring Matrix**

Using the 5C+3R framework from Section 2, compute $WS(V_j)$ for each shortlisted vendor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
WS(V_j) \;=\; \sum_{m=1}^{9} w_m \cdot s_{mj}, \qquad \text{with } \sum_{m=1}^{9} w_m = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Accept if:

$$
\begin{aligned}
WS(V_j) \;\geq\; WS_{\text{threshold}} = 0.70 \times s_{\max}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Step 6 — Site Audit & Trial Build (DFM Validation)**

For the top 1–2 vendors (by $WS$), conduct an **on-site audit**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Audit Score} &= \alpha \cdot \text{Housekeeping} + \beta \cdot \text{Calibration} + \gamma \cdot \text{ESD}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$. Pass threshold = **0.80**.

Then issue a **paid trial build** of $Q_{\text{trial}} = 5\text{–}10$ units. Inspect for:
- Dimensional conformance to print,
- Surface finish (Ra value),
- Functional test results.

If trial build passes acceptance criteria, proceed to full pilot.

---

### **Step 7 — Award & Contract**

Sign NDA + MSA + SOW + Tooling Ownership Agreement + PO. Define acceptance sampling per ISO 2859-1 (AQL level). Define **Exit Clause** in case of vendor failure (bankruptcy, quality collapse).

---

### **Step 8 — Post-Pilot Review (Continuous Loop)**

After pilot delivery, compute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Vendor Performance Index (VPI)} \;=\; 0.4 \cdot \text{FPY} \;+\; 0.3 \cdot \text{OTD} \;+\; 0.2 \cdot \text{CostAdherence} \;+\; 0.1 \cdot \text{Responsiveness}
\end{aligned}
$$

If VPI $\geq 0.85$, retain for mass production. Otherwise, restart the funnel.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Summary — The Selection Equation

The full partner-selection decision can be expressed as a multi-criteria optimization:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^* \;=\; \arg\max_{V_j \,\in\, V_{\text{short}}} \Big[\, w_{\text{tech}} s_{\text{tech},j} + w_{\text{cost}} s_{\text{cost},j} + w_{\text{qual}} s_{\text{qual},j} + \cdots \,\Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

subject to the **hard knockout constraints**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cert}_j &\;\supseteq\; \text{RequiredCert} \\
Q_{\text{pilot}} &\;\leq\; \text{Cap}_j \\
P_{\text{unit},j} &\;\leq\; P_{\max}
\end{aligned}
$$

This formulation is essentially a **constrained weighted-sum multi-attribute decision model** — the standard tool taught in engineering management and operations research courses.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Partner Selection Funnel (Mermaid Flowchart)

The following Mermaid diagram captures the complete identification-and-selection workflow as a **process flow** with decision gates. All node IDs are alphanumeric, prefixed with letters, and labels contain clean uppercase text only.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[INPUT PILOT REQUIREMENTS] --> A2[DEFINE BOM TOLERANCE MATERIAL BUDGET]
    A2 --> A3[UNIVERSE MAPPING LONG LIST 15 TO 30 VENDORS]
    A3 --> A4[ISSUE NDA AND RFI TO ALL VENDORS]
    A4 --> A5{CERTIFICATIONS AND PROCESS PRESENT?}
    A5 -- NO --> A6[REJECT VENDOR]
    A5 -- YES --> A7[ADD TO SHORT LIST]
    A7 --> A8[ISSUE RFQ WITH CAD AND BOM]
    A8 --> A9[COMPUTE TOTAL LANDED COST]
    A9 --> A10[APPLY WEIGHTED SCORING MATRIX 5C PLUS 3R]
    A10 --> A11{WS GREATER THAN THRESHOLD 0.70?}
    A11 -- NO --> A12[ARCHIVE FOR FUTURE]
    A11 -- YES --> A13[SHORT LIST 3 TO 5 VENDORS]
    A13 --> A14[CONDUCT SITE AUDIT]
    A14 --> A15{AUDIT SCORE GREATER THAN 0.80?}
    A15 -- NO --> A16[DISQUALIFY VENDOR]
    A15 -- YES --> A17[ISSUE PAID TRIAL BUILD 5 TO 10 UNITS]
    A17 --> A18{DIMENSIONAL AND FUNCTIONAL PASS?}
    A18 -- NO --> A19[FEEDBACK DFM ITERATION]
    A19 --> A17
    A18 -- YES --> A20[AWARD CONTRACT NDA MSA SOW PO]
    A20 --> A21[EXECUTE PILOT BUILD 50 TO 500 UNITS]
    A21 --> A22[INCOMING QUALITY CHECK AQL ISO 2859]
    A22 --> A23{COMPUTE VPI}
    A23 -- VPI GREATER 0.85 --> A24[PROMOTE TO MASS PRODUCTION PARTNER]
    A23 -- VPI LESS 0.85 --> A25[RETURN TO SHORT LIST OR RESTART FUNNEL]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this diagram for KTU ESE answers:**
> Draw the same funnel with **5 horizontal bands**: Universe → Short List → Audit → Trial → Contract. Annotate each band with the document issued (RFI, RFQ, NDA, PO). This visually conveys the funnel-of-evidence logic and earns full methodology marks.

## 4.2 The 5C Plus 3R Criteria Map (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following diagram shows the inter-relationship among the nine evaluation criteria, grouped into the **5C** (capability) cluster and the **3R** (risk) cluster.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[5C CLUSTER EVALUATE] --> B2[CAPABILITY]
    B1 --> B3[CAPACITY]
    B1 --> B4[COST]
    B1 --> B5[COMPLIANCE]
    B1 --> B6[CULTURE AND COMMUNICATION]
    C1[3R CLUSTER DE RISK] --> C2[OPERATIONAL RISK]
    C1 --> C3[QUALITY RELIABILITY]
    C1 --> C4[IP AND LEGAL RIGHTS]
    B2 --> D1[WEIGHTED SCORE WS]
    B3 --> D1
    B4 --> D1
    B5 --> D1
    B6 --> D1
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[VENDOR SELECTION DECISION]
```

## 4.3 Decision-Cascade Block Diagram

The following diagram shows the **decision cascade** — each gate narrows the candidate pool.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[UNIVERSE OF VENDORS K EQUALS 30] --> F2[RFI GATE K NARROWS TO 12]
    F2 --> F3[RFQ AND SCORING GATE 12 NARROWS TO 5]
    F3 --> F4[SITE AUDIT GATE 5 NARROWS TO 3]
    F4 --> F5[TRIAL BUILD GATE 3 NARROWS TO 2]
    F5 --> F6[CONTRACT AWARD FINAL 1 OR 2 PARTNERS]
    F6 --> F7[PILOT BUILD AND VPI REVIEW]
    F7 --> F8{VPI GREATER 0.85?}
    F8 -- YES --> F9[MASS PRODUCTION PARTNER]
    F8 -- NO --> F10[RESTART FUNNEL AT RFQ STAGE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a block diagram and not a physical sketch:**
> The pilot-build partner-selection process is a **decision process**, not a physical artifact. A block/flow diagram is the correct visual primitive for KTU ESE answers. Reserve hand-drawn sketches (workshop layouts, jig drawings) for actual manufacturing-process questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q]`

**List any three criteria that should be evaluated when selecting a manufacturing partner for a pilot build of a newly designed electronic consumer product.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Any three of the following (1 mark each):

1. **Technical Capability** — possession of SMT lines, reflow ovens, AOI, and ICT equipment compatible with the product's PCB and component package types.
2. **Quality Certifications** — compliance with **ISO 9001** at minimum; **IATF 16949** if automotive-grade, **ISO 13485** if medical-grade.
3. **Capacity Headroom** — spare production capacity of at least **30%** above pilot volume to absorb scale-up without re-tooling.
4. **Cost Transparency** — willingness to disclose tooling amortization, NRE, and unit-price breakdown.
5. **IP Protection Posture** — readiness to sign a mutual NDA and an IP-assignment clause before any CAD data is shared.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q]`

**What is the difference between an RFI and an RFQ in the manufacturing partner selection process? When is each issued?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | RFI — Request for Information | RFQ — Request for Quotation |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Gather vendor capability and qualification data | Obtain firm pricing for a defined scope |
| Stage | Stage 3 (long-list narrowing) | Stage 4 (short-list evaluation) |
| Output | Vendor capability profile, certifications, machine list | Quoted unit price, tooling cost, lead time |
| Issued after | NDA signature | Successful RFI evaluation |

(2 marks for distinction, 1 mark for sequencing.)

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **Internal Choice Applicable — Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.**
> Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. KTU valuation gives 5 marks for content depth and 2 marks for clarity/diagrams/examples.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Adapted]`

**You are leading the product-development team of a startup that has prototyped a smart wearable fitness band. You must now select a manufacturing partner for a pilot build of 200 units. The product has an aluminum housing (CNC machined), a flexible PCB with BLE module, and a silicone strap.**

#### **(a)** Design a vendor evaluation framework. List the criteria you would use, assign weights (totaling 1.00), and justify the choice of weights for this specific product. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

For this **mixed-technology product** (CNC + electronics + polymer), the framework should be:

| Criterion | Weight $w$ | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Technical Capability (CNC, SMT, overmolding) | 0.20 | Three distinct process technologies must be in-house or tightly managed. |
| Quality / Compliance | 0.20 | Wearable touches skin → biocompatibility, ISO 9001 mandatory. |
| Cost / TLC | 0.15 | Startup budget sensitivity — but not the dominant criterion. |
| Capacity & Scalability | 0.15 | Pilot 200 → scale 50,000; vendor must not require re-tooling. |
| IP & Confidentiality | 0.10 | Proprietary BLE firmware and industrial design must be protected. |
| Lead Time | 0.10 | Time-to-market window for fitness category is **6–9 months**. |
| Location / Logistics | 0.05 | Domestic (India) preferred for agility and duty savings. |
| Communication | 0.05 | Startup needs a responsive vendor that tolerates DFM iterations. |
| **Total** | **1.00** | — |

**Justification narrative (3 marks for content):**
- The **highest weights** go to *Technical Capability* and *Quality* because three dissimilar processes (CNC, SMT, polymer overmolding) are involved — any single weakness cascades.
- *IP weight is 0.10* (not higher) because basic NDAs are industry standard; but it remains a **hard knockout** if the vendor refuses.
- *Communication weight is low but a hard gate*: a non-responsive vendor during DFM iterations will blow the pilot timeline.

#### **(b)** Three vendors have submitted the following RFQ responses. Apply the weighted scoring matrix and recommend the optimal partner. **(7 marks)**

Scoring is on a 1–5 scale; weights as above.

| Criterion (Weight) | Vendor P | Vendor Q | Vendor R |
|---|---|---|---|
| Technical Capability (0.20) | 4 | 5 | 3 |
| Quality / Compliance (0.20) | 5 | 4 | 4 |
| Cost / TLC (0.15) | 4 | 3 | 5 |
| Capacity & Scalability (0.15) | 3 | 5 | 4 |
| IP & Confidentiality (0.10) | 4 | 4 | 3 |
| Lead Time (0.10) | 5 | 4 | 4 |
| Location / Logistics (0.05) | 4 | 3 | 5 |
| Communication (0.05) | 4 | 5 | 4 |

**Step 1 — Compute $WS(P)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WS(P) &= (0.20)(4) + (0.20)(5) + (0.15)(4) + (0.15)(3) \\
&\quad + (0.10)(4) + (0.10)(5) + (0.05)(4) + (0.05)(4) \\
&= 0.80 + 1.00 + 0.60 + 0.45 + 0.40 + 0.50 + 0.20 + 0.20 \\
&= 4.15
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $WS(Q)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WS(Q) &= (0.20)(5) + (0.20)(4) + (0.15)(3) + (0.15)(5) \\
&\quad + (0.10)(4) + (0.10)(4) + (0.05)(3) + (0.05)(5) \\
&= 1.00 + 0.80 + 0.45 + 0.75 + 0.40 + 0.40 + 0.15 + 0.25 \\
&= 4.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $WS(R)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WS(R) &= (0.20)(3) + (0.20)(4) + (0.15)(5) + (0.15)(4) \\
&\quad + (0.10)(3) + (0.10)(4) + (0.05)(5) + (0.05)(4) \\
&= 0.60 + 0.80 + 0.75 + 0.60 + 0.30 + 0.40 + 0.25 + 0.20 \\
&= 3.90
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply selection rule:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WS_{\text{threshold}} = 0.70 \times 5.00 = 3.50
\end{aligned}
$$

All three pass the threshold, but **Vendor Q wins** with $WS(Q) = 4.20$.

**Recommendation:** Award the pilot contract to **Vendor Q**, with Vendor P as a **dual-source backup** (especially strong on quality and lead time). Vendor R is archived.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Adapted]`

**A design-thinking team has completed prototyping of a low-cost solar-powered water purifier for rural deployment. They must now choose a manufacturing partner for a pilot build of 300 units. The product combines injection-molded plastic components, a small PCB with MPPT controller, and a UV sterilization lamp module.**

#### **(a)** Discuss the importance of intellectual property protection and contractual safeguards when selecting a manufacturing partner for this product. What specific clauses must the contract contain? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

For a **socially-impactful product** with potential **government / NGO procurement**, IP leakage could mean the product gets reverse-engineered by a low-cost copyist, wiping out the startup's commercial and social-impact advantages.

**Specific contractual safeguards (1 mark each, with a maximum of 7):**

1. **Mutual NDA** — signed before any CAD, schematic, or BOM is shared. Covers drawings, firmware source code, and supplier lists.
2. **IP Assignment Clause** — all improvements, derivatives, and design-for-manufacturability feedback belong to the startup, not the vendor.
3. **Tooling Ownership Clause** — explicitly state that jigs, fixtures, and molds fabricated for the pilot are owned by the startup, with a Vendor Lien waiver.
4. **Non-Compete / Exclusivity Window** — vendor shall not produce competing water-purification products for any third party for **24 months**.
5. **Quality & Acceptance Clause** — AQL level (typically **1.0** for general industrial, **0.65** for safety-critical), right to refuse, and rework/repair cost allocation.
6. **Exit Clause & Tooling Buy-Back** — in case of vendor failure, vendor must return all tooling and tooling drawings within 30 days, against a pre-agreed buy-back price.
7. **Insurance & Indemnity** — vendor carries product liability insurance; indemnifies the startup against third-party IP claims arising from vendor-side processes.

#### **(b)** Propose a 6-step procedure to identify and select the manufacturing partner for the pilot build, citing the documents issued at each step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Pilot Requirement Definition (1 mark)**
Define: $Q = 300$, plastic = ABS/PP food-grade, PCB = 2-layer FR4, MPPT IC, UV lamp wavelength 254 nm, certifications needed = **BIS, ISO 9001, RoHS**, deadline = 4 months, budget = ₹30 lakh.

**Step 2 — Long List Generation & Universe Mapping (1 mark)**
Identify 15–20 vendors via IndiaMART, PlastIndia exhibitor lists, and local industrial parks in Coimbatore, Rajkot, and Faridabad. Issue **NDA** before sharing any technical data.

**Step 3 — RFI Issuance (1 mark)**
Send **RFI** capturing machine list, certifications, past reference customers in water-purification or solar-electronics, FPY history. Knockout filter narrows to 5–6 vendors.

**Step 4 — RFQ Issuance & TLC Computation (1 mark)**
Issue sealed **RFQ** with full CAD pack and BOM. Compute TLC for each:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TLC_j = P_{\text{unit},j} \cdot 300 + C_{\text{tool},j} + C_{\text{NRE},j} + F_{\text{freight},j} + D_{\text{duty},j}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Weighted Scoring & Site Audit (2 marks)**
Apply 5C+3R matrix. Top 2 vendors get a **paid site audit** and a **trial build of 10 units**. Inspect dimensional accuracy, UV-lamp integration, IP-65 housing seal, MPPT efficiency.

**Step 6 — Contract Award & Pilot Execution (1 mark)**
Sign **MSA + SOW + PO + Tooling Ownership Agreement**. Define AQL, define VPI = 0.4·FPY + 0.3·OTD + 0.2·Cost + 0.1·Responsiveness. If post-pilot VPI ≥ 0.85, promote to mass production.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize weights** so they sum to 1.00 — KTU examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this arithmetic error.
> 2. **Confusing RFI with RFQ** — these are *different* documents issued at *different* stages. Most students lose the 1-mark distinction.
> 3. **Ignoring the IP / NDA angle** — a complete answer on vendor selection without IP protection is marked down by **2 marks minimum**.
> 4. **No diagram in Part B** — KTU 2024 OBE rubric awards marks for visual representation. Always include at least one funnel or block diagram.
> 5. **Quoting only unit price**, not **Total Landed Cost (TLC)** — this signals shallow commercial understanding and is penalized.
> 6. **Skipping the trial-build step** — examiners view this as evidence that the student does not understand the iterative nature of Design Thinking.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Pilot build ≠ mass production.** Pilot builds are 50–500 units and serve to **de-risk DFM, freeze BOM, and validate supply chain** before commercial scale-up.
- The selection process is a **funnel**, not a single decision: Universe → RFI → RFQ → Audit → Trial → Contract.
- The **5C + 3R framework** is the KTU gold-standard for vendor evaluation:
  - **5C** = Capability, Capacity, Cost, Compliance, Culture.
  - **3R** = Risk, Reliability, Rights (IP/Legal).
- The **Weighted Score (WS)** formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
WS(V_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot s_{ij}, \quad \text{where } \sum w_j = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Total Landed Cost (TLC)** = Unit Price × Quantity + Tooling + NRE + Freight + Duty. Use **TLC**, not unit price, for fair comparison.
- **Hard knockout filters** must run *before* weighted scoring — non-negotiable items like missing certifications eliminate vendors regardless of score.
- **Documents to know (in order):** NDA → RFI → RFQ → MSA → SOW → PO → Tooling Ownership Agreement.
- **Always conduct a paid trial build** (5–10 units) before committing to a 300-unit pilot — this is the *Design Thinking iteration* principle applied to manufacturing.
- **Post-pilot VPI** = $0.4 \cdot \text{FPY} + 0.3 \cdot \text{OTD} + 0.2 \cdot \text{CostAdherence} + 0.1 \cdot \text{Responsiveness}$. Threshold = **0.85** for mass-production promotion.
- **Selection threshold rule:** $WS(V_i) \geq 0.70 \times s_{\max}$ (i.e., vendor must score at least 70% of the maximum possible).
- **IP and tooling ownership clauses** are non-negotiable for any startup or design-thinking-driven product.
- **Dual sourcing** is a strategic recommendation in early stages to avoid vendor lock-in.
- **Soft factors (communication, culture)** are typically given low weight (~0.05) but treated as **hard gates (knockouts)**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing procedures in pilot build
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Testing procedures in pilot build

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Testing Procedures in Pilot Build** refer to the systematic, pre-defined set of validation and verification activities performed on a limited-quantity physical prototype (the *pilot build*) of a product, prior to committing to full-scale mass production. In the KTU 2024 Design Thinking framework, this stage sits at the critical bridge between *concept prototyping* and *commercial launch*, where design intent is empirically validated against real-world performance, safety, durability, and user-acceptance benchmarks.

The pilot build typically involves producing **5 to 500 functional units** (the exact number depends on product complexity, statistical sampling needs, and regulatory requirements). Each unit is subjected to a multi-layered test protocol that combines *engineering verification*, *design validation*, and *user-experience evaluation*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis:** Module 4 of GZEST605 treats testing procedures as a *gate-keeping mechanism* in the Design Thinking lifecycle. The syllabus explicitly clusters pilot-build testing under the broader theme of *“prototyping, validation, and iteration,”* aligning it with **Course Outcome CO4**: *"Evaluate product designs through prototyping, testing, and iterative refinement."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a chef developing a new menu item for a national restaurant chain. Before rolling the dish out to all 200 outlets, you don't just cook it once in your home kitchen and call it done. Instead, you:

1. Cook the dish **30–50 times** in a *test kitchen* (pilot build).
2. Invite a panel of **focus-group diners** to taste and critique (user-acceptance testing).
3. Run the dish through **hygiene, calorie, and allergen** checks (compliance testing).
4. Stress-test the recipe by having cooks prepare it **rapidly under timed conditions** (manufacturability/reliability testing).
5. Only after all these checks pass do you **greenlight** the recipe to every outlet (mass production).

**Testing procedures in pilot build are exactly this — but for engineered products.** The pilot build is your *test kitchen batch*, and the testing procedures are the rigorous, documented checks that decide whether the product is *safe, functional, desirable, and manufacturable enough* to scale.

## 1.3 Why This Stage is Non-Negotiable

- **Risk Mitigation:** Catching a defect at the pilot-build stage is **100× to 1000× cheaper** than catching it after mass-production tooling has been cut (the industry-standard *Rule of Ten*).
- **Statistical Validity:** A pilot build provides enough physical units to perform **statistically significant reliability testing** (e.g., MTBF calculations, Weibull analysis on small samples).
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Industries such as medical devices, automotive, and aerospace **legally mandate** pilot-build testing under frameworks like ISO 13485, IATF 16949, and AS9100.
- **Design Verification vs. Design Validation:** Pilot builds are often the **first opportunity** to perform *design validation* (does the product solve the user's problem?) as opposed to mere *design verification* (does the product meet the specification?).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Exams:** Remember the acronym **"PIVR"** for the four pillars of pilot-build testing:
> **P**erformance · **I**ntegrity (structural/durability) · **V**alidation (user) · **R**eliability (long-term).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Strategic Position of Pilot-Build Testing

Within the **Design Thinking Double Diamond** model adopted in the KTU 2024 GZEST605 syllabus, pilot-build testing occupies the convergence zone of the second diamond (*Develop → Deliver*). It is the empirical counterpart to the conceptual *prototype testing* of the first diamond. While prototype testing answers *"Will it work?"*, pilot-build testing answers *"Will it work reliably, repeatedly, safely, and at scale?"*

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Testing Procedures in a Pilot Build

The procedures are not a single monolithic test, but a **layered hierarchy** of test families. Below is the high-yield classification matrix that KTU examiners frequently reference.

| # | Test Family | Primary Question Answered | Typical Methods / Tools | KTU 2024 CO Mapping |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Functional Testing** | Does each feature work as specified? | Manual test scripts, automated test rigs, pass/fail matrices | CO4 (Apply) |
| 2 | **Performance Testing** | Does it meet speed, throughput, efficiency targets? | Benchmark suites, load testing, stress curves | CO4 (Apply) |
| 3 | **Reliability / Durability Testing** | Will it survive its intended service life? | HALT, HASS, accelerated life testing, MTBF/MTTR | CO4 (Analyze) |
| 4 | **Environmental Testing** | Will it survive its operating environment? | Temperature, humidity, vibration, ingress (IP rating), salt-fog | CO4 (Apply) |
| 5 | **Safety / Compliance Testing** | Is it legally and ethically safe? | UL/CE/IEC standards, EMC/EMI tests, regulatory pre-certification | CO4 (Evaluate) |
| 6 | **User Acceptance Testing (UAT)** | Do real users accept and value it? | Field trials, beta programs, surveys, A/B comparison | CO4 (Evaluate) |
| 7 | **Manufacturability Testing (DFM/DFA)** | Can it be built at scale with consistent quality? | First-article inspection, process capability indices (Cp, Cpk) | CO4 (Analyze) |
| 8 | **Destructive Testing (Destructive Physical Analysis – DPA)** | What are the failure modes and safety margins? | Tensile, impact, fatigue, drop, crush tests | CO4 (Analyze) |

## 2.3 The KTU Reference Sheet — Key Metrics, Standards & Formulas

> [!NOTE]
> Even though this is a Design Thinking course, pilot-build testing borrows formal metrics from Quality Engineering. The table below lists the *exact* formulas examiners expect you to know for solving numerical or analytical KTU questions.

| Metric / Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical Boundary / Standard | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sample Size for Pilot Build** | $n = \dfrac{Z^{2} \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^{2}}$ | $Z = 1.96$ for 95% confidence; $p = 0.5$ (max variance) | Units |
| **Process Capability Index** | $Cpk = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | $Cpk \ge 1.33$ (industry acceptable); $\ge 1.67$ (world-class) | Dimensionless |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{\text{Units passing all tests on first attempt}}{\text{Total units tested}}$ | Target $\ge 90\%$ for a mature pilot build | % |
| **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | $DPMO = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per unit}}$ | $\le 3.4$ corresponds to Six-Sigma ($6\sigma$) | ppm |
| **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | $MTBF = \dfrac{\text{Total test hours}}{\text{Number of failures}}$ | Higher is better; compared against reliability spec | Hours |
| **Failure Rate (λ)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | Used in exponential reliability: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Failures/hour |
| **Reliability Function** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | For non-repairable systems under constant failure rate | Probability (0–1) |
| **HALT Stress Margin** | $M = T_{failure} - T_{operating}$ | Larger margin = more robust design | °C or g |
| **Beta Test Sample Size** | $n_{beta} = \dfrac{N}{1 + (N-1) \cdot e^{2}}$ where $Z$ is the standard normal value and $e$ is the margin of error | Heuristic: 5%–10% of addressable market | Units |

> [!TIP]
> **Why `Cpk` matters in pilot builds:** KTU examiners often ask *"Why is Cpk critical during pilot-build testing?"* — the answer is that Cpk quantifies whether the *manufacturing process* (not just the design) is capable of consistently producing units within specification limits. A design can be perfect on paper, but a low Cpk will doom mass production.

## 2.4 The Test Pyramid in Pilot-Build Context

A powerful conceptual framework (borrowed from software/hardware QA) is the **Test Pyramid**, adapted for physical-product pilot builds:

- **Layer 1 (Base — Most units, fastest):** Component-level bench tests (e.g., individual PCB, individual motor).
- **Layer 2 (Middle):** Sub-assembly tests (e.g., motor-controller integration, battery-pack validation).
- **Layer 3 (Upper):** Full-system functional & performance tests on the assembled pilot unit.
- **Layer 4 (Top — Fewest units, most expensive):** Field/user-acceptance trials under real conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to understand that the test pyramid is an *economic optimization* — running expensive full-system tests on every pilot unit is wasteful, while running only cheap bench tests misses integration defects. The pyramid balances **defect-detection coverage** against **test cost and time**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** OEMs (e.g., Tata Motors, Toyota) build *prototype fleets* of 50–100 vehicles for pilot-build testing, running them through **1 lakh km durability cycles** before greenlighting a model.
- **Medical Devices:** Class III devices require **Design Validation** on pilot builds as part of FDA 21 CFR 820.30 and ISO 13485.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Companies like Apple and Xiaomi run pilot builds through **drop, bend, water-ingress, and EMI chambers** before mass production.
- **Furniture / D2C Goods:** Pilot batches are subjected to **BIFMA, EN, or BIS** load and stability tests depending on the target market.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Procedure

## 3.1 The Canonical Pilot-Build Testing Workflow (10-Step Procedure)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following 10-step procedure is the **canonical answer framework** KTU examiners expect when a 14-mark question asks *"Describe the testing procedures followed in a pilot build"* or *"Explain with a flowchart how a pilot build is tested."*

### **Step 1 — Define the Test Plan (Test Plan Document)**

The Test Plan is the master document. It is created **before** any pilot units are built. It defines:

- Test objectives (mapped to design requirements)
- Test types (functional, performance, reliability, UAT, etc.)
- Pass/fail criteria (numerical thresholds)
- Sample size (computed statistically)
- Equipment, fixtures, and test environments
- Roles, responsibilities, and sign-off authority
- Risk classification of each test

$$\text{Sample Size (Infinite Population)} \quad n_{0} = \dfrac{Z^{2} \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^{2}}$$

$$\text{Sample Size (Finite Population Correction)} \quad n = \dfrac{n_{0}}{1 + \dfrac{n_{0} - 1}{N}}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The first formula assumes an infinite population; the second adjusts for a finite pilot-batch size $N$ (say, 50 units). For typical settings ($Z = 1.96$, $p = 0.5$, $E = 0.10$), $n_{0} \approx 96$. If $N = 50$, the corrected $n \approx 33$ units to test.

---

### **Step 2 — Build the Pilot Units (Under Controlled Conditions)**

The pilot batch is fabricated using **production-intent tooling and processes**, not lab prototypes. Critical rules:

- Use the **same bill of materials (BOM)** planned for mass production.
- Record every parameter (temperature, torque, cure time) for traceability.
- Mark each unit with a **unique serial number** for data correlation.

---

### **Step 3 — Incoming Quality Control (IQC) of Components**

Before testing the assembled unit, test the *parts*:

- Dimensional inspection (CMM, gauges)
- Material verification (spectroscopy, hardness)
- Supplier certificate review

$$\text{IQCR} = \dfrac{\text{Components passing IQC}}{\text{Components inspected}} \times 100\%$$

---

### **Step 4 — First-Article Inspection (FAI) on First Assembled Unit**

The very first fully-assembled pilot unit undergoes **FAI** — a comprehensive dimensional and functional comparison against the 3D model and CAD tolerances. This is the **manufacturability litmus test**.

$$\text{Capability Index} \quad Cp = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

**Conversion Logic:** $Cp$ measures process *spread*; it is centered on the nominal mean. If $Cp < 1.33$, the process spread is wider than the tolerance window — the design may need tolerancing review.

---

### **Step 5 — Functional Verification Testing**

Each pilot unit is run through a **Functional Test Procedure (FTP)**:

- Every feature exercised (on/off, mode change, calibration).
- Outputs measured against spec (e.g., voltage, current, force, flow).
- Pass/fail recorded per test step.

$$\text{Functional Pass Rate} \quad FPR = \dfrac{\text{Units passing all functional tests}}{\text{Total units tested}}$$

---

### **Step 6 — Performance & Stress Testing**

Units are pushed to **specification limits and beyond**:

- Thermal cycling (−20°C to +60°C)
- Vibration (random and sinusoidal profiles per ISO 16750 for automotive)
- Humidity / salt-fog for corrosion resistance
- Ingress protection (IP rating verification)

For **Highly Accelerated Life Testing (HALT)**:

$$\text{Acceleration Factor} \quad AF = \dfrac{\text{Test stress level}}{\text{Normal operating stress}}$$

**Conversion Logic:** $AF$ converts accelerated test hours into equivalent field hours. For example, $AF = 25$ means 1 hour of HALT ≈ 25 hours of real-world use.

---

### **Step 7 — Reliability / Endurance Testing**

A subset of pilot units is run **continuously until failure or test-end**:

$$\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{i}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} F_{i}}$$

where $T_{i}$ is the test duration of the $i^{th}$ unit and $F_{i}$ is the number of failures.

The reliability at time $t$:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}, \quad \text{where } \lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$$

---

### **Step 8 — User Acceptance Testing (UAT) / Field Trials**

Pilot units are deployed to a **representative user sample** in real or simulated real conditions. Data collected:

- Task completion rate
- Net Promoter Score (NPS)
- Mean Opinion Score (MOS)
- Failure modes observed in the field

$$\text{UAT Pass Rate} \quad UPR = \dfrac{\text{Users who complete core tasks successfully}}{\text{Total users}} \times 100\%$$

---

### **Step 9 — Design Validation Review (DVR) & Sign-off**

A **cross-functional review board** (Design, Manufacturing, Quality, Marketing, Compliance) reviews all test data against:

- Design Inputs (specifications)
- User Needs (from earlier Empathize/Define phases)
- Regulatory requirements

Decision outcomes:

| Outcome | Action |
|---|---|
| **Pass** | Proceed to mass production tooling |
| **Pass with minor fixes** | Engineering Change Order (ECO), re-test affected units |
| **Fail** | Return to Design phase (loop back into Design Thinking cycle) |

$$\text{Design Validation Effectiveness} \quad DVE = \dfrac{\text{Validated requirements}}{\text{Total requirements}} \times 100\%$$

---

### **Step 10 — Documentation, Lessons Learned & Knowledge Transfer**

Every defect, failure, workaround, and decision is **documented**. This forms the **Design History File (DHF)** in regulated industries and feeds back into:

- FMEA updates
- Test plan refinement for future revisions
- Supplier feedback loops
- Continuous-improvement (Kaizen) initiatives

$$\text{Lessons-Learned Implementation Rate} \quad LIR = \dfrac{\text{Actions implemented}}{\text{Actions identified}} \times 100\%$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Release vs. Re-iterate

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love presenting a *scenario* and asking whether to release the product. Use this matrix:

| KPI | Release Threshold | Re-iterate Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| First Pass Yield (FPY) | $\ge 90\%$ | $< 80\%$ |
| $Cpk$ | $\ge 1.33$ | $< 1.00$ |
| Field failure rate (first 30 days) | $< 2\%$ | $> 5\%$ |
| UAT task success | $\ge 85\%$ | $< 70\%$ |
| Safety-related defects | 0 open | Any open critical defect |
| Regulatory non-compliance | 0 open | Any open |

If **any KPI** falls in the re-iterate column, the pilot build has **failed** — the design must return to the Develop phase of the Design Thinking cycle.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flowchart — Pilot-Build Testing Lifecycle

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the complete, end-to-end pilot-build testing process described in Section 3. It maps directly to the 10 steps and is the diagram most often requested in KTU 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Test Plan Approved"] --> B["Build Pilot Units N=5 to 500"]
    B --> C["IQC of Components"]
    C --> D{"FAI Pass?"}
    D -- No --> D1["Rework Supplier / Reject Lot"]
    D1 --> B
    D -- Yes --> E["Functional Verification Testing"]
    E --> F{"All Functional Tests Pass?"}
    F -- No --> F1["Root Cause Analysis 8D / 5 Why"]
    F1 --> G["Engineering Change Order ECO"]
    G --> B
    F -- Yes --> H["Performance and Stress Testing"]
    H --> I{"Performance within Spec?"}
    I -- No --> F1
    I -- Yes --> J["Reliability Endurance Testing MTBF"]
    J --> K{"MTBF meets Target?"}
    K -- No --> F1
    K -- Yes --> L["User Acceptance Testing UAT Field Trials"]
    L --> M{"UAT Pass Rate >= 85 percent?"}
    M -- No --> F1
    M -- Yes --> N["Design Validation Review Board DVR"]
    N --> O{"All KPIs Green?"}
    O -- No --> P["Return to Design Thinking Develop Phase"]
    P --> Q["Iterate Design"]
    Q --> B
    O -- Yes --> R["Sign-off for Mass Production Tooling"]
    R --> S["Documentation DHF Lessons Learned"]
    S --> T["End: Product Released to Market"]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Layers of Pilot-Build Testing

This block diagram shows *what* is being tested at each layer, *with what tools*, and *who owns the test* — a frequent 7-mark sub-question in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L1["Layer 1 Component Bench Testing"]
        A1["PCB and Motor Bench"] --> A2["Spectroscopy and CMM"]
    end
    subgraph L2["Layer 2 Sub-assembly Testing"]
        B1["Battery Pack"] --> B2["EMC Pre-compliance"]
        B3["Motor Controller"] --> B2
    end
    subgraph L3["Layer 3 Full System Integration"]
        C1["Assembled Pilot Unit"] --> C2["HALT and HASS Chamber"]
        C1 --> C3["Environmental Chamber"]
    end
    subgraph L4["Layer 4 Field and User Validation"]
        D1["Beta Users"] --> D2["NPS and Surveys"]
        D1 --> D3["Field Failure Logs"]
    end
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4
    L4 --> E["Design Validation Review DVR"]
    E --> F{"Decision: Release or Iterate?"}
    F -- Release --> G["Mass Production"]
    F -- Iterate --> H["Return to Design"]
```

---

## 4.3 The Feedback Loop Within Design Thinking

This final diagram illustrates the **closed-loop nature** of pilot-build testing inside the broader Design Thinking cycle — emphasizing why testing is not a linear endpoint but a learning engine.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Empathize and Define User Needs"] --> P2["Ideate Concepts"]
    P2 --> P3["Prototype in Lab Low Fidelity"]
    P3 --> P4["Concept Validation Small Group"]
    P4 --> P5["Pilot Build Testing THIS TOPIC"]
    P5 --> P6{"Pass All Gate Criteria?"}
    P6 -- Yes --> P7["Launch to Market"]
    P6 -- No --> P8["Refine Design Based on Test Data"]
    P8 --> P2
    P7 --> P9["Post Market Surveillance"]
    P9 --> P8
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define "pilot build" in the context of product development. Why is pilot-build testing considered a critical milestone?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **pilot build** is a limited-quantity production run (typically 5 to 500 units) of a product using **production-intent tooling, processes, and bill of materials**, manufactured specifically to validate the design before committing to full-scale mass production. **(1 mark)**

Pilot-build testing is considered a **critical milestone** because: **(2 marks)**

1. It is the **first opportunity** to validate the design under *real manufacturing conditions* and *real-world usage*, bridging the gap between lab prototypes and commercial production.
2. It enables **statistically valid reliability, performance, and compliance testing** on physical units, uncovering defects that simulation alone cannot reveal.
3. It acts as a **go/no-go gate** — defects caught here are 100–1000× cheaper to fix than after mass-production tooling is finalized (the *Rule of Ten*).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List and briefly explain any three types of testing performed during a pilot build.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Functional Testing (1 mark):** Verifies that every feature of the product performs its intended function as per the design specification (e.g., a smartphone's touchscreen responding to multi-touch gestures). Pass/fail is binary.

2. **Reliability / Durability Testing (1 mark):** Subjects units to continuous or cyclic operation under elevated stress to estimate **MTBF** and identify wear-out failure modes before the product reaches customers.

3. **User Acceptance Testing (UAT) (1 mark):** Deploys pilot units to representative end-users in real environments; measures task-completion rates, satisfaction scores (NPS), and field failure modes to validate that the product genuinely solves the user's problem.

*(Other acceptable answers: Environmental Testing, Safety/Compliance Testing, Manufacturability Testing, Destructive Physical Analysis.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **step-by-step testing procedure** that should be followed during a pilot build of a new consumer electronic wearable device (e.g., a smartwatch). Your answer must include the test plan, sample-size justification, and the layers of testing.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of **Cpk, MTBF, and First Pass Yield** as decision metrics in deciding whether to release the pilot build to mass production. Provide typical target thresholds.

---

### **Model Answer for Question A**

**(a) Step-by-Step Testing Procedure for a Smartwatch Pilot Build [7 marks]**

1. **Step 1 – Test Plan Preparation (1 mark):** The cross-functional team drafts a Test Plan Document (TPD) covering functional specs (heart-rate sensor accuracy, step-counting, display brightness, battery life), environmental specs (IP68 water resistance, 1.5 m drop survival), regulatory specs (FCC, CE, BIS), and sample size.

2. **Step 2 – Sample Size Calculation (1 mark):** Using $n = \dfrac{Z^{2} \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^{2}}$ with $Z = 1.96$, $p = 0.5$, $E = 0.10$, we get $n \approx 96$ units (infinite population). If the planned pilot lot is $N = 200$, the finite-population correction gives $n \approx 65$ units to be formally tested.

3. **Step 3 – IQC and FAI (1 mark):** Incoming electronic components (PCBs, sensors, batteries) undergo IQC; the first assembled unit undergoes First-Article Inspection (FAI) against CAD.

4. **Step 4 – Functional Verification (1 mark):** Each unit's heart-rate sensor, SpO2 sensor, accelerometer, display, touch, Bluetooth pairing, and charging are exercised against spec. Failures are logged with root-cause codes.

5. **Step 5 – Performance & Environmental Testing (1 mark):** Units are placed in environmental chambers: temperature cycling (−10 °C to +55 °C), humidity 95% RH, and 1.5 m concrete drop tests per IEC 60068.

6. **Step 6 – Reliability Endurance Test (1 mark):** A subset (say 15 units) is run continuously for 720 hours (30 days), simulating wrist-wear cycles, charge-discharge cycles, and button-press cycles. Failures are tallied and MTBF computed.

7. **Step 7 – UAT / Field Trial (1 mark):** 20 smartwatches are given to beta users for 2 weeks. Task-completion rates, NPS, comfort ratings, and field-failure logs are captured. A UAT pass rate of ≥ 85% is mandated.

---

**(b) Cpk, MTBF, and FPY as Release-Decision Metrics [7 marks]**

**1. Cpk – Process Capability Index (2 marks):**

$$Cpk = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$$

$Cpk$ measures whether the *manufacturing process* can consistently produce units within specification. **Target: $Cpk \ge 1.33$.** A smartwatch with a battery-capacity specification of $400 \pm 20$ mAh requires the assembly process to demonstrate $Cpk \ge 1.33$; otherwise mass production will yield excessive scrap.

**2. MTBF – Mean Time Between Failures (2 marks):**

$$MTBF = \dfrac{\text{Total test hours}}{\text{Number of failures}}$$

For a smartwatch, the reliability spec might be $MTBF \ge 10{,}000$ hours. If during the 720-hour endurance test on 15 units (10,800 cumulative hours) 2 units fail, then $MTBF = 10{,}800 / 2 = 5{,}400$ hours — **failing** the spec. The design must be iterated.

**3. FPY – First Pass Yield (2 marks):**

$$FPY = \dfrac{\text{Units passing all tests on first attempt}}{\text{Total units tested}}$$

For a smartwatch pilot, **target $FPY \ge 90\%$.** An FPY of 75% indicates excessive rework — economically unsustainable for mass production — and signals upstream design or supplier-quality issues.

**Synthesis (1 mark):** All three metrics must *simultaneously* clear their thresholds. Any one falling below its target triggers a return to the Develop phase of the Design Thinking cycle.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **flowchart**, explain the **end-to-end testing procedure** of a pilot build, and discuss how the loop-back mechanism ensures iterative design refinement.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **design verification** and **design validation** in the context of pilot-build testing. Why are both necessary before mass production?

---

### **Model Answer for Question B**

**(a) Flowchart of Pilot-Build Testing with Loop-Back [7 marks]**

*(Refer to the Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.1 for the visual; the textual explanation below carries the marks.)*

The flow begins with **Test Plan approval**, proceeds to **build pilot units**, then through **IQC → FAI → Functional → Performance → Reliability → UAT → Design Validation Review (DVR)** in sequence. **(3 marks for correct sequence)**

At each gate (FAI, Functional, Performance, Reliability, UAT, DVR), a **decision diamond** is present. If the gate is failed, the process loops back to either *rework/ECO* (for assembly or component defects) or *return to Develop phase* (for design defects). **(2 marks)**

The **loop-back mechanism** ensures iterative refinement: every failed gate produces a **root-cause analysis** (5-Why, 8D, Fishbone/Ishikawa) whose findings feed back into the Design phase. This is the **empirical heart of Design Thinking** — design assumptions are tested against reality, failures are learned from, and the next prototype cycle begins with reduced uncertainty. **(2 marks)**

---

**(b) Design Verification vs. Design Validation [7 marks]**

| Aspect | **Design Verification** | **Design Validation** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition (2 marks)** | Confirmation that the *output* meets the *specified design input* (specs). | Confirmation that the *product* meets the *user's needs* and intended use. |
| **Question Answered (2 marks)** | *"Did we make the product right?"* | *"Did we make the right product?"* |
| **Typical Stage (1 mark)** | Performed during and after prototype testing (engineering lab). | Performed on **pilot builds** in (or close to) the real use environment. |
| **Methods (1 mark)** | Bench tests, simulations, dimensional inspection, accelerated stress. | Field trials, UAT, beta programs, simulated-use studies. |
| **Example (1 mark)** | Verifying that a smart bulb draws exactly 9 W as specified. | Confirming that users find the smart bulb's app easy to pair and use. |

**Why both are necessary (summing up):** Verification alone can yield a product that meets every specification but is unusable in practice (over-engineered, wrong feature set, poor ergonomics). Validation alone can yield a user-loved product that cannot be reliably manufactured. **Pilot-build testing is the unique stage where both can be performed on the *same physical units*,** providing the empirical foundation for the go/no-go release decision.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Pilot-Build Testing Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing prototype testing with pilot-build testing** (lose 2–3 marks). Prototypes are low-fidelity, lab-made, one-off units used to *explore* concepts. Pilot builds are production-intent, multi-unit batches used to *validate* the design. Examiners are strict about this distinction.
>
> 2. **Omitting the loop-back / iteration aspect** (lose 2 marks). Pilot-build testing is *not* a linear pass/fail exercise. The iterative feedback to the Develop phase is a hallmark of Design Thinking — always draw at least one feedback arrow.
>
> 3. **Listing test types without explaining *why* each test is needed** (lose 1–2 marks). Naming "HALT" or "MTBF" without stating the design question it answers is treated as superficial knowledge.
>
> 4. **Forgetting documentation** (lose 1 mark). The Design History File (DHF) / test report is a deliverable examiners expect. Always close your answer with a documentation step.
>
> 5. **Ignoring statistical rigor** (lose 1–2 marks). For 14-mark questions that mention a sample size, *show the formula*. For Cpk/MTBF/FPY questions, *state the threshold*.
>
> 6. **Skipping user validation** (lose 1 mark). Even in technical engineering answers, KTU's Outcome-Based Education framework requires mention of the **user** as the ultimate validator. Never end at the lab bench — always extend to UAT/field trial.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section or screenshot it for last-minute exam revision.**

- ✅ **Definition (must memorize):** Pilot build = limited-quantity (5–500 unit), production-intent batch used for validation testing before mass production.
- ✅ **Position in Design Thinking:** Sits at the boundary of *Develop → Deliver* in the Double Diamond; closes the loop with *Empathize/Define* through iterative feedback.
- ✅ **Four Pillars (PIVR):** Performance · Integrity (durability) · Validation (user) · Reliability.
- ✅ **Eight Test Families (know at least 5):** Functional, Performance, Reliability, Environmental, Safety/Compliance, UAT, Manufacturability (DFM/DFA), Destructive (DPA).
- ✅ **Canonical 10-Step Procedure:** Test Plan → Build → IQC → FAI → Functional → Performance → Reliability → UAT → DVR → Documentation.
- ✅ **Three Decision Metrics (with formulas):** $Cpk \ge 1.33$, $MTBF \ge$ spec, $FPY \ge 90\%$.
- ✅ **Sample Size Formula:** $n = \dfrac{Z^{2} \cdot p (1-p)}{E^{2}}$ (with finite-population correction for small $N$).
- ✅ **Test Pyramid (base→apex):** Component → Sub-assembly → Full system → Field/UAT.
- ✅ **Verification vs. Validation:** *"Right"* vs. *"Right product"* — both must pass on the same pilot units.
- ✅ **Rule of Ten:** Defect-cost multiplier grows 10× per stage (design → prototype → pilot → production → field).
- ✅ **Always include in your answer:** Flowchart, loop-back arrow, user perspective, documentation step, and at least one numerical metric.
- ✅ **Standards to mention (impresses examiners):** ISO 13485 (medical), IATF 16949 (auto), AS9100 (aerospace), IEC 60068 (environmental testing), IP rating (ingress).
- ✅ **Course Outcome mapping:** This topic directly satisfies **CO4** of GZEST605 — *"Evaluate product designs through prototyping, testing, and iterative refinement."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scaling from pilot build to full-scale production / implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Scaling from pilot build to full-scale production / implementation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Scaling** is the structured, risk-mitigated process of transitioning a validated prototype or pilot build into a repeatable, high-volume manufacturing system capable of delivering consistent quality, performance, and unit economics at commercial volumes. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, scaling bridges the **Design-Build-Test** phase of the Design Thinking cycle with the **Commercialization / Market Deployment** phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination-Critical):** Scaling is *not* simply "making more units." It is the engineered transition across **Technology Readiness Levels (TRL 6 → TRL 9)**, encompassing process validation, supply-chain hardening, regulatory compliance, and unit-cost optimization.

**Pilot Build (TRL 6 – TRL 7):** A small-batch, controlled-volume production run (typically **50–1,000 units**) used to validate the manufacturing process, identify bottlenecks, and gather real-world performance data before committing capital to full-scale tooling and supply chains.

**Full-Scale Production / Implementation (TRL 8 – TRL 9):** The mature, high-volume manufacturing state where the product is produced at commercial quantities (typically **10,000+ units per batch**) using hardened tooling, qualified suppliers, and certified processes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Board Exams:** A *prototype* proves the **concept works**. A *pilot build* proves the **process works**. *Full-scale production* proves the **business works**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **🍳 The "Home Cooking vs. Catering for 500" Analogy**
